a: Satan himself: she calls gradual abolition 'the very masterpiece of satanic policy,' a transformation of the prince of slaveholders into an angel of light who deceives even the wise and good with humane-sounding arguments for delay.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual
qt: But this GRADUAL ABOLITION, has been the grand marplot of human virtue and happiness; the very masterpiece of satanic policy. By converting the cry for immediate, into gradual emancipation, the prince of slave holders, ‘transformed himself, with astonishing dexterity, into an angel of light,’ and thereby ‘deceived the very elect.’

q: How does AR map Heyrick's diagnosis onto the modern pro-life movement?
a: Millions of Christians have been cajoled into ending abortion gradually, which has produced gradual indifference; apart from a few flashes of outrage, the church is largely silent, though that is beginning to change.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual
qt: It’s hard to read Heyrick’s work without seeing a depiction of the pro-life movement. Millions of Christians have been cajoled into gradually ending abortion which causes a gradual indifference to ending abortion itself.

q: What does the article claim would actually end abortion?
a: A faithful, uncompromising delineation of abortion's horrors by the hundreds of millions of Christians in the country; settling for ultrasound laws and waiting periods while calling small percentage reductions a win guarantees abortion will never end.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual
qt: A faithful, uncompromising delineation of the horrors of abortion by the hundreds of millions of Christians in this country would bring abortion to an end. But if those millions of Christians settle for Right to Life, and Right to Life-allied centrist Republicans passing ultrasound laws and waiting periods which reduce abortion by one or two or five percent and call that winning, there will never be an end to abortion

q: How does the article summarize the contrast between abolitionist and pro-life demands?
a: Abolitionists demand abolition because abortion is murder; pro-life leaders demand incremental regulation, conceded Roe's legitimacy, and treat abortion as a form of health care to be limited rather than as murder to be outlawed.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual
qt: Abolitionists demand abortion’s abolition. Pro-life leaders demand abortion’s incremental regulation. Pro-lifers conceded Roe’s legitimacy. Abolitionists called Roe what it is: illegitimate, wicked, and was never binding on the states. Pro-lifers regulate abortion as if it were health care. Abolitionists demand abortion be immediately abolished because it’s murder.

q: Whose arguments against incrementalism does the article point readers to for further study?
a: T. Russell Hunter at the 2021 'Pro-Life is Not Enough Conference,' where he also addresses 'Smashmouth Incrementalism,' a term coined by Moscow, ID pastor Doug Wilson.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual
qt: Check out T. Russell Hunter taking on the Incrementalist approach at the "Pro-Life is Not Enough Conference" in 2021. He also takes on "Smashmouth Incrementalism", which is a term coined by Moscow, ID pastor Doug Wilson.

q: What is the core problem Anglin says Kristan Hawkins and the Pro-Life Movement share?
a: Anglin argues both Hawkins and the broader Pro-Life Movement appeal to a manmade pragmatic standard rather than Scripture when defending compromised legislation, leaving them unable to justify their moral charges of "sin" or "pride" against abolitionists.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: Kristan Hawkins has a standard problem, and so does the broader Pro-Life Movement.

q: What specific scriptural objection did the SFA officer raise against Hawkins's heartbeat bill?
a: Jeremiah, the Students for Abolition officer, argued that Hawkins's legislative approach violates God's explicit commands against unequal weights and measures by appealing to Proverbs 20:23 and 11:1.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: a Students for Abolition (SFA) officer challenged Hawkins—founder and president of SFLA—arguing that her legislative approach violates God’s explicit commands against “unequal weights and measures” (Proverbs 20:23; 11:1).

q: How does Anglin formalize the Pro-Life Movement's reasoning, and what does he say is wrong with it?
a: He frames it as a syllogism prioritizing saving as many lives as possible through incremental laws, then runs a reductio ad absurdum: if maximizing lives saved is the only standard, even immoral acts like bombing clinics could be justified by their outcomes—which Pro-Lifers themselves rightly reject.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: If the standard is only “saving as many lives as possible,” then any means, no matter how immoral, could be justified by its outcomes. For instance, if bombing an abortion clinic delayed or deterred abortions, would that make it right? Of course not!

q: Why does Anglin call incremental laws an act of partiality?
a: Because Scripture defines partiality as applying the law unequally—excusing one class while condemning another—and incremental bills do exactly that by sorting victims and perpetrators by gender or circumstance instead of moral truth.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: Biblically, partiality means applying the law unequally, excusing one class of sinners while condemning another (Leviticus 19:15; Proverbs 17:15). Incremental laws commit this very injustice by distinguishing between victims and perpetrators based on gender or circumstance rather than moral truth.

q: What three functions of civil law does Anglin say the Pro-Life Movement misunderstands?
a: He identifies instruction (teaching right and wrong), restraint (deterring evil), and justice (executing judgment) as God's designed functions of civil law, all rooted in cited passages from Galatians, Romans, Deuteronomy, Ecclesiastes, Proverbs, and Amos.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt:
Instruction – It teaches what is right and wrong (Gal. 3:24; Rom. 3:20; Deut. 11:18–21).  
2. **Restraint** – It deters and restrains evil (Deut. 13:11; Rom. 13:1–4; Eccl. 8:11).  
3. **Justice** – It executes justice (Prov. 21:15; Amos 5:24).

q: How do incremental laws affect a mother's view of repentance, according to Anglin?
a: By framing mothers as victims rather than moral agents, incremental laws give a repentant mother nothing to repent of—the wrongdoing is portrayed as something done to her, not by her.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: Even a repentant mother would find, under such laws, nothing for which to repent, since the wrongdoing is framed as something done *to* her, not *by* her.

q: What did the Students for Life regional director's answer reveal about the movement's true governing principle?
a: When pressed on why SFLA opposes equal-protection bills that would save more lives, the director appealed to mothers being victims of abortion, exposing that a feminist "second-victim" premise—not maximizing lives saved—is the actual governing principle.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: Her response exposed the movement’s true governing principle: not the unqualified goal of saving lives, but submission to a feminist premise that casts mothers as the “second victims” of abortion.

q: What evidence does Anglin offer that abolitionist legislative efforts are bearing fruit?
a: He points to 23% of Georgia House Republicans supporting HB 441, an Equal Protection bill advancing out of committee in Louisiana, and similar efforts emerging nationwide as signs that faithfulness to God's standard is producing political gains.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: In Georgia, 23% of House Republicans supported HB 441; in Louisiana, an Equal Protection bill advanced out of committee; and similar efforts are emerging nationwide.

q: How does Anglin push back on Pro-Life claims of legislative success?
a: He notes that compromised bills preserve legal exemptions for self-managed abortions that go unreported, that documented self-managed abortions have risen even in "Total" ban states, and that citing rising birth rates as proof of lives saved is unreliable—birth rates also surged during COVID lockdowns.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: Reputable sources have documented increases in self-managed abortions in states with “Total” bans... When hard data is lacking, Pro-Lifers cite rising birth rates as proof of success, but birth rates are not a one-to-one measure of lives saved. By that logic, will they also take credit for the surge in births during the COVID-19 lockdowns?

q: What is the abolitionist syllogism Anglin offers as a contrast?
a: It begins with man's chief end being to glorify God, holds that God forbids partiality and unjust decrees on child sacrifice, observes that incremental Pro-Life laws show partiality, and concludes such laws are unjust, unbiblical, and immoral disobedience to God.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt:
**Premise 1:** The chief end of man is to glorify God and enjoy Him forever.  
**Premise 2:** God forbids partiality, unequal weights and measures, and unjust decrees concerning child sacrifice.  
**Premise 3:** Incremental Pro-Life laws show partiality (protecting some while excusing others).  
**Conclusion 1:** Therefore, such laws are unjust and unbiblical.

q: What does Anglin say is the ultimate question behind every legislative argument?
a: He insists the decisive question is "By what standard?"—and he rejects worldly-wise pragmatism, the view that the ends justify the means, in favor of obedience as the Christian standard.
t: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
qt: Ultimately, every moral and legislative argument returns to one question: *By what standard?* The Pro-Life establishment answers with worldly-wise pragmatism, the ever-shifting measure of what seems to “work” or “save the most.” God’s people cannot be worldly-wise pragmatists.

q: What was the author returning from when this airport encounter happened?
a: She was on her last leg home from a DC mission trip described as a Christian effort against abortion aimed at abolishing it and securing equal justice under law for all human beings.
t: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
qt: A “mission trip” of sorts with a bunch of Christians against abortion. Fighting to abolish abortion and establish equal justice under law for all human beings.

q: What pro-choice arguments did the woman raise during the conversation?
a: She claimed childbirth is the leading cause of death for women, that the unborn baby is just a clump of cells, and that abolishing abortion would kill women because miscarriage removal is also classified as abortion.
t: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
qt: from her, on how “childbirth is the leading cause of death for women,” “the baby is just a clump of cells,” “removing a miscarriage is also an abortion so if we abolish abortion, we’re killing women …”

q: How did the author feel after the conversation ended?
a: She felt isolated, exhausted, and emotionally fraught, perceiving the interaction as unsuccessful and fighting back tears in the airport and on the plane.
t: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
qt: There I was, sitting alone in a busy airport at 7 p.m., dead tired, emotionally fraught, feeling quite discouraged after what I perceived to be an unsuccessful interaction.

q: Where did the author redirect her focus to find comfort?
a: She deliberately turned her gaze to Christ rather than fixating on the woman, herself, or seeking comfort from the surrounding strangers.
t: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
qt: I fought to turn my gaze on Christ — not on that woman, not on myself, not desperately on the crowd of strangers around me.

q: How does the author reframe her sense of responsibility for the outcome of evangelism?
a: She separates duty from results, asserting that her responsibility is sharing the truth of the gospel, while changing minds and saving souls belong to God alone.
t: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
qt: I am not responsible for changing minds on abortion. I am responsible for sharing the truth. I am not responsible for anyone’s salvation. I am responsible for sharing the Gospel, but not for saving anyone.

q: What spiritual diagnosis does the author offer of the woman she spoke with?
a: She views the woman as lost and spiritually under the authority of the devil, in the same condition she herself was once in before being saved.
t: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
qt: That woman was lost; she needed a Savior just as much as I did once (and still do). She spoke and thought and acted not entirely on her own authority but under that of her father, the devil.

q: What attitude does the author commend toward worst-case scenarios after a hard mission?
a: She rests in God's sovereignty even in catastrophic outcomes, trusting that He is in control, unsurprised, and will work everything for her good and His glory.
t: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
qt: If the conversation bombs, if the plane goes down (I had that worry too), if everything goes south: He is in control, He is not surprised, and He will work it all for my good and His glory.

q: Who is the author and what document does she affiliate herself with?
a: Emily Curtis is a first-generation abolitionist, writer, and agriculture enthusiast from Michigan, and she is a signer of the Norman Statement.
t: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
qt: Emily Curtis is a first-generation abolitionist, writer and agriculture enthusiast from Michigan, striving by the grace of God to live life “Coram Deo.” Emily is a signer of the [Norman Statement]

q: Why do abolitionists insist that abortion be abolished totally, without any exceptions?
a: Because abortion is murder in every case, and the same moral logic that demands its immediate abolition also demands its total abolition. Carving out exceptions would be admitting that some children may be murdered based on circumstance, which contradicts the very reason abolition is required.
t: No Exceptions
qt: For the same reasons that we demand abortion’s immediate abolition, we demand abortion’s total abolition. That means no exceptions. No exceptions which allow for murdering babies because of the circumstances in which they were conceived. No exceptions that allow for murdering babies because they have a disease or disorder. No exceptions whatsoever. If abortion is murder, and we all know it is, it must be abolished entirely.

q: Isn't accepting some exceptions a pragmatic way to make progress against abortion?
a: No. The article argues that exceptions are not only morally repugnant but also pragmatically devastating, because compromising on a capital evil destroys the moral authority that drives the cause forward.
t: No Exceptions
qt: Not only are exceptions morally repugnant, they are devastating to the cause pragmatically. To compromise on an issue like child sacrifice is to fatally weaken the foundation of the moral argument.

q: What does Elizabeth Heyrick's warning to slavery abolitionists have to do with abortion exceptions?
a: Heyrick observed that compromise weakened, rather than strengthened, the anti-slavery cause. The article applies the same lesson: every concession on abortion erodes the conviction that abolitionists are right and ultimately undermines their success.
t: No Exceptions
qt: On this great questions, the spirit of accommodation and conciliation has been a spirit of delusion. The abolitionists have lost, rather than gained ground by it; their cause has been weakened, instead of strengthened. The great interests of truth and justice are betrayed, rather than supported, by all softening qualifying concessions. Every iota which is yielded of their rightful claims, impairs the conviction of their rectitude, and, consequently, weakens their success.

q: How is the abolitionist case against abortion different from the standard pro-life case?
a: The pro-life movement appeals to suffering, heartbeat, or harm to women, while abolitionism grounds opposition in the image of God in human beings and in Christ's incarnation to rescue mankind, making it a gospel conflict with the evils of the age rather than a political argument.
t: No Exceptions
qt: While the pro-life movement will argue that abortion is wrong because it causes pain, stops a beating heart, or betrays women, abolitionists maintain that abortion is wrong because human beings are created in the image of God and because the Creator Himself became a man in order to rescue mankind from sin, death, and eternal separation from God.

q: Does a child conceived in rape or incest forfeit the right to live?
a: No. The article frames any exception based on the circumstances of conception as murder, since the child's humanity is not contingent on how he or she came to exist. The article groups rape and incest exceptions with all other circumstantial exceptions that must be rejected.
t: No Exceptions
qt: No exceptions which allow for murdering babies because of the circumstances in which they were conceived.

q: What about a child diagnosed with a serious disability or genetic abnormality?
a: A shorter or harder life does not justify killing the child. The article calls this exception facially evil and heartless toward both the preborn child and disabled people who are already born.
t: No Exceptions
qt: Regarding physical or genetic abnormalities, the fact that a child might have a shorter or more difficult life does not justify the right to murder them. This exception for abortion is so facially evil, absurd, and heartless toward the preborn child as well as born people with disabilities that it’s hard to believe anyone makes it.

q: What if continuing the pregnancy puts the mother's life in danger?
a: The article concedes that early delivery may sometimes be necessary, lowering the child's odds of survival, but insists intentional killing is never permitted. Doctors must remain healers, not executioners.
t: No Exceptions
qt: Regarding situations where the life of the mother is in jeopardy, there is no circumstance where the baby must be intentionally murdered. There are cases where the child must be delivered early, and in those cases, the child may have a lower probability of survival than a child born at full-term, but intentional murder must not be allowed as an option. Doctors must be healers, not killers.

q: Why does the article call exception-based compromise 'double-minded wickedness'?
a: Because if abortion truly is murder, then negotiating exceptions treats a capital crime like ordinary politics. That posture betrays the very truth that justifies opposing abortion in the first place.
t: No Exceptions
qt: To compromise with and allow exceptions for a capital crime would be double-minded wickedness. To make concessions with an evil such as slavery or abortion as if issues of murder and dehumanization were like any other political back-and-forth is to weaken the power that truth and justice hold over the discussion.

q: What does it actually mean to be an 'abolitionist' rather than just someone who wants abortion to end?
a: Abolitionism is more than a goal of ending a practice. It is a confrontation between God's revealed truth and the evils and worldly wisdom of the age, modeled on figures like William Lloyd Garrison who sought to make Christianity the enemy of all sin.
t: No Exceptions
qt: To be an abolitionist is more than simply working to end something. Anyone can work to end something. Abolitionism is about bringing the truth revealed in God’s word into conflict with the evils of the age and the worldly wisdom of man.

q: How does William Lloyd Garrison's posture inform the abolitionist stance against exceptions?
a: Garrison framed his work as making Christianity the enemy of everything sinful, which the article cites as the model for refusing any compromise with abortion. Successful human rights campaigns have historically rested on this uncompromising foundation.
t: No Exceptions
qt: Successful human rights campaigns throughout history have been built on this foundation. Perhaps the best articulation of what it means to bring the Gospel into conflict with the evil of the age comes from slavery abolitionist William Lloyd Garrison when he said, “My fanaticism is to make Christianity the enemy of all that is sinful.”

q: What is the article's closing call to action for those who oppose abortion?
a: Reject every compromise on dehumanization, demand equal justice and protection for all human beings, and pursue total and immediate abolition. Any spirit of accommodation strips the movement of the power of truth and justice.
t: No Exceptions
qt: Never compromise on dehumanization. Demand equal justice and protection for all human beings, meaning abortion's total and immediate abolition. To have a spirit of compromise, or accommodation and conciliation as Heyrick put it, is to fight against abortion without the power of truth and justice. God forbid we ever do so.

q: Why does the Norman Statement insist that fighting abortion must be explicitly gospel-centered rather than purely a humanitarian or secular cause?
a: Because the statement defines abortion as fundamentally sin (specifically the sin of child sacrifice) which arises from suppression of the truth, the drafters argue that only the gospel of Jesus Christ can address the root cause. Activism detached from the gospel is therefore deemed insufficient, and activism centered on a false gospel is denied the label 'abolitionist' altogether.
t: Norman Statement
qt: WE DENY that anti-abortion activism detached from the gospel and a Christian worldview is sufficient to address abortion because abortion is, fundamentally, sin resulting from suppression of the truth.⁷ We deny that activism centered on a false gospel is abolitionist in nature.

q: What does the statement say should be the primary institution leading the work of abolition, and what does it warn against?
a: Article III asserts that abolition is to be led by the body of Christ—the Church—expressed in local churches, and that no political party, parachurch ministry, or abolitionist organization should be the primary driving force. It also rebukes churches that remain silent in the midst of what it calls a holocaust.
t: Norman Statement
qt: WE DENY that any political party, parachurch ministry, or abolitionist organization should be the primary driving force behind the Abolitionist Movement.⁷ We deny that God approves of any church that remains silent and refuses to act in the midst of a holocaust.

q: How does the Norman Statement frame pro-life incremental legislation, and why does it reject it?
a: Article V brands incremental legislation that regulates and thereby allows abortion as an 'iniquitous decree,' arguing it forfeits the moral standard needed to combat abortion and serves as a distraction from genuine repentance. The drafters summarize the position with the maxim that incrementalism in theory is perpetuity in practice.
t: Norman Statement
qt: We affirm that incremental legislation which regulates and thereby allows abortion is an iniquitous decree.³ We affirm that pro-life legislation is often used as a substitute that distracts Christians from the call for abolition and allows pro-life civil magistrates to be celebrated for doing less than they are commanded by God in Scripture.

q: Does the statement claim immediate abolition is guaranteed if the Church demands it?
a: No. While the statement insists that the demand must be for immediate abolition without compromise, it explicitly denies that demanding immediacy guarantees immediate results, acknowledging that national repentance and abolition usually take time and is not guaranteed for any nation.
t: Norman Statement
qt: We deny that our demand for immediate abolition means that the goal will necessarily be achieved immediately because the work of bringing about national repentance and abolition usually takes time and is not guaranteed for any nation.

q: What is the statement's position on whether mothers who procure abortions should be subject to prosecution?
a: Article VII denies that mothers should receive blanket statutory immunity, arguing that such partiality is unjust and enables a self-managed abortion industry. It also affirms that varying circumstances should be weighed by prosecutors, judges, and juries on a case-by-case basis, and that anyone who knowingly and willfully participates in killing a preborn child is guilty.
t: Norman Statement
qt: WE DENY that the mother of the preborn child should be given blanket statutory immunity from prosecution in the killing of her child because this partiality is unjust and enables a self-managed abortion industry.

q: What constitutional and biblical grounds does the document offer for criminalizing abortion as homicide?
a: Article VII grounds equal protection in two pillars: Scripture's repeated prohibitions of partiality, which the drafters say forbid classifying abortion as anything less than homicide, and the equal protection clause of the 14th Amendment of the U.S. Constitution, which they argue prohibits states from treating abortion differently from other homicides.
t: Norman Statement
qt: WE AFFIRM that abortion is murder and must be criminalized as such, that preborn children are image-bearers who deserve the exact same legal protection afforded to everyone else,¹ that scripture’s repeated prohibitions of partiality² prohibit the legal classification of abortion as anything other than homicide, and that the equal protection clause of the 14th amendment of the U.S. Constitution prohibits states from classifying abortion as anything other than homicide.

q: What does the statement say a lesser civil magistrate should do when a higher authority commands compliance with abortion laws?
a: Article VIII teaches the doctrine of the lesser magistrate: when a lesser official must choose between obeying a higher magistrate or obeying God, he must follow the higher Law of God, and he should defy the higher magistrate whenever necessary to abolish abortion. The statement further declares that compliance with any future federal pro-abortion ruling would be a sin worthy of church discipline.
t: Norman Statement
qt: We affirm that whenever it is necessary for a lesser magistrate to defy a higher magistrate in order to abolish abortion, the lesser magistrate should do so.⁴ We affirm that every state and federal governing official who complied with Roe v. Wade between 1973-2022 sinned in so doing. We affirm that compliance with any future pro-abortion ruling, order, or law of the federal government would be a sin worthy of church discipline.

q: Why does the Norman Statement insist on linguistic separation between 'abolitionist' and 'pro-life'?
a: Article IX argues that careful linguistic differentiation is strategically necessary because the Pro-Life Movement is so adrift in secularism and compromise that conflating the two blurs the line between sound and false doctrine and hinders necessary repentance. The drafters deny this is about tribalism or branding for any particular organization.
t: Norman Statement
qt: We affirm that careful linguistic differentiation between abolitionist and pro-life ideas, organizations, bills, and candidates is necessary to the existence of the Abolitionist Movement as a separate entity from the compromised Pro-Life Movement and helpful to the success of abolitionist initiatives and efforts.

q: Does the statement consider abolishing abortion to be part of the gospel itself?
a: No. Article I carefully distinguishes that abolishing abortion is not the content of the gospel, but rather an implication of it and a Great Commission issue, in which Christians are commanded to teach people to obey all that Christ has commanded. Article XI separately defines the gospel proper as Christ's person and work for the salvation of sinners.
t: Norman Statement
qt: We affirm that abolishing abortion, while not the content of the gospel (see Article XI), is an implication of the gospel, thus, a Great Commission issue, wherein Christians are charged to teach people to obey all that Christ has commanded⁶ (See Article X).

q: How does the statement view the role of public polling and political pragmatism in shaping the movement's strategy?
a: Article II rejects worldly pragmatism as both unfaithful to Scripture and ineffective for lasting change. It explicitly denies that public polling or political winds should dictate what Christians say or do in the political sphere, instead grounding strategy in obedience to God whose sovereignty governs results.
t: Norman Statement
qt: WE DENY that worldly-wise pragmatism is faithful to Scripture, and that it can achieve real, lasting social and political change. We deny that public polling or the political winds should dictate what Christians say and do in the political sphere.

q: What does the statement say about America's standing before God and what is required to avert judgment?
a: Article VI declares the United States is under God's judgment, evidenced by widespread acceptance of child sacrifice, and that the only remedy is national repentance, which must include forsaking sin, prayer, fasting, solemn assemblies, and bearing fruit through the establishment of justice for preborn children. The statement denies that any substitute for national repentance can avert national judgment and ruin.
t: Norman Statement
qt: We deny that the United States has any special status that would exempt us from God’s judgment.⁵ We deny that there is any effective substitute for national repentance that would avert national judgment and ruin.

q: What stance does the Norman Statement take on the sufficiency of secular argumentation in the abortion debate?
a: Article IV denies that politics is a neutral sphere in which God's Word is not binding, and further denies that secular argumentation alone is sufficient to address abortion, on the grounds that secular reasoning cannot ultimately justify objective truth or human rights.
t: Norman Statement
qt: WE DENY that politics is a neutral sphere where the Word of God is not binding.⁸ We deny that secular argumentation alone is sufficient to address the sin of abortion because secular reasoning cannot provide a justification for objective truth or human rights.

q: Who drafted the Norman Statement and what kinds of organizations do they represent?
a: The drafters listed include pastors and leaders such as T. Russell Hunter (founder of Abolitionists Rising), Dusty Deevers (Grace Reformed Baptist Church of Elgin), Bill Ascol (Founders Ministries board member), Brett Baggett (Ekklesia Muskogee), Austin Beigel (End Abortion Ohio), Wes Scroggins (Abolish Abortion Missouri), and many others connected to state-level abolish-abortion organizations and Reformed Baptist churches.
t: Norman Statement
qt:
#### T. Russell Hunter

Founder | [Abolitionists Rising](https://abolitionistsrising.com/index.html)

q: Why do abolitionists say Roe v. Wade was 'null and void' the moment it was handed down, rather than a binding law that had to wait until 2022 to be undone?
a: Because no government has authority to legalize child sacrifice. A decree that purports to authorize mass murder is not a law at all; it is invalid from the moment it is uttered, and the states sinned by complying with it for fifty years.
t: Nullification
qt: As abolitionists, we believe that no government possesses the rightful authority to legalize child sacrifice. The moment Supreme Court Justice Harry Blackmun delivered the majority decision in *Roe v. Wade*, the decision was legally null and void and should have been treated as such. By complying with the Supreme Court’s order to allow mass murder for 50 years, the states made themselves complicit in the murder of more than 65 million human beings.

q: With Roe overturned, why is the doctrine of nullification still relevant in 2025?
a: Federal Democrats have openly stated they intend to codify Roe by statute, pack the Court, or both, and pro-abortion states are mandating legal abortion of all their municipalities. Counties, cities, and states must therefore be ready to defy any future federal or state-level abortion mandate.
t: Nullification
qt: With *Roe* now overturned, there isn’t presently the same need for state-level nullification of federal decrees, but there very well could be in the not-too-distant future. Federal democrats have made clear their desire to pass a law codifying *Roe*, stacking the Supreme Court with abortion supporters, or both. We must be ready to defy tyrants once again if the time comes. Further, county- or city-level nullification might need to be practiced in states that mandate legal abortion of all its municipalities.

q: What is the underlying authority dispute that nullification really turns on?
a: Decades of propaganda have trained Americans to treat the Supreme Court as the ultimate, unappealable arbiter of every legal conflict. Nullification challenges that premise by asking which authority — God's law, the Constitution, or nine unelected justices — actually has the final word.
t: Nullification
qt: Nullification relates to a dispute of authority: Who is legally authorized to do what, and what is the standard in legal conflict? Through decades of propaganda, many have come to believe that the federal government, and the Supreme Court especially, is the standard in any legal conflict – that its authority is ultimate and unassailable.

q: How does the article argue that submitting to the Supreme Court has become an act of idolatry inside the pro-life movement?
a: When leaders like Joe Pojman compare obeying SCOTUS to obeying gravity, they are confessing what they actually worship. Allegiance to God is reduced to a fair-weather alliance that holds only when His word happens to coincide with the Court's opinions.
t: Nullification
qt: This view was most precisely articulated by Texas Alliance for Life Executive Director Joe Pojman who told the Austin Chronicle in April, 2019 that “We could no sooner ignore SCOTUS than the force of gravity.” The problem with Pojman’s statement is much bigger than politics. In equating obedience to the Supreme Court with a law of nature, Pojman is telling us who his god is. His allegiance to the true and living God is reduced to a temporary alliance whenever God’s word happens to match the Supreme Court’s opinion.

q: What constitutional and biblical grounds does the article give for ignoring Roe outright?
a: Article VI says only the Constitution and laws made pursuant to it count as legitimate law, and Roe is neither. The Bible commands believers to rescue those being led to slaughter, bring justice to the fatherless, and love their neighbors — duties that flatly forbid complying with any decree that murder must be legal.
t: Nullification
qt: Article VI of the Constitution specifies that only the Constitution and the laws made pursuant to it are legitimate laws. Roe is neither a law nor in any way pursuant to the Constitution. As former dean of Stanford Law John Hart Ely put it, “[Roe v Wade] is not constitutional law and gives almost no sense of an obligation to try to be.”

q: What did Thomas Jefferson actually say about treating the Supreme Court as the final arbiter of constitutional questions?
a: In his 1820 letter to Charles Jarvis, Jefferson called judicial supremacy 'a very dangerous doctrine' that would deliver the country to an oligarchy. He insisted the Constitution made all branches co-equal and co-sovereign precisely so no single tribunal could become despotic.
t: Nullification
qt: “You seem to consider the judges as the ultimate arbiters of all constitutional questions; a very dangerous doctrine indeed, and one which would place us under the despotism of an oligarchy. Our judges are as honest as other men, and not more so. They have, with others, the same passions for party, for power, and the privilege of their corps…. Their power [is] the more dangerous as they are in office for life, and not responsible, as the other functionaries are, to the elective control. The Constitution has erected no such single tribunal, knowing that to whatever hands confided, with the corruptions of time and party, its members would become despots. It has more wisely made all the departments co-equal and co-sovereign within themselves.”

q: Did any Founder explicitly endorse nullification as the constitutional remedy?
a: Yes. Jefferson wrote the Kentucky Resolution declaring that every state has a natural right to nullify, and that nullification is the rightful remedy when the federal government oversteps the Constitution.
t: Nullification
qt: Jefferson also endorsed the constitutionality of nullification explicitly in the Kentucky Resolution, in which Jefferson wrote that “Every state has a natural right to nullify,” and that “nullification… is the rightful remedy” to Constitutional infractions committed by the Federal Government.

q: Even honest legal scholars on the left admit Roe was indefensible — what does that tell us?
a: Liberal jurisprudes from John Hart Ely to Laurence Tribe to Edward Lazarus have conceded Roe is essentially indefensible as constitutional interpretation. The 'right' to abortion was invented by the 1973 Court, which means submitting to it was never about respecting law — it was about fearing men.
t: Nullification
qt: In addition to Ely, leftist and Harvard Law professor Laurence Tribe wrote of Roe v Wade, “Behind its own verbal smokescreen, the substantive judgement on which it rests is nowhere to be found.” Edward Lazarus, a former clerk of Roe author Harry Blackmun who “loved Blackmun like a grandfather,” said “as a matter of constitutional interpretation, even most liberal jurisprudes — if you administer truth serum — will tell you [Roe] is basically indefensible.”

q: What if the Constitution itself sanctioned an evil — should it still be obeyed?
a: No. Garrison was right to burn a copy of the Constitution on July 4, 1854 because of its slavery compromises. A legal document that authorizes the dehumanization and brutalization of image bearers of God is, in his words, a covenant with death and an agreement with hell — and must be defied.
t: Nullification
qt: Slavery Abolitionist William Lloyd Garrison was right to burn a copy of the US Constitution during a July 4th, 1854 speech to the Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society because of the pre- 13th and 14th amendment compromises which allowed for slavery. A legal document which allows for the complete dehumanization and brutalization of image bearers of God is, as Garrison called it, “a covenant with death and agreement with hell.”

q: What about the practical objection that defying the federal government would cost states their funding?
a: The article identifies this as fear-driven idolatry. Pro-life leaders cite lost Medicaid, education funds, and disaster relief to justify continued compliance, but they fear what the Supreme Court — which has no enforcement power — might do more than they fear what God will do to a nation that refuses to outlaw murder.
t: Nullification
qt: Again, fear is the driving force here. Fear of what the Supreme Court, which actually has no enforcement power, will do to a state which defies it, rather than fear of what God will do to a state and nation that defies Him.

q: Is there American precedent for states nullifying a federal decree on behalf of human life?
a: Yes. Wisconsin, Michigan, Vermont, New York, and Massachusetts all interposed on behalf of fugitive slaves by nullifying the federal Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 and the federal courts demanding its enforcement. Abolitionists Rising is asking states to do the same for the preborn today.
t: Nullification
qt: State governments in Wisconsin, Michigan, Vermont, New York, and Massachusetts interposed on behalf of the victims of slavery by nullifying the federal Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 and the federal courts which demanded its enforcement. We are asking state governments to do the same today.

q: What concrete duty does the doctrine of interposition place on state governors, legislators, and judges?
a: They are morally and legally obligated to put themselves between the tyrant and the victims being deprived of their God-given right to life. The only appropriate response to an order to allow mass murder is non-compliance and resistance — ignoring Roe and abolishing abortion outright.
t: Nullification
qt: With *Roe* being morally execrable and legally null, state governors, legislators, and judges are morally and legally obligated to *interpose* – to put themselves between the tyrant and the victims who are being deprived of their God-given right to life.

q: Why does the author claim the abolition movement will fail without prayer?
a: Because abolition is God's work before it is man's work. Without entrusting the labor to God, activists will exhaust themselves in vain effort, and the movement itself will collapse under the weight of unaided human striving.
t: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
qt: If the abolition movement does not begin with prayer, it will die. This may seem a hyperbolic statement, but unless the builders entrust the work of abolition to God, we will certainly labor in vain.

q: What three things does the author say abolitionist prayer must include?
a: Repentance of apathy toward the slaughter of the preborn, supplication that God would give us sorrow for the lost, and remembrance that the labor exists to represent and glorify Christ rather than to vindicate ourselves.
t: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
qt: This prayer needs to be, firstly, one of repentance of apathy, supplication to cause us to be sorrowful for the lost, and remembrance that we represent and glorify Christ in this labor.

q: How did watching Sen. Dusty Deevers convict the author about his own abolitionist convictions?
a: Deevers' tender, near-sobbing care for the preborn during the SB-456 hearing exposed that the author's own abolitionism had been merely intellectual rather than a faith-shaped position that allowed God's word to expose his apathy.
t: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
qt: For me, holding to the abolitionist viewpoint beforehand was simply intellectual; it was not a position I had taken in faith, allowing for the Lord to correct me, a sinner amid a holocaust, who was trying to point other sinners to their inconsistency.

q: What sinful posture did the author discover in himself toward those involved in abortion?
a: He realized he had been treating himself as morally superior to people directly complicit in abortion, while ignoring that only God's restraining grace had kept him from the same sin. He also lacked sorrow for the unsaved souls of mothers who have killed their children.
t: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
qt: I was stunned to realize I thought of myself as above those that were directly involved in the slaughter but denied God’s grace that restrained me from being involved myself. I also lacked sorrow for those lost souls who have murdered their children and are not covered by Christ.

q: Where does the author say the fight against abortion actually begins?
a: Not on the streets or at the capitol, but on one's knees before a holy God who hates the death of the innocent and detests human apathy toward it. The starting line is repentance.
t: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
qt: The fight against abortion does not begin on the streets; rather, it starts on your knees before a holy God that abominates the death of the innocent and abhors the apathy of man. It begins with repentance for ignoring such a horrific act.

q: What was the author's biggest regret from the Washington, DC mission?
a: That he prayed too little and talked too much, defaulting to self-justification in front of those he was supposedly trying to win, rather than graciously pursuing their reconciliation with God.
t: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
qt: My greatest regret from the DC mission was that I prayed little and said much.

q: Which line from 1 Peter 3:15 does the author say apologists most often forget, and why does it matter?
a: The closing phrase 'yet do it with gentleness and respect.' Forgetting it tempts Christians to defend Christ in a way that contradicts Christ's own character, attacking image-bearers instead of loving them as God loved us.
t: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
qt: The last line of 1 Peter 3:15, which is frequently used in apologetics — “yet do it with gentleness and respect” — is the most forgotten aspect of the passage. It demonstrates how easily humans are tempted to defend Christ “Christlessly.”

q: What does the author promise will happen when abolitionists pray faithfully?
a: God will plant seeds that supernaturally turn darkness into light, and abortion will be abolished for His glory. The believer's confidence rests not in the strategy but in Christ, who sanctifies and protects His bride.
t: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
qt: When we faithfully pray, God plants seeds that will supernaturally transform darkness into light, and, likewise, abortion **will** be abolished to the glory of God.

q: How does abolitionism's view of abortion differ from the standard pro-life position?
a: Abolitionists go beyond calling abortion 'bad' to identifying it as the sin of murder, which means it must be treated with the moral weight and legal response due to murder, while still holding out forgiveness in Christ.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: We, as an abolitionist organizations, agree with the pro-life position that abortion is bad, but further, we believe that abortion is the sin of murder and that it must be treated as such. Of course, there is forgiveness to be found in Christ for abortion, but that does not mean it ought to be legal, and it does not negate the necessity of establishing justice.

q: Why does calling abortion a 'sin' matter strategically for how the movement fights it?
a: If abortion is fundamentally sin, then the only sufficient remedy is the gospel of Jesus Christ, because Scripture consistently shows that human strength and schemes cannot push back darkness; only God does that, often through His people.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: The most important word in the previous paragraph is "sin." Abortion being a sin has huge implications for how we must fight it. That it's a sin means that the only ultimate answer to it is the gospel of Jesus Christ. There is hardly a more common theme of the scriptures than that of man's strength and schemes being impotent to push back darkness. It is always and only God who works -- often through His people -- to overcome evil.

q: What does the article mean by 'the gospel of the kingdom,' and why is that broader than personal salvation?
a: The gospel includes the forgiveness of sins, but it also includes Christ's reign, judgment, and teaching; Mark calls his entire account 'the gospel,' and Paul ties the gospel to Christ destroying every rule and authority and reigning until His enemies are under His feet.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: In 1 Corinthians, Paul writes of the crucifixion and resurrection of Christ, but he then turns to the reign of Christ - the gospel of the kingdom. He quotes from Psalm 110, saying, “Then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power. For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death.”

q: How do abolitionists understand their public activism in relation to the kingdom of God?
a: Abolitionists see themselves as bringing Christ's kingdom of justice and mercy into direct conflict with the world's kingdom of sin and death, advancing the kingdom as souls are redeemed and darkness is pushed back.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: It is this gospel of Christ's kingdom that abolitionists bring into conflict with the culture of death. We proclaim the excellency of Christ's kingdom of justice and mercy over against the world's kingdom of sin and death. We proclaim God's wisdom against the foolishness of man. We proclaim repentance and belief in the One who died to make us right with God, and who will one day set everything right.

q: Why is the abolition movement described as inescapably Christian rather than a broad secular coalition?
a: Because the cure for sin is the gospel, and the gospel has been entrusted only to the Church; unbelievers operating with common grace can support just laws, but only the Church possesses the actual remedy for the curse that produces abortion.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: So if abortion is sin, and the answer to sin is the gospel, then whose work is it to push back the darkness of abortion? It can only be those to whom the gospel has been entrusted: the Church (Matthew 28:19-20). While unbelievers operating with common grace are welcome to support just laws and candidates, this movement is unavoidably a Christian one. No one else has the cure to the curse that plagues mankind and leads to abortion.

q: Is fighting abortion an optional ministry for specially gifted Christians, or a duty for the whole Church?
a: It is a universal duty for the whole Church, not a niche calling; the parable of the Good Samaritan shows that ignoring a neighbor in need is sin that transcends individual gifts, and Christ rebuked the Pharisees for neglecting justice.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: This work does not belong to a special few within the Church, but the Church as a whole. As R.C. Sproul put it, “When the church is silent in the midst of a holocaust, she ceases to be a real church.” Whether one should work to stop a holocaust that is all around them is not a matter of Christian liberty. In the parable of the good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37), Jesus demonstrates that religious people will often pass by a neighbor in need, and that this is an example of sin — the neglect of a universal duty that transcends any particular gifts, skills, or callings that individual Christians may have.

q: If a state has 'banned' abortion, can Christians there consider the work finished?
a: No. Even where surgical abortion mills have closed, mothers still kill their children with mail-order pills under cover of law, and pharmacies, OBGYN offices, and websites continue to facilitate abortion; the Church must keep engaging the culture and exposing the evil.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: In the modern American context, there is almost no one who does not pass by the places of murder on a daily basis, and if you live in a state where abortion has supposedly been made "illegal", mothers are still murdering their babies with mail-order pills under the cover of law. If there is not an abortion mill in your area, there is a hospital or OBGYN office that performs abortions, a pharmacy that sells abortifacient drugs, or internet access to sites that sell abortion pills. Child sacrifice is all around us.

q: What is the abolitionist standard for what laws and proposals to support?
a: Scripture alone. If Christ and the prophets demanded immediate repentance and refused to endorse partial bans on injustice, abolitionists must do likewise; they will not support legislation that bans abortion after a certain week while permitting it before.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: If Christ and the prophets demanded immediate repentance of the kings and nations, so too must we. If Jesus and the prophets called the magistrates to establish justice, so too must we. If Jesus and the prophets would not support legislation banning abortion after 15 weeks but allowing it previously, then neither can we.

q: How does the article critique pro-life leaders who keep their faith out of their activism?
a: By excluding God's Word, pro-life leaders forfeit the only objective standard by which abortion can be adequately rebuked as evil; the article cites a pro-life outreach that forbade volunteers from evangelizing and a pro-life senator who said he leaves his Christianity at the door.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: Before abolitionists convinced them to stop, one pro-life outreach organization made their volunteers sign a form promising not to evangelize unless the unbeliever brings up religion first. A pro-life Oklahoma State Senator told a constituent that he leaves his Christianity at the door when he’s legislating. The pro-life leaders believe they must downplay their Christianity, and the result is that they have given up the only objective standard by which abortion can be adequately rebuked as evil.

q: How do abolitionists answer the charge that their positions are politically unrealistic or don't poll well?
a: They reject pragmatism as the standard. Faithfulness to God's commands is their duty; results belong to God, who alone opens eyes, softens hearts, and raises up or brings down rulers.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: Accordingly, abolitionists take our marching orders from God’s Word, not from secularist political experts who kick against abolitionism because many of our positions don’t poll well in the current political climate. But abolitionists don’t care. God’s prescriptions are not only for applying in favorable environments. We are to call for justice as God defines it in faith that He is the one who opens eyes, softens hearts, sets up and brings down kings, and vanquishes the darkness.

q: What role does faith play in actually overcoming abortion, according to the article?
a: Because abortion is spiritual, the victory over it is spiritual: 1 John 5 teaches that the victory that overcomes the world is faith in Jesus as the Son of God, so the destruction of abortion cannot be detached from belief in God's Word, promises, and prescriptions.
t: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
qt: 1 John 5:4-5 teach us, “For everyone who has been born of God overcomes the world. And this is the victory that has overcome the world—our faith. Who is it that overcomes the world except the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God?” The overcoming of abortion cannot be detached from its spiritual nature and implications. Faith in God, His Word, His promises, and His prescriptions are crucial to the destruction of abortion.

q: What is Abolitionists Rising actually asking people to do at the Florida and Missouri polling places?
a: They are asking abolitionists to legally stand outside polling places in Florida and Missouri while holding images of the abortions the ballot referendums would enshrine in those state constitutions. State-specific signs are available shipped from their store or as free downloadable PDFs that activists can print themselves.
t: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
qt: In Florida and Missouri, we are asking abolitionists to stand outside of polling places (legally) while holding images of the abortions that these referendums are asking voters to enshrine in their state constitutions.

q: Why focus the campaign on Florida and Missouri instead of every state with an abortion ballot measure?
a: Weisser says Florida and Missouri were chosen for strategic reasons but encourages abolitionists in any other state to do the same work if they have the resources. General abortion-imagery signs are also offered for use outside the two focus states.
t: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
qt: We are focusing on Florida and Missouri for strategic reasons, but obviously we’d encourage anyone in any of the other states to do the same if they have the resources.

q: Why use graphic abortion imagery at all, given how disturbing it is?
a: Weisser argues the imagery is an effective tool that provokes one of three reactions: denial, anger at the exposer, or a seared conscience that can radically change a person's life. Many abolitionists themselves point to seeing such an image as the moment God turned them toward fighting abortion.
t: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
qt: Abortion imagery is an awesome tool, and people tend to respond to it in different ways. Either they deny it is real, they just get mad that we’re exposing it, or the image is seared into their conscience, and they are never the same again.

q: Did the fall of Roe v. Wade actually end abortion in states like Florida?
a: No. Weisser says many Christians, including many in Florida, mistakenly assumed the overturning of Roe ended abortion in their state, but that is far from reality. The Dobbs decision merely returned the question to the states rather than upholding federal protection for the preborn.
t: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
qt: Most Christians thought that the fall of Roe meant the end of abortion in a lot of these states. Believe it not, even a lot of Floridian Christians thought abortion was gone in their state.

q: How does the article characterize the Dobbs decision itself?
a: Weisser calls Dobbs a wicked Supreme Court decision because the justices refused to uphold the 14th Amendment and protect preborn life federally. Instead, he says, they washed their hands of the issue and handed the question down to the states, who have predictably moved swiftly toward evil.
t: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
qt: Dobbs, the decision that overturned Roe, *was actually* a wicked supreme court decision, where they refused to uphold the 14th amendment and protect life federally.

q: If the federal government has punted abortion to the states, what does the article think states should do?
a: Weisser argues that abdication by the federal government does not justify states doing the same. Just as abolitionists pre-Roe urged states to defy their federal head and abolish abortion without permission, states today should still break the cycle and refuse to enshrine the killing rather than meekly accepting the choice handed to them.
t: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
qt: Just because the federal government has been abdicating its duty, doesn’t mean the states should, too.

q: If these ballot amendments are defeated, would that abolish abortion?
a: No. Weisser is candid that defeating the amendments would only stop abortion from being written into state constitutions; children would still be killed under existing state law. So victory would not really feel like a win, but it would suggest some remaining moral life in the country and reason for hope.
t: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
qt: Keeping these amendments from passing would not abolish abortion. Not even close. All they would do is keep the legal nullity that is abortion from being enshrined into the state constitutions.

q: What biblical framing does Weisser use to describe the polling-place witness?
a: He frames the sidewalk sign-holding as confronting the culture with the Deuteronomy choice between life and death. Standing in front of voters with abortion imagery is, in his words, calling heaven and earth to witness against them and pleading with them to choose life so that they and their offspring may live.
t: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
qt: We are essentially holding a sign in front of the culture, and saying to them, “I call heaven and earth to witness against you today, that I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse. Therefore choose life, that you and your offspring may live.”

q: How does Weisser use the story of Uzzah to frame the question of voting for a pro-abortion candidate?
a: He casts Uzzah as a man caught between two evils—let the Ark fall or touch it—who chose what looked like the lesser evil and was struck dead. The lesson he draws is that no contextual reasoning makes an evil permissible, and 'choosing neither' is always the correct option, which he then applies to voting for a pro-abortion candidate.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: The lesson? No amount of context can make an evil permissible. No amount of “good that may come” can allow you to do evil. There is no such thing as the lesser of two evils. “Choosing neither” is always the correct option.

q: What does Weisser mean by labeling Trump, Vance, and similar Republicans 'pro-abortion'?
a: He argues Republicans who once called Democrats pro-abortion for the 'safe, legal, and rare' position now refuse to apply the same label to Republicans holding the same position. For consistency, any candidate who is not planning to curtail abortion in any way is pro-abortion.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: When Democrats were pro “safe, legal, and rare” abortion, we all agreed with calling them pro-abortion. Yet, now that prominent Republican candidates are pro “safe, legal, and rare” abortion, we pretend they aren’t. “They aren’t *for* abortion, they just don’t want to do anything about it right now”, folks say. Let’s be consistent here, folks. They’re pro-abortion.

q: How does Exodus 18:21 disqualify pro-abortion candidates in Weisser's argument?
a: Exodus 18:21 sets four qualifications for civil officers—able, God-fearing, trustworthy, and hating bribes. Weisser claims any pro-abortion candidate fails at least two of these regardless of party affiliation, and that the standard binds democratic voters as the distributed equivalent of Moses' appointing role.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: They are to be men who are 1) Able, 2) God-fearing, 3) Trustworthy, and 4) Hate bribes. ***Any* pro-abortion candidate fails at least two of these guidelines,** no matter what letter they have next to their name.

q: Why does Weisser reject the argument that Exodus 18:21 only applies to primary elections, not generals?
a: He uses an analogy: if a church membership were forced to choose between two disqualified elders, the right answer is 'neither'—not picking one. The same logic, he says, applies to a binary general election where both candidates fall short of God's standards.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: If that is true, does that not then bind a church membership, if they are given a choice between two elders—both who are disqualified—from exercising the same flawed mindset? No, of course “neither” is always an option.

q: How does Proverbs 28:17 apply to voting for a candidate who refuses to prosecute abortion as murder?
a: Weisser reads Proverbs 28:17—'no one is to support him'—as a direct prohibition against helping those bloodguilty for shed innocent blood. Casting a vote, he argues, is precisely the act of supporting, helping, and promoting such a man to office.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: Yet, isn’t that exactly what a vote is? It’s a political device to *help* him, *support* him, or *promote* him to an office. It’s clear to me that Proverbs has rulers in mind when it speaks of this man, laden with bloodguilt.

q: What is the etymological case Weisser makes for treating a vote as an alliance?
a: He points to the Latin root 'vovere,' meaning 'to vow,' arguing that a vote is by definition a vow of support. To deny that a vote is an alliance is to deny the basic meaning of the act being performed.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: There’s a reason we call it “supporting” a candidate when you intend to vote for them. The etymology itself of the word “vote” betrays this. It comes from the root “vovere”, *to vow*. **You are vowing your support to him, come what may.**

q: How does Weisser predict the trajectory of a 'lesser of two evils' vote over time?
a: He warns of a three-stage degradation: a 'lesser of two evils' is first tolerated, then preferred as evil, and finally redefined as good. Without confrontation, the compromise hardens into idolatry of the politician.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: **A “lesser of two evils” will always become a “preferable evil”, and if left unconfronted, will become a “preferable good”.**

q: What is Weisser's response to invoking Ordo Amoris to justify voting for a flawed candidate to protect one's own children?
a: He calls this an inversion of the doctrine into 'Ordo Neglectus.' Because all true love is rooted in love of God, and loving God means keeping His commandments, any 'love' that requires sin against the unborn is not ordered love at all but pagan affinity.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: Let the truth be stated clearly: **“Love” that betrays the higher love of God is not love at all; it is mere pagan affinity.**

q: How does Weisser address the 'I'm not voting for the abortion stuff, I'm voting for the other stuff' objection?
a: He compares it to a Congressman voting for an evil omnibus bill because of attached good provisions. Citing Romans 3:8, he argues no expected good can permit doing evil, and the rationalization is the same condemned slander.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: By now, you may be realizing the problem, as stated earlier in this article: No amount of “good that may come” can permit an evil.

q: Why does Weisser consider the overturning of Roe via Dobbs to be harmful rather than victorious?
a: Before Dobbs, abolitionists were pushing state nullification of Roe while pro-life groups defended regulation 'with compliance to Roe.' Dobbs let pro-life leaders drop the Roe-submission talking point and lulled Christians into believing the fight was won, while abortion numbers nationally have not meaningfully dropped.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: Now that *Dobbs* is in place, and in many states abortion clinics have been closed, Christians all over the country are lulled into a feeling of accomplishment, as if we’ve won a massive victory over abortion. The fact of the matter is that abortion numbers have not gone down in any meaningful way, and have even gone up nationally.

q: How does Weisser characterize the sin of presuming what God will do if Christians refuse to vote for a pro-abortion candidate?
a: He calls it the sin of presumption, paralleled by the ten faithless spies in Numbers, and worse—false prophecy of the Ezekiel 13 variety, where men 'prophesy smooth things from their own hearts' about the gains a compromise vote will bring.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: This is the sin of **presumption**. You *presume* what will happen based on your human reason, and what God has commanded falls to second place in your heart, behind the fear you have of the future.

q: Why does Weisser reject the 'you compromise too by buying from Amazon' objection?
a: He calls it a category error. Doing business with pagans is permitted in Scripture (Deuteronomy 2:6) and does not institute their laws or appoint pagan lawmakers, whereas voting directly installs evil rulers and frames evil statutes.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: This is a category error. By doing business with pagans, you are not instituting their laws, or instituting a pagan lawmaker. The Old Testament allows Christians to do business with pagans.

q: What does Weisser warn about politically connected sycophants within the abolitionist movement?
a: He warns that platform-seekers and political ladder-climbers who attack the prophetic voices of abolitionists rather than the candidate's evils are a Doeg-the-Edomite type, divisive by nature, and should not be heralded as examples of the movement.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: I want to posit that as abolitionists, we should not platform or yolk ourselves to these men. Now, I’m not saying that we can’t work with folks who have voted for Trump. People will accuse me of that, but that’s not what I’m saying. I am saying that platform-seekers and political ladder-climbing types are not to be heralded as examples of our movement.

q: How does Weisser frame the abolitionist's posture toward worldly pragmatism in voting?
a: He says abolitionism is providential rather than pragmatic, and that the most pragmatic action is always doing what God says, even when it appears unlikely to produce success—as illogical as marching around Jericho's walls thirteen times with trumpets.
t: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
qt: There’s nothing pragmatic about marching around a wall thirteen times while blasting trumpets if you want that wall to be torn down. It doesn’t make sense with worldly wisdom. Rather, the most wise and most pragmatic thing to do at every given moment is *what God says to do,* and never *what God says not to do.*

q: What did Roxanne Green's clinic arson actually accomplish, in Weisser's assessment?
a: It produced a viral mugshot and five years in prison, while the clinic she burned simply reopened a year later and continues to kill children, leaving her absent from the actual rescue work she could have been doing.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: the only thing she actually achieved was putting herself behind bars and wasting 5 years of her young and potentially productive life. The clinic she torched in 2022 merely delayed their opening by a year, where it stands today actively killing children while she's helpless – behind bars.

q: How does Weisser respond to the charge that abolitionists are cowards unwilling to 'get their hands dirty'?
a: He flips the framing: vigilantism is not bold action but a worthless incremental strategy, while abolitionists pursue the harder work of immediatism that calls the entire culture to repent rather than chasing a single method of killing.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: What they don't understand is that we are *immediatists*, and we reject incremental strategies that delay the progress towards national repentance. Vigilantism is just that: a worthless incremental strategy.

q: What scriptural argument does Weisser use to ground the rejection of vigilante violence?
a: He appeals to 2 Corinthians 10:4-6, where Paul says the weapons of Christian warfare are not carnal but have divine power to destroy arguments and lofty opinions, with prophetic speech as the prescribed instrument of punishing disobedience.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: *…We destroy arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and take every thought captive to obey Christ, being ready to punish every disobedience, when your obedience is complete.* (2 Corinthians 10:5-6 ESV)

q: Does Weisser claim vigilantism is always inherently sinful?
a: No. He distinguishes his position from pacifism and concedes there are conceivable cases like Hitler or Jael and Sisera, but argues the abortion situation is not analogous and that Romans 13 normatively assigns the sword to the governing authorities.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: My argument is not necessarily that vigilantism is inherently unjust, but that in this specific context, it's wrongheaded, and incrementalist in nature.

q: Why does Weisser say bombing clinics is structurally identical to incremental pro-life bills?
a: Because both attack a single method of abortion rather than the root demand for abortion itself, leaving every other method intact and conceding the basic legitimacy of the practice.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: If we tried to burn down abortion mills, we would be merely going after one of those methods, just like pro-lifers do when they try and ban forceps abortions, but leave many other methods legal.

q: What case does Weisser make about George Tiller's assassination?
a: Tiller was reportedly about to lose his license for illegal activity, but Scott Roeder's killing turned a soon-to-be-disgraced abortionist into a pro-abortion martyr, and the women he would have aborted simply went elsewhere.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: George Tiller, the abortionist assassinated by Scott Roeder, for example, was reportedly about to have his license revoked for his illegal activity, but then right before that happened he was killed by a pro-lifer and he became a pro-abortion martyr instead. Then, all those babies that were supposedly "saved" were just murdered some other way.

q: What does Weisser say went wrong with the Rescue movement?
a: Its clinic-centric tactics like blocking doors and bike-locking entrances jailed good fathers and emptied churches, while the clinics resumed business as usual the moment the activists were removed.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: They would peacefully sit outside of abortion clinics and block people from going in, or they would bike lock the doors shut, etc. What would happen is they would just get arrested and the clinic would continue operating as normal as soon as they were out of the way.

q: How does Weisser use his own state as evidence against the clinic-bombing strategy?
a: He notes that he lives in a state with no operating clinics and yet, because pro-lifers settled for clinic-centric wins, abortion has actually increased through other channels, proving incrementalism just teaches the other side to be more efficient.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: I, for one, live in a state with no clinics operating, so I get to live somewhere where the "good" the clinic bombers were trying to achieve HAS ALREADY been achieved! There's no abortionists, there's no abortion clinics. But because pro-lifers had this short-sighted clinic-centric mindset, there's now MORE abortion than there was previously.

q: Why does Weisser insist mothers, not abortionists, are the principal actors in abortion?
a: Because abortionists are merely hitmen meeting demand; the woman who hires the abortion is the one committing murder, which is why pro-lifers' instinct to make the doctor the villain misidentifies the real perpetrator.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: The people who are perpetuating the genocide of abortion are not the abortionists, they're the mothers. Abortionists are merely meeting a demand. Pro-lifers have always refused to see it that way, which is why it's understandable when they see the abortionists as the villain that needs to be stopped.

q: What concrete personal example does Weisser offer to show the long-term cost of vigilantism?
a: A friend whose daughter aborted his grandchild chose prayer and gospel preaching over killing the abortionist Larry Burns; the daughter became a Christian abolitionist, and the friend now lobbies Oklahoma legislators rather than rotting in a prison cell.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: Imagine instead, if he had killed Larry Burns, the abortionist. This man wouldn't be around now to be the incredible help that he is to our organization, because he'd be in jail. Plus, all the babies he and his daughter have had a hand in saving since then would currently be dead.

q: What metaphor does Weisser use to describe vigilantism's relationship to the abortion industry?
a: He likens it to chopping the tail off a dragon: the visible appendage is severed, the activist feels heroic, but the beast itself regenerates and the underlying system is untouched.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: Vigilantism is a form of incrementalism, because it eschews the hard work of abolition in favor of something easier and more accessible. It's like chopping the tail off of a dragon. It just grows back.

q: Whose work does Weisser hold up as the real model of abolitionist faithfulness?
a: The unglamorous blue-collar Christian who emails legislators, donates, talks honestly with co-workers, attends rallies, and stands at the mill, performing the boring repeated faithfulness no one applauds online.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: The blue-collar worker who spends his free time emailing his legislators, giving his hard-earned money to good organizations, and having hard conversations with his co-workers about abortion. How about the guy who joins his church going to rallies for abolition, or standing outside of abortion mills trying to save lives? There's very little glory sent their way.

q: What spiritual diagnosis does Weisser offer of the Army of God admirers?
a: He sees vainglory: the appeal of public mugshots and dramatic displays gives trolls a cheap counter to abolitionists' call to repent of apathy, while accomplishing nothing for the children.
t: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
qt: I think there's a sort of vainglory that comes along with the Army of God folks, the Rescue movement to a lesser degree, and a lot of these chronically-online trolls who prop up vigilantes who have "done what needed to be done."

q: Why is the April 8th hearing on TX HB896 historically significant?
a: It is positioned as the first time since Roe v. Wade that a true abortion abolition bill will receive a hearing before a state legislative committee, breaking a 46-year pattern in which legislatures only considered regulatory or incremental pro-life bills.
t: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
qt: Texas will make history on Monday as the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act (TX HB896) is scheduled to become the first abortion abolition bill to be heard by a legislative committee since the Supreme Court opined that all 50 states should allow abortion in 1973.

q: How does HB896 differ from the typical pro-life bill being passed in 2019?
a: Unlike the more than 250 pro-life bills introduced in 2019 that produce minimal change, HB896 would actually end abortion in Texas by extending equal legal protection to preborn humans rather than merely regulating the killing.
t: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
qt: There have been more than 250 pro-life bills introduced in State legislatures across the country in 2019 alone, most of which accomplish little-to-nothing. This is not one of those bills. TX HB896 would be the end of abortion in Texas.

q: What constitutional principle does HB896 invoke to grant preborn children legal personhood?
a: The bill borrows language from the 14th Amendment's equal protection guarantee, declaring that a living human child from the moment of fertilization is entitled to the same rights and protections as any other child under Texas law.
t: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
qt: A living human child, from the moment of fertilization on fusion of a human spermatozoon with a human ovum, is entitled to the same rights, powers, and privileges as are secured or granted by the laws of this state to any other human child.

q: How does the bill handle conflicting federal authority such as Roe v. Wade?
