a: HB896 directs the Texas Attorney General and state subdivisions to enforce homicide and assault statutes against abortion regardless of contrary federal action, and it declares any federal law, executive order, or court ruling to the contrary void under both the Texas and US Constitutions.
t: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
qt: Any federal law, executive order, or court decision that purports to supersede, stay, or overrule this Act is in violation of the Texas Constitution and the United States Constitution and is therefore void.

q: Which committee members will determine whether HB896 advances?
a: The bill needs all five Republicans on the House Judiciary and Civil Jurisprudence Committee. James White is a co-sponsor and chairman Jeff Leach previously sponsored identical legislation, so the swing votes are Matt Krause, Morgan Meyer, and Reggie Smith, whose positions are publicly unknown.
t: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
qt: The leanings of Reps. Matt Krause (R-Fort Worth), Morgan Meyer (R-Dallas), and Reggie Smith (R-Sherman) are unknown. Without their unanimous support, TX HB896 will die, and with it thousands of preborn human beings.

q: What concrete action does Silberman ask Texans and out-of-state supporters to take?
a: Texans able to attend should show up at the Capitol with a two-to-three minute prepared speech to deliver testimony for the bill, while non-Texans should follow Abolish Abortion Texas's Facebook live stream of the hearing.
t: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
qt: There will be a time to give testimony on behalf of the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act. Come prepared with a two-to-three minute speech and you will be able to speak on behalf of the vulnerable preborn directly to those whose responsibility it is to protect them.

q: Why does the title cite 110,000 lives rather than the annual abortion total?
a: Because the Texas legislature meets only every other year, the failure of HB896 in 2019 would mean roughly two years' worth of Texas abortions, around 110,000 preborn babies, before the next opportunity to outlaw the practice.
t: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
qt: About 55,000 babies are murdered annually in Texas. With the State legislature meeting every other year, the fate of 110,000 babies rests with TX HB896.

q: Why does Silberman argue the Dobbs decision was wrongly decided despite overturning Roe?
a: Because Dobbs treats the Constitution as neutral on abortion, which Silberman argues requires accepting that preborn humans are not 'persons' protected by the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments. Affirming the human non-person concept abdicates the Court's duty to provide equal protection.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: Because the fifth and 14th amendments protect the rights to life and equal protection of all “persons,” the only way to justify the idea of Constitutional neutrality on abortion is to argue that preborn human beings are not “people” for purposes of those amendments; a preposterous assertion.

q: What is the danger of accepting the 'human non-person' concept?
a: Once any class of living humans can be legally classified as non-persons, no one's rights are secure. Silberman cites the historical treatment of preborn, Jewish, and black people as proof that this concept has produced atrocity in the recent Western past.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: If we concede the validity of the human non-person concept, then no one’s God-given, Constitutionally protected rights are safe. If the majority decides you’re a human non-person, all legal protections no longer apply to you.

q: Did the Supreme Court actually need to grant permission before states could abolish abortion?
a: No. Silberman insists the states never required the Court's permission to obey Christ or uphold constitutional rights to life and equal protection, and that abolition bills could and should have been advanced regardless of Roe.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: Of course, we [never needed the court’s permission](https://freethestates.org/nullification-and-interposition/index.html) to obey Christ or uphold the Constitutional rights to life and equal protection, but we now have it.

q: Why does Silberman warn that the political fight is far from over after Dobbs?
a: Because abortion remains legal in some form in every state since none have made preborn children equal under law, and pro-life dogma against penalizing mothers leaves a self-induced abortion loophole everywhere.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: Though abortion regulations just became much more restrictive in about half the states, it is still legal to murder a preborn children in one form or another in every state because not a single state has made preborn children equal under law. Many loopholes for abortion remain, including a loop hole of self-induced abortion which is available in every state because of the pro-life dogma that mothers should never face legal penalties for murdering their children.

q: What positive shift does the article see in the post-Dobbs political landscape?
a: Legislators can no longer hide behind the Supreme Court and claim their hands are tied. The debate now turns squarely on equal protection: whether preborn children should receive the same protections as everyone else, and whether those who kill them should face appropriate penalties.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: Now, the dispute will focus singularly on the question of equal protection: should preborn children receive the same protections and justice that all of us get? Should those who intentionally kill preborn children — including the mother — be liable to face penalties, as appropriate given the facts of the case, for doing so?

q: Why does Silberman dispute incrementalist claims of credit for the Dobbs ruling?
a: He argues Dobbs was the first abortion case in which abolitionists submitted an amicus brief warning the Court that states would abolish abortion regardless, the first since the SBC affirmed an abolition resolution, and the first since Roe-defying bills were filed in over a dozen states. The pressure that produced Roe's reversal came from abolitionist demand, not from decades of incrementalist begging.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: *Dobbs* — the case in which Roe was finally overturned — is the first abortion-related case in which an [abolitionist *amicus brief*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CstBZF-VDBE) was submitted communicating to the court that they must abolish abortion or else the states will.

q: How does Silberman characterize the difference between the pro-life and abolitionist approaches to the Court?
a: Pro-lifers spent 49 years requesting the Court's permission, while abolitionists issued a demand. Quoting Frederick Douglass, he argues power only yields to demands, not to begging.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: As Frederick Douglass famously said, “Power concedes nothing without a demand.” The Pro-Life Movement begged for the court’s permission for 49 years. The Abolitionist Movement made a demand: abolish abortion or else we will.

q: What does the article say is required if Roe-style federal action is reimposed?
a: States must learn from past compliance, refuse to obey unconstitutional federal mandates, and proceed to abolish abortion regardless. Repenting of the idolatry of obeying man over God remains essential.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: If that happens, we must be prepared to learn from the mistakes of the past, ignore evil and unconstitutional diktats, and abolish abortion.

q: What is Silberman's theological framing for why preborn children must be granted equal protection?
a: Because all humans are created in the image of God, they are persons whose lives must be protected equally under law. Continuing to deny this is the moral and political battle that remains.
t: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
qt: All humans are created in the image of God and are persons who must be provided the equal protection of the law. The battle continues until our laws and our society reflect these principles.

q: Why does the article insist that abolitionist candidates must outwork their opponents rather than relying on funding parity?
a: Because the abolitionist movement does not yet have the financial or institutional muscle of the pro-life establishment, candidates will almost always be outspent and out-endorsed; the only way to overcome that gap is sheer volume of voter contact through calls, door-knocking, mailers, ads, signs, and local Republican event presence.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: The Abolitionist Movement is rapidly growing in size and organization, but we do not have the resources of the Pro-Life Establishment. Especially if you are challenging an incumbent, your opponent will likely have more money than you, and will certainly have more political connections.

q: What concrete examples does the article cite to prove that an outworked, underfunded abolitionist can still beat an incumbent?
a: The 2020 races of Warren Hamilton in Oklahoma SD7 (outspent more than four-to-one against Larry Boggs, who was backed by the governor, both U.S. Senators, and nearly half the State Senate) and John Jacob in Indiana HD93 (outspent more than two-to-one against Dollyne Sherman, endorsed by the governor, Indiana Right to Life, the NRA, and the Indiana Chamber of Commerce). Both candidates won their Republican primaries and the general election by aggressive door-to-door work and coalition-building with liberty-minded organizations.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: Hamilton and organizations supporting him were out-spent by Boggs and Boggs-supporting organizations more than four-to-one.

q: How should an abolitionist candidate's messaging differ from the standard pro-life vocabulary?
a: Candidates should use sharp, specific phrases like 'Abolish Abortion,' 'Equal Protection for Preborn Children,' and 'End Abortion Now' while explicitly avoiding vague terms like 'pro-life,' 'I support pro-life bills,' or 'the life issue,' because those phrases are interchangeable with the language used by the politicians who keep abortion legal and therefore destroy the contrast voters need in order to choose.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: Avoid using phrases that make you blend in with your opponent and with the establishment that is keeping abortion legal.

q: Why does the author argue that contrast is more important in an abolitionist primary campaign than in ordinary races?
a: Because pro-life slogans are deliberately vague tools that incumbents use to disguise their compromise; without explicit contrast, an abolitionist will be lumped in with the very politicians keeping abortion legal. Drawing the line publicly is not just a campaign tactic, it is the same line of demarcation the movement uses to convert pro-lifers into abolitionists.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: Pro-life political messaging is a cesspool of meaningless generalities which serve to delay the abolition of abortion. Abolitionist candidates should not try to blend in with this cesspool. They should rise above it and expose the cesspool for what it is.

q: What five points does the article say should anchor an abolitionist candidate's platform?
a: The wickedness of the status quo, the failure of compromising pro-life politicians, that abortion must be abolished and not regulated, interposition against Roe v. Wade, and equal protection for preborn children. The article supplies a sample stump statement built around these five points that candidates are free to adapt or reuse wholesale.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt:
1. The wickedness of the status quo
2. The failure of compromising pro-life politicians
3. Abortion must be abolished, not regulated
4. Interposition against *Roe v. Wade*
5. Equal protection for preborn children

q: Does the article tell candidates to run as single-issue abortion candidates?
a: No. Silberman explicitly says candidates should articulate just positions on school choice, economic freedom, family rights, gender sanity, free speech, and the Second Amendment, but he insists that none of those issues, even taken together, equal the moral weight of the abortion holocaust, so abolition must remain prominently featured rather than buried alongside other planks.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: You should address many topics well, but abolishing abortion should feature prominently in your campaign.

q: How should an abolitionist candidate handle questionnaires from pro-life establishment organizations?
a: Refuse to endorse compromise. Specifically, the article recommends ignoring questions that ask candidates to support pro-life bills which compromise with abortion, or ignoring the questionnaire entirely. The candidate should not provoke pro-life groups unnecessarily, but if those groups intervene against the campaign, the candidate must publicly expose them as frauds working to keep abortion legal.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: Most of them will ask whether you support pro-life bills which compromise with abortion. Do not agree to support these bills. We recommend that you ignore those particular questions or ignore the whole questionnaire.

q: What is the theological basis the article gives for why politics cannot be a 'sacred vs. secular' carve-out?
a: Silberman argues that treating politics as a separate amoral sphere of pure power-negotiation is the doorway to compromise and is itself a denial of the fifth tenet of abolitionism. He grounds the rejection in 1 Corinthians 10:31 and Abraham Kuyper's claim of Christ's sovereignty over every square inch of human existence.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: There is not a square inch in the whole domain of our human existence over which Christ, who is Sovereign over all, does not cry, 'Mine!'

q: How does the article respond to political consultants who advise 'noncommittal, timid, status quo' messaging?
a: It dismisses that advice as outdated. Silberman says today's Republican primary voters have rejected wishy-washy lip service and are actively yearning for candidates who will speak strongly and clearly to important issues, including a clear demand for the abolition of abortion rather than pro-life triangulation.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: Today's Republican voters have no stomach for milquetoast smooth talkers. They are yearning for candidates who will speak clearly and strongly to important issues. They want abortion abolished. Not wishy-washy pro-life lip service.

q: Does honoring Christ in a campaign exclude strategic thinking and tactical flexibility?
a: No. The article makes room for discernment, efficiency, and varied tactics, including times to push, times to pull, times for direct confrontation, and times for wise maneuvering. What it forbids is any framework that severs prophetic witness and gospel preeminence from political work, which it calls both unbiblical and ultimately ineffective.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: This is not to say there isn't room for strategic thinking. We should strive for effectiveness and efficiency. There are times to push and times to pull; times for direct confrontation and times for wise maneuvering; times for the hard truth and times for an encouraging word.

q: What sample platform language does the article offer that candidates can adopt verbatim?
a: Silberman supplies a model paragraph framing abortion as state-sanctioned crime against humanity, repudiating do-nothing pro-life legislation, pledging immediate abolition legislation with equal protection, invoking love-of-neighbor, calling for an end to compromise with evil, and rejecting submission to Roe v. Wade. Candidates are explicitly invited to use any part or the whole of it.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: More than 5,000 babies are murdered in Oklahoma every year with the approval of Oklahoma State law. This is a crime against humanity and I am not running for office so that I can pay lip service to the issue and pass do-nothing legislation like most pro-life politicians. I am running to establish justice for preborn children.

q: Who does the article recommend candidates contact for practical campaign mentoring beyond abolitionist messaging?
a: Former Oklahoma State Senator Joseph Silk at the Liberty Rising Institute, and Tim Gillespie, a pastor and founder of the Oklahoma Second Amendment Association, are named as resources for the general mechanics of running for office that the article itself does not cover.
t: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
qt: we recommend [contacting former State Senator Joseph Silk at Liberty Rising Institute](https://www.liberty-rising.org/#contact) and Tim Gillespie, Pastor and Oklahoma Second Amendment Association Founder (timgillespieok@gmail.com).

q: What specific reason did Abby Johnson give for opposing the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act?
a: Johnson opposed the bill on financial grounds, arguing that abolishing abortion in Texas would cost the state federal money and programs, while labeling abolitionists 'ignorant' and accusing them of having 'no legislative backbone.'
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: Like many other pro-life leaders, Johnson opposed The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act. Responding to an abolitionist on Facebook, she criticized the bill on the grounds that abolishing abortion would cost Texas federal money and programs. She then calls supporters of abolishing abortion in Texas “ignorant” and supporters of a strategy with “no legislative backbone.”

q: Why does Silberman say Johnson's federal-funding objection is also economically illiterate?
a: Because Texas is a net donor state to the federal government—getting back only 57 cents per dollar sent to Washington—so the framing that federal money is a generous subsidy worth preserving over abolition collapses on its own terms.
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: Leaving aside that Johnson herself appears to be ignorant of the financial relationship between Texas and Washington D.C. (according to [Wallet Hub via *Dallas News*](https://www.dallasnews.com/business/economic-snapshot/2017/04/17/many-happy-returns-taxes-texas), Texas gets only $0.57 back for every dollar it sends to the federal government), what she’s saying here goes far beyond economic illiteracy.

q: What pattern of behavior does the article allege about Johnson's response to abolitionist criticism?
a: Silberman reports that Johnson deleted the original comment thread and blocked the commenter, and that he has personally spoken with sixteen other people who say Johnson has blocked them for criticizing her opposition to abolition bills.
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: I would provide a link to the whole comment thread but, Johnson deleted the comments and blocked the commenter, Susan Burklund, according to Burklund. This appears to be a pattern as I’ve spoken directly with 16 other people who say Johnson has blocked them for criticizing her for opposing abolition bills.

q: Why does the article argue that exempting parents from legal penalty effectively keeps abortion legal?
a: If only the abortionist faces prosecution while parents have automatic immunity, then do-it-yourself abortion remains legal—and since the industry is already shifting toward DIY methods, such laws merely accelerate that evolution rather than ending abortion.
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: First, abortion isn’t actually illegal if parents who murder their sons and daughters have automatic immunity. If only the abortionist faces justice, do-it-yourself abortion is legal. And with the abortion industry moving toward DIY abortion anyway, a bill providing legal penalty only for the abortionist accomplishes nothing but expedite the evolution of the abortion industry.

q: How does Jason Storms's testimony illustrate the deterrent function of criminalizing parents?
a: Storms told the Texas committee that he and his high school girlfriend would never have aborted their child had they known they would face homicide charges, arguing that consistent equal-protection law would prevent the killings rather than merely punish them.
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: “Here’s a fact: my girlfriend and I, if we knew we would’ve been facing homicide charges, would never have aborted that child. That child would be alive today. I’d have a 22-year-old little child that I could celebrate life with right now that’s not here. The law is a deterrent to crime. We shouldn’t think of this only as a matter of putting a woman on the stand. We should think of this as a great deterrent. Men and women would not think of doing this if we stood firm on the law and provided equal protection for these children.”

q: How does the article connect parental immunity in pre-Roe abortion statutes to the legalization of abortion itself?
a: Justice Harry Blackmun's footnote 54 in Roe v. Wade pointed to Texas's inconsistency—not treating the woman as a principal or accomplice and assigning lesser penalties than for murder—as evidence that even pro-life lawmakers did not really regard the preborn as persons, providing the legal opening for legalization.
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: “There are other inconsistencies between Fourteenth Amendment status and the typical abortion statute. It has already been pointed out, n. 49, supra, that in Texas the woman is not a principal or an accomplice with respect to an abortion upon her. If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?”

q: How does the article respond to the claim that hard circumstances should give mothers automatic legal immunity?
a: It analogizes to other crimes—armed robbery driven by hunger, violence rooted in past abuse, drunk driving after personal trauma—where mitigating circumstances may justify lenient sentences but never blanket immunity, and notes that abolition bills leave specific charges to prosecutors, judges, and juries.
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: An armed robber might go hungry without the $150 in the till at the corner store. A man might be driven to acts of violence by the physical abuse he suffered as a child. A drunk driver who kills someone might have been drinking because her husband just left her. Such circumstances may warrant a more lenient sentence in some cases, but they do not remove the need for justice to be served.

q: What is the practical body count Silberman attributes to killing the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act?
a: Because the Texas legislature meets only every two years, the bill's defeat is described as a literal death sentence for 110,000 preborn human beings who will be killed and discarded before another opportunity arises.
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: The Texas legislature meets only every two years, meaning the killing of the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act is a literal and undeniable death sentence for 110,000 preborn human beings. They will die and be cast into landfills, incineration chambers, and down garbage disposals so that Jeff Leach and his allies among pro-life lobbyists and state Republican leaders can virtue signal that they’re not like those mean abolitionists who actually think preborn human beings should get equal justice and protection.

q: Does the article note any later shift in Abby Johnson's position?
a: Yes—an editor's note prepended to the piece reports that Johnson has since amended her position on this subject, and links to a video where she states her updated view.
t: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
qt: *\*We are very happy to report that Abby Johnson has since amended her position on this subject. [See this video for her updated position.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bBmMZvGn6Cw)*

q: Why does the article describe the committee vote as a historic first?
a: Because no prior bill seeking the immediate abolition of abortion (as opposed to incremental restrictions) had ever made it out of a legislative committee. HB813's 7-to-2 passage in the Louisiana House Criminal Justice Committee broke that ceiling.
t: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
qt: For the first time, a bill to immediately abolish abortion has passed committee!

q: How does HB813 attempt to classify abortion as homicide under existing Louisiana law?
a: By amending the homicide code's definitions of 'unborn child' and 'person' to extend protection to human beings from fertilization, thereby pulling preborn children inside the existing murder statutes rather than creating a new standalone abortion crime.
t: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
qt: HB813, filed by State Rep. Danny McCormick, amends the Louisiana homicide codes to define “unborn child” as “an individual human being from fertilization until birth,” and “person” as “a human being from the moment of fertilization.” These redefinitions would classify abortion as homicide by including preborn children among those protected by the laws against murder.

q: What does the bill say about federal authority that contradicts its provisions?
a: It declares that any conflicting federal action—statute, regulation, treaty, executive order, or court ruling—is itself unconstitutional and void, rather than treating Louisiana's law as preempted. This is a textbook nullification posture.
t: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
qt: Any federal statute, regulation, treaty, executive order, or court ruling that purports to supersede, stay, or overrule this Section shall be in violation of the United States Constitution and the Constitution of Louisiana and is therefore void.

q: How does HB813 try to discipline state judges who might block enforcement?
a: It invokes the Louisiana Constitution's impeachment authority to make any state judge who enjoins, stays, overrules, or voids the section subject to impeachment or removal—an explicit pushback against judicial supremacy.
t: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
qt: Pursuant to the powers granted to the Legislature by Article X, Part III, of the Constitution of Louisiana, any judge of this state who purports to enjoin, stay, overrule, or void any provision of this Section shall be subject to impeachment or removal.

q: What concrete action does the article ask readers to take next?
a: Call and email Speaker Clay Schexnayder and Speaker Pro Tem Tanner Magee to request that HB813 be granted a hearing on the House floor; phone numbers and email addresses for both are provided.
t: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
qt: Please call and email Louisiana House Speaker Clay Schexnayder and Speaker Pro Tem Tanner Magee to respectfully request a floor hearing for HB813, the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act.

q: Why would an immediatist abolitionist see HB813 as different from a typical pro-life restriction?
a: Because it doesn't regulate, delay, or carve exceptions around abortion—it reclassifies it as homicide by treating the preborn as legal persons from fertilization, with no incremental limits attached.
t: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
qt: These redefinitions would classify abortion as homicide by including preborn children among those protected by the laws against murder.

q: Why does the Foundation to Abolish Abortion consider this Dobbs amicus brief historically significant for the Abolitionist Movement?
a: Because despite dozens of post-Roe abortion cases reaching the Supreme Court, this filing represents the first time the Court will receive arguments framed explicitly from the abolitionist perspective rather than from incrementalist pro-life regulators.
t: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
qt: There have been 39 abortion-related cases since *Roe v. Wade*, but for the first time, the Supreme Court will be hearing directly from the Abolitionist Movement.

q: On what constitutional basis does Pierce argue that preborn children must receive equal protection?
a: He grounds the argument in the Fourteenth Amendment, citing the historical record that at the time of ratification states recognized human life as beginning at conception and made no distinction between human beings and persons.
t: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
qt: When the amendment was ratified in 1868, [the states acknowledged](https://www.thepublicdiscourse.com/2017/08/19758/) both that human life begins at conception, and that there was no difference between human beings and persons.

q: Why does the article reject Mississippi's HB 1510 as inadequate even though it claims to restrict abortion?
a: Because the bill submits to Roe's framework, leaves all preborn children younger than 15 weeks unprotected, depends on the abortionist self-reporting the killing, and imposes only trivial professional or financial penalties.
t: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
qt: HB 1510 itself not only submits to *Roe*, but it does nothing to protect persons younger than 15 weeks gestation from being murdered, and thereby denies those people the equal protection of the law.

q: What role does the Tenth Amendment play in the brief's argument about state authority?
a: It establishes that states retain police power to enforce homicide law against the killing of preborn children and need not await Supreme Court permission before protecting their citizens' right to life.
t: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
qt: Second, according to the [Tenth Amendment](https://www.law.cornell.edu/constitution/tenth_amendment), states have the constitutional authority to use police power to control their own homicide enforcement.

q: How does the article justify state defiance of a Supreme Court ruling?
a: It argues that Roe is an illegitimate, unconstitutional ruling that violates God's law, and that when SCOTUS exceeds its constitutional authority states have both the right and duty to nullify and interpose rather than defer.
t: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
qt: Furthermore, the ruling is against God’s law, and states have the power to [defy its ruling](https://freethestates.org/nullification-and-interposition/index.html).

q: What recent ecclesial action does the article cite as backing for state nullification of unjust court decisions?
a: It points to a Southern Baptist Convention resolution declaring that civil authorities have a duty to establish equal protection for the preborn by intervening in, ignoring, or nullifying unjust decisions like those of the Supreme Court.
t: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
qt: “governing authorities at every level have a duty before God to uphold justice asserting their God-ordained and constitutional authority to establish equal protection under the law for all, born and preborn, by intervening, ignoring, or nullifying iniquitous decisions when other authorities, such as the Supreme Court, condone such injustices as the legal taking of innocent life.”

q: What evidence does the article offer that the incrementalist pro-life litigation strategy has failed?
a: It notes that HB 1510 is the 40th pro-life law to reach SCOTUS in the 48 years since Roe, and not a single sentence of Roe has been rolled back, even with Republican-appointed majorities on the Court for nearly that entire span.
t: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
qt: HB1510 is the 40th pro-life law to come before the Supreme Court in the 48 years since *Roe*, and not one sentence of the Roe decision has been rolled back

q: What ultimatum does Pierce's brief deliver to the Supreme Court?
a: It does not merely request that Roe be overturned; it tells the Court that overturning Roe in full is constitutionally mandatory, and warns that abolitionists will pursue impeachment and state defiance if SCOTUS refuses.
t: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
qt: He is telling them they must overturn it in full. Otherwise, we will utilize the constitutional checks and balances available to us: impeachment of corrupt officials rather than indifference towards them, and defiance of a tyrannical court rather than deference.

q: Why did the 5th Circuit and Supreme Court decline to enjoin the Texas Heartbeat Bill?
a: Because the bill's enforcement was structured to bar Texas state officials from enforcing it, leaving no State actor for abortion providers to sue preemptively. Plaintiffs would have to wait until private citizens actually filed suit and then challenge those suits as unconstitutional.
t: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
qt: The reason the 5th circuit court and supreme court declined to give the serial killers at Whole Women’s Health the injunction they were asking for is because the bill prohibits Texas state officials from enforcing the new law.

q: If the private-enforcement mechanism really evades Roe and Casey, why does Silberman say the heartbeat cutoff is incoherent?
a: If lawmakers genuinely believed they had found a workaround to federal abortion jurisprudence, then there was no pragmatic reason to limit protection to babies with detectable heartbeats. Drawing the line at a heartbeat reveals an addiction to compromise rather than a strategic legal necessity.
t: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
qt: Even with a bill that they believe will or could be a successful end run around the tyranny of the federal judiciary, they still chose to violate God’s law absent any even pragmatically compelling reason to do so. This is indicative of an addiction to compromise on the part of the pro-life establishment.

q: How does Silberman respond to the claim that opposing SB8 means abolitionists wanted those babies to die?
a: He flips the accusation, arguing that incremental bills like SB8 are precisely what allow the ongoing killing of every aborted baby to continue, because they provide political cover for legislators who refuse to abolish abortion outright.
t: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
qt: The truth is the exact opposite. It is the passage of bills like the heartbeat bill that allow every baby that is killed to be killed.

q: What happened to the 2021 Texas bill that would have abolished abortion entirely?
a: The Texas Republican establishment killed it. Representative Stephanie Klick denied the abolition bill a committee hearing and then appeared front-and-center celebrating SB8, using the heartbeat bill as cover for refusing to abolish abortion.
t: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
qt: There was a bill to abolish abortion in Texas this session. The Texas Republican establishment killed that bill... The particular representative who denied the abolition bill a committee hearing (Stephanie Klick) got to be front-and-center in the celebrations of the passage of the heartbeat bill.

q: How does the article frame incremental victories in spiritual terms?
a: Silberman casts incrementalism as a tool of the powers and principalities, comparing it to a chess game in which the devil happily sacrifices pawns to keep control of the board and prevent Christians from uniting in prophetic calls for repentance and total abolition.
t: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
qt: In chess terms, the devil is fine giving us some pawns as long as doing so keeps him in control of the game; as long as doing so keeps Christians from joining in a unified, prophetic voice calling the state to repentance in the name of Jesus Christ and working for the abolition of abortion.

q: What historical abolitionist precedent does Silberman invoke against gradualism?
a: He cites Elizabeth Heyrick, who argued that the slave holder counted on gradual emancipation to neutralize the abolitionist movement, calling gradual abolition the masterpiece of satanic policy that turned the demand for immediate justice into indefinite acquiescence.
t: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
qt: But this GRADUAL ABOLITION, has been the grand marplot of human virtue and happiness; the very masterpiece of satanic policy. By converting the cry for immediate, into gradual emancipation, the prince of slave holders [Satan], ‘transformed himself, with astonishing dexterity, into an angel of light,’ and thereby ‘deceived the very elect.’

q: Why does Silberman think private plaintiffs are unlikely to win SB8 suits in practice?
a: Private suits still depend on government courts that have repeatedly sided with the abortion industry, and private citizens lack the state's investigative powers needed to prove abortionists deliberately failed to detect a heartbeat, especially given how easily ultrasounds can be manipulated.
t: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
qt: Further, private citizens will have to prove in court that the abortionist did not detect a heartbeat without the many powers of investigation that only the state enjoys. And as has been demonstrated by ultrasound tech Sarah Cleveland, ultrasound images are easy to manipulate.

q: What does Silberman say Christians ought to do instead of supporting SB8?
a: He calls Christians to refuse to support the law and to unite in prophetic witness demanding justice as God defines it, rather than settling for ungodly compromise legislation.
t: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
qt: This law is ungodly and Christians should not support it. Instead, they must join in unified, prophetic voice calling for justice, as God defines it, to be established.

q: What did the Southern Baptist Convention actually vote on and pass at the 2021 meeting in Nashville?
a: The SBC passed the Southern Baptist Resolution to Abolish Abortion after amending it by adding a single word ('alone') to article 14. The resolution was motioned out of committee by Pastor Bill Ascol and approved by roughly 60 percent of the messengers.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: Following an amendment of a single word, the Southern Baptist Convention voted to pass [the Southern Baptist Resolution to Abolish Abortion]

q: How did the attempt to table (kill) the resolution play out, and what was the stated reason for it?
a: An anti-abolitionist messenger moved to table the resolution indefinitely, arguing it rejected the SBC's prior pro-life statements. The convention rejected the motion to table by a strong margin.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: One anti-abolitionist messenger proposed tabling the resolution, meaning that the debate and vote would be postponed indefinitely, effectively killing it. The reasoning given was that the resolution is a rejection of all previous statements of the SBC calling for pro-life legislation. When put to a vote, the SBC answered with a resounding no.

q: How did Bill Ascol respond to the ERLC member's claim that the resolution 'walks back' previous SBC statements?
a: Ascol denied that the resolution retreats from anything and instead framed it as forward movement toward immediate, uncompromising abolition.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: Ascol responded, "This resolution doesn't walk anything back. It walks us forward toward the immediate abolition of abortion without exception or compromise."

q: What was the one-word amendment to article 14, and why does the article view its narrow passage as significant?
a: A messenger added the word 'alone' to article 14 so it would no longer condemn incremental approaches outright. Because the amendment passed only narrowly, the author treats it as evidence that roughly 40 percent of more than 15,000 messengers were committed enough to abolitionism to oppose any softening language.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: another messenger proposed an amendment of a single word: adding "alone" to part (14), making it read, "that we will not embrace an incremental approach **alone** to ending abortion…"

q: Why does the author argue that adding 'alone' did not gut the resolution?
a: The amendment touched only one article while leaving more than a dozen other anti-incrementalist statements intact, including condemnations of partiality, calls for equal protection of the preborn, and direct rejection of pro-life incremental laws.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: While the amendment ruins article 14, the amendment did not touch the more than a dozen instances of anti-incrementalism written into the resolution: calling partiality and unequal standards abominations (6), establishing equal protection under the law for all preborn (9), condemning incremental pro-life laws outright (10), and many more.

q: How does the author use point (13) of the resolution to argue against incrementalism even after the amendment?
a: Point (13) rejects any position allowing exceptions to the legal protection of preborn neighbors, which the author argues logically forecloses incrementalism even though article 14 was softened.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: Consider an excerpt of point (13): "we reject any position that allows for any exceptions to the legal protection of our preborn neighbors compromises God's holy standard of justice, or promotes any God-partiality…" In light of just this clause – of which there are many more like it – any incrementalism is Godless.

q: Did any prominent pro-life incrementalist agree that the resolution still hurts the pro-life movement after the amendment?
a: Yes. Pro-life incrementalist Scott Klusendorf publicly conceded that adding the qualifying word did not undo the resolution's harsh language toward pro-lifers.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: Pro-life incrementalist Scott Klusendorf agrees. In response to a comment on his Facebook page today, Klusendorf said that his "concern is that adding [sic] 'only' doesn't undo the damning language towards pro-lifers, nor the historically ignorant claims that pro-lifers have failed."

q: How did The Tennessean characterize the resolution's significance for SBC policy?
a: The Tennessean reported that the nonbinding resolution marks a shift away from the SBC's prior advocacy for restrictions on abortion and instead calls for immediate abolition rather than regulatory restrictions.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: "The resolution, which is nonbinding, marks a shift in the formally held viewpoint of the convention, whose public policy arm has advocated for restrictions on abortion.

q: Why does the author insist that incrementalism and abolitionism cannot both be embraced at once?
a: The two strategies are defined by opposite commitments: gradual versus immediate, partial versus impartial, with versus without exceptions. They are inherently contradictory, so embracing both is logically impossible.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: Furthermore, on another note, incrementalism and abolitionism are inherently contrary. One is gradual, partial to some babies and not others, and includes exceptions to when, where and how the preborn can be murdered. The other is immediate, impartial and equal to all babies, and includes no exceptions to preborn murder. Embracing both is not possible.

q: How does the article ultimately characterize the SBC vote despite the asterisk in the headline?
a: The author calls the outcome a God-given victory for the Abolitionist Movement and treats the added 'alone' as a minor concession against the resolution's overwhelmingly abolitionist body.
t: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
qt: Though there is an asterisk, what transpired is undoubtedly a God-given victory for the Abolitionist Movement. The pastors who authored the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion wrote it as a lengthy and robust theological argument for a reason. The word "alone" is a pebble in the ocean of strong, decidedly abolitionist language, demonstrating the SBC's open support of the immediate, uncompromising abolition of abortion.

q: Why is Free the States closing its doors?
a: The current cultural and political landscape requires a refocused strategy emphasizing nationwide movement-building, cultural engagement, education, and national repentance, which is best pursued under a new organization rather than continuing FTS's prior structure focused primarily on state sovereignty and legislative initiatives.
t: Free the States Has Closed It's Doors... What's Next? Abolitionists Rising!
qt: To best achieve these long-held goals, FTS is closing it's doors and we are restructuring under a new organization, Abolitionists Rising.

q: What was Free the States' original strategic focus before the transition?
a: Since 2018, Free the States pursued abolition primarily by asserting state sovereignty and pushing legislative initiatives, work the team credits with significant progress for preborn children.
t: Free the States Has Closed It's Doors... What's Next? Abolitionists Rising!
qt: Since 2018, Free the States sought to abolish abortion through various projects primarily focused on asserting state sovereignty and legislative initiatives.

q: What kind of movement is Abolitionists Rising trying to build?
a: A robust, nationwide movement united under biblical ideology and uncompromising commitment to Christ, with Abolitionists Rising acting as both catalyst and resource hub for abolitionists across the country.
t: Free the States Has Closed It's Doors... What's Next? Abolitionists Rising!
qt: We strive to help build a movement united under biblical ideology and commitment to Christ, never compromising on those core principles.

q: What is the legal and organizational status of Abolitionists Rising?
a: Abolitionists Rising is currently active and pursuing 501(c)3 tax-deductible status, will retain former Free the States staff, and has assembled a board of directors.
t: Free the States Has Closed It's Doors... What's Next? Abolitionists Rising!
qt: AR is currently active and is in the process of becoming a 501(c)3 tax-deductible organization. It will maintain former FTS staff and has established a robust board of directors.

q: What new resources and content can supporters expect from Abolitionists Rising?
a: A new website with a knowledge center, The Norman Statement, news updates, and local connection tools; expanded social media (already nearing two million daily views); more educational videos, podcasts, and documentaries; and an overhauled gear store.
t: Free the States Has Closed It's Doors... What's Next? Abolitionists Rising!
qt: We are building a website that will contain many resources, including a knowledge center, The Norman Statement, regular news updates, and ways to connect with other abolitionists in your area.

q: When and where is the next major in-person abolitionist gathering?
a: The 2024 Abolition Now Conference will be held in Charlotte, North Carolina from April 18-21, and the team intends to make it the biggest and most culturally engaging conference yet.
t: Free the States Has Closed It's Doors... What's Next? Abolitionists Rising!
qt: Also, mark your calendars for the 2024 Abolition Now Conference in Charlotte, North Carolina April 18-21, which we intend to make the biggest and most culturally engaging conference yet!

q: What should existing Free the States donors do about their giving?
a: Donations to Free the States have been ceased; supporters are asked to redirect their giving to Abolitionists Rising via AbolitionistsRising.com/Donate, ideally as a monthly contribution.
t: Free the States Has Closed It's Doors... What's Next? Abolitionists Rising!
qt: Donations to Free the States have all been ceased, but your support to Abolitionists Rising is critical to keep us driving forward.

q: Why does the post emphasize signing up for email updates?
a: Big tech platforms have decreased the team's social media reach in recent months, so email is the most reliable channel for receiving important content and abolition bill news.
t: Free the States Has Closed It's Doors... What's Next? Abolitionists Rising!
qt: Big tech is cracking down on our ability to reach you! Our social media reach has been greatly decreased in recent months.

q: Who introduced the Abolition of Abortion in Washington Act and when?
a: State Representative Matt Shea (R-Spokane) introduced the bill on March 21st, 2019, with co-sponsors Reps. Vicki Kraft (R-Vancouver), Brad Klippert (R-Kennewick), and Jesse Young (R-Gig Harbor).
t: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
qt: On March 21st, State Representative Matt Shea (R-Spokane) introduced a bill that would end abortion in Washington State. The bill is co-sponsored by Reps. Vicki Kraft (R-Vancouver), Brad Klippert (R-Kennewick), and Jesse Young (R-Gig Harbor).

q: How does WA HB2154 define when human life begins, and what legal status does it grant the unborn?
a: The bill recognizes life as beginning at fertilization and, mirroring the 14th Amendment, requires equal protection of the laws for all human beings including the unborn, which would classify abortion as homicide.
t: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
qt: The Abolition of Abortion in Washington Act (WA HB2154) would recognize the observable truth that human physical life begins at “the moment of fertilization upon the fusion of a human spermatozoon with a human ovum.” Mirroring the wording of the 14th amendment of the US Constitution, the bill establishes that all human beings, including the unborn, shall be “[p]rovide[d] the equal protection of the laws of this state.” Abortion would thus be classified as homicide.

q: How does the bill respond to potential federal court interference?
a: Section 7 declares any federal act, law, treaty, order, regulation, or court decision that prohibits protecting the unborn's right to life null, void, and unenforceable in Washington, and denies federal courts jurisdiction over the state's protection of human life from fertilization.
t: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
qt: Any act, law, treaty, order, or regulation of the United States government that denies or prohibits protection of a human person’s inalienable right to life is null, void, and unenforceable in this state. (2) The courts of the United States have no jurisdiction to interfere with this state’s interest in protecting human physical life from the moment that human physical life begins. (3) Any court decision purporting to strike down or enjoin the provisions of this chapter or enjoin this state from protecting innocent human physical life from the moment of fertilization is nonauthoritative, void, and of no force.

q: Where does Washington fit in the national wave of abolition bills, and what's happened to the others?
a: Washington is the fifth state to introduce such a bill, after Idaho, Indiana, Oklahoma, and Texas; Idaho, Indiana, and Oklahoma efforts were killed by Republican leadership and National Right to Life, while Texas's TX HB896 was still alive and expected to receive a hearing on April 8th.
t: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
qt: Washington is the fifth state to introduce such a bill, following [Idaho](https://www.facebook.com/watch/?v=1975827382724998), [Indiana](https://freethestates.org/2019/03/indiana-pro-life-leaders-kill-protection-at-conception-act/index.html), [Oklahoma](https://freethestates.org/2019/02/the-three-pro-life-men-keeping-abortion-legal-in-oklahoma/index.html), and Texas. The Idaho, Indiana, and Oklahoma efforts have been killed by Republican leadership and National Right to Life. The effort to abolish abortion in Texas is still alive, as TX HB896 is expected to receive a hearing on April 8th.

q: Why pursue an abolition bill in Washington when Democrats control every lever of state government?
a: Silberman argues the law functions as a teacher (citing Galatians 3) and that the legislative pro-life movement has been teaching false exceptions; the message the culture must hear is the unconditional prohibition 'you shall not murder,' so the demand must be made even where passage looks impossible.
t: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
qt: Still, it is crucially important that the demand for abortion’s abolition be made. As Paul wrote in Galatians 3, the law is a teacher. And what has the legislative wing of the pro-life movement been teaching the culture? You shall not murder unless the victim is killed with a certain technique? You shall not murder unless the victim is incapable of pain? You shall not murder unless the victim doesn’t yet have a detectable heartbeat? The message the world needs to hear is “you shall not murder.”

q: What does the article claim incremental pro-life laws teach besides accepting some abortions?
a: They also teach that unconditional submission to the Supreme Court is required, which the author rejects as a fiction contradicted by an honest reading of Article VI of the U.S. Constitution.
t: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
qt: Not only that, but incremental laws teach people that unconditional submission to the Supreme Court is [required of them](https://lessermagistrate.com/why-trigger-laws-are-prolonging-the-slaughter-of-the-preborn/). This is not the case. An honest reading of Article VI of the US Constitution clearly contradicts the fiction of judicial supremacy.

q: What historical authority does Silberman cite against judicial supremacy?
a: He quotes Thomas Jefferson's 1820 letter to Charles Jarvis warning that treating judges as ultimate arbiters of constitutional questions would place the nation under 'the despotism of an oligarchy,' since the Constitution makes all departments co-equal and co-sovereign.
t: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
qt: You seem to consider the judges as the ultimate arbiters of all constitutional questions; a very dangerous doctrine indeed, and one which would place us under the despotism of an oligarchy. Our judges are as honest as other men, and not more so. They have, with others, the same passions for party, for power, and the privilege of their corps…. Their power [is] the more dangerous as they are in office for life, and not responsible, as the other functionaries are, to the elective control. The Constitution has erected no such single tribunal, knowing that to whatever hands confided, with the corruptions of time and party, its members would become despots. It has more wisely made all the departments co-equal and co-sovereign within themselves.

q: What scale of killing does the article say abolition would address in Washington?
a: Roughly 17,000 unborn children are murdered annually in Washington, and the author urges prayer that HB2154 would one day become law.
t: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
qt: With roughly 17,000 murdered annually in Washington, pray that it will one day become law.

q: Why does Free the States insist on distinguishing the Abolitionist Movement from the Pro-Life Movement instead of just sharing the 'pro-life' label?
a: Because the two movements hold meaningfully different ideological tenets, and blurring the labels obscures the choice people need to make between them. Without a clear category distinction, abolitionist ideas, bills, and candidates cannot be communicated or contrasted with their pro-life counterparts, and the movement's growth stalls.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Affirm that distinguishing the Abolitionist Movement from the Pro-Life Movement is crucial to clearly communicating the ideological distinctions between the tenets of abolitionism and the tenets of pro-lifeism; and that the maintenance of this ideological distinction is crucial toward people understanding the difference between abolitionism and pro-lifeism, abolition bills and pro-life bills, abolitionist candidates and pro-life candidates.

q: Is fighting over the term 'pro-life' a productive use of abolitionist energy?
a: No. Silberman explicitly says that battling over ownership of 'pro-life' produces confusion rather than clarity and actively stunts the movement's growth. The point is to maintain a clean ideological distinction, not to litigate vocabulary.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: Fighting over who gets to use the term “pro-life” will lead to confusion and will stunt the growth of the movement.

q: Are people who hold abolitionist convictions but still call themselves 'pro-life' in sin for that choice?
a: No. Free the States considers it an unwise decision but explicitly denies it is a sin. The label issue is a strategic and clarity question, not a charge of moral guilt against the individual.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Deny that those who affirm the tenets of abolitionism but choose to use a different label, including “pro-life,” are in sin for that unwise decision.

q: Is Free the States being petty or tribal by drawing such sharp lines between abolitionism and pro-lifeism?
a: Silberman flips the accusation: he says it is often pride, tribalism, and pettiness that drive people who already believe abolitionist tenets to keep clinging to the 'pro-life' identity. Pro-life pride, in his view, is the deeper motive at play, not abolitionist tribalism.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: On the contrary, it is often for petty, tribal, and/or prideful reasons that people who have come to embrace the tenets of abolitionism insist on still calling themselves “pro-life.” Pro-life pride in particular plays a big role here.

q: Does identifying as 'pro-life' automatically disqualify a person or organization from partnership with abolitionists?
a: No. Free the States is willing to work with people and organizations who use the pro-life label. The concern is that some who claim abolitionist ideology while clinging to the pro-life label are actually resisting parts of abolitionism, and those underlying ideological gaps may or may not preclude partnership case-by-case.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Deny that an individual or organization choosing to identify as “pro-life” is enough reason to not partner with them. We are willing to work with such people and organizations.

q: How does Free the States justify treating abortion as a uniquely high priority compared to other kingdom causes?
a: By echoing R.C. Sproul: a church silent during a holocaust ceases to be a real church. Other kingdom work is real and important, but the active mass killing of image-bearers takes priority over nearly all of it, and the church must act as if preborn neighbors actually are image bearers.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Affirm, with R.C. Sproul, that “When the church is silent in the midst of a holocaust, she ceases to be a real church.” Busyness with other things, even important kingdom endeavors, is no excuse for being apathetic regarding a present holocaust.

q: Is idolatry of abolitionism a real, widespread problem inside the movement?
a: Silberman denies it is widespread. Because abolitionism is defined as the gospel applied to the evil of the age, anyone who elevates the movement above the gospel ceases to be a real abolitionist by definition. Gospel-centeredness is a non-negotiable tenet, which makes the idolatry charge particularly off-target.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Deny that idolatry of abolitionism is a widespread problem in the Abolitionist Movement. Abolitionism is simply the gospel applied to the evil of the age. The gospel and obedience to Christ is our highest objective. An abolitionist who elevates anything above the gospel of Jesus Christ ceases to be a real abolitionist.

q: How does Free the States contrast the Abolitionist Movement with most political movements, including pro-life organizations?
a: Most political movements, including the major pro-life organizations, are built on ecumenical big-tent strategy and operate as officially secular groups so as not to be dismissed as religious. The Abolitionist Movement, by contrast, refuses to subordinate gospel proclamation to political pragmatism, which is rare in the political world.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: Virtually every political movement in existence is rooted in ecumenicism and big tent philosophies which are viewed by worldly thinkers as the only way to social and political change. Nearly all of the major pro-life organizations are officially secular organizations, and they refuse to integrate the gospel into their mission for fear of being labeled a religious movement by abortion supporters.

q: What is Free the States' position on incrementalist anti-abortion strategy?
a: They affirm that incrementalism, defined as changing abortion law by degrees, is unbiblical and that it actively delays abolition. It is not viewed as a slower path to the same goal but as an obstacle to that goal.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Affirm that an incrementalist approach toward fighting the abortion holocaust (ie. a strategy of changing abortion law by degrees) is unbiblical and that it delays the abolition of abortion.

q: Who does Free the States blame for the continued legality of abortion in heavily Republican states?
a: Pro-life incrementalist politicians, lobbyists, organizations, thought leaders, and the pro-lifers who support them. In states like Oklahoma, Texas, Idaho, Indiana, Arizona, South Carolina, and Missouri, where pro-life Republicans hold all the levers, those incrementalists are named as the primary obstacle to abolition.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Affirm that pro-life incrementalist politicians, lobbyists, organizations, thought leaders, and the pro-lifers who support them are the primary obstacles to the abolition of abortion in states like Oklahoma, Texas, Idaho, Indiana, Arizona, South Carolina, Missouri, and others where pro-life Republicans control all the levers of power in the State government.

q: Does Free the States believe pro-life incrementalists can be saved Christians?
a: Yes. Silberman explicitly denies the idea that pro-life incrementalists cannot be saved and grants that there are many well-meaning, born-again believers within the Pro-Life Movement. He treats it as somewhat absurd that this even has to be clarified.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Deny the idea that pro-life incrementalists cannot be saved. On the contrary, there are many well-meaning, born again believers in the Pro-Life Movement. It is crazy that we have to clarify of our view on this, but unfortunately, we do.

q: Why does Free the States care so much about the historical narrative of how abortion gets abolished?
a: Because they want future generations to credit the church awakening from apathy and embracing biblical abolitionist tenets, not worldly pragmatism. That historical record matters as a testimony for future believers fighting their own age's evils.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: We Affirm that it is our desire that when abortion is abolished, commentators and historians and everyone else knows that it was abolished because the Church of Jesus Christ awoke from its apathy and rejected worldly pragmatism by embracing the tenets of abolitionism. That this occurs will be an important testimony for future generations to look at and learn from as they fight the evil of their age.

q: Is Free the States drawing distinctions with the Pro-Life Movement out of youthful vanity or platform-building?
a: Silberman argues the financial incentive runs the opposite direction. The Pro-Life Movement holds the money, political access, and large platforms, so anyone seeking those things would gravitate to it. Choosing the Abolitionist Movement is choosing harder truth-telling over access, even if that gap is shrinking as abolitionism grows.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: On the contrary, the Pro-Life Movement is where the money, political connections, and huge platforms are. Those seeking large platforms and influential partnerships will be drawn to identify with the Pro-Life Movement. Those willing to speak the hard truth without regard for access to larger platforms and bigger tents will be drawn to the Abolitionist Movement.

q: How does Silberman defend Russell Hunter against the charge of seeking personal credit?
a: By personal testimony. He calls Hunter the least credit-seeking leader he has ever worked with: Hunter routinely platforms others instead of himself, dislikes putting his name on things, and explicitly tells staff to disagree with him in meetings. Unlike most organizations that exist to grow the CEO's brand, Hunter is described as zealous for the tenets of abolitionism but indifferent to his own reputation.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: Russell, who started the Abolitionist Movement and founded Free the States, takes every opportunity to platform others instead of himself. While I would naturally give him radio and podcast appearances that we are offered, he often tells me that people need to know me better and that I should do them. He doesn’t like putting his name on things.

q: Is this article meant to be the start of an ongoing public debate over these accusations?
a: No, the opposite. Silberman frames it as the last time Free the States plans to address these slanderous charges for the foreseeable future. The point is to put a definitive answer on the record so the staff can return to the actual work of fighting abortion.
t: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
qt: Thus far, Free the States staff members have combatted these accusations using their personal social media pages, but this article will be the last time we address the subject for the foreseeable future. After this, we will be moving past it to the important work there is to be done.

q: Why does the resolution not explicitly address ectopic pregnancies, and is that an oversight?
a: It is not an oversight. The authors deliberately omitted ectopic pregnancies because the standard treatment is not properly classified as abortion. Removing a baby who has already died is not abortion, and the preterm delivery of a baby with no chance of survival is morally distinct from the intentional destruction of the child by D&E or D&C. Conflating the two slanders grieving parents and gives moral cover to actual abortion.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: The authors did not explicitly address ectopic pregnancies because the treatment of ectopic pregnancies is not properly defined as abortion.

q: What amendment did abolitionists prepare in case the convention insisted on addressing ectopic pregnancy?
a: Lacey Adamson, an abolitionist nurse and messenger from Ekklesia Muskogee, was prepared to offer an amendment between articles 13 and 14 framing ectopic pregnancy as a tragedy requiring medical triage in which doctors treat both mother and baby as human beings and exhaust all options under the Hippocratic Oath. The article holds this up as the proper template for any future SBC language on the topic, rather than the professors' approach of equating treatment with abortion.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: (14) RESOLVED, that we recognize that ectopic pregnancies are a tragedy during which medical triage is necessary, with doctors never ceasing to treat both mother and baby as human beings, and upholding the Hippocratic Oath by exhausting all possible options to save as many human lives as they can.

q: Does abolitionism actually expect abortion to end overnight, as the SBC professors' Normandy analogy implies?
a: No. Silberman calls the Normandy analogy a sign of either ignorance or bad faith. Abolitionists, like their 19th century predecessors, expect a long, costly fight. The dispute with incrementalism is not about timeline but about what we demand: immediate, principled abolition versus compromise legislation that ratifies the legality of killing some preborn children.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: abolitionism is not overnight-ism. Abolitionists are not under the impression that the culture and politics will drastically change overnight or that the object of our protest will be abolished overnight.

q: How does William Lloyd Garrison's framing illuminate the abolitionist demand?
a: Garrison conceded that even the most urgent demand for immediate abolition would in practice unfold gradually, but insisted the demand itself must remain uncompromising. The article uses this to show that the abolition-vs-incrementalism debate is about the content of the demand, not the duration of the struggle. Abolitionists set a high principled bar and let the culture move toward them.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: Urge immediate abolition as earnestly as we may, it will alas! be gradual abolition in the end. We have never said that slavery would be overthrown by a single blow; that it ought to be we shall always contend.

q: Why does the article say incremental pro-life bills cannot be grounded in Scripture or the Constitution?
a: Because incremental laws like 20-week bans or heartbeat bills implicitly concede that abortion before some arbitrary line is permissible. That concession violates both the Sixth Commandment and the Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection clause. By contrast, the abolitionist demand for total abolition can be defended directly from 'You shall not murder' and from equal protection under the law.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: An incrementalist's demand for a 20-week abortion ban or a heartbeat bill cannot be defended from God's Word or the Constitution, as these laws – by implying the legality of abortion prior to the arbitrary standard they set for who counts as fully human – violate both.

q: How does the article turn Proverbs 24:11, the professors' lone proof text, against them?
a: Silberman points out that the verse contains no exception for state-sanctioned killing. The pro-life claim that 'all can't be saved right now' presupposes unconditional submission to Roe and Casey, treating SCOTUS opinions as binding even when they authorize murder. He argues the verse actually demands the opposite of incremental compromise: it demands rescue of those being led to slaughter, full stop.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: Notice that Proverbs 24:11 does not say, "Deliver those who are being taken away to death, unless the supreme court authorizes their murder."

q: What does the article identify as idolatry in mainstream pro-life leadership?
a: It points to statements from figures like Joe Pojman of Texas Alliance for Life and Scott Klusendorf of Life Training Institute that frame SCOTUS authority as a law of nature or as 'on high.' Silberman calls this an idolatrous reduction of allegiance to God into a temporary alliance whenever God's Word and the Court happen to agree. Civil authorities are due only conditional submission, conditioned on their own submission to Christ.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: Equating the authority of the supreme court to a law of nature or referring to the justices with phrases related to divinity are a form of idolatry.

q: What concrete means does the resolution propose for actually abolishing abortion?
a: Article 9 of the resolution names interposition by governing authorities at every level: intervening, ignoring, or nullifying iniquitous SCOTUS decisions and establishing equal protection for all, born and preborn. The article cites Daniel 3, 1 Kings 12, 2 Kings 11, and Jeremiah 26, 36, 37, and 39 as biblical warrant. Silberman dismisses the professors' suggestion that the means require armed resistance or civil war as scare tactics; abolitionism has always been a peaceful movement.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: (9) WHEREAS, governing authorities at every level have a duty before God to uphold justice asserting their God-ordained and constitutional authority to establish equal protection under the law for all, born and preborn, by intervening, ignoring, or nullifying iniquitous decisions when other authorities, such as the Supreme Court, condone such injustices as the legal taking of innocent life

q: Why does the article cite the SBC's resolution on the Uyghur genocide as a parallel?
a: Resolution #8 demanded that China cease its Uyghur genocide 'immediately,' with no incremental qualifier. Silberman argues that any messenger who proposed weakening that demand to merely gradual cessation would have been instantly recognized as failing the oppressed. The same standard exposes the absurdity of demanding only gradual cessation of abortion.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: Had a messenger suggested weakening this language to demand only the gradual ceasing of the genocide, it would have been immediately recognized as a failure to be consistently Christian, and a failure to make a strong stand for the oppressed Uyghur people.

q: How does the article respond to the charge that abolitionists 'fail to act in the day of distress'?
a: Silberman calls the accusation evidence of ignorance. Abolitionist rallies at state capitols dwarf incremental pro-life rallies, and abolitionists outnumber pro-lifers at most abortion mills. The movement deliberately reserves the term 'abolitionist' for people who actually act; mere holders of a pro-life opinion are not counted.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: There is not a more active group of people anywhere for any cause than the abolitionists.

q: Is the resolution misogynistic for hinting at prosecution of post-abortive women?
a: Silberman calls this an outright lie. The resolution simply demands equal protection under the law for preborn children, which logically entails the possible prosecution of any culpable person, man or woman, who knowingly participates in the murder. He says conflating equality for the preborn with misogyny is the rhetorical move of pro-aborts, and he calls on the SBC professors to issue a correction and apology.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: The willful conflation of the abolitionist desire for equality for preborn children with misogyny is the tactic of pro-aborts. The SBC professors lied, sinning against God and the pastors who authored the resolution.

q: What is Silberman's overall verdict on the SBC professors' Public Discourse article?
a: Despite the credentials of its eight signatories, the article was almost entirely without scriptural reference, and its one biblical argument from Proverbs 24:11 was worse than no argument at all. Silberman concludes the piece is at times ignorant and at times dishonest, and that its authors should unpublish it.
t: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
qt: At times ignorant, and at times dishonest, this woefully poor attempt at refuting the Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion should be unpublished by the authors.

q: What did the Louisiana House actually do to HB813 on May 12, 2022?
a: The House adopted an amendment that stripped HB813 of its abolition language and substituted trigger language that merely raised penalties slightly above an existing 2006 trigger law, turning the bill into a duplicate of Senate Bill 342 and leading the author to pull it from consideration.
t: Rep. Alan Seabaugh and the Louisiana House Ensure Murder by Abortion Continues
qt: The Louisiana State House voted on Thursday to strike all language from the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act (HB813) and replace it with trigger language despite a trigger bill already having been passed in 2006.

q: Who filed the gutting amendment, and how is that politically significant?
a: The amendment was filed by self-described pro-life Rep. Alan Seabaugh, who had voted enthusiastically for the unamended bill in committee just days before, illustrating the article's claim that the danger to abolition comes from inside the pro-life caucus.
t: Rep. Alan Seabaugh and the Louisiana House Ensure Murder by Abortion Continues
qt: The amendment that gutted the bill was filed by pro-life State Rep. Alan Seabaugh, who enthusiastically supported HB813 as originally written in the committee hearing.

q: How did the final vote break down?
a: Sixty-five representatives voted to adopt the gutting amendment and twenty-six voted against it, with many of the same Republicans who had advanced the bill out of committee voting to erase it on the floor.
t: Rep. Alan Seabaugh and the Louisiana House Ensure Murder by Abortion Continues
