But when Rapert's bill was announced in November, the legislator who was considering putting forward an actual abolition bill thought that all the momentum inside the capitol was behind Rapert's bill and decided not to put forward the Abolition of Abortion in Arkansas Act.

q: What does HB2650 actually do to Arizona law if enacted?
a: It redefines 'person' to include the unborn at any stage of development and immediately ends abortion in the state by stripping the existing legal exemptions that shielded abortionists and mothers from prosecution under homicide statutes.
t: AZ Rep. Walt Blackman Presents Abolition of Abortion in Arizona Act to 1,200+ Supporters
qt: HB2650 establishes that a “Person includes a child in the womb at any state of development” and would totally and immediately abolish abortion in the state in defiance of the supreme court.

q: How does the bill instruct Arizona to handle conflicting federal authority?
a: It directs the legislature to treat any federal act, court decision, or executive order that would shield abortion as void, and it commands county attorneys to enforce homicide law against abortion regardless of federal opposition.
t: AZ Rep. Walt Blackman Presents Abolition of Abortion in Arizona Act to 1,200+ Supporters
qt: Treat as void and of no effect any and all federal acts, laws, treaties, decisions, orders, rules or regulations that would deprive an unborn child of the right to life or prohibit the protection of such right.

q: What is the bill's stated philosophical purpose for the unborn?
a: The purpose clause asserts equal protection: a living unborn child from fertilization onward must enjoy the same rights and legal protections as any other human person under Arizona law.
t: AZ Rep. Walt Blackman Presents Abolition of Abortion in Arizona Act to 1,200+ Supporters
qt: Provide unborn children the equal protection of the laws of this state, to establish that a living human unborn child from the moment of fertilization and at every stage of development is entitled to the same rights, powers, privileges, justice and protections as are secured or granted by the laws of this state to any other human person.

q: Who co-authored HB2650, and how much institutional support did it have at filing?
a: The bill launched with ten co-authors, including members of House leadership such as Majority Leader Ben Toma and Majority Whip Leo Biasiucci, signaling more than fringe sponsorship.
t: AZ Rep. Walt Blackman Presents Abolition of Abortion in Arizona Act to 1,200+ Supporters
qt: HB2650 debuts with 10 authors, including Blackman, House Majority Leader Ben Toma, House Majority Whip Leo Biasiucci, and Reps. Brenda Barton, Shawnna Bolick, Judy Burges, Frank Carroll, David Cook, John Fillmore, and Jake Hoffman.

q: What specific statutory carve-outs does HB2650 strike from current Arizona code?
a: It deletes the affirmative defenses that currently exempt abortionists who obtain consent, providers framing abortion as 'medical treatment,' and the mother herself from prosecution.
t: AZ Rep. Walt Blackman Presents Abolition of Abortion in Arizona Act to 1,200+ Supporters
qt: The person was the unborn child’s mother.

q: How big was the public turnout, and who spoke at the events?
a: Between 1,200 and 1,500 people attended the press conference and rally, which featured Pastor Jeff Durbin, Dennis Sarfate, Rusty Thomas, Bradley Pierce, and Blackman himself, among others.
t: AZ Rep. Walt Blackman Presents Abolition of Abortion in Arizona Act to 1,200+ Supporters
qt: The rally featured speeches from Rep. Blackman, his wife Kristi Blackman, Durbin, Sarfate, Rusty Thomas, Luke Pierson, Zack Morgan, Zach Lautenschlager, and Bradley Pierce.

q: What is the next procedural hurdle the bill must clear?
a: It needs a committee assignment; Judiciary or Health and Human Services are most likely, with a slim chance of going to Criminal Justice, where Blackman serves as chairman.
t: AZ Rep. Walt Blackman Presents Abolition of Abortion in Arizona Act to 1,200+ Supporters
qt: The bill now awaits a committee assignment, with the House Judiciary and Health and Human Services committees being the most likely assignments. There is an outside chance of an assignment to the House Criminal Justice Committee of which Blackman is the chairman.

q: How do abolition bills actually handle medically dangerous situations like ectopic pregnancy, contrary to Barber's claim that they leave women to die?
a: Abolition bills address such situations by restating or strengthening existing legal defenses (self-defense, duress) and, in some cases, providing explicit statutory exemptions. The article cites Texas HB3326 as one example that explicitly justifies lawful medical procedures intended to remove an ectopic pregnancy when no alternative is available to save both lives. Barber's worry that doctors would be hauled into court for every such case ignores how prosecutors actually exercise discretion.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: Conduct is justified if the conduct charged is a lawful medical procedure performed by a physician or other licensed health care provider and intended to remove an ectopic pregnancy that seriously threatens the life of the mother when a reasonable alternative to save the lives of both the mother and the unborn child is unavailable.

q: Why does the authors say Barber's affirmative-defense objection is really an objection to the entire American legal system, not to abolition bills specifically?
a: Affirmative defenses are the standard mechanism by which American criminal law (and most pro-life law) handles justified conduct. If Barber objects to doctors having to raise a defense, he is objecting to how nearly all criminal statutes work, not to anything unique to abolition bills, which simply extend existing law to children in the womb.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: Even as a non-issue, Bart's problem is not fundamentally with abolition bills: his problem is with the entire American legal system and almost all pro-life bills that operates with affirmative defenses.

q: What is wrong with Barber's classification of treatments for ectopic pregnancy as 'abortion'?
a: Treatments for ectopic pregnancy (salpingostomy and salpingectomy) are categorically distinct from abortion: they are not the cause of the baby's death and may save the mother's life. Calling them abortion conflates morally and medically different procedures and muddles the abolitionist position.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: Barber, however, classifies these medical procedures as abortion, writing that these procedures "take the ectopic baby's life to save the mother."

q: How does the article use James 1:14-15 to challenge the 'second victim' narrative?
a: James 1:14-15 teaches that each person is tempted by their own desire, which then conceives sin. External pressure from boyfriends, families, or stigma does not exonerate a person who consents to murder; Scripture locates moral responsibility in the individual who acts on sinful desire.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: But each person is tempted when he is lured and enticed by his own desire. Then desire when it has conceived gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown brings forth death.

q: How do the authors expose the internal contradiction in Barber's position that aborting mothers are simultaneously victims and morally culpable?
a: Barber affirms that women who consent to abortion are morally and spiritually culpable while also calling them victims in every case. The authors argue Scripture has no category for 'innocent murderers' or 'guiltless violence' and that calling someone a victim while also calling them culpable destroys the call to repentance.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: If every woman, in every abortion, every time is a victim, then can they simultaneously be morally and spiritually culpable? There is no biblical category for what Barber is grasping for here. There is no category set aside for innocent murderers or guiltless violence.

q: What is the article's clarification about whether laws can 'mandate' prosecution?
a: No law mandates prosecution. Prosecutors retain discretion in every jurisdiction. Laws make prosecution possible; they do not bind a prosecutor to bring charges where the facts do not warrant it. The authors say Barber and the Light Magazine editors betray legal ignorance by framing it otherwise.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: The first point which must be noted is that no law "mandates" prosecution. That's not how laws work, and this is another example of Barber's legal ignorance (and that of Light Magazine's editors) rearing its head. Laws make possible the prosecution of crimes but prosecutors have discretion regarding whether to bring charges to court.

q: How does the article apply Leviticus 19 and Genesis 9:6 to argue against legal partiality toward preborn neighbors?
a: Loving one's preborn neighbor as oneself, per Jesus' citation of Leviticus 19:18, requires showing no legal partiality and judging in righteousness per Leviticus 19:15. The righteous standard, per Genesis 9:6, is that whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed because man is made in God's image.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: Meaning, if you want to love your neighbor—especially your preborn neighbor—show no legal partiality to them and judge them in righteousness. Judge them according to the righteous standard God has given us: the one that says, "Whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed, for God made man in his own image" (Genesis 9:6).

q: Why do the authors call Barber's appeal to pragmatism a worldview problem rather than a methodological one?
a: Pragmatism is a distinct philosophical worldview (Peirce/James) that locates the meaning and truth of beliefs in their practical consequences. The authors argue this is categorically distinct from a Christian worldview that judges actions by God's revealed standard, not by outcomes, so labeling pragmatism as 'wisdom employed to obey God' smuggles in a non-Christian epistemology.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: Pragmatism is not an issue of nuance—it is a philosophical worldview, one that is categorically distinct from the Christian worldview.

q: How does the article respond to Barber's appeal to Mosaic case law (cities of refuge, manslaughter laws) as evidence that wisdom permits incremental application of the sixth commandment?
a: The authors say Barber conflates two different things: God justly distinguishing between categories of homicide (intentional vs. accidental) versus humans choosing to write laws that strip some preborn children of all rights and equal protection. The first is righteous categorization; the second is making the fatherless prey, per Isaiah 10:2.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: This a conflation of God justly differentiating between categories of homicide and man choosing to write laws which deny some preborn children of all of their rights and legal equality, thereby "making the fatherless prey" (Isaiah 10:2).

q: Why have abolition bills failed to pass even in pro-life states, according to the article?
a: Pro-life organizations themselves have organized against abolition bills. The Louisiana Abolition of Abortion Act passed committee 7-2, but after the ERLC and 75 other pro-life organizations signed an open letter against it, supporting legislators flipped and killed the bill on the House floor. The very groups complaining that abolition bills don't pass are often the reason they don't.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: It is first and foremost pro-life organizations who have stood against bills to abolish abortion at every turn, most famously in Louisiana when the ERLC (which publishes Light Magazine and is an SBC entity) signed an open letter with 75 other pro-life organizations opposing and killing the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act.

q: What do the authors ask the ERLC and pro-life organizations to do at minimum if they will not support abolition bills?
a: The authors say the best outcome would be repentance and active support for abolition, but if that will not happen, the second-best outcome is for these organizations to stop actively killing abolition legislation and step aside.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: The best outcome would be these pro-life and national SBC entities repenting and supporting the abolition of abortion, but second best would be for them to get out of the way.

q: How do the authors respond to Barber's stated desire to sit across the table from abolitionists?
a: They call him to make it happen by responding to Dusty Deevers' text message. Deevers, an SBC pastor at Grace Community Church in Elgin, OK, has repeatedly sought private reconciliation per Matthew 18 and offered moderated, documented public dialogue, but Barber has refused while continuing to publicly misrepresent him.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: As God commands of me, I have—along with the elders of my church—sought reconciliation directly with Bart from his slanders and lies and am willing and have offered multiple times to sit down across the table from Bart and have these conversations. However, at this point, that conversation would need to be moderated and documented in order stop any further distortion.

q: How does the article distinguish a genuine victim of abortion from a culpable mother?
a: A woman who has an abortion pill slipped into her drink without her knowledge (such as the 2022 Texas case) is genuinely a victim. A mother who knowingly, mindfully, and willfully kills her child is the murderer, and the child is the only victim. Circumstances of temptation do not transfer culpability away from a willful agent.
t: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
qt: The mindful, knowledgeable, and willful abortion of a child by the woman, though? That is murder and the child is the only victim.

q: How does this article define the dividing line between Pro-Life leaders and abortion abolitionists?
a: Deevers argues the disagreement is fundamentally definitional: Pro-Life leaders treat abortion as a regulated act performed on a woman who is always a victim, while abolitionists define abortion as prenatal homicide for which any culpable party (mother, doctor, pill industry, accomplices) can be prosecuted under equal protection laws.
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: ultimately, the division between Barber, the ERLC, and the Pro-Life establishment on one hand and the Abortion Abolitionists on the other comes down to different definitions and foundations of what it means to abolish abortion.

q: What scriptural foundation does the article give for criminalizing abortion as homicide rather than regulating it as healthcare?
a: Deevers grounds the abolitionist position in the Sixth Commandment, arguing that consistent application of 'You shall not murder' requires extending equal legal protection to preborn lives from fertilization, citing Genesis 9:6, Exodus 21:22-24, and Proverbs 6:17.
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: Biblically speaking, to abolish abortion is consistently to apply the Sixth Commandment to preborn lives, “You shall not murder” (Exodus 20:13). The Sixth Commandment requires giving equal protection from fertilization/conception by criminalizing the act of abortion as prenatal homicide (Genesis 9:6; Exodus 21:22-24; Proverbs 6:17).

q: What specific position of Bart Barber does the article cite as proof he does not actually want abortion abolished?
a: The article quotes Barber's own words rejecting prosecution of women who seek or obtain abortions, calling that stance internally contradictory with his claim that 'abortion should be abolished'—comparable to advocating for 'married bachelors.'
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: Should abortion laws mandate the prosecution of women who seek or obtain an abortion? *I think not. I think it is unjust, unnecessary, and unwise to include in abortion laws the prosecution of women who seek or obtain an abortion*

q: What happened with Louisiana HB 813, and what role did the ERLC play?
a: HB 813 was a Louisiana abolition bill that would have granted equal protection to all preborn from fertilization. According to Deevers, over 75 Pro-Life organizations—led by Roman Catholics and the ERLC under Brent Leatherwood—released a coalition letter at the exact moment of the floor debate, helping kill the bill.
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: over 75 Pro-Life organizations, led by Roman Catholics and the ERLC, with Brent Leatherwood at its helm, torpedoed LA House Bill 813, an abolition bill that would have granted equal protection of the laws to all preborn from fertilization in Louisiana.

q: How does the article say ERLC leadership defied the SBC messengers' own 2021 Resolution on Abolishing Abortion?
a: Deevers contrasts the 2021 Nashville resolution—which rejected exceptions and demanded that 'the murder of preborn children…must be punished equally under the law'—with Leatherwood's anti-equal-protection coalition letter and platform statements that no woman who has an abortion should be 'behind bars.'
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: The messengers . . . state unequivocally that abortion is murder, and we reject any position that allows for any exceptions to the legal protection of our preborn neighbors, compromises God’s holy standard of justice, or promotes any God-hating partiality. . . . We affirm that the murder of preborn children is a crime against humanity that must be punished equally under the law.

q: Why does the article accuse Barber of giving 'bookless instruction' to a 'people of the Book'?
a: Deevers charges that Barber's roughly 3,500-word article against abolitionism made almost no use of Scripture, failing the Berean standard of Acts 17:11 and the Titus 1:9 requirement to instruct in sound doctrine.
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: Barber spends around 3,500 words lobbing numerous charges at the abolitionist movement, scarcely referencing God’s Word.

q: How does the article connect equal protection to the Royal Law of loving one's neighbor?
a: Deevers argues that to claim love for the preborn while denying them equal legal protection is the partiality James 2:8-9 condemns as sin—meaning fulfillment of the Second Commandment requires criminalizing prenatal homicide for everyone.
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: If we say we love our preborn neighbor as ourselves, but we show partiality by denying them equal protection of the laws, we deceive ourselves.

q: What slogan does the article propose to truly make abortion 'unthinkable'?
a: Rather than the ERLC's 'illegal, unnecessary, and unthinkable' framework that still treats abortion as healthcare, Deevers proposes treating it as homicide and criminalizing it for all parties—so that only murderers would do it.
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: If we really want to make abortion unthinkable, our motto must become, “Make abortion something only murderers would do.” How does a nation do this? By criminalizing the act of prenatal homicide–for everyone.

q: From what moment does the article say human life and equal worth begin, and on what grounds?
a: Deevers argues life begins at fertilization/conception when God forms the child, and that all humans bear God's image with equal worth that does not vary by ethnicity, age, size, means of conception, development, sex, potential, or social contribution.
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: all humans display His divine worth, power, and attributes, and thus possess objective and equal worth not varying based on incidental characteristics; such as ethnicity, age, size, means of conception, mental development, physical development, sex (gender), potential, or contribution to society

q: What spiritual posture does the article say abolitionists must take when fighting this battle?
a: Deevers calls abolitionists to wield the Word of God as a necessity rather than rely on 'puny, pragmatic reasoning,' framing the fight against child sacrifice as spiritual warfare per Ephesians 6 in which success is left to God.
t: Bible or Barber on Abolishing Abortion?
qt: This battle opposing the horrendous cultural evil of child sacrifice is not against flesh and blood, so we must not use puny, pragmatic reasoning. We must fight the Lord’s battles with the spiritual weapons He has put into our hands—and leave the “success” to Him (Ephesians 6:10-20).

q: What was the result of the March 2022 Republican primary in Texas House District 91?
a: Incumbent Stephanie Klick finished first with 48.96 percent but failed to clear the 50 percent threshold needed to avoid a runoff, while abolitionist challenger David Lowe took second with 39.06 percent, sending the race to a May 24 runoff.
t: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
qt: Texas House District 91 abolitionist challenger David Lowe advanced to a runoff against incumbent State Rep. Stephanie Klick in Tuesday's Republican Primary, garnering 39.06 percent of the vote. Klick received 48.96 percent, short of the 50 percent plus one vote needed to advance to the general election.

q: Why does Free the States hold Stephanie Klick personally responsible for abortion remaining legal in Texas?
a: As chair of the Texas House Public Health Committee, Klick refused to give the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Through Equal Protection Act so much as a hearing during the 2021 session, blocking the bill that would have ended abortion in the state.
t: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
qt: Klick, as chair of the Texas House Public Health Committee, refused to even give the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Through Equal Protection Act a hearing in the 2021 session. She is the reason babies are still murdered in Texas.

q: How did Lowe perform against Klick despite a major spending disadvantage?
a: Even though Klick outspent Lowe by roughly three to one, she still couldn't avoid a runoff, suggesting Lowe's grassroots reach is punching well above his fundraising weight.
t: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
qt: She was unable to advance despite outspending Lowe by a roughly 3:1 margin.

q: What historical precedent does Free the States cite for second-place candidates winning runoffs?
a: The article points to Warren Hamilton's 2020 race, in which he trailed incumbent Larry Boggs 42.21 to 46.45 in the first round but then jumped to 52.30 percent and won the runoff outright.
t: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
qt: In 2020, abolitionist Warren Hamilton received 42.21 percent compared to incumbent Republican Larry Boggs' 46.45. But in the primary, Warren increased his vote share to 52.30, defeating Boggs handily.

q: Why does Free the States compare the Lowe-Klick race to the 2020 Garvin-Scott race in Oklahoma?
a: The first-round numbers were nearly identical, and Garvin came back from a similar deficit to defeat the incumbent in the runoff, providing a non-abolitionist precedent that the same dynamics can flip a race.
t: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
qt: Though neither is an abolitionist, their numbers in the first primary vote are nearly identical to the Lowe-Klick race. Scott finished in first with 49.89 percent, just 14 votes shy of winning the race outright. Garvin finished in second place with 39.26 percent. But in the runoff, Garvin overtook Scott to win 51.56-to-48.44.

q: What policy commitments did Lowe make to the abolitionist movement?
a: Lowe pledged to Free the States and Abolish Abortion Texas that he would back immediate abolition of abortion and reject the incrementalist strategy of the Pro-Life Establishment.
t: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
qt: Lowe has pledged to Free the States and Abolish Abortion Texas that he will support the immediate abolition of abortion and reject the incrementalism of the Pro-Life Establishment.

q: What is Free the States asking abolitionists to do between the March primary and the May 24 runoff?
a: They are asking the Abolitionist Movement to mobilize behind Lowe and help educate District 91 voters on the contrast between Klick's pro-life incrementalism and Lowe's abolitionism over the seven weeks leading up to the runoff.
t: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
qt: Lowe vs. Klick is the most important race in Texas, and he is going to need the support of the Abolitionist Movement if he is going to gain the ground he needs to gain over the next seven weeks! Lowe, Free the States, Abolish Abortion Texas, and Texas abolitionists will be working hard to educate District 91 voters about the difference between the pro-life incrementalism of Klick vs the abolitionism of David Lowe between now and May 24.

q: How did the other minor candidates in the District 91 primary perform?
a: Anthony Reed pulled 6.22 percent, David Silvey 3.37 percent, and Benjamin Damico 2.40 percent, leaving roughly 12 percent of the vote up for grabs in the runoff.
t: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
qt: After Klick and Lowe, Anthony Reed finished with 6.22 percent, David Silvey received 3.37 percent, and Benjamin Damico finished in fifth with 2.40 percent.

q: What bill was introduced and what would it actually do in South Carolina?
a: H4046, the South Carolina Unborn Victims of Violence Act of 2021, would totally and immediately abolish abortion in South Carolina by treating any deliberate termination of an unborn child as murder under the state's existing homicide statute (Section 16-3-10), giving preborn children the same protection as anyone else.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt: H4046 establishes that “The deliberate termination of an unborn child by any means or at any stage of development is murder under Section 16-3-10.” Abortion would be homicide and the protection afforded to our right to life would be extended equally to children in the womb.

q: When did human life begin according to the bill's findings?
a: The bill stakes its entire premise on the finding that personhood and the right to life begin at conception, which is what justifies treating abortion at any stage as homicide rather than as a regulated medical procedure.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt: (1)    The life of an individual human being begins at conception.

q: How does the bill justify defying federal court rulings like Roe v. Wade?
a: The bill argues that the U.S. Constitution grants no right to abortion, that the Tenth Amendment reserves such powers to the states, and that any federal judicial ruling purporting to create such a right is not 'in pursuance of' the Constitution and therefore is not the supreme law of the land and not binding on South Carolina.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt: (11)    Any action of the federal judiciary that purports to provide a right to kill an unborn child is not made in pursuance of the Constitution of the United States, is not the supreme law of the land, and is not binding upon this State.

q: How does the bill characterize the moral magnitude of legal abortion in America?
a: The bill explicitly labels nearly five decades of legal abortion as genocide, and uses that framing to argue that no judicial body has legitimate authority to authorize it.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt: (10)    Taking the life of more than sixty-two million unborn children over the last forty-eight years and counting constitutes genocide, and authorizing genocide is not within the legitimate powers of the federal judiciary.

q: What's actually new about Hill's bill compared to other abolition bills filed in other states?
a: Beyond the standard defiance language, H4046 adds two enforcement teeth: it makes it a misdemeanor for any South Carolina official or law enforcement officer to cooperate with federal efforts that violate the right to life, and it makes any state judge who violates that right subject to impeachment under Article XV of the South Carolina Constitution.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt:
1. Any South Carolina official or law enforcement officer who cooperates with the federal government to violate the right to life is guilty of a misdemeanor, and
2. Any South Carolina judge who violates the right to life of preborn children shall be subject to impeachment in accordance with Article XV of the South Carolina Constitution.

q: Where does the bill stand procedurally and what action does the article ask readers to take?
a: H4046 has been assigned to the South Carolina House Judiciary Committee under Chair Chris Murphy. Free the States is asking constituents to call Murphy at (803) 734-3120 to demand a hearing and a 'Yes' vote on the bill.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt: The bill has been assigned to the South Carolina House Judiciary Committee chaired by State Rep. Chris Murphy. Call Murphy at (803) 734-3120 to request a hearing and a ‘Yes’ vote for H4046.

q: Why does the bill invoke legislators' oaths of office as a basis for defying federal rulings?
a: The bill grounds its defiance in the personal oath every General Assembly member swears, framing the protection of unborn life as a sworn duty to defend both the state and federal constitutions, not merely a policy preference.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt: (4)    The members of the General Assembly have solemnly sworn to the best of their abilities to “discharge the duties of our office and preserve, protect, and defend the Constitution of this State and of the United States. So help us God.”

q: Does the bill claim states can ignore the federal judiciary anytime they disagree with it?
a: No. The bill explicitly cautions against reflexive nullification, conceding that prudence requires restraint, but argues that the federal judiciary is fallible and limited, leaving room for states to reject rulings that fall outside the Constitution's actual text.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt: (7)    Though prudence dictates that states should not declare actions of the federal judiciary void for light or indefinite causes, the federal judiciary is not infallible nor are its powers unlimited.

q: Is this the first time South Carolina has considered abolishing abortion outright?
a: Yes. According to Free the States, this is the first abolition bill ever filed in South Carolina, marking the state's entry into the growing list of states with active equal-protection abolition legislation.
t: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
qt: For the first time, South Carolina has a bill to abolish abortion!

q: Why does Silberman believe scheduling SB495 so early in the session is a hostile move rather than a sign of good faith?
a: Early committee hearings are unusual, and Silberman reads the timing as a deliberate attempt by leadership to kill the bill before abolitionists can mobilize lobbying or rally support around it.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: We are praying and working for the best, but the motivation for the Senate to hear SB495 this early likely is not a good one. It is not common practice to hold committee hearings this early in the session. The objective here is to kill SB495 on day 3 of the session before we have much of a chance to lobby and rally for the bill.

q: Why is the makeup of the Senate HHS Committee considered unfavorable to SB495?
a: Hamilton himself is not on the committee, neither are senators known to have supported SB13's general order vote, and nine of the eleven members previously voted to kill SB13.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: The committee makeup is not favorable. Warren Hamilton, the author of the bill, is not on the committee, nor are Nathan Dahm, Chris Kidd, or Roland Pederson who voted in favor of placing SB13 on general order last year. The committee has 11 members. Nine of them voted to kill SB13 last year.

q: What outcome does Silberman predict if the vote takes place on February 3, and what does he say leadership wants?
a: He projects a lopsided defeat ranging from 10-3 to 13-0 against SB495, which he frames as exactly what Senate leadership wants to occur on day 3 so that the abolition push collapses before it gains momentum.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: The likely outcome if the vote takes place tomorrow is a 10-3, 11-2, 12-1, or 13-0 vote to keep child murder legal in Oklahoma, and that's exactly what leadership wants to happen on day 3 of the session.

q: Why would a defeat in committee on February 3 effectively kill abolition legislation for two years?
a: Oklahoma's legislature runs on two-year cycles, and once a bill's language is voted down in the first year, identical legislation cannot be reintroduced until the next cycle.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: What makes this even worse is the fact that SB495 would be dead for two years, as the Oklahoma legislature operates in two-year cycles. This is the first year, and new legislation cannot be introduced between the first and second year of the cycle if the same language has already been voted down in the form of another bill.

q: How is Senator Hamilton trying to keep SB495 alive in the face of an unfavorable committee vote?
a: Hamilton is exploring procedural options to delay the vote so he can whip support among colleagues, give Oklahomans more time to lobby for added coauthors, and let senators hear from the Abolition Day Rally attendees on February 9.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: Accordingly, Senator Hamilton is exploring options to buy some time. This would allow him to whip support for the bill among his colleagues. It would give us and the people of Oklahoma more time to meet with Senators to add coauthors to the bill. It would give Senators time to hear from the Abolition Day Rally attendees on February 9th while the bill to abolish abortion is still alive.

q: What is the on-the-ground call to action for Oklahoma activists on the morning of the hearing?
a: Activists are asked to gather at 9 a.m. outside Room 535 to line the halls as senators arrive and then attend the hearing inside, regardless of whether SB495 is actually heard that day.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: So the bill may or may not be heard tomorrow. Regardless of whether it is heard, we will be meeting outside the committee hearing room (room 535) at 9 a.m. to make our presence felt.

q: What action does Silberman ask Oklahomans who cannot attend in person to take?
a: He urges them to email every member of the HHS Committee asking for a yes vote on SB495, and provides the full list of senator email addresses.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: It is EXTREMELY important that Oklahomans email all members of the committee asking them to vote in favor of the bill in case the bill is heard tomorrow.

q: Why does the committee include Greg Treat and Kim David even though they are not regular HHS members?
a: As Senate Pro Tem and Floor Leader respectively, Treat and David are voting members of every committee, which gives them the ability to cast votes against SB495 on top of the standard eleven members.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat and Floor Leader Kim David are voting members of every committee, so they also are able to vote against SB495.

q: How does Silberman characterize the two freshman senators on the committee?
a: He labels Jo Anna Dossett a pro-abortion Democrat certain to vote no, and says Jessica Garvin has not gone on record either way but communications with her have not been promising.
t: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
qt: Freshman Senator Jo Anna Dossett is a pro-child murder Democrat, so she will vote against it. Freshman Senator Jessica Garvin has never gone on record for or against abolishing abortion like the other members of the committee, but our communication with her thus far has not been promising.

q: What were the actual margins of victory in the two primary races?
a: Warren Hamilton beat Larry Boggs 52.3 to 47.7 percent in the SD7 Senate primary runoff, while Margie Alfonso took 59.41 percent of the vote against Clay Iiams in the HD79 House race.
t: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
qt: Warren Hamilton defeated incumbent State Senator Larry Boggs by a 52.3-to-47.7 margin in the SD7 primary runoff to become the second abolitionist to unseat a Republican establishment incumbent and the first to do so in Oklahoma.

q: How significant was the financial disadvantage Hamilton overcame?
a: Boggs spent more than twice what Hamilton spent on his own campaign ($103,794.80 vs. $45,650.69), and on top of that two establishment PACs (Advance Oklahoma Fund and Senate Majority fund) reportedly poured in over a hundred thousand additional dollars supporting Boggs.
t: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
qt: Boggs spent $103,794.80 to Hamilton's $45,650.69. Beyond the campaign expenditures, Boggs had two big-money establishment PACs (Advance Oklahoma Fund and Senate Majority fund) spending more than a hundred thousand dollars more on his behalf.

q: What does Free the States want from Margie Alfonso if she is elected to the State House?
a: They want her to author The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act in the State House, which she has already promised to do as a condition of their qualified endorsement.
t: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
qt: We gave Alfonso a qualified endorsement, and she has promised to author The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act in the State House should she be elected.

q: Who else does Free the States have moving into the November 3 general election?
a: Ben Janloo, a qualified-endorsement candidate who didn't face a primary opponent, joins Hamilton and Alfonso in November, while Senator Roland Pederson was already reelected since he has no Democrat opponent.
t: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
qt: Hamilton and Alfonso join Ben Janloo, who we also gave a qualified endorsement to, in the November 3 general election. Janloo did not face an opponent in the Republican primary.

q: Why does Free the States consider Senator Pederson worth supporting?
a: Because Pederson actually voted to bring The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act out of committee during the 2020 legislative session, distinguishing him from establishment Republicans who blocked the bill.
t: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
qt: Pederson voted to bring The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act out of committee during the 2020 legislative session.

q: What general election opponents do the winning abolitionist candidates now face?
a: Hamilton will face Democrat Jerry Donathan in SD7, and Alfonso will face incumbent Democrat Melissa Provenzano in HD79, both on November 3.
t: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
qt: Hamilton, who we endorsed, moves on to face Democrat Jerry Donathan in the general election on November 3.

q: Why does the article single out Hamilton's win as more astonishing than Alfonso's?
a: Because Hamilton was outspent by Boggs's campaign and outside PACs by a wide margin and faced numerous prominent establishment endorsers, yet still won as an outspoken abolitionist.
t: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
qt: Both victories are important, but Hamilton's victory is especially astonishing.

q: How does Free the States characterize the political establishment Hamilton beat?
a: They describe it as a coalition of big-money PACs and prominent figures who joined forces behind Boggs, framing the loss as a defeat for entrenched political power rather than just one incumbent.
t: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
qt: Numerous prominent establishment figures joined forces behind Boggs. With all of this money and these political connections on Boggs' side, Hamilton, the outspoken abolitionist, came out victorious.

q: Why does Free the States believe abolitionists must speak up to legislators who file abolition bills but also vote for pro-life regulations?
a: Because filing abolition bills, while honorable, does not cancel out the harm of supporting incremental measures that prop up the very pro-life politicians who keep abortion legal. The movement must hold even its friends to a biblical standard rather than soften its message to avoid offense.
t: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
qt: Filing abolition bills is important to the growth of the movement and we ought to honor those who self-sacrificially do so; but at the same time, it would be disastrous if the movement softened its stance on the immorality and counter-productivity of pro-life incrementalism for the sake of not offending a bill author or supporter.

q: What concrete actions did Bryan Slaton take in 2021 that earned both abolitionist appreciation and concern?
a: Slaton repeatedly filed bills to abolish abortion in regular and special sessions and tried to leverage House rules to force a vote on abolition, but he also backed every abortion regulation bill that came up and publicly celebrated the heartbeat bill, which abolitionists view as compromise.
t: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
qt: He filed a bill to abolish abortion in normal session and in each of the special sessions called by Governor Greg Abbott. He motioned to amend House rules so that bridges and roads could not be renamed unless a vote was taken on the abolition of abortion. He made powerful enemies among the Republican establishment by fighting to abolish abortion. But he also supported every abortion regulation bill that was presented, and celebrated the heartbeat bill on Facebook.

q: What posture does C.R. Cali tell abolitionists to take when contacting a mixed-record legislator?
a: Don't lead with what you like or dislike about the legislator. Instead, acknowledge the political pressure they face and pledge to stand with them when they take the right stand, focusing the conversation on calling them to a biblical standard.
t: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
qt: Cali highlights the need for abolitionists to call legislators to a Biblical standard and recommends not focusing on what we appreciate or don't appreciate about a legislator, but rather communicate to legislators that you understand the difficult position they're in and will stand with them strongly if they do what is right.

q: Why does Cali advise against thanking a legislator for past abolition bills or criticizing their compromises in these messages?
a: Both praise for prior actions and criticism for compromises distract from the goal of the message, which is to fortify the legislator for the next stand. The strategy keeps the focus on courage going forward, not a balance sheet of past behavior.
t: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
qt:
- Thank him for things that he has done in the past such as his abolition bills or rule changes
- Criticize him for his compromise in supporting pro-life healthcare laws
- Criticize him for attending the Texas Right to Life gala
- Threaten withdrawal of support if he doesn't respond rightly to our exhortations

q: What promise should abolitionists make to a legislator who is being pressured by the pro-life establishment?
a: They should pledge that when establishment attacks come for refusing to vote for pro-life healthcare regulations, the people will stand with that legislator in his principled, biblical stance against abortion.
t: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
qt: Most importantly let him know that WHEN these attacks come, we will be there with him standing to support him in his righteous stance and the only consistent biblical and moral stance against abortion

q: What email subject line does Cali recommend, and why does framing matter?
a: Cali instructs activists to use a specific subject line that frames the message as encouragement against unjust pro-life healthcare laws, keeping the tone supportive and the topic narrow so the legislator hears courage rather than condemnation.
t: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
qt: Use the subject line in email "Encouragement to make a principled stand against unjust pro-life healthcare laws"

q: What is the explicit purpose of the email campaign according to Cali?
a: The email's job is to supply the courage a legislator needs to be the lone voice standing on God's Word against pro-life healthcare laws by promising the unwavering support of the people, and the writer must stay on that single subject.
t: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
qt: The purpose of this email to give him the courage he needs to be the lone legislator to stand firmly on God's Word against these pro-life healthcare laws by giving him the unwavering support of the people. Stay focused on that subject matter.

q: Does Free the States want this strategy applied only to Bryan Slaton, or more broadly?
a: Free the States explicitly endorses Cali's approach and urges abolitionists nationwide to apply it whenever a legislator supports both abolition bills and incremental pro-life bills.
t: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
qt: Free the States endorses Cali's position and encourages abolitionists all around the country to reach out to legislators who support both incremental and abolition bills with exhortations along these lines.

q: Why is abortion not considered sinful by those who defend it?
a: According to the catechism's biting refrain, the only reason abortion escapes the label of sin is that its victims are preborn and thus dehumanized in the eyes of those who tolerate it. The killing itself is identical to murder; only the age and visibility of the victim shield it from moral condemnation.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
1) Why is abortion not sinful?  
Because the victims are preborn.

q: Why do self-proclaimed champions of equality and justice support abortion?
a: Their entire framework of equality has a hidden exclusion clause: it does not extend to the preborn. The catechism exposes this contradiction by giving the same one-sentence answer that names the excluded class.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
2) Why do those who most fancy themselves the champions of equality and justice support abortion?  
Because their victims are preborn.

q: Why is it considered wrong or judgmental to call abortion 'murder'?
a: Calling abortion 'murder' is treated as impolite only because society has agreed to discount the personhood of the preborn. Were the victims visible and post-natal, the same act would be universally named what it is.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
3) Why is it wrong and judgemental to call abortion “murder?”  
Because the victims are preborn.

q: Why is it claimed to be unconstitutional to abolish abortion immediately?
a: The supposed constitutional barrier exists only because the preborn have been categorically denied the constitutional protections owed to every human being. Strip away that one exception and the immediate abolition becomes obviously lawful.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
4) Why is it unconstitutional to abolish abortion immediately?  
Because those who have been denied all constitutional rights are preborn.

q: Why must poverty, mental illness, and every social problem be solved before abolition can even be discussed?
a: This endless prerequisite list is only deployed in the case of abortion because its victims are preborn. No one demands that other categories of murder wait until society is perfected before being criminalized.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
5) Why must poverty, mental illness, and every social malady be cured before the subject of abolition be raised?  
Because the victims of abortion are preborn.

q: Why must we pursue gradual rather than immediate strategies to end abortion?
a: Gradualism is tolerated for abortion uniquely because the victims are preborn. We would never accept a multi-decade phase-out for the murder of any other class of human beings.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
6) Why must we pursue gradual strategies to abolish abortion?  
Because those children in need of saving are preborn.

q: Why is immediate abolition derided as wrong, imprudent, and impracticable?
a: These objections are reserved for the preborn alone. Immediate abolition is considered the only acceptable response to every other form of murder; only the preborn must wait.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
7) Why is immediate abolition wrong, imprudent, and impracticable?  
Because the victims of abortion are preborn.

q: Why is regulating when, where, and how someone can be murdered treated as a just and pragmatic strategy?
a: Such regulatory schemes are accepted as 'pro-life wisdom' only because the victims are preborn. Applied to any other class of victim, the same approach would be recognized as monstrous.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
8) Why is dictating when, where, and how someone can be murdered a just and pragmatic strategy?  
Because the victims of abortion are preborn.

q: Why must we submit to the Supreme Court's opinion that abortion is legal?
a: Judicial supremacy is invoked here because the court's targets are preborn. The catechism implies that no honest Christian or constitutionalist would defer to a court ruling marking any other class of human being for slaughter.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
9) Why must we bow to the supreme court’s obviously false, obviously evil opinion that abortion is legal?  
Because those the court has marked for slaughter are preborn.

q: Why is criminalizing abortion called cruel?
a: The 'cruelty' charge gains traction only because it dismisses the preborn as the relevant party. Real cruelty is the killing itself; calling its prohibition cruel is only possible if the victim does not count.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
10) Why is it cruel to criminalize abortion?  
Because the victims are preborn.

q: Why are abolitionists dismissed as extremists, cultists, and monomaniacs?
a: The label sticks only because the people they plead for are preborn. Pleading singlemindedly for any visible class of murder victims would be lauded, not mocked.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
11) Why are abolitionists extremists, cultists, and monomaniacs?  
Because those for whom they plead are preborn.

q: Why are pulpits silent on abolition?
a: Pastoral silence is excused because the parties needing pulpit advocacy are preborn. A faithful pulpit would not justify silence about the murder of any other neighbor.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
12) Why are the pulpits right to be silent on abolition?  
Because those whom preachers would have to speak up for are preborn.

q: Why do pro-life politicians invoke nullification when convenient yet call it a 'debunked legal theory' for abortion?
a: The double standard exists because the people who would benefit from interposition are preborn. The same politicians readily nullify federal action on issues whose victims are visible and politically prized.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
13) Why is nullification often employed by the same pro-life politicians who call nullification a “debunked legal theory” when it comes to abortion?  
Because those who would be interposed for are preborn.

q: Why is it sufficient merely to defund abortion rather than abolish it?
a: Settling for defunding instead of criminalization is only thinkable because the surviving victims are preborn. No one would ever say the murder of toddlers is adequately addressed by withdrawing federal funding.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
14) Why is it sufficient to call for the defunding of abortion rather than the abolition of abortion?  
Because the victims of abortion are preborn.

q: Why is 'empowering women to choose life' prioritized above abolishing abortion?
a: This priority inversion only makes sense if the victims do not count fully. Because the preborn are dehumanized, persuasion campaigns aimed at the killer are treated as more urgent than legal protection of the killed.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
15) Why is “empowering women to choose life” more important than the abolition of abortion?  
Because the victims of abortion are preborn.

q: Why are abolitionists said to be wrong in both principles and methods?
a: The blanket condemnation of abolitionist principles and tactics is sustained only by the unspoken premise that the preborn matter less. Apply abolitionist principles to any other class of victim and they are uncontroversial.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
16) Why are abolitionists wrong in their principles and measures?  
Because the victims of abortion are preborn.

q: Why should the priests and Levites of our society who pass by on the other side not be sternly rebuked?
a: Echoing the parable of the Good Samaritan, the catechism notes that society excuses the modern priests and Levites only because the wounded neighbor is preborn. Faithful rebuke is owed precisely because every neighbor, born or unborn, deserves the response of a Samaritan, not a passerby.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt:
17) Why ought not the priests and levites of our society passing by on the other side be sternly rebuked?  
Because those being led to slaughter are preborn.

q: What is the source and structure of this catechism?
a: Silberman explicitly modeled the piece on William Lloyd Garrison's nineteenth-century abolitionist work, drawing a direct line between the rhetorical strategies used to excuse chattel slavery and those used today to excuse abortion. The shared single-line answer mirrors Garrison's style of exposing systemic dehumanization.
t: Catechism for Anti-Abolitionists (Pro-Choice and Pro-Life)
qt: The above catechism is adapted from William Lloyd Garrison’s *A Short Catechism*.

q: What did Oklahoma's SB13 actually propose, and why does the author say it required repealing existing pro-life statutes?
a: SB13 would have recognized a newly conceived child as a human being with unalienable rights and treated killing that child as homicide. To make that prohibition coherent, the bill repealed roughly 20 existing pro-life regulatory statutes, because a law saying 'you cannot murder babies' cannot stand alongside laws that effectively say 'you can murder some babies sometimes.'
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: SB13 would have repealed close to 20 pro-life statutes from the state's laws. Why was this necessary? You can't have an effective law on the books that says, "you cannot murder babies" and at the same time have a law that says "you can murder some babies sometimes."

q: How does the author respond to the rape and incest exception that even many pro-lifers concede?
a: He grants that rape and incest are heinous crimes deserving the fullest punishment of the rapist, but argues the unborn child committed no crime and should not be killed for the sins of the father. The mother is not required to raise the child, but killing the child should never be on the table.
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: Rape and incest are heinous crimes, but why would we kill the baby for the sins of the father? The child did not commit a crime. If there is anyone to stand up for and protect, it is the child without a loving father. There is never a scenario where the answer is to kill the baby.

q: How does Butler distinguish abortions 'to save the life of the mother' from legitimate medical care like ectopic pregnancy treatment?
a: He argues that genuine life-threatening pregnancies (such as ectopic pregnancies) are emergencies handled by trained doctors using medical triage, not scheduled abortions performed by abortionists. Abolition bills like SB13 do not criminalize physicians whose intent is to save life, because abortion as such exists only to end life, never to preserve it.
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: Abolition bills like SB13 do not criminalize doctors whose intent is to save life. Abortion is never needed to save anyone's life; its only purpose is to cease life.

q: Why does the author say pro-life regulations actually entrench rather than weaken abortion law?
a: He argues that every regulation implies a right to the underlying act. A 20-week ban implies abortion is permitted before 20 weeks; a forceps ban implies suction abortions are fine. By codifying conditions under which abortion is allowed, pro-life statutes reinforce the assumption that women have a rightful access to abortion in the first place.
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: The bill that boldly claims that you cannot abort a baby after 20 weeks of gestation implies that you can abort a baby prior to 20 weeks.

q: What is the author's football-field analogy for the difference between pro-life and abolitionist strategy?
a: He pictures a field with 'ROE' in both endzones and SCOTUS at the 50-yard line. Pro-lifers play yard-by-yard since 1973, gaining a few yards then losing them to lawsuits, with no real reduction in abortions. Abolitionists refuse to play by Roe's rules at all and instead 'cut the lights and turn on the sprinklers,' walking off the field by simply ignoring federal abortion law, as SB13's defiance clause did.
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: Abolitionists will not play by the rules of *ROE V. Wade*. Abolitionists would simply pull the switch to the stadium lights and turn the sprinklers on as they walk out the gates. SB13 actually had a clause that said all lawsuits, executive orders, and federal statutes regarding legal abortion would be ignored by all Oklahoma agents.

q: Is state defiance of the Supreme Court the same as anarchy?
a: No. Butler argues the founders intended states to check and even ignore an out-of-control federal government. He points to Oklahoma already defying Gonzalez v. Raich on medical marijuana, and asks why the state will defy SCOTUS for medical cannabis but not to stop the killing of children.
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: Did you know that right now Oklahoma is in open defiance to the supreme court decision Gonzalez V. Raich (2005) which states that marijuana is a federally recognized controlled substance and is, therefore, illegal in all 50 states? So, for the love and care of children who need medical marijuana to deal with seizures, we are willing to defy the SCOTUS, but when it comes to preventing the murder of children in the womb we suddenly get weak in the knees and cave like a bunch of pansies!

q: What biblical texts does the author marshal to support abolition over regulation?
a: He cites Acts 17 (God as author of life), Exodus 20 ('Thou shall not murder'), Psalm 139 (God's omniscience), Jeremiah 7:31 (the murder of children is so irrational it never even entered God's mind), and Proverbs 6 (God hates hands that shed innocent blood). These passages, he says, are the logical and theological foundation of the Abolition Movement.
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: However, God says that it is so irrational that a mother and father would hire out the murder of their own child that it has never even entered His mind (Jeremiah 7:31). Abortion is so incomprehensible that even the creator Himself cannot rationalize its practice.

q: What concrete change does the author predict if pro-lifers became abolitionists?
a: He claims a wholesale conversion would end legal abortion in Oklahoma: voters would demand abolition, the legislature would pass it, the governor would sign it, and abortion would shift from a normalized option to a criminal act with real consequences, much like robbing a bank vault.
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: Put simply, if every abolitionist were to convert back to pro-life tomorrow, absolutely nothing would change in the culture of abortion. However, if every pro-lifer were to become an abolitionist, legalized abortion would end in Oklahoma.

q: Despite the harsh rhetoric, what does the author say is the abolitionist's posture toward those complicit in abortion?
a: He insists that calling abortion sin is not finger-pointing but a call to repentance, paired with the gospel of Christ crucified. The abolitionist sees himself as a 'wretched worm' apart from grace and offers redemption to anyone, including those who have participated in abortion, who turns to Christ.
t: Christian, Not Pro-Life
qt: Apart from the transforming grace of a loving redeemer the best abolitionist is simply a wretched worm who deserves the full and merciless wrath of God. It is not our intention to judgingly point any fingers to what anyone has done in their past, but rather to call the sin a sin, and point the sinner to the one and only savior, Christ Jesus.

q: Why does Silberman reject Klusendorf's claim that pro-lifers lack the power to immediately end abortion?
a: Because the Supreme Court is not God; states with pro-life supermajorities and governors already possess the votes and the constitutional authority to defy Roe and abolish abortion, but choose not to.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: Pro-life politicians in states like Oklahoma, Idaho, Indiana, Missouri, and others have the bill to abolish abortion (or “magic wand,” as Klusendorf refers to it) before them and the supermajorities necessary to pass the bill easily. There is nothing stopping them from doing so except themselves; their own cowardice and Biblical and Constitutional ignorance.

q: What does the article identify as the root theological error driving pro-life submission to the Supreme Court?
a: It identifies an idolatrous treatment of the Supreme Court as a quasi-divine authority, demanding unconditional submission even when the court legalizes murder, which the author labels unbiblical and contrary to the founding documents.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: Where does this idea that the supreme court is “on high” and that the rest of us are obliged to unconditionally submit to the supreme court come from? It is blatantly unbiblical, yet it is the nearly unanimous position of the pro-life establishment.

q: How does Silberman characterize the silence of pro-life leaders who privately disagree with judicial supremacy?
a: He argues an unwritten code of mutual non-criticism keeps dissenters quiet, which functionally props up the dominant SCOTUS-idolatry posture and keeps abortion legal.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: There is a code among the pro-life leaders that none should ever publicly criticize another. The few who don’t ascribe to SCOTUS idolatry hold their tongues as their colleagues keep the Pro-Life Movement in a submissive posture, and thereby keep abortion legal.

q: What is the immediatist counter to Klusendorf's 'some steps are better than no steps' argument?
a: Immediatists do not deny that partial victories beat none in principle; they insist that abolition is actually achievable now, and that tolerating compromise gives politicians an inexhaustible menu of incremental substitutes for justice.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: Politicians will always take the easiest path available to them. If we tolerate compromise, they will compromise; and there are an infinite amount of ways to compromise with abortion. They could invent three new pro-life victories to pass every year for a millennium.

q: How does the article argue that incremental bills actively worsen the moral landscape?
a: Because law functions as a tutor, heartbeat and pain-capability bills teach the public that arbitrary developmental markers determine human worth, spreading a dehumanizing definition of life that erodes recognition of personhood at conception.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: The law is a tutor, meaning that the pro-life movement, with the laws they write, is teaching people that heartbeats, pain-capability, or other arbitrary factors are what make us valuable and worthy of legal protection. Thanks to the heartbeat bill, the false belief that life begins at a heartbeat has spread like wildfire.

q: What is Elizabeth Heyrick's nineteenth-century critique of gradualism that Silberman invokes against pro-life incrementalism?
a: Heyrick argued the slave holder's most effective weapon was persuading abolitionists to accept gradualism, because contemplating gradual emancipation breeds gradual indifference to emancipation itself.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: He knew very well, that the contemplation of a gradual emancipation, would beget a gradual indifference to emancipation itself. He knew very well, that even the wise and the good, may, by habit and familiarity, be brought to endure and tolerate almost any thing…

q: What does Wilberforce himself say about gradual abolitionists, and how does this undercut Klusendorf's appeal to him?
a: Wilberforce called gradualists the most efficient supporters of the slave trade, directly contradicting any claim that he functioned as an incrementalist in tactics.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: [L]et me most seriously urge on the conscientious deliberations of those who…by their fatal proposal of gradual, instead of immediate abolition, dashed the cup of happiness from the lips of the wretched African, at the very moment when at last he appeared likely to taste it, and who thus proved in fact the most efficient supporters of the Slave Trade.

q: How does Silberman correct the popular claim that Wilberforce supported refitting slave ships through the Dolben Act?
a: Wilberforce regarded the slave-carrying bill as beneficial to the West Indian slave power because regulation made the trade more palatable and entrenched it; he did not endorse it as a tactical step toward abolition.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: If Klusendorf had read Wilberforce’s position on the Slave Trade Act of 1788, also known as the Dolben Act, instead of relying on the erroneous (and indeed slanderous) historical accounts of other pro-life incrementalists, he would have known Wilberforce’s belief that “the Slave-carrying bill [was] a measure which a very few years after was acknowledged, as it is still universally confessed, to have been beneficial to [the West Indian Body] [p. 275].”

q: Who, according to Silberman, is the real historical analogue for today's pro-life movement, and why?
a: Henry Dundas—the British politician who professed agreement with abolition's end goal while killing immediate-abolition motions and substituting gradual schemes—mirrors the maneuver pro-life lobbyists use against state abolition bills.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: This is precisely the scheme pro-life politicians and lobbyists have concocted wherever an abolition bill has been introduced. The pro-lifers kill the abolition bill and pursue the incremental strategy with more gusto than they ever have before… The Pro-Life Movement, very clearly, is Henry Dundas, not William Wilberforce.

q: Why does the article say heartbeat and 'life at conception' bills do not actually save the percentages Klusendorf credits them with?
a: Every recent pro-life bill is contingent on Supreme Court approval and is enjoined by federal courts before taking effect, meaning their real-world impact on abortion numbers is zero.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: These pro-life bills do not save 97 percent. They save zero percent because they do not go into effect.

q: How does Silberman describe the asymmetric pressure exerted on the Supreme Court by the left versus the pro-life movement?
a: The left aggressively threatens impeachment and court-packing while pro-lifers respond with deferential submission, so the court only ever feels coercive pressure from one direction.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: The left is full-court pressing the supreme court. Pro-lifers, on the other hand, take a posture of timidity and unconditional submission to the supreme court. Accordingly, the supreme court is never under any strain to take action against abortion.

q: What does Silberman say abolitionists would do if a real bill banned 97 percent of abortions while permitting 3 percent?
a: They would still oppose it on principle but the situation is hypothetical because no such bill exists; abolitionists would instead amend it to end all abortion, since a legislator willing to ban 97 percent has no principled reason to refuse 100 percent.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: If a given pro-life bill actually reduced abortion by 97 percent while implicitly or explicitly consenting to the murder of the other three percent, we would still argue against it… If there were such a bill, we would work to amend it so that it abolished abortion. There’s no reason that a public official who supports abolishing 97 percent of abortion shouldn’t support abolishing 100 percent of abortion.

q: What does the article concede about Lincoln's incrementalism, and why does it not transfer to Wilberforce?
a: Silberman grants that Lincoln was an incrementalist who long opposed abolitionists and supported colonization, but maintains that Wilberforce's own writings refute incrementalists' attempts to claim him.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: Incrementalists can have Lincoln, but they cannot have Wilberforce. Their attempt to claim Wilberforce is irrefutably contradicted in the most clear and strong possible terms by Wilberforce’s own words consistently over a period of decades.

q: What is the article's final verdict on Klusendorf's piece and Desiring God's role in publishing it?
a: Silberman calls the article a mix of unbiblical reasoning and clearly misleading claims that, given Desiring God's credibility, should be substantially amended or unpublished.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: Klusendorf’s arguments against immediatism largely vary from being based on unbiblical reasoning to being clearly misleading. The credibility lent to the article by Desiring God’s masthead is a shame. For both its effect in delaying the abolition of abortion and its serious factual inaccuracies, the article should either be substantially amended or, ideally, unpublished.

q: How does the article frame the moral responsibility for abortions occurring in pro-life-controlled states?
a: Where pro-lifers control the legislature and governorship, they bear full responsibility for every legally murdered preborn child because they possess but refuse to use the power to abolish abortion.
t: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
qt: We are not responsible for the 97 percent, but in states where pro-lifers control the legislature and governorship, pro-lifers are responsible for 100 percent of the children legally murdered in utero.

q: What is the 'Nuremberg Defense' and why does Silberman invoke it against pro-life politicians?
a: Silberman uses the Nazi defense of 'an order is an order' as a moral analogue for pro-life officials who refuse to abolish abortion because they say they must obey the Supreme Court. The point is that obedience to an unjust order is never a legitimate moral cover.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: When Nazi war criminals went before an international court in Nuremberg, the primary legal defenses were versions of “befehl ist befehl” – “an order is an order” or “I was just following orders.” This plea is now known as the “Nuremberg Defense.”

q: On what grounds does Silberman claim that any government order to commit murder is automatically void?
a: He appeals to a higher law above human law—what the Declaration of Independence calls 'the law of nature and nature's God'—which renders any command to murder the innocent null at its inception.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: Many nazis were executed and rightly so because there is a law higher than man’s law. The Declaration of Independence refers to it as the law “of nature and nature’s God.” Any government order to murder the innocent is null by its nature because it is in violation of the highest law.

q: How does psychological research on coercion reinforce the article's moral argument?
a: Silberman cites Milgram and a UCL/Brussels study showing that people who act under orders psychologically detach from responsibility, which he says explains how pro-life politicians can enable abortion while feeling morally clean.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: actions taken under coercion decreases one’s “sense of agency.” The report further explained that “people who obey orders may subjectively experience their actions as closer to passive movements than fully voluntary actions.”

q: What distinction do pro-life lawyers draw between Roe and the Nazi orders, and how does Silberman answer it?
a: Pro-lifers argue Roe merely permits abortion rather than commanding it, like China's one-child policy. Silberman counters that permitting mass murder is itself a crime against humanity that legislators are obligated to nullify.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: “The logic of [comparing submission to *Roe* to submission to Nazi orders] is faulty, since Roe does not ‘order’ abortions to be carried out (like China’s’ ‘one-child’ policy) but provides for their legal availability to women as a matter of right.”

q: How does Silberman characterize the moral position of pro-life leaders in states they control?
a: He says they are not bystanders but active enablers of genocide, because they hold the power to abolish abortion in their states and choose not to use it.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: Pro-life legislators and executives in states where pro-lifers control the state government are allowing crimes against humanity to take place in their states. **This genocide is enabled by the pro-life leaders.**

q: What historical American authority does Silberman cite to ground a state's right to nullify federal tyranny?
a: He invokes Thomas Jefferson's Kentucky Resolutions, which held that nullification is the rightful remedy whenever the federal government acts tyrannically.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: In the Kentucky Resolutions, Thomas Jefferson wrote that “every state has a natural right to nullify,” and that when the federal government behaves tyrannically, “nullification… is the rightful remedy.”

q: Why does Silberman frame nullification as a human right and sometimes a duty rather than a mere political tactic?
a: Because the only alternative is to grant governing authorities unlimited power to inflict evil on neighbors with no recourse, which he says aligns the citizen with the Nazi defense.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: This is why it is a human right, and sometimes a human obligation, to nullify when a governing authority commands that which is evil, and especially if that command rises to the level of being a crime against humanity.

q: How does the article connect its moral argument to a Christian theological foundation?
a: Silberman argues that the very categories of good and evil presuppose the Christian God revealed in Romans 1, and that atheism cannot account for the moral indignation we all feel toward atrocities like abortion.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: The simple act of calling something “good” or “evil” is to borrow from the Christian worldview. Atheism cannot justify these concepts.

q: What scale of harm does the article attribute to Roe v. Wade?
a: Silberman calls Roe one of history's greatest mass murders, citing more than 63 million dead, and argues this scale obligates state governments to stop treating it as binding law.
t: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
qt: *Roe v. Wade* is a crime against humanity that ushered in one of the greatest mass murders the world has ever seen with 63+ million dead and counting.

q: What specific claim by Doug Wilson prompted this rebuke from Free the States?
a: On a February 2nd, 2022 episode of his podcast, Wilson argued that if the Dobbs decision overturns Roe and returns abortion regulation to the states, abolitionists ought to gratefully thank the pro-life incrementalists they have long criticized for handing them the opportunity to act at the state level.
t: Dang it, Doug.
qt: If the Dobbs case goes the way it ought to, and the decision whether to restrict abortion is handed back to the states, it will have been the incrementalists who gave the abolitionists on the state level their opportunity, and I think it’d be nice if the abolitionists were gracious enough to say ‘thank you.’

q: What is the empirical track record of pro-life incrementalist litigation at the Supreme Court since 1973?
a: Silberman points out that 38 abortion-related cases have reached the Supreme Court since Roe and not one has produced any rollback of the decision; Casey in 1992 actually strengthened Roe with an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority, with the lone Democrat being the pro-life dissenter Byron White.
t: Dang it, Doug.
