t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: Roe’s most damning indictment comes from former dean of Stanford Law school John Hart Ely who [proclaimed that](https://www.washingtonexaminer.com/in-criticizing-roe-sessions-aligns-with-most-legal-scholars) “[*Roe v. Wade*] is not constitutional law and gives almost no sense of an obligation to try to be.” What Ely is saying is that in *Roe*, Blackmun hardly tried to make his decision appear legitimate. *Roe*amounts to nothing more than simple tyranny; a transparent power grab by a branch of government determined to become the engine of societal upheaval.

q: What does the article propose state legislatures actually do about Roe?
a: It points to Oklahoma SB13 as the model: legislatively recognize unborn children as persons entitled to equal protection from fertilization, and treat any contrary federal statute, regulation, executive order, or court ruling as void. This is framed as the appropriate, even the only, lawful response when a federal entity declares a class of humans non-persons.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: “It is the intent of the Legislature,” the bill states, “to provide to unborn children the equal protection of the laws of this state; to establish that a living human child, from the moment of fertilization upon the fusion of a human spermatozoon with a human ovum, is entitled to the same rights, powers, privileges, justice and protections as are secured or granted by the laws of this state to any other human person; and to treat as void and of no effect any and all federal statutes, regulations, executive orders and court rulings, which would deprive an unborn child of the right to life.”

q: What historical precedent does Silberman invoke for nullifying Roe?
a: He grounds the strategy in Wisconsin's 1854 nullification of the Fugitive Slave Act, presenting nullification as the rightful response when the federal government strips a class of humans of personhood. He bluntly rejects 'just following orders' as a defense for any modern complicity.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: Following in the footsteps of Wisconsin’s 1854 nullification of the Fugitive Slave Act, OK SB13 is the appropriate response to a federal entity declaring a group of humans non-persons. Dare I say defiance and nullification is the *only*appropriate response to such a ruling. “I’m just following orders” wasn’t a legitimate excuse for mass murder-enablers of the past and it isn’t a legitimate excuse now.

q: Why does the article cite Elizabeth Heyrick's 1824 pamphlet at such length?
a: Heyrick argued that the call for gradual emancipation originated with the slaveholder because gradualism breeds indifference and moral acclimation, and Silberman draws the parallel directly to abortion. The pro-life movement, on his read, has been seduced by the same satanic sophistry that defanged the early antislavery cause.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: “The slave holder knew very well that his prey would be secure, so long as the abolitionists could be cajoled into a demand for gradual instead of immediate abolition. He knew very well, that the contemplation of a gradual emancipation, would beget a gradual indifference to emancipation itself. He knew very well, that even the wise and the good, may, by habit and familiarity, be brought to endure and tolerate almost any thing…

q: What does Silberman say has happened to the church under the incremental strategy?
a: He says millions of Christians have been gradually anesthetized to abortion by being told to settle for ultrasound laws, waiting periods, and small percentage reductions. Settling for those wins, he argues, lulls the church to sleep and prevents the only demand that could actually end abortion.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: A faithful, uncompromising delineation of the horrors of abortion by the hundreds of millions of Christians in this country would bring abortion to an end. But if those millions of Christians settle for Right to Life, and Right to Life-allied centrist Republicans passing ultrasound laws and waiting periods which reduce abortion by one or two or five percent and call that winning, there will never be an end to abortion because the church will have been lulled to sleep and the actual demand that abortion be outlawed because it is murder will never have been made.

q: How does the article answer worries that nullifying Roe would create a 'crisis of authority'?
a: Silberman invokes Jefferson's 1820 letter to William Jarvis to argue that treating the Supreme Court as the final arbiter of all constitutional questions establishes an oligarchy the Constitution never authorized. The departments are co-equal, and judges are not immune from the same passions as other officials.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: “You seem to consider the judges as the ultimate arbiters of all constitutional questions; a very dangerous doctrine indeed, and one which would place us under the despotism of an oligarchy. Our judges are as honest as other men, and not more so. They have, with others, the same passions for party, for power, and the privilege of their corps…. Their power [is] the more dangerous as they are in office for life, and not responsible, as the other functionaries are, to the elective control. The Constitution has erected no such single tribunal, knowing that to whatever hands confided, with the corruptions of time and party, its members would become despots. It has more wisely made all the departments co-equal and co-sovereign within themselves.”

q: Does the article think immediatism makes Roe's reversal more or less likely?
a: Silberman argues the immediatist position is actually more likely to bring Roe down, because the Court's authority depends on the deference it commands. A state openly defying judicial activism signals that the people will no longer tolerate it and gives the Court reason to retreat.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: The immediatist position is that the Supreme Court’s permission is not necessary to abolish abortion, but the immediatist position is also more likely to bring about Roe’s reversal. A state challenging the court’s self-appointed authority to legalize murder signals to judges and justices that the people will no longer put up with judicial activism and legislation from the bench. The Supreme Court has no enforcement power meaning their authority is contingent upon the degree to which it respects the constitution and the respect it commands from the other branches, from the states, and from the people.

q: How does Blackmun's footnote 54 in Roe figure into the argument against compromised pro-life laws?
a: Blackmun observed that the Texas statute treated abortion inconsistently with murder, exempting the woman and imposing only a short prison sentence on the abortionist, and used that inconsistency to undermine personhood. Silberman argues this proves that pro-life laws which exempt mothers and treat abortion as something less than murder actively sabotage the case against it.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: “There are other inconsistencies between Fourteenth Amendment status and the typical abortion statute. It has already been pointed out, n. 49, supra, that in Texas the woman is not a principal or an accomplice with respect to an abortion upon her. If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?”

q: What parallel does Silberman draw between Roe v. Wade and Dred Scott?
a: He treats both rulings as evil and unconstitutional acts that legally severed humanity from personhood, the pinnacle of injustice. When courts go that far, he argues, the states are obligated to disregard them rather than wait for the Court's permission to do right.
t: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
qt: Whether it be *Roe v. Wade* or *Scott v. Sanford*, the legal separation of humanity from personhood is the pinnacle of injustice. Both were evil and unconstitutional rulings, and when the courts disregard the constitution so egregiously and deliberately as to legalize murder, the states must disregard the courts.

q: What is the long-standing legislative position of the Abolitionist Movement regarding which bills it supports?
a: The movement holds 'no bill but abolition,' meaning any legislation other than total and immediate abolition of abortion is treated as either intrinsically unjust or a distraction from the goal.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: The position of the Abolitionist Movement regarding which bills we support has long been “no bill but abolition.” Any other legislation is either intrinsically unjust or a distraction from the point.

q: What is a 'righteous increment' as defined in the internal abolitionist debate?
a: It refers to incremental bills that don't abolish abortion but also don't explicitly approve murder the way a 20-week ban or heartbeat bill does—a defund Planned Parenthood bill being the prototypical example.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: incremental bills that do not abolish abortion, but also do not give approval to murder in the way a 20-week ban or heartbeat bill does. A Planned Parenthood defunding bill is one such example.

q: Why does Silberman say that avoiding explicit violations of God's law isn't enough to make a bill acceptable?
a: Because the case against pro-life incrementalism rests on more than technical compliance with God's law—it also rests on abolitionism's prophetic call for the repentance necessary to abolish abortion.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: The problem with this line of reasoning is that its explicit violations of God’s law are not the only aspect of the case against pro-life incrementalism.

q: How does Silberman characterize the injustice in typical pro-life regulatory bills?
a: He argues that bills like a pain-capable act or heartbeat bill choose an arbitrary point at which life is valuable and legally discriminate against those deemed not valuable, which is unjust partiality rooted in ageism.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: a pain-capable unborn child protection act chooses an arbitrary point at which life is valuable and legally discriminates against those deemed not valuable. This is unjust partiality rooted in ageism, as is a heartbeat bill, 15-week ban, or any other law that instructs abortionists and parents when, where, why, and how to murder children.

q: What does Silberman mean when he says 'Abolitionism is repentance'?
a: He means that approaching a legislator with an abolition bill is an act of calling them to repentance, asking them to go in a fundamentally different direction from the path every pro-life group has urged on them.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: When we approach a legislator requesting their support for an abolition bill, we are calling them to repentance, whatever terms we choose to put it in. We are asking them to go in a fundamentally different direction than they’ve gone before, and in a fundamentally different direction than they’ve been asked to go by every pro-life group.

q: What internal struggle does Silberman expect a born-again legislator to face when called to support abolition?
a: The Spirit will press the legislator to author a just law upholding abolition, while the flesh will weigh political costs—relationships, donors, and reelection chances—and seek schemes that appease pro-life voters while dodging the abolition controversy.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: The Spirit will be urging them to author or co-author a law upholding justice, but their flesh will be weighing the political cost, worrying about how doing something controversial like fighting to immediately abolish abortion will affect their relationships, donors, and reelection chances.

q: Why does bringing a defunding bill alongside an abolition bill make abolitionists complicit in a legislator's compromise?
a: Because doing so feeds the legislator's flesh by handing them a substitute scheme labeled as good, adding the abolitionist to the impediments to that legislator's repentance.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: we are enabling their flesh. We are providing the temptation. We have added ourselves to the list of impediments to their repentance. We have brought them abolition’s substitute scheme and told them that it’s good.

q: Why is taking 'easy options off the table' essential to getting legislators to abolish abortion?
a: Silberman argues legislators will only choose the politically hardest path—abolition—when they cannot be celebrated as heroes for passing a lesser bill instead; as long as easier substitutes earn them praise, they will pursue those instead of abolition.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: The only way to get them to do the most politically difficult thing is to take the easy options off the table. For as long as they can be celebrated for doing something instead of abolishing abortion, they will do something instead of abolishing abortion.

q: What is T. Russell Hunter's quoted insight about how abortion remains legal?
a: Hunter argues that the never-ending stream of incremental pro-life victories is itself the key to keeping abortion legal, because those victories provide political cover that lets pro-life politicians stay in office while continuing to permit abortion.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: “The key to keeping abortion legal is a never-ending stream of incremental pro-life victories.” The many pro-life bills passed each year provide political cover to the very pro-life politicians keeping abortion legal, thereby allowing them to remain in office and continue keeping abortion legal.

q: Why does Silberman argue 'incremental abolitionism' inevitably becomes its own never-ending stream of victories?
a: Because there are infinite ways to regulate abortion without technically violating God's law, the same political incentives that fuel the pro-life establishment will lead 'incremental abolitionists' to chase a permanent pipeline of incremental wins, denying immediatism in practice.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: While there are infinite ways to regulate abortion that violate God’s law, there are also an infinite amount of ways to regulate abortion without technically violating God’s law. The “incremental abolitionists” could just as easily have a never-ending stream of incremental victories as the pro-life establishment does; and because of the realities addressed in the previous section, this is where the “incremental abolitionist” position inevitably leads, even if unintentionally.

q: How does Silberman rebut the argument that defunding Planned Parenthood meaningfully harms abortion providers?
a: He notes that in the state where defunding was loudest, Planned Parenthood received only about $82,000/year from the state—a drop in the ocean they could easily fundraise back tenfold, making the bill political theater rather than real harm to opponents.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: Were Planned Parenthood to be stripped of $82,000/year, they could fundraise off of that and get back ten times as much. We are not hurting Planned Parenthood by defunding them in a state like that. We are playing Politics 101: looking like we’re doing something without doing anything.

q: Why does Silberman call platform-building through empty bills 'fundamentally anti-abolitionist'?
a: Because abolitionism demands being Christian all the way through; using empty rhetoric, empty legislation, and empty campaigning to grow contact lists and donor credibility is the way of the world and contradicts abolitionist principles.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: This is a fundamentally anti-abolitionist mindset. Abolitionism is about being Christian all the way through. Empty rhetoric, empty legislation, and empty campaigning for the sake of building a larger platform is the way of the world. It’s politics 101. It’s not what abolitionists are about.

q: How does Silberman handle the argument that Planned Parenthood will need defunding eventually anyway?
a: He concedes the point but argues the timing matters: defund them after abortion is abolished, not while doing so undermines repentance and gives political cover to the politicians and lobbyists keeping the slaughter going.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: Defund them after it’s abolished, not when doing so undermines repentance and gives cover to the very politicians and lobbyists keeping the slaughter going.

q: What is the standard Silberman insists abolitionists must hold in legislative work?
a: He says the standard is abolition—equal protection of preborn children and the defiance of Roe—and abolitionists must not budge from it or enable legislators' refusal to repent by offering them easier things to do.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: That means setting a standard of abolition — equal protection and the defiance of *Roe* — and not budging from it. It means not enabling the flesh of legislators in their refusal to repent by bringing them easier things to do.

q: What distinction does the article's UPDATE draw between filing a defund bill and rejecting a budget that funds Planned Parenthood?
a: Filing a standalone defund bill is treated as a likely scheme to delay abolition, while a righteous official's refusal to vote for or sign a budget that funds depraved entities like Planned Parenthood is legitimate, even when the funding amount is small.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: The former is a likely scheme to delay abolition, the latter is not. Righteous governing officials should refuse to support budgets that include funding of depraved entities like Planned Parenthood, even if the amount being allocated is small.

q: Why does Silberman highlight Pastor Blake Gideon as an example tied to the 'Repent With Us' conference theme?
a: Gideon, the former President of Oklahoma Baptists, had written in 2019 urging SBC churches to oppose the bill to abolish abortion in Oklahoma; his public repentance and embrace of abolitionism is presented as evidence that repentance fuels further repentance and is central to the movement.
t: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
qt: the pastor of EFBC is Blake Gideon, the former President of Oklahoma Baptists who wrote a letter in 2019 to every SBC church in the State asking them to oppose the bill to abolish abortion in Oklahoma. He publicly repented of doing so at last year’s Abolition Day rally and in the new documentary [*A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain*](https://vimeo.com/ondemand/astormcomesrolling). His repentance and embrace of abolitionism is bringing about the repentance of many others, and is going to continue doing so.

q: Why does the author reject the common claim that abolitionists and pro-lifers ultimately want the same thing?
a: Because the two camps actually pursue legally different ends: abolitionists demand that preborn children receive equal protection and that abortion be classified as murder, whereas the Pro-Life Establishment wants abortion treated as a lesser crime and accepts denying preborn children equal protection.
t: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
qt: Abolitionists want preborn children treated as our equals under the law; we want abortion treated as murder. The Pro-Life Establishment wants abortion classified as a lesser crime, and for preborn children to be denied the equal protection of the law.

q: Even granting that both sides wanted the same thing, why would shared goals still not justify shared strategies?
a: Because identical objectives do not require identical methods; some strategies for reaching a goal succeed and others ruin it, so the means must be evaluated on their own merits.
t: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
qt: But clearly, similar ends imply neither the necessity nor the propriety of employing similar means to accomplish those ends. Some strategies are good, others are disastrous.

q: How does the Chamberlain-Churchill comparison frame the difference between the two abortion strategies?
a: Both Chamberlain and Churchill said they wanted peace, but their methods diverged absolutely: Chamberlain compromised, conceded, and appeased Hitler, while Churchill defied him, issued ultimatums, and stood on principle even at the cost of war.
t: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
qt:
Where Chamberlain would compromise with Hitler, Churchill would defy him. 

Where Chamberlain would issue concessions, Churchill would issue ultimatums. 

Where Chamberlain would appease, Churchill would stand on principle and, if necessary, go to war.

q: How does the article characterize the incrementalist legislative approach?
a: Incrementalists are portrayed as appeasers who advance bills that ostensibly stop the killing of some preborn children while implicitly permitting in law the murder of all others, hoping the enemy will gradually relent.
t: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
qt: Incrementalists (pro-lifers) are willing to meet the enemy halfway, appeasing him with concessionary proposals of legislation that ostensibly halt the murder of some preborn children while implicitly sanctioning in law the murder of all the rest. Gradually (so the thinking goes), the enemy will be worn down or come to his senses.

q: What is the moral case Haggard makes for immediatism?
a: If abortion is murder, then any compromise is itself immoral; abolitionists must therefore demand the total and immediate criminalization of all abortion, since trading the protection of some lives for the continued killing of others is unacceptable.
t: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
qt: Immediatists (abolitionists), on the other hand boldly assert that if abortion is murder (and it is), then compromise is immoral. All abortion ought, [on principle](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GBPV_ZFjtnY), to be immediately criminalized. It is wrong to attempt to purchase the supposed deliverance of some at the price of the continued murder of the rest.

q: What does the author say is at stake if Christians fail to learn from the Chamberlain-Churchill lesson?
a: Failure to learn the lesson means disobeying God's prohibition of murder and ensuring continued failure, with the result being the perpetuation of mass abortion potentially without end.
t: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
qt: To fail to glean from Chamberlain and Churchill lessons applicable to our own war against abortion is not only to disobey God (who prohibits all murder), but to actively court failure. Failure which, [as the past 49 years amply demonstrate](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C2XyfdMxyOc), equates to the continuation of the mass slaughter of the preborn, perhaps to the end of time.

q: How does Haggard summarize the core distinction between the two movements in one image?
a: He boils the distinction down to a single comparison: pro-life incrementalism is Chamberlain and abolitionist immediatism is Churchill.
t: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
qt: In a very real sense, the fundamental difference between incrementalism and immediatism (i.e.,  pro-lifeism and abolitionism) is hardly more complicated than this: the former is Chamberlain, the latter is Churchill.

q: Does the Churchill analogy mean abolitionists endorse violence as a means of resistance?
a: No. The endnote clarifies that abolitionists explicitly reject violence; the analogy only illustrates that abolitionists will not back down from peaceable principled stands even when they provoke hostile opposition.
t: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
qt: This is not to suggest that abolitionists promote or condone violence. We emphatically do not (see our [statement](https://abolishhumanabortion.com/abolitionism/why-abolition-must-be-non-violent/) on why abolitionism must be non-violent). The point is simply that we will not shrink from standing firm on the peaceable [principles](https://abolishhumanabortion.com/abolitionism/the-five-tenets-of-abolitionism/) of abolition, even if the price of fidelity to their practice is hostile opposition.

q: What's the headline result from Indiana State House District 93, and why is it being treated as a first?
a: Abolitionist John Jacob defeated pro-life incumbent Dollyne Sherman in the Republican primary, and the article frames it as the first time an abolitionist successfully unseated a sitting pro-life officeholder rather than merely converting one already in office.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: Last week, abolitionist John Jacob defeated pro-life incumbent Dollyne Sherman in the Republican primary race for Indiana State House District 93. There are a number of sitting State Senators and Representatives in various states who have converted to abolitionism, but Jacob's victory marks the first time an abolitionist has successfully unseated a pro-lifer.

q: How explicit was Jacob about being an abolitionist rather than just another pro-life candidate?
a: Jacob led with the word 'abolish' on his Facebook header and campaign mailer, and the term 'pro-life' did not appear in his original campaign content at all except in one shared third-party endorsement post.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: Nowhere on Jacob's website or Facebook page does the term "pro-life" appear except for one endorsement post shared by Jacob in which an organization calls him "pro-life." The term does not appear in the Jacob campaign's original content or materials, while the a-word is featured prominently both at the top of his Facebook page and on his abortion-related campaign mailer.

q: Which organizations endorsed each side, and what does that lineup signal?
a: Jacob was backed by Abolish Abortion Indiana and Hoosiers for Life, while the establishment pro-life group Indiana Right to Life backed Sherman, showing a clean split between abolitionist groups and the pro-life establishment.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: Jacob was endorsed by Abolish Abortion Indiana and Hoosiers for Life. Sherman was endorsed by Indiana Right to Life.

q: What policy commitment did Jacob make tying him to the most controversial abolition bill in Indiana?
a: He pledged to co-author Rep. Curt Nisly's Protection at Conception bill, the same bill that had drawn the ire of the Indiana pro-life establishment, instead of distancing himself from it for political safety.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: If elected as State Representative, John will co-author with Representative Curt Nisly's Protection at Conception bill to protect all life from conception to natural death; including the support for and passing of this critical bill.

q: What does the Protection at Conception Act actually do, in plain terms?
a: It is a total and immediate abortion abolition bill that also nullifies any conflicting federal law, treaty, or order within Indiana that fails to protect the right to life.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: The Protection at Conception Act is a bill that totally and immediately abolishes abortion and establishes that "any act, law, treaty, order, rule, or regulation of the United States government that fails to protect a person's unalienable right to life is null, void, and unenforceable in Indiana."

q: How does Jacob's result compare to other abolition-friendly Indiana primary challengers?
a: Three other Indiana challengers supportive of the same bill ran with predominantly pro-life branding and lost; only Jacob, who led with the a-word, won.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: There were three other primary challengers in Indiana with connections to the Abolitionist Movement who were supportive of the Indiana Protection at Conception Act. Two of these three ran explicitly as "pro-life" candidates and did not use the a-word at all. The third included the a-word a few times, but used "pro-life" more often. Jacob was the only winner.

q: Does Silberman claim using the a-word is the reason Jacob won?
a: No. He explicitly disclaims a single-cause explanation and instead uses the result to refute the narrower claim that abolitionists categorically can't beat pro-life incumbents by running on an openly abolitionist platform.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: But it is to say conclusively that the idea that primary challengers can't unseat pro-life incumbents by running on an explicitly abolitionist platform needs to be put to rest.

q: Even when an abolitionist candidate loses, what upside does the article say running openly buys you?
a: It produces an educational dividend by exposing huge audiences to abolitionism as distinct from pro-lifeism, generating new converts to the movement long after the votes are counted.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: Dan Fisher did not win the race, but by running on a loud-and-proud abolitionist platform, he exposed hundreds of thousands of people to the ideology of abolitionism as distinct from pro-lifeism which has born fruit in the form of many converts to the movement.

q: How does Silberman answer the worry that using 'abolish' invites establishment smear campaigns?
a: He argues the smears are unavoidable for any serious abolitionist candidate, and that softening the language only forfeits the campaign's strongest contrast with the pro-life opponent.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: This is a possibility, but that possible ire is unavoidable for the abolitionist candidate regardless of the language used unless the candidate completely hides the fact that they want to immediately abolish abortion. In that case, the abolitionist challenger loses one their primary lines of attack on their pro-life opponent – the fact that they're serious about abolishing abortion and their opponent isn't.

q: What's the underlying theological framing Jacob uses on his campaign site?
a: He grounds the right to life in the imago Dei and divine authorship of life, treating abortion abolition as a creational and constitutional duty rather than a policy preference.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: God is the Author and Creator of all life. God created all human beings in His image. Every human life is a gift from the Creator. It is an irrefutable scientific fact that human life begins at conception.

q: What is the rhetorical case for the word 'abolish' itself?
a: Silberman closes by arguing the word communicates exactly the intended action, attaches to a biblically defensible ideology, and belongs on a banner rather than in a closet.
t: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
qt: "Abolish" is a word that communicates boldly and precisely what one intends to do about abortion. It is associated with an ideology that is Biblically unimpeachable and a movement that is growing exponentially. It is nothing to be ashamed of. It is a banner to be held high.

q: Why did HB1430 fail to receive a hearing in the Indiana legislature?
a: House Committee on Public Policy Chairman Ben Smaltz declined to schedule the bill before the February 18th deadline for first hearings, killing it without debate.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: Indiana House Committee on Public Policy Chairman Ben Smaltz (R-Auburn) decided against hearing the bill as the deadline for first hearings passed on February 18th.

q: What would HB1430 have actually done if passed and signed?
a: It would have defined human physical life as beginning at conception, asserted Indiana's Ninth and Tenth Amendment authority to protect that life, nullified federal interference, and completely abolished abortion in the state.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: HB1430, authored by State Rep. Curt Nisly (R-Goshen), would have added an article to Indiana criminal codes specifying that human physical life begins at conception, asserted the state’s authority to protect human physical life grounded in the ninth and tenth amendment of the US Constitution along with the Declaration of Independence, and established that any attempt to interfere with Indiana’s protection of human physical life is legally null.

q: What was Smaltz's stated objection to the Protection at Conception Act?
a: Smaltz claimed in 2017 that passing the bill would be 'catastrophic to the pro-life movement' because a hostile judge could strike down the abolition sections while keeping the regulation-repeal sections intact.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: He told the Fort Wayne Journal Gazette then that passing the Protection at Conception Act would be “catastrophic to the [pro-life movement].”

q: How does the bill's non-severability clause refute Smaltz's argument?
a: Indiana law states that if any part of a non-severable statute is invalidated, the entire statute is void, meaning a court could not selectively kill the abolition portions while preserving the repeal of pro-life regulations.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: According to Indiana law, “[i]f a statute contains a nonseverability provision and if any part of that statute is declared invalid, the whole statute is void.” HB1430 also had a nonseverability clause.

q: Where is the misinformation about non-severability originating?
a: The same false talking point used by Oklahoma pro-life politicians is being deployed by Indiana lobbyists, traced back to state and county affiliates of National Right to Life, including Allen County Right to Life.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: The same misinformation is being spread by Oklahoma pro-life politicians and lobbyists and it comes from the same source: National Right to Life’s state- and county-level affiliates.

q: What did House Speaker Brian Bosma say when asked about the Nisly bill?
a: Bosma rejected the Nisly bill at a press conference, saying he would only support a 'life bill' that he believed had 'Constitutional legs.'
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: Bosma: No. I’m talking about one that has some Constitutional legs.

q: How does the article answer the constitutional objection raised by Bosma?
a: Silberman argues Bosma's oath is to the Constitution rather than the Supreme Court, that Article VI only binds states to laws made pursuant to the Constitution, and that Jefferson and Madison endorsed state-level nullification of unconstitutional federal acts.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: Article VI of the Constitution establishes that only those made pursuant to the Constitution are legitimate laws and binding on the states.

q: What did Thomas Jefferson warn about treating judges as ultimate arbiters of constitutional questions?
a: In his 1820 letter to Charles Jarvis, Jefferson called judicial supremacy 'a very dangerous doctrine' that would place the country under an oligarchic despotism, since judges share the same passions for party and power as other men.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: You seem to consider the judges as the ultimate arbiters of all constitutional questions; a very dangerous doctrine indeed, and one which would place us under the despotism of an oligarchy.

q: What does the article cite to argue Roe v. Wade is not legitimate constitutional law?
a: It quotes former Stanford Law dean John Hart Ely, who acknowledged Roe is not constitutional law and shows no obligation even to try to be.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: [Roe v Wade] is not constitutional law and gives almost no sense of an obligation to try to be.

q: How does the article frame the moral obligation to defy unjust judicial rulings?
a: It argues defiance is the only suitable response when a higher authority commands an egregious act, and that 'just following orders from D.C.' cannot justify allowing mass murder.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: “I’m just following orders from D.C.” is no excuse for allowing mass murder.

q: Did Indiana Right to Life endorse Smaltz's primary challenger who supported abolition?
a: No. Indiana Right to Life gave Smaltz an unqualified endorsement in 2018 over William Carlin, despite Smaltz publicly supporting the legality of killing babies conceived in rape or incest.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: Indiana Right to Life endorsed Smaltz in 2018 over challenger William Carlin, who ran on a platform of supporting the Protection at Conception Act. Their endorsement was unqualified despite Smaltz’s public support for the legality of murdering babies conceived in rape or incest.

q: What is Hoosiers for Life planning in response to the bill being killed?
a: President Amy Schlicter said Hoosiers for Life intends to recruit primary challengers against Smaltz and other state-level Republican leaders in 2020 and will keep doing so until the legislature contains people who will stand for all life without exception.
t: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
qt: “Until there are people in [the legislature] that will stand for life, all life, without exception, we will continue to find people to run in their districts.”

q: What recent Supreme Court decision is Riley reacting to and why does he say abolitionists were not surprised?
a: Riley is reacting to Bostock v. Clayton County, in which the Court ruled that the 1964 Civil Rights Act's protection against sex discrimination also covers transgenderism and homosexuality. Abolitionists were unsurprised because they have long warned that the Court legislates from the bench, and the ruling simply confirmed that warning.
t: It Happened Again
qt: It was no shock to those who have seen the writing on the wall for some time, and no one who has consistently advocated abolishing abortion in defiance of the court was embarrassed when it happened again.

q: How does Riley characterize the Supreme Court's interpretive method in Bostock?
a: He calls it 'Constitutional-blank-pageism' - the practice of treating the Constitution and statutes as blank pages onto which the Court can write whatever left-wing policy it wants, redefining ordinary words like 'sex' to import ideologies that were nowhere in view when the law was written.
t: It Happened Again
qt: In *Bostock v. Clayton County*, the supreme court unilaterally altered the meaning of words and read into the law far-left policy objectives which plainly are not there.

q: Why does Riley believe pro-life Republican judicial appointments have failed?
a: Because the strategy of electing Republican presidents to appoint pro-life justices to overturn Roe has not worked: 47 years after Roe, two of those very appointees joined a sweeping post-modern decision on a social issue, proving the court-centered approach is bankrupt.
t: It Happened Again
qt: Here we are 47 years after Roe and two of our pro-life Republican supreme court appointments have just given us one of the most post-modern, far-left decisions on a social issue that we've ever seen.

q: What posture does Riley accuse the pro-life establishment of taking toward the federal judiciary?
a: He accuses them of perpetual groveling - of waiting passively for a hostile court to grant permission to protect preborn children, even as that court repeatedly humiliates them. He frames it as lying supinely on their backs and clinging to false hope.
t: It Happened Again
qt: The conservative establishment of our day has resolved themselves to perpetual groveling to a federal judiciary that perpetually stomps on their face.

q: How does Riley use Patrick Henry's words to indict the 'just wait' mentality?
a: He invokes Patrick Henry's rhetorical questions about gathering strength through inaction to argue that pro-life leaders have functionally answered 'yes' - they have chosen irresolution and delusive hope over effectual resistance, and so remain bound hand and foot.
t: It Happened Again
qt: The rhetorical questions of Patrick Henry have been resoundingly answered in the affirmative, "Shall we gather strength by irresolution and inaction? Shall we acquire the means of effectual resistance by lying supinely on our backs and hugging the delusive phantom of hope, until our enemies shall have bound us hand and foot?"

q: What practical course of action does Riley call abolitionists to?
a: He calls them to disabuse themselves of the Court's supposed ultimate power and to act by defying Roe and abolishing abortion at the state level, refusing to wait for the Court's permission to protect preborn children.
t: It Happened Again
qt: We must disillusion ourselves from the supposedly ultimate power of the court. We must do this by defying *Roe* and abolishing abortion.

q: How does Riley frame obedience to an unjust Supreme Court ruling theologically?
a: He frames it as moral catastrophe: a court that orders the murder of children must not be obeyed, and a nation that has obeyed Roe for nearly half a century stands under God's judgment and needs God's help.
t: It Happened Again
qt: If the court said we must allow the murder of toddlers, God help the man who obeys, and God help the nation which has obeyed for 47 years.

q: What was Jake Merrick's margin of victory in the SD22 special general election?
a: Merrick took more than 65 percent of the vote against Democrat Molly Ooten in the Tuesday special general election to win the SD22 seat.
t: Jake Merrick Elected to Oklahoma State Senate!
qt: With more than 65 percent of the vote, Jake Merrick defeated Democrat Molly Ooten in Tuesday’s special general election to become the next Senator from OK SD22.

q: How does Merrick's win compare to Warren Hamilton's 2020 race in terms of money spent?
a: Both abolitionists won despite being heavily outspent—Hamilton beat incumbent Larry Boggs even though Boggs and his supporting PACs outspent him roughly four-to-one, mirroring Merrick's victory over better-funded Republican and Democrat opponents.
t: Jake Merrick Elected to Oklahoma State Senate!
qt: A similar situation played out last year when abolitionist Warren Hamilton defeated incumbent Senator Larry Boggs despite Boggs and Boggs-supporting PACs outspending Hamilton roughly four-to-one.

q: What lesson does Free the States draw from these back-to-back Oklahoma upsets?
a: Money is being eclipsed by clear, biblically grounded positions—especially on abortion—so candidates with consistently Christian convictions can defeat establishment-backed opponents.
t: Jake Merrick Elected to Oklahoma State Senate!
qt: In Oklahoma, it’s becoming clear that money is no substitute for strong, consistently Christian stances on important issues, beginning with abortion.

q: When did Jake Merrick first get on abolitionists' radar?
a: He drew abolitionist attention during his earlier run for Oklahoma's fifth congressional district when he made abolishing abortion the headline plank of his platform.
t: Jake Merrick Elected to Oklahoma State Senate!
qt: Merrick first appeared on the radar of many abolitionists when, during his previous candidacy for Oklahoma’s fifth congressional district, he made “Abolish[ing] abortion” his campaign’s top priority.

q: What specific commitment did Merrick make to voters about abortion legislation?
a: He pledged to draft legislation ending abortion in Oklahoma, citing the 4,995 abortions performed in the state in 2019 as proof that the status quo must change.
t: Jake Merrick Elected to Oklahoma State Senate!
qt:
When elected as State Senator of District 22, I intend to represent and protect the rights of Oklahomans by drafting legislation that will:
>
> 1.) End Abortion in Oklahoma.
>
> In 2019 there were 4,995 abortions performed in Oklahoma. Abortion is currently legal in Oklahoma, and this needs to change. We must end this egregious practice in our state.

q: How does Free the States frame the strategic significance of pairing Merrick with Warren Hamilton?
a: FTS sees the two senators as future co-authors and floor champions for total and immediate abolition bills, doubling abolitionist firepower in the Oklahoma legislature.
t: Jake Merrick Elected to Oklahoma State Senate!
qt: Keep Merrick and Hamilton in your prayers and prepare to support them as they come together as co-authors and champions of bills of total and immediate abolition in the state of Oklahoma.

q: What does the article ask supporters to do beyond celebrating?
a: It calls readers to pray for both senators and to be ready to actively support them when abolition bills are filed and brought to the floor.
t: Jake Merrick Elected to Oklahoma State Senate!
qt: We look forward to supporting Senator-elect Merrick as he joins Senator Hamilton in the Oklahoma Legislature.

q: What event prompted Free the States to publicly call on Senator Lankford to revoke his endorsement?
a: On May 24, 2020, a video of U.S. Senator James Lankford endorsing State Senator Larry Boggs was posted to Boggs' campaign Facebook page, in which Lankford described Boggs as a principled conservative warrior. Free the States published this article five days later arguing the endorsement is incompatible with Boggs' actual legislative record on abortion.
t: James Lankford Should Revoke His Endorsement of Larry Boggs
qt: On May 24th, a video featuring U.S. Senator James Lankford endorsing Oklahoma State Senator Larry Boggs was posted to Boggs’ campaign Facebook page.

q: How did Lankford characterize Boggs in the endorsement video?
a: Lankford praised Boggs as a man of integrity and wisdom, an old-school freedom-fighter, and a consummate warrior who has spent his life defending the Constitution and biblical principles, including protecting life from preborn to natural death.
t: James Lankford Should Revoke His Endorsement of Larry Boggs
qt: “He’s a consummate warrior who fought his whole life to defend the Constitution and Biblical principles.”

q: What specific legislative action did Boggs take that Free the States says contradicts Lankford's praise?
a: On March 11, 2020, Boggs abstained from the vote to place the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act on General Order, effectively withholding the support needed to advance a bill that would have ended abortion in the state.
t: James Lankford Should Revoke His Endorsement of Larry Boggs
qt: Boggs chose not to support that bill, abstaining from the vote to place the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act on General Order on March 11.

q: How has Boggs publicly characterized abolitionist organizations?
a: Boggs has labeled abolitionist organizations as 'wolves in sheep's clothing' on Facebook and his campaign has organized events specifically aimed at criticizing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, despite repeated requests from constituents who support abolition.
t: James Lankford Should Revoke His Endorsement of Larry Boggs
qt: He has called abolitionist organizations “wolves in sheep’s clothing” on Facebook.

q: Why does the article argue that 'pro-life' submission to the Supreme Court is unprincipled?
a: The article contends that politicians like Boggs treat God's word and the Constitution as binding only when the Supreme Court permits, which inverts the meaning of standing on biblical principles—if the Court demands sin (allowing abortion), they comply rather than resist.
t: James Lankford Should Revoke His Endorsement of Larry Boggs
qt: If the supreme court tells the State of Oklahoma that we have to sin by allowing the mass murder of preborn children, then sin we must, according to Boggs and many other pro-life politicians.

q: How does the article contrast Warren Hamilton with Larry Boggs?
a: Boggs is described as accepting submission to Roe v. Wade and the regulatory pro-life model, while Hamilton has pledged to support the immediate abolition of abortion and to reject Roe v. Wade as an unconstitutional opinion that should not be obeyed.
t: James Lankford Should Revoke His Endorsement of Larry Boggs
qt: In Hamilton, we’re getting someone who will treat the issue of abortion with the urgency and gravity that it rightly demands; someone who **supports rejecting *Roe v. Wade* as the evil, unconstitutional opinion that it is and the abolition of abortion.**

q: What ultimate moral consequence does the article warn Lankford about if he refuses to revoke the endorsement?
a: Beyond predicting political loss in Oklahoma, the article warns that Lankford will be culpable as a delayer of abolition and will have to give an account before God for endorsing a politician who blocks legislation to end abortion.
t: James Lankford Should Revoke His Endorsement of Larry Boggs
qt: More importantly, if he does not change course, he will have to answer to the Supreme Judge for this endorsement.

q: What concrete action does the article direct readers to take in response?
a: Readers are given Senator Lankford's office phone number and a link to his contact form so they can directly pressure him to withdraw the endorsement.
t: James Lankford Should Revoke His Endorsement of Larry Boggs
qt: U.S. Senator James Lankford’s contact info:

q: What was the immediate political situation that prompted this article?
a: Hundreds of abolitionists had just packed a marathon Capitol hearing for TX HB896, and only days later Rep. Jeff Leach announced he would refuse to bring the bill to a vote, effectively killing it for the two-year session.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: The culture of death was assailed on Monday as 446 abolitionists showed up to the Texas State capitol to support the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act (TX HB896), a bill which would completely abolish abortion in the state. 330 of them testified in favor of the bill, causing the hearing to last until nearly 3 a.m.

q: What reason did Jeff Leach give for blocking TX HB896?
a: Leach, the Judiciary and Civil Jurisprudence Committee Chair, publicly said the bill went too far because it would expose women who get abortions to criminal liability.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: Leach lambasted the bill because it criminalizes abortion by “subject[ing] women who undergo abortions to criminal liability.”

q: Why does Silberman say a bill that punishes only the abortionist is functionally pointless?
a: Because if mothers and fathers have automatic immunity, self-managed (do-it-yourself) abortion remains legal, and the abortion industry is already shifting in that direction, so the law would simply accelerate the shift instead of stopping abortion.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: First, abortion isn’t actually illegal if parents who murder their sons and daughters have automatic immunity. If only the abortionist faces justice, do-it-yourself abortion is legal. And with the abortion industry [moving toward DIY abortion anyway](https://reproaction.org/campaign/self-managed-abortion/), a bill providing legal penalty only for the abortionist accomplishes nothing but expedite the evolution of the abortion industry.

q: How does Jason Storms's personal testimony support the deterrence argument?
a: Storms told the committee that he had been an accomplice to his own child's abortion in the 1990s and that he and his girlfriend would never have gone through with it had they known they could face homicide charges; the law itself would have saved that child.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: “Here’s a fact: my girlfriend and I, if we knew we would’ve been facing homicide charges, would never have aborted that child. That child would be alive today. I’d have a 22-year-old little child that I could celebrate life with right now that’s not here. The law is a deterrent to crime. We shouldn’t think of this only as a matter of putting a woman on the stand. We should think of this as a great deterrent. Men and women would not think of doing this if we stood firm on the law and provided equal protection for these children.”

q: What parental-duty principle does Storms invoke to justify holding parents legally accountable?
a: He points out that Texas already prosecutes parents for neglect, abuse, and homicide of their born children because parents have a duty to protect their children, and that duty begins at conception, not birth.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: “Mothers and fathers – parents – right now in Texas can be charged with parental neglect, parental abuse, and even parental homicide when we see the tragedy of parents taking the lives of their own children. It’s because mothers and fathers have a duty to love and protect their children. That responsibility doesn’t start when they’re born, but it starts when they’re conceived.”

q: How does Silberman tie pre-Roe abortion statutes to the Roe v. Wade decision itself?
a: He argues that Texas's old anti-abortion law, which exempted the mother from prosecution, gave Justice Harry Blackmun the opening to claim pro-lifers themselves did not really treat the unborn as full persons — an inconsistency Blackmun spelled out in footnote 54 of Roe.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: Third, abortion bans which exempted parents from legal penalty are what led to the legalization of abortion in the first place. Dallas District Attorney Henry Wade, who was representing the State of Texas in Roe v Wade, argued that the equal protection clause of the 14th amendment guaranteed preborn human beings equal protection of the law against murder. But the inconsistency of Texas’ anti-abortion laws at the time which didn’t legally penalize mothers for getting abortions provided Justice Harry Blackmun an avenue by which to claim that even pro-life people didn’t really believe abortion was murder. He details this inconsistency in footnote 54 of the Roe v Wade decision.

q: What exactly did Blackmun say in footnote 54 that Silberman quotes?
a: Blackmun pointed out that Texas treated the woman as neither principal nor accomplice and assigned a far lower penalty for criminal abortion than for murder, which he argued was incoherent if the fetus were truly a person under the Fourteenth Amendment.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: “There are other inconsistencies between Fourteenth Amendment status and the typical abortion statute. It has already been pointed out, n. 49, supra, that in Texas the woman is not a principal or an accomplice with respect to an abortion upon her. If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?”

q: How does the article respond to the worry that mothers seeking abortions are themselves victims of difficult circumstances?
a: Silberman concedes that hard circumstances are real and may justify leniency in individual cases, but compares the logic to other crimes — difficult situations don't grant blanket immunity, they're factors for prosecutors, judges, and juries to weigh case by case.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: An armed robber might go hungry without the $150 in the till at the corner store. A man might be driven to acts of violence by the physical abuse he suffered as a child. A drunk driver who kills someone might have been drinking because her husband just left her. Such circumstances may warrant a more lenient sentence in some cases, but they do not remove the need for justice to be served.

q: Why does the article frame Leach's decision as a literal death sentence for 110,000 babies?
a: Because the Texas legislature only meets every two years, killing the bill in 2019 means that roughly 110,000 unborn children will be aborted in Texas before another abolition bill can even be considered.
t: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
qt: The Texas legislature meets only every two years, meaning the killing of the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act is a literal and undeniable death sentence for 110,000 preborn human beings. They will die and be cast into landfills, incineration chambers, and down garbage disposals so that Jeff Leach and his allies among pro-life lobbyists and state Republican leaders can virtue signal that they’re not like those mean abolitionists who actually think preborn human beings should get equal justice and protection.

q: Why does Free the States classify Jerry Donathan as a pro-abortion candidate rather than a moderate?
a: Because Donathan publicly endorses rape, incest, health, and life-of-the-mother exceptions, which under the legal framework of Roe and Doe v. Bolton functionally permit all abortions. Sam Riley argues this position is identical to that of prominent national Democrats and amounts to defending the pro-abortion status quo, regardless of how Donathan frames it.
t: Jerry Donathan is the Pro-Abortion Candidate in SD7
qt: Jerry Donathan thinks he can trick the voters of Senate District 7 into thinking he’s some sort of pro-life with exceptions candidate who deserves the support of people who care about vulnerable preborn children, but his position is no different than Diane Feinstein, Alexandra Ocasio Cortez, or Joe Biden – namely that *Roe* (ie. legal murder) is the law and we must allow the poisoning, dismemberment, and decapitation or preborn humans in our state.

q: What specific exceptions did Donathan say he supports in his campaign video?
a: Donathan named four exceptions: rape, incest, the health of the woman, and the life of the woman. The article emphasizes that he treated 'health' and 'life' as separate categories, which the author considers legally and morally significant.
t: Jerry Donathan is the Pro-Abortion Candidate in SD7
qt: Donathan said, “I feel that in the cases of rape, incest, *health*, and *life* of the woman should be considered as exceptions.”

q: Why does the article insist that 'life of the mother' is not a legitimate medical justification for abortion?
a: Riley contends that medical professionals—pointing to the Dublin Declaration—have rebutted the notion that intentionally killing a preborn child is ever required to save a mother's life. He argues alternative treatments consistent with the Hippocratic oath always exist.
t: Jerry Donathan is the Pro-Abortion Candidate in SD7
qt: There is absolutely no warrant to the claim that intentionally murdering a baby is a necessary treatment for saving women’s lives, even when doctors prescribe it. There are always other treatments that are in line with the Hippocratic oath.

q: How does Doe v. Bolton's definition of 'health' function as a loophole legalizing abortion on demand?
a: The Supreme Court defined 'health' so expansively—covering physical, emotional, psychological, familial, and age-related factors—that virtually any pregnancy qualifies. Riley argues this is how abortion was effectively legalized in all 50 states, since pregnancy hormones alone create emotional effects that satisfy the standard.
t: Jerry Donathan is the Pro-Abortion Candidate in SD7
qt: In the accompanying case of [Doe v. Bolton](https://caselaw.findlaw.com/us-supreme-court/410/179.html), which was decided the same day as Roe, the court defined health of the mother as, “all factors – physical, emotional, psychological, familial, and the woman’s age-relevant to the wellbeing of the patient. All these factors may relate to health.” In case you can’t see the sinister nature of this definition, what this means is that if a woman is emotionally affected by a pregnancy, which all women are because of the associated hormones, she can get an abortion.

q: On what moral grounds does the article reject the rape and incest exception?
a: Riley invokes the principle that children should not be punished for their fathers' crimes, calling it unjust to impose 'capital punishment' on a preborn child because of how he was conceived. The exception alone, he argues, is sufficient reason to reject Donathan.
t: Jerry Donathan is the Pro-Abortion Candidate in SD7
qt: If it was only for his position that you can stab a baby to death because his father is a rapist it would be more than enough reason to never vote for him. A child should never be given a capital punishment for the sins of his father.

q: What does the article frame as the actual choice facing SD7 voters?
a: Riley presents a binary: voting Donathan perpetuates legalized 'child sacrifice' in Oklahoma, while voting Warren Hamilton supports a 'principled conservative and anti-abortion candidate' who would work to override the Supreme Court and end abortion in the state.
t: Jerry Donathan is the Pro-Abortion Candidate in SD7
qt: The choice is clear: If you want child sacrifice to remain legal in Oklahoma, vote for Jerry Donathan. If you want equal justice and protection to be upheld for all image bearers of God in Oklahoma, vote for Warren Hamilton, a principled conservative and anti-abortion candidate who will work to check the abuses perpetrated by the supreme court and bring an end to the slaughter of preborn children in the state of Oklahoma.

q: How does the article characterize the rhetorical strategy Donathan used to win pro-life votes?
a: Riley accuses Donathan of using vague language to appear moderate—claiming opposition to abortion while politically supporting it—in order to peel off voters who oppose abortion but don't understand how Roe was constructed.
t: Jerry Donathan is the Pro-Abortion Candidate in SD7
qt: In an attempt to appear moderate on abortion and entice voters who are against it, he stated in vague language that he was essentially pro-abortion politically while trying to maintain that he was against it.

q: When does Haggard say the Incarnation actually occurred?
a: He insists the Incarnation took place at Christ's conception in Mary's womb, not at His birth in Bethlehem. The manger is the public unveiling, but the union of divinity and humanity happened nine months earlier at the zygote stage.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: It is important to remember that the Incarnation occurred not at Christ’s birth, but at His conception in Mary’s womb.

q: Why does the timing of the Incarnation matter for the abortion debate?
a: Because the moment God joined Himself to humanity is the moment that fixes when human life becomes sacred and legally protectable. If God treated a zygote as fully human in the person of Christ, then every zygote must be treated the same way in law.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: Important, because it defines very clearly the point at which human beings become human beings (when they are actually made in God’s image), the point at which human life becomes sacred, and the point at which human life should be protected in law.

q: How does Haggard describe Christ's transition from heaven to the womb?
a: He paints it as an instantaneous move from the throne of God to embryonic existence inside Mary, with no intermediate stage of sub-human or pre-human development. The God-Man existed as a single-celled embryo.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: One moment He was at the right hand of God the Father as the Second Person of the Trinity; the next He was in the womb of the Virgin as the God-Man in embryonic form.

q: What does Haggard say is the only acceptable legal response for a God-fearing person?
a: Total abolition of abortion, with no settling for restrictions or regulations. Anything less, he argues, fails to honor the dignity Christ conferred on humanity at conception.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: If He thus dignified humanity at this earliest possible stage of human development, so must every God-fearing person – BY ABOLISHING ABORTION, not by merely *restricting* it or *regulating* it.

q: Why does Haggard reject regulation-style pro-life laws?
a: Because regulating abortion legally permits the killing of some preborn children while protecting others, which functionally denies that all human life is sacred. The intent of the lawmaker is irrelevant; the effect of the law is what condemns it.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: For to merely *restrict* or *regulate* abortion is to say (by mere force of inescapable effect, regardless of intent) that SOME preborn humans may legally be put to death.

q: What provocative test case does Haggard use against heartbeat-style bills?
a: He asks whether a law would have permitted the abortion of Jesus Himself before Jesus had a detectable heartbeat. If such a law would have been monstrous when applied to Christ, the same law is monstrous when applied to anyone else.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: It is to say that it would have been acceptable – lamentable, surely, but technically legal nonetheless – for Jesus to have been aborted under certain circumstances; for example, before He had a detectable heartbeat.

q: Which Old Testament texts does Haggard tie to the Incarnation argument?
a: He invokes Genesis 9:6's prohibition against murder as the rule that takes effect at conception, and he labels regulation-style abortion laws as the "iniquitous decrees" condemned in Isaiah 10:1-2.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: Laws that merely restrict or regulate abortion are “iniquitous decrees” (Isa 10:1-2).

q: What does Haggard claim Christians have effectively done for the last fifty years?
a: By tolerating laws that regulate rather than abolish abortion since Roe v. Wade, the church has, in practical effect, repudiated the Incarnation itself. Acceptance of partial protection is treated as a denial of what Christmas means.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: To fail to do this (as we have failed to do ever since Roe v Wade almost 50 years ago) is to effectively repudiate Christ’s incarnation.

q: How does Haggard tie the Incarnation to the call to abolition?
a: He frames the Incarnation as a divine "clarion call"—God's own announcement that the killing of preborn humans must end. To honor Christmas is to push for immediate state-by-state abolition rather than continued regulation.
t: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
qt: Christ’s incarnation is the death knell of abortion because it is the clarion call of abolition.

q: What was the margin of John Jacob's victory in Indiana House District 93, and who did he defeat in the general election?
a: Jacob won with more than 65 percent of the vote against Democrat Angela Elliot, after first defeating incumbent Rep. Dollyne Sherman in the Republican primary.
t: John Jacob Wins!
qt: Abolitionist John Jacob has been elected to the Indiana State house with more than 65 percent of the vote, defeating Democrat Angela Elliot.

q: How does this race illustrate the divide between the pro-life establishment and the abolitionist movement?
a: Sherman, the incumbent Jacob unseated in the primary, carried the endorsement of Indiana Right to Life (the state's pro-life incrementalist organization), while Jacob was backed by abolitionist groups Abolish Abortion Indiana and Hoosiers for Life that demand total and immediate abolition.
t: John Jacob Wins!
qt: Sherman was endorsed by the state’s pro-life incrementalist organization, Indiana Right to Life, while Jacob was endorsed by Abolish Abortion Indiana and Hoosiers for Life, organizations which advocate the total and immediate abolition of abortion.

q: Why does Free the States consider Jacob's election (alongside Warren Hamilton's) historically significant for abolitionism?
a: Earlier abolitionist legislators, beginning with Oklahoma's Joseph Silk in 2016, had converted to abolitionism after taking office; Jacob and Hamilton are the first to run as abolitionists against incumbent anti-abolitionists and win their seats outright.
t: John Jacob Wins!
qt: While a number of state legislators have converted to abolitionism in recent years beginning with Oklahoma State Senator Joseph Silk in 2016, Jacob and [Oklahoma State Senator-elect Warren Hamilton](https://freethestates.org/2020/11/warren-hamilton-wins/index.html) are the first abolitionists to challenge incumbent anti-abolitionists and enter the legislature as abolitionists.

q: What concrete legislative agenda is Jacob expected to push when he arrives at the Indiana State House?
a: His top priority is criminalizing abortion by passing the Protection at Conception Act, the immediate-abolition bill that Rep. Curt Nisly has introduced every year since 2017 and which Jacob will now help advance.
t: John Jacob Wins!
qt: John has repeatedly stated that his number one priority is to criminalize abortion by working to pass the Protection at Conception Act.

q: How did Derin Stidd of Abolish Abortion Indiana characterize the kind of campaign Jacob ran?
a: Stidd credits a disciplined, voter-focused campaign for letting Jacob defeat both a pro-life establishment Republican and a pro-abortion Democrat, framing Jacob as a man of Christian principle and integrity.
t: John Jacob Wins!
qt: He was able to unseat a pro-life establishment Republican in the primary, and a pro-abortion Democrat in the general election, because he ran a disciplined campaign that connected with the voters in his district.

q: How does Jacob himself frame the work that lies ahead, and what does he ask of supporters?
a: Jacob treats the win as a beginning rather than an end, casts the fight in spiritual terms drawn from Ephesians, and asks supporters to pray for him as he expects opposition from spiritual as well as political powers.
t: John Jacob Wins!
qt: Please pray for me as I know there will be attacks and I realize that we do not struggle against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places.

q: What political message does Free the States believe Jacob's victory sends to the Republican establishment in Indiana?
a: It signals that the abolitionist movement now has the electoral muscle to remove sitting officeholders who refuse to legislate the immediate criminalization of abortion.
t: John Jacob Wins!
qt: Jacob’s victory is a signal to the pro-life Republican establishment in Indiana that the Abolitionist Movement is now a political force in the state with the ability to remove people from office who will not legislate justly.

q: Why does Silberman think Van Maren's podcast attack on Russell Hunter may have been timed strategically?
a: Silberman speculates the assault on Hunter's character coincides with Free the States exposing the pro-life myth of an incrementalist Wilberforce, just as Hunter is preparing a three-part video series to bust that myth. Van Maren is a primary historian for the Pro-Life Movement and had been silent on abolitionism for six years before swinging at Hunter precisely as that historiographical project nears release.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: Van Maren is one of the primary historians for the Pro-Life Movement. It's been near complete radio silence on abolitionism from Van Maren for six years, and now he comes back swinging the sledgehammer against Russell in particular just as Russell is busting the Pro-Life Establishment's favorite historical myth.

q: How do leading historians of American slavery abolitionism define 'abolitionist'?
a: From the 1830s onward, scholars treat 'abolitionist' and 'immediatist' as effectively synonymous in the American context. Pulitzer Prize-winning historian James McPherson defines an abolitionist as one who agitated before the Civil War for the immediate, unconditional, and total abolition of slavery, and History.com gives essentially the same definition.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: Pulitzer prize-winning civil war historian James McPherson defines abolitionist "as one who before the Civil War had agitated for the immediate, unconditional, and total abolition of slavery in the United States."

q: Do abortion abolitionists deny that gradual abolitionists existed in British history?
a: No. Silberman explicitly concedes that England had a substantial group of gradualists and that Wilberforce contrasted himself as an 'immediate abolitionist' against 'gradual abolitionists' like Henry Dundas and William Dolben. Modern abolitionists work to maintain the immediatist/incrementalist linguistic distinction not by denying that history but to keep two ideologies separate.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: Back then, the linguistic distinction among British anti-slavery factions was between the immediate abolitionists of the slave trade (ie. Wilberforce and Thomas Clarkson) and the gradual abolitionists (ie. Henry Dundas and William Dolben).

q: Are abolitionists being inconsistent when they cite Wilberforce historically and also appeal to Scripture?
a: No. Silberman argues these are two complementary claims, not a bait-and-switch: Wilberforce was historically an immediatist whose writings instruct us, AND God's word is the ultimate standard that prohibits regulating murder. Holding both arguments simultaneously is not bad faith; it is layered argumentation.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: There is nothing dishonest about making both of these arguments simultaneously, yet Brahm and Van Maren accuse abolitionists of being dishonest, bad faith actors for doing so.

q: Is immediatism the same thing as expecting abortion to end overnight?
a: No. Silberman calls this a strawman. Immediatism concerns what the demand should be, not how long the fight will take. Even Garrison wrote in The Liberator that immediate abolition urged earnestly will 'alas! be gradual abolition in the end' through what Free the States calls the 'steel ball effect' on culture and lawmakers.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: The immediatism-incrementalism debate does not revolve around how long the battle takes (though the immediatist strategy will certainly achieve the desired end more quickly). The debate revolves around what our demand should be.

q: Could abortion realistically be abolished quickly in any U.S. state?
a: Silberman argues yes in states like Oklahoma and Missouri, where pro-lifers dominate the population, legislature, and statewide offices. The barrier is not impossibility but the 'delusive scheme of gradualism in submission to the supreme court' gripping Christian politicians who could otherwise suspend the rules and pass an abolition bill.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: If the delusive scheme of gradualism in submission to the supreme court lost its grip on the minds of Oklahoma Christians, abortion could be abolished before the legislative session is over.

q: How does Silberman defend Russell Hunter's imprecatory prayer video?
a: The video responded to Christian leaders branding the entire Abolitionist Movement 'terrorists' and 'jihadis' over the pre-conversion actions of two men who later became Christians, pacifists, and abolitionists. Russell asked God to vindicate whichever side was wrong. Silberman says wielding pre-regeneration sins against born-again believers denies the power of the gospel.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: People who would use their actions before they were born again to slander the whole Abolitionist Movement as terrorists were acting wickedly and they were denying the power of the gospel to renew men's souls and minds.

q: Wouldn't a court strip out the abolition portion of an abolition bill while leaving the repeal of pro-life regulations standing?
a: Abolition bills include explicit inseverability clauses that bind the courts. Silberman cannot find a single Oklahoma case of a court ignoring such a clause. Even if a court tried, the same legislative posture that passed an abolition bill would have already shifted power back from the courts and would disregard the ruling.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: "The provisions, words, phrases and clauses of this act are declared to be inseverable."

q: How does Free the States respond when pro-life lobbyists keep generating new excuses to oppose abolition bills?
a: They strip the excuse from the bill year after year without watering it down. In 2019 it was nullification; they showed abolitionists were the original nullifiers. In 2020 it was secession; they added a clause disclaiming withdrawal from the Union. In 2021 it became severability; they will adjust the language and the goalposts will move again.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: They jump from excuse-to-excuse every year as a way of delaying the abolition of abortion. That'll be gone next year, and the pro-life lobbyists and politicians will be touting their new reason why we can't abolish abortion.

q: Is it true that pro-lifers like Van Maren are helping keep abortion legal?
a: Silberman says yes, and points to Wilberforce's own warning to gradual abolitionists who could not foresee the practical effect of their opposition. By echoing the lobbyist excuses against abolition bills in eight states, Van Maren is functionally aiding the politicians blocking abolition.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: Van Maren is helping keep abortion legal in the eight states an abolition bill has been put forward by parroting the excuses of the very people keeping abortion legal.

q: Why does Silberman call the 'anarchy' charge against state-level abolition bills idolatrous?
a: Because it presupposes that SCOTUS has unconditional authority — there is no immorality the Court could commit that would justify defying it. That posture treats the Court as supreme over God and the Constitution. Silberman frames upholding the right to life as the most basic duty of a state official acting as God's deacon of justice.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: Their view presupposes that there is no authority above the supreme court. SCOTUS' power is unconditional. The justices are entirely unconstrained by any law of God or man.

q: What does the 'flag bill' actually demonstrate about Wilberforce's strategy?
