a: It was not an incremental compromise but a wartime measure designed to disarm the slave lobby's economic and geopolitical arguments — analogous to a modern state-sovereignty bill like Arkansas SB298 that strips federal-overreach excuses without implying that murdering some children is acceptable. Such bills speed abolition rather than delay it.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: The flag bill was not an incremental bill in the sense that pro-life incrementalists use that word. It was a war measure put forward with the intention of taking away economic and geopolitical arguments that the slave lobby was making against abolition.

q: What did Wilberforce himself say about incrementalists like the supporters of the Dolben Act?
a: In A Letter on the Abolition of the Slave Trade, Wilberforce wrote that gradualists who substituted gradual for immediate abolition 'dashed the cup of happiness from the lips of the wretched African' and were the most efficient supporters of the slave trade. He treats incremental half-measures with at least as much contempt as modern abortion abolitionists do.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: [T]he gradual Abolitionists have been, in fact, the only real stay of that system of wickedness and cruelty which we wish to abolish; though that assertion is unquestionably true; but it is trying beyond expression that they should be the real maintainers of the Slave Trade

q: Did Wilberforce remain a supporter of the colonization scheme that deported freed slaves to Africa?
a: No. Wilberforce publicly repented after deliberation among British abolitionists informed by Garrison. James Cropper announced to the gathering that Wilberforce 'now deeply regrets that he was ever led to say anything in approbation of the Colonization Society.' Silberman says defining a man by views he renounced is bad historiography.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: "It is with very great pleasure that I can add the name of William Wilberforce as having changed his opinion. He now deeply regrets that he was ever led to say anything in approbation of the Colonization Society."

q: What was Clarkson's actual disagreement with Garrison, according to Silberman?
a: Not immediatism — both held it firmly — but Sabbatarianism. Clarkson worried Garrison's rejection of strict Sabbath observance would erode Christian moral principle, while Garrison saw Sunday-only piety as enabling weekday cruelty. Van Maren's own writing on Clarkson's letter at CatholicCitizens.com nowhere claims the dispute was about immediatism.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: In reality, Clarkson's concerns with Garrison had nothing to do with immediatism. (Clarkson, like Wilberforce, detailed his fierce opposition to incremental legislation like the Dolben Act in his writings…) Clarkson's concerns were about Garrison's rejection of Sabbatarianism.

q: Why does Silberman insist that even implicitly unjust laws teach injustice?
a: Because law is inherently a tutor. A 20-week ban grounded in pain capability teaches that pain capability gives humans value; a heartbeat bill produces legislators who claim 'life begins at a heartbeat'; rape exceptions tell rape-conceived people their lives are less valuable. Implicit allowances dehumanize preborn children just as explicit ones do.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: Laws teach. It is inherent to their nature. Van Maren thinks that by simply implying the legality of killing certain babies rather than explicitly stating it, these laws aren't teaching injustice to the culture. But they are.

q: What did Van Maren and Brahm conspicuously fail to address?
a: A scriptural defense of pro-life incrementalism or a scriptural critique of abolitionism. Van Maren simply said he hadn't 'quite figured out their theology yet' and moved on, even though he has clearly studied the movement at length. Silberman finds this telling given that Free the States has published its biblical case publicly.
t: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
qt: It is notable that Van Maren and Brahm did not attempt to make a scriptural defense of pro-life incrementalism or a scriptural critique of abolitionism.

q: Why does Silberman argue that the makeup of the Supreme Court disproves the pro-life strategy of relying on Republican judicial appointments?
a: Because every decision mandating legal abortion has come from courts dominated by Republican appointees, including the 1992 Casey court that consisted of eight Republican appointees and a pro-life Democrat dissenter from Roe, yet still upheld legal abortion in full.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: These decisions to mandate that the lethal brutalization of preborn humans be allowed in every state have come exclusively from courts made up of a majority of Republican appointees.

q: What does the June v. Russo lineup reveal about the willingness of current justices besides Thomas to overturn Roe?
a: Although Roberts, Alito, Gorsuch, and Kavanaugh have endorsed regulations within the Roe/Casey framework, none have joined Thomas's separate calls to overturn Roe, and Silberman believes hoping for five such votes any time soon is delusional.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: Optimism for five such votes any time soon is likely a delusion.

q: How does Silberman frame the upside of the June v. Russo loss for the abolitionist cause?
a: He sees it as potentially the best thing that could happen to the cause of equal protection for the preborn if it finally pushes pro-lifers to abandon judicial supremacy and incrementalism.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: The court's *June v. Russo* opinion has the potential to be the best thing that ever happened to the cause of equal justice and protection for preborn humans if it shakes pro-lifers free from judicial supremacy and incrementalism.

q: What strategic alternative does Josh Craddock point to in the wake of the June v. Russo decision?
a: Craddock points to bills like the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, a measure of total and immediate abolition, and rebukes National Right to Life for opposing it.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: Craddock then points to bills like the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (a bill of total and immediate abolition) as the solution and rebukes National Right to Life for their opposition to it.

q: Why does Silberman insist that submitting to Roe is sinful, not merely unwise?
a: Because Roe decrees the mass killing of preborn humans, and no human authority can lawfully bind the conscience to such an order; obedience to it constitutes participation in murder.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: Such a decree can never, in the true sense, be legally binding, and submission to such a decree is a sin.

q: How does Silberman use the Fugitive Slave Act as a rhetorical test for pro-life lawyers?
a: He asks whether it was illegal or immoral for northern states to nullify the Fugitive Slave Act; the lawyers dodge the question because they know the underground railroad and the state nullifications protecting it were righteous.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: Pro-life judicial supremacists will often not give an answer because they know the underground railroad was a righteous cause and that state-level nullification to defend the underground railroad from an evil federal law was a righteous cause.

q: What constitutional argument does Silberman make for state-level protection of preborn persons?
a: He argues state magistrates are bound by the 14th Amendment's equal-protection guarantee, and that creating a category of human non-persons defeats the entire purpose of the amendment.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: If "person" as used in the 14th amendment does not include all human beings, it provides no guarantee to anyone.

q: What lexical evidence does Craddock cite to support reading "person" in the 14th Amendment as inclusive of preborn humans?
a: He shows that 19th-century dictionaries, including Webster's 1864 edition, defined "person" as a living human being without referencing birth, making the terms "person," "human being," and "man" largely interchangeable.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: No dictionary of the era referenced birth or the status of being born in its definition of 'person,' 'man,' or 'human being.'

q: What consequence does Silberman say should follow for officials who enforce abortion laws while sworn to the Constitution?
a: He calls them hypocrites who should be voted out or impeached for moral turpitude and assault on the Constitution.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: Anyone who does so should be voted out or impeached for moral turpitude and assault on the constitution.

q: How does Silberman invoke William Lloyd Garrison to discredit pro-life incrementalism?
a: He cites Garrison's 1833 Declaration of Sentiments, which condemned any scheme that substituted gradualism for the immediate and total abolition of slavery, as the model for rejecting pro-life compromise.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: We regard as delusive, cruel, and dangerous, any scheme of expatriation which pretends to aid, either directly or indirectly, in the emancipation of the slaves, or to be a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of slavery.

q: What concrete Texas case does Silberman use to illustrate how incrementalism functionally protects abortion?
a: He recounts how Rep. Jeff Leach buried the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act in committee and then filed the largely empty Born-Alive Infant Protection Act, earning awards and pro-life accolades while abortion remained legal in Texas.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: That is how abortion was kept legal in Texas.

q: Why does Silberman argue that incremental compromise bills actively delay abolition?
a: Because they let politicians appease moral outrage with cosmetic wins, fundraise off them, and quiet the public demand that would otherwise force real abolition.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: It's a cozy arrangement between pro-life politicians and pro-life lobbyists where they reward each other for preventing abolition and then use their "victories" to fundraise and solicit votes.

q: How does Elizabeth Heyrick characterize the moral effect of gradualism on the conscience of a society?
a: Heyrick argues that the contemplation of gradual emancipation breeds gradual indifference to emancipation itself, allowing even the wise and good to tolerate atrocity by habit and familiarity.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: He knew very well, that the contemplation of a gradual emancipation, would beget a gradual indifference to emancipation itself.

q: What is Silberman's posture toward pro-life leaders who continue to oppose immediate abolition bills?
a: He calls on those who have stayed silent to publicly and repeatedly rebuke National Right to Life and other pro-life groups fighting state abolition bills until pro-life submission to Roe ends.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: With Craddock and the abolitionists, deliver a well-deserved rebuke to those pro-life leaders opposing the immediate abolition of abortion.

q: What does Silberman believe should have happened immediately after Roe was decided?
a: He says Roe should have been defied in every state within weeks of January 22, 1973, and that delayed obedience to God is preferable to continued submission that prolongs the killing of preborn children.
t: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
qt: *Roe* should have been defied in every state within weeks of January 22, 1973.

q: What did the Kay County GOP actually do at their March 2020 convention regarding SB13?
a: At their county convention, the 21 Republican delegates unanimously voted in favor of a resolution directing their state-level officeholders, Sen. Bill Coleman and Reps. John Pfeifer and Ken Luttrell, to support the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (SB13), with Pfeifer and Luttrell additionally being asked to author a House companion bill.
t: Kay County GOP Directs Sen. Bill Coleman to Support Senate Bill 13
qt: At their convention last Saturday, the Kay County (OK) GOP voted in favor of a resolution directing State Senator Bill Coleman and State Representatives John Pfeifer and Ken Luttrell to support the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (SB13). Tonkawa activist Daniel Watts introduced and spoke in favor of the resolution and the vote of the 21 Kay County Republican delegates was unanimous.

q: How does the resolution argue that the Oklahoma Republican Party's own platform already commits the party to advancing abolition?
a: The resolution cites the Addendum on page 5 of the state GOP platform, which explicitly resolves that abolition of abortion be brought to a full floor vote in both Oklahoma legislative chambers, framing SB13 support as fulfillment of an existing party commitment rather than a novel demand.
t: Kay County GOP Directs Sen. Bill Coleman to Support Senate Bill 13
qt: WHEREAS, The Oklahoma Republican Party Platform directs on the Addendum of page 5 “Be it resolved that the abolition of abortion be brought to a full floor vote in both Oklahoma Legislative Chambers.”

q: On what legal and constitutional grounds does the resolution claim Oklahoma may abolish abortion?
a: The resolution invokes existing Oklahoma criminal statutes that already classify abortion as a crime and the Tenth Amendment's reservation of non-delegated powers to the states, arguing the state has both pre-existing law and constitutional authority to enforce the abolition of abortion.
t: Kay County GOP Directs Sen. Bill Coleman to Support Senate Bill 13
qt:
WHEREAS. Abortion is considered a crime under Oklahoma Statutes 21-861 and 21- 862; and
>
> WHEREAS, The 10th Amendment states that “(t)he powers not delegated to the United States by the Constitution, nor prohibited by it to the States respectively, or to the people.”

q: Why does the article frame this as part of a wider county-level movement rather than an isolated event?
a: Silberman situates Kay County as the third Oklahoma county GOP to pass such a resolution, following Cimarron and Woodward Counties, signaling a replicable, county-by-county pressure campaign that organizers in other counties can model.
t: Kay County GOP Directs Sen. Bill Coleman to Support Senate Bill 13
qt: Kay County is the third county to pass such a resolution, following [Cimarron](https://kennybobtapp.wordpress.com/2020/03/07/cimarron-county-republican-party-calls-upon-senator-casey-murdock-representative-kenton-patzkowsky-to-support-sb-13/) and [Woodward](https://kennybobtapp.wordpress.com/2020/02/25/woodward-county-republicans-direct-sen-murdock-reps-sanders-and-newton-to-support-sb-13-ok-abolition-of-abortion-act/) Counties.

q: What immediate procedural moment is the article trying to mobilize readers around?
a: The piece is timed to a Wednesday motion in the Oklahoma Senate to bring SB13 to the floor, which would force a recorded vote on whether to hear the bill or table it, putting Sen. Coleman in a position of either ratifying or repudiating his county party's new platform.
t: Kay County GOP Directs Sen. Bill Coleman to Support Senate Bill 13
qt: On Wednesday, Senator Coleman will have an opportunity to agree with or reject the new Kay County Republican platform when a motion to bring SB13 to the Senate floor is going to be filed. There will be a vote on whether to hear SB13 or to table it.

q: What concrete action is being asked of Oklahoma constituents, and how specific is the call?
a: The article calls on Oklahomans, especially residents of Kay County and Senate District 10, to email and phone Sen. Coleman to demand both that SB13 be brought to a floor vote and that he cosponsor the bill, and it provides his direct email and Capitol phone number to remove friction.
t: Kay County GOP Directs Sen. Bill Coleman to Support Senate Bill 13
qt: If you live in Oklahoma, and especially if you live in Kay County or Senate District 10, email and call Senator Coleman to tell him to support SB13 being brought to the floor and to cosponsor the bill as the resolution lays out.

q: What action did Governor Stitt actually take, and how did he frame it?
a: Stitt announced a ban on state-funded travel by Oklahoma public officials to California, framing it as a tit-for-tat response to California's existing travel ban against Oklahoma and as a defense of Oklahoma's pro-life position.
t: Kevin Stitt Makes Pro-Life Gesture With California Travel Ban
qt: Governor Kevin Stitt announced on Thursday that there will be no State-funded travel by Oklahoma public officials to California because of California's travel ban against Oklahoma and because of California's pro-choice policies.

q: How did Stitt himself describe his motivation for the travel ban?
a: Stitt cast the decision as returning California's gesture, declaring he was proud to govern a state that fights for the unborn as 'the most vulnerable among us.'
t: Kevin Stitt Makes Pro-Life Gesture With California Travel Ban
qt: If California's elected officials don't want public employees traveling to Oklahoma, I am eager to return the gesture on behalf of Oklahoma's pro-life stance. I am proud to be Governor of a state that fights for the most vulnerable among us, the unborn.

q: Why does Silberman argue that pro-life gestures, even good ones, are insufficient?
a: Because gestures don't change anything on the ground; they only let politicians appear to be doing good while avoiding substantive action that would actually stop abortions.
t: Kevin Stitt Makes Pro-Life Gesture With California Travel Ban
qt: But the problem with gestures is that they don't actually change anything. They're great if you're a politician and you want your constituents to think you're doing something good without doing anything of substance or impact.

q: What concrete actions does Silberman say Stitt is morally and constitutionally obligated to take?
a: Stitt is obligated to abolish abortion outright, shut down the killing centers, and use his executive influence to help pass the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.
t: Kevin Stitt Makes Pro-Life Gesture With California Travel Ban
qt: Governor Stitt is morally and Constitutionally obligated to abolish abortion. He needs to do that. He needs to shut down the killing centers and use his influence to help pass the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.

q: Who does Silberman blame for killing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act in 2019, and how did the pro-life base enable it?
a: He blames pro-life Republican Senator Greg Treat, and argues that the celebration of three compromise pro-life bills as victories gave Treat political cover to kill abolition.
t: Kevin Stitt Makes Pro-Life Gesture With California Travel Ban
qt: The fact that there were still people in Oklahoma who celebrated the three compromise pro-life bills in 2019 is what allowed pro-life Republican Senator Greg Treat to get away with killing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.

q: What does Silberman say it will take for the killing to actually stop in Oklahoma?
a: A critical mass of Oklahomans must refuse to be impressed by gestures and compromises and instead demand the full abolition of abortion.
t: Kevin Stitt Makes Pro-Life Gesture With California Travel Ban
qt: The day when a critical mass of Oklahomans are no longer impressed by gestures and compromises and demand abortion's abolition is the day the killing will stop.

q: Does Silberman think Oklahoma is morally superior to California on abortion?
a: No. He insists that as long as abortion remains legal in Oklahoma, Oklahomans cannot look down on California because they are equally complicit.
t: Kevin Stitt Makes Pro-Life Gesture With California Travel Ban
qt: Until then, we can't look down our noses at California. We're just as bad.

q: What is 'smashmouth incrementalism' and where did the term come from?
a: The term was coined by Doug Wilson in an October 2017 Blog & Mablog post and expanded by Toby Sumpter days later. Speed offers a working definition: aggressive and confrontational changes toward ending abortion pursued through incremental legislative steps.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: The term "smash mouth incrementalism" was coined by Pastor Doug in a blog article called "Smashmouth Incrementalism" where it was not so much defined as described (*Blog & Mablog*, Tuesday October 10, 2017).  A few days later Toby expanded on Doug's blog post with one of his own

q: What was Toby Sumpter's specific claim about King Josiah that triggered this article?
a: On Cross Politic, Sumpter argued that Josiah did not execute the idolatrous priests but more likely removed them from Judah, making him a model 'smashmouth incrementalist' who was still recorded as a godly king and therefore a precedent for modern incremental abortion bills.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: He cited King Josiah's actions from 2 Kings as an example of godly incrementalism. He claimed that Josiah did not execute the priests but rather was more likely to have removed them from Judah. Therefore, reasoned Toby, Josiah was a smashmouth incrementalist and was still recorded as a godly king.

q: How does the Hebrew word shabat in 2 Kings 23:5 actually function, and does it support 'exile' as a translation?
a: Speed shows that shabat in the Hiphil typically means 'to put an end to, to cause to cease,' and is a favorite prophetic verb for God's judgment. While 'remove' is a rare possibility, the immediate context of Josiah's covenantal zeal for the whole Law of Moses makes it an unlikely reading.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: Victor P. Hamilton, the author of the article for *shabat* in TWOT, says that in the Hiphil, "…the meaning is 'to put an end to, to cause to cease'.  Preponderantly, in the Hiphil of *shabat*, God is the subject of the verb.  It is a favorite verb of the prophets to describe God's judgment on His people (Is. 13:11)"

q: What does the Septuagint suggest Josiah did to the idolatrous priests?
a: Speed's pastor, a trained Greek scholar, parses the Septuagint verb in 2 Kings 23:5 as an aorist active indicative meaning 'burn down, burn up, consume by fire,' rendering the line, 'And he burned up the pagan priest.' This aligns with the Hebrew burning language used of Jehoshaphat in 1 Kings 22:46.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: He says, "The Greek word is in the Aorist Active Indicative, 3rd Singular and is used to describe the whole event that happened.  The word's definition is 'burn down, burn up, consume by fire' (Danker.  A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature.  517)". In a private message Pastor Easter said that he would translate the first line of the Septuagint's version of the verse, "And he burned up the pagan priest"

q: What does the Syriac Peshitta tradition contribute to the interpretation?
a: Multiple Semitic versions render the verb as 'he slew' or 'they killed.' Because the Peshitta is one of the oldest dated Bible manuscripts in any language, its agreement with execution carries significant evidential weight for the meaning of 2 Kings 23:5.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: In the Syriac Peshitta Bible with English Translation, the verse is translated, "***They killed*** the pagan priests whom the kings of Judah had established to burn incense on the high places…"

q: Did any Bible scholar Speed consulted teach that the priests of 2 Kings 23:5 emigrated rather than were executed?
a: After consulting roughly fifty volumes at Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary plus his own library, Speed found that not a single scholar in those collections suggested the idolatrous priests or male cult prostitutes emigrated under Josiah's direction.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: However, the big takeaway from my study of these many commentaries, Bible dictionary articles and monographs is this: ***not ONE Bible scholar represented in these volumes suggested that the idolatrous priests of 2 Kings 23:5 or the male cult prostitutes of 2 Kings 23:7 emigrated out of the land under Josiah's direction.***  Not one.

q: Why does Speed think nineteenth-century commentators softened Josiah's zeal, and what's wrong with that?
a: Many older interpreters were embarrassed by Josiah and read New Covenant grace back into his reforms, treating Deuteronomy's commands as merely figurative. Speed sees this as an anachronism and warns that Sumpter is repeating the same move in his pastoral reluctance to criminalize abortion-murder.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt:
May not Deut. xiii. 6-18 have been intended to express in a concrete but ideal form the spirit of execration to be entertained toward idolatry?  Perhaps in thinking so we are only guilty of an anachronism, and are applying to the seventh century before Christ the feelings of the nineteenth century after Christ" (391-92). 

I suspect that Pastor Toby is guilty of the same anachronism in his pastoral desire to withhold criminal penalty from those who commit abortion-murder.

q: Why does Speed reject the 'we're all on the same team' framing of the pro-life movement?
a: He argues the secular, Roman Catholic-led pro-life movement consistently runs counterproductive plays while keeping abolitionists benched, and that pro-life lobbyists and politicians collude with the system to keep abortion legal. He calls this rigged game and urges a #PLEXIT.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: While it appears that abolitionists and pro-lifers are on the same team, the game is fixed.  It's rigged. Do not bet against Roe v. Wade or the SCOTUS' desire to uphold that wicked decree.   It's not likely that anyone in Vegas is betting against the pro-choice\pro-life collusion to keep abortion legal.  At some point we need to leave the secular, Roman Catholic-led pro-life establishment with its doctrine of Balaam and we need to field a team that actually has a plan to win the game.  It is high time for a #PLEXIT (pro-life exit).

q: How does Speed answer the pastoral objection that criminalizing abortion is unmerciful to post-abortive women?
a: He calls the objection a category error: the church ministers grace, but the government, per Romans 13, ministers justice and bears the sword against evildoers. The standard for civil good and evil is the Law of God, which prescribes the death penalty for causing the death of a child in the womb.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: When we as pastors suggest that we should legislate with grace toward the abortive parents they commit a logical fallacy–a category error. The church ministers grace.  But the government ministers justice. Romans 13 is clear: it bears the sword against the evil doer.

q: What does Speed see as the cumulative lesson from Asa, Jehoshaphat, and Josiah for today's strategy?
a: Even Josiah's valiant abolitionism could not undo generations of incremental compromise; Judah still went into Babylonian captivity. Read individually, Josiah models abolition, but the dynastic pattern warns that incrementalism postpones reform until it is too late.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: Additionally, the cumulative effect of the reigns of all of the kings of Judah, in spite of Josiah's valiant efforts, was total judgment in the form of Babylonian captivity.  Josiah's abolitionism was not enough to overcome the generations of incrementalism and compromise.

q: How does Speed use Augustine to frame the duty of Christian magistrates?
a: Speed closes by quoting Augustine's distinction that a sovereign serves God as a man by personal faith, but as a king by enacting laws that command goodness and prohibit its opposite. Magistrates therefore have a uniquely kingly service of God that pastors should call them to.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: A sovereign serves God one way as man, another way as king; he serves Him as man by living according to faith, he serves Him as king by exerting the necessary strength to sanction laws that command goodness and prohibit its opposite…It is thus that kings serve the Lord as kings when they perform acts in His service that none but kings can perform

q: What is the controlling hermeneutical rule Speed invokes against Sumpter's reading?
a: Speed appeals to a basic interpretive principle that an interpretation no other scholar verifies is, by that fact, suspect. Granting Sumpter respect, he still concludes the 'exile' reading of 2 Kings 23:5 is exegesis read into the text rather than out of it.
t: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
qt: It is a basic rule of hermeneutics that if you come up with an interpretation that cannot be verified by other scholars that you have a misinterpretation of the text.  With all due respect to Pastor Toby–and he is due MUCH more respect than I have space for here–his interpretation is a misinterpretation.

q: Which two senators did Larry Boggs publicly campaign with, and why does Free the States consider that newsworthy?
a: Boggs posted a Facebook photo of himself standing between Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat (the 2019 Abortion Rights Man of the Year) and HHS Committee Chair Greg McCortney (the 2020 honoree) during a McAlester campaign stop. Free the States treats that as a public endorsement of the same leaders they blame for keeping SB13 from a hearing.
t: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
qt: Larry Boggs, a candidate for Oklahoma's 7th Senate District, posted a picture on his campaign Facebook page on Friday of himself in between 2019 Abortion Rights Man of the Year Greg Treat and 2020 Abortion Rights Man of the Year Greg McCortney.

q: How did Greg McCortney's role on the HHS Committee allow him to single-handedly stop SB13?
a: SB13 was assigned to the Senate Health and Human Services Committee, and as chair McCortney had unilateral authority to grant or deny it a hearing. He chose not to schedule one, which prevented the bill from advancing.
t: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
qt: State Senator Greg McCortney is the current chair of the Senate Health and Human Services (HHS) Committee, the committee where The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (SB13) was assigned. As committee chair, McCortney had sole discretion over whether SB13 would receive a hearing. It did not receive a hearing.

q: What happened when Joseph Silk tried to pull SB13 out of committee onto General Order?
a: Silk made a motion to bring SB13 to General Order, but Senator Kim David moved to table that motion and McCortney voted with her, killing SB13 for the 2020 session.
t: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
qt: When Senator Joseph Silk motioned to bring SB13 out of committee and on to General Order, McCortney voted in favor of [Senator Kim David's motion](https://www.facebook.com/FreeTheStates/videos/549375959015145/) to table Silk's motion and kill SB13 for good; and that's the short version of how abortion was kept legal in Oklahoma in 2020.

q: Why does Free the States hold Greg Treat ultimately responsible for SB13's death even though McCortney cast the deciding vote?
a: As Senate Pro Tem, Treat assigned McCortney to chair the HHS Committee and has been the most vocal opponent of SB13 since 2019. The article suggests Treat installed McCortney specifically because McCortney would block SB13.
t: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
qt: The man who assigned McCortney to be the chair of the Senate HHS Committee was Oklahoma Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat, the 2019 Abortion Rights Man of the Year. While it was ultimately McCortney's call on whether to hear SB13, Treat has been the primary opponent of SB13 since the beginning of 2019, and likely only gave McCortney the position on the condition that he deny SB13 a hearing.

q: What public arguments has Greg Treat used against abolishing abortion?
a: Treat has argued that abolishing abortion is unconstitutional, that doing so without permission from the Supreme Court would nullify the US Constitution, that nullifiers are historical villains, and that abolitionists are really pursuing secession rather than saving babies.
t: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
qt: Over the past year-and-a-half, Greg Treat has claimed that [abolishing abortion is unconstitutional](https://theresurgent.com/2019/02/17/three-men-keeping-abortion-legal-oklahoma-sb13/) and even that abolishing abortion without permission from the supreme court [nullifies the US Constitution](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-HBoXhPwWYk). He has falsely claimed that [nullifiers are the villains of history](https://theresurgent.com/2019/03/26/are-nullifications-advocates-the-villains-of-american-history/), when in reality, the abolitionists of slavery were the fiercest advocates of nullification.

q: What consequences did Senator Joseph Silk face for championing SB13?
a: Treat stripped Silk of his committee leadership assignment and blackballed most of his bills. Free the States cites this as a chilling effect explaining why other senators won't publicly back SB13.
t: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
qt: Treat [punished Senator Joseph Silk](http://tulsabeacon.com/gop-leaders-punish-senator-silk-for-sb13/) severely for fighting for abolition and for his criticisms of the Oklahoma Senate for their refusal to abolish abortion. Silk was stripped of his committee leadership assignment and most of his bills were blackballed.

q: Beyond appearing in a photo with Boggs, how else did McCortney materially support the campaign?
a: McCortney transferred $2,800 from his own campaign account to the Boggs campaign, a contribution traceable through Oklahoma's Guardian filing system.
t: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
qt: Beyond campaigning with him, McCortney also [donated](https://guardian.ok.gov/PublicSite/SearchPages/FilingAmendmentSelect.aspx?FilingID=146925) $2,800 from his campaign to the Boggs campaign.

q: Why is Free the States backing Warren Hamilton instead of Larry Boggs in the SD7 primary?
a: Hamilton has pledged to support the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, which Free the States has formally endorsed. They argue the Treat/McCortney support for Boggs is precisely the proof that Hamilton is the candidate leadership fears.
t: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
qt: Warren Hamilton has promised to support The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act and we have [endorsed his campaign](https://freethestates.org/endorsements/index.html). Those like McCortney and Treat, who are doing everything in their power to avoid having to abolish abortion, do not want men like Warren Hamilton in the State Senate.

q: Why does the author insist she is not asking to be excluded from criminal liability for her past abortion?
a: She views her own past abortion as participation in murder for which she has been forgiven by Christ, not as a victim status that should shield future women from accountability. She wants legislators to stop weaponizing women like her as a rhetorical reason to deny equal protection to the preborn.
t: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
qt: I am writing to you as a woman who has participated in murder by abortion.  By God's grace, I am a repentant murderer redeemed by the Lord Jesus Christ! As a woman that falls in the camp of those that you refuse to “criminalize”, as demonstrated by your 10-0 vote on SB495, this is a serious and urgent appeal to each one of you to CEASE from using women, like myself, as justification to deny equal protection to the preborn.

q: How does the author rebut the claim that equal-protection bills 'criminalize women'?
a: She argues equal protection does not single women out at all; it criminalizes the act of murder by abortion regardless of who commits it, and existing statutes already cover women who are genuinely coerced or victimized.
t: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
qt: Equal protection under the law does NOT single out women for criminalization. It establishes the ACT of murder, by abortion, as criminal.  I fully and ONLY support bills like SB495. I was prepared to be “criminalized as a woman” long before I knew it would not be a retroactive law. There is no ‘right’ for anyone to unjustly shed the innocent blood of our sons and daughters. Laws already exist to protect/provide equal justice for women truly coerced and victimized.

q: What does the author mean when she calls the committee's posture 'false chivalry'?
a: True chivalry, she says, is courage and readiness to defend the truly weak. By casting women seeking abortions as the weak who need protection from prosecution, the committee inverts chivalry and abandons the actually defenseless party, the preborn child being taken to slaughter.
t: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
qt: CHIVALRY includes the combination of qualities…especially courage, honor, courtesy, justice, and a readiness to help the weak – – – Sadly, the committee is holding up women as the weak in need of “protection” from “criminalization” versus a readiness to help the truly weak — the preborn who cannot speak and are being taken to slaughter.  This is not praiseworthy.

q: Why does the author call the committee's vote 'false compassion' rather than mercy toward women?
a: She defines compassion as recognizing suffering and acting to relieve it. Killing the bill, she argues, refuses to act for the suffering preborn and shows partiality toward those who kill them, which is not mercy but injustice dressed up as kindness.
t: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
qt: COMPASSION is to recognize the suffering of others and then take action to help – – – By refusing a bill like SB 495, the committee is not taking appropriate action in recognizing the suffering of the innocent preborn but rather it is practicing the sin of partiality towards those who murder them.

q: How does the author respond to the idea that leaving abortion legal spares women from suffering?
a: Drawing on her own experience, she says legalized abortion actually multiplies suffering for women, men, children, families, and the broader culture by encouraging sin and the painful consequences of rebellion against God, and that real relief came only through repentance and the cross of Christ.
t: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
qt: In showing preference to those involved in their murder, we are hating our preborn neighbors to death. It actually promotes the “suffering” of women, men, children, extended family and our entire culture by encouraging sin and letting us live with the painful consequences of rebellion before a Holy God. I wouldn't wish that on anyone. I know because so once was I until someone took me, as a sinner, to the cross of Christ and the sufficiency of His shed blood to forgive my sins.

q: What theological framing does the author apply to the senators' 10-0 vote?
a: She frames the unanimous vote as resisting God Himself, invoking the language of Acts 9 ('kicking against the goads') and Romans 13 (the civil magistrate as God's servant to punish wrongdoing) to insist that legislators are not the standard of good and evil but accountable to it.
t: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
qt: Ultimately your 10-0 vote is kicking against the goads of Almighty God to Whom we all must give an account.  We have a space of grace to change our ways…let's not take that grace of God in vain! You are God's servant, an agent of retribution to the wrongdoer…sent by Him to punish those who do wrong and to praise those who do right. He determines the standard for good and evildoing — not us.

q: What specific call does the author leave with the senators at the end of the letter?
a: She calls the senators to Godly repentance, to fear God rather than men or women, and to act now while there is still time, framing immediate obedience as the only acceptable response.
t: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
qt:
I am praying for you to be granted Godly repentance to see though you may justify yourselves before men… God knows your hearts. For that which is highly esteemed among men is an abomination (detestable) in the sight of God. May He grant you wisdom to fear Him, not man (which includes women), and turn from evil!

NOW is always the right time to do the right thing! Please, don't harden your heart…May you bear fruit in keeping with repentance! Amen.

q: Why does the author argue that Heartbeat Bills are not a faithful step toward abolishing abortion?
a: Heartbeat Bills, in the author's view, simply set a window during which abortion remains legal, violate biblical impartiality and Constitutional equal protection, and submit to a federal judiciary that will block them. They regulate murder rather than prohibit it.
t: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
qt: This includes Heartbeat Bills, which simply tells people to commit murder before the abortionist detects a heartbeat; which violates the Biblical commands for impartiality in the courts and the rescue of those being led to slaughter; which violates the Constitutional mandates of equal protection and the right to life; which submit to the federal judiciary and thereby will never go into effect.

q: How does the author use Romans 1 to indict men who back pro-life legislation?
a: He applies Paul's condemnation of those who 'give approval to those that do such things' to anyone supporting laws that license murder under any conditions, treating such approval as participation in the sin itself.
t: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
qt: Toward the end of Romans 1, when Paul is describing unrighteousness that is characteristic of idolators who are under "the wrath of God" and who have therefore been "given up to a debased mind," he says they not only "murder" but they "give approval to those that do such things."

q: What personal example of repentance does Brett Baggett offer his readers?
a: He admits he once supported Heartbeat Bills out of willful ignorance and compromise, calls that a failure of courage, and publicly repents while inviting other men to do the same.
t: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
qt: I once supported Heartbeat Bills due to my willful ignorance. I was wrong. I compromised. I thought "this is as good as we can get." This is not how courageous men act. Courageous men investigate the truth and stand boldly for what is righteous. I had to repent. Will you repent from any apathy or compromise with me?

q: Why are Athanasius and Martin Luther invoked as patterns for present-day abolitionists?
a: Both stood essentially alone against the prevailing powers of their day to defend doctrines they believed Scripture required. The author argues that if they could stand alone for doctrine, modern men should at least stand together to defend preborn children's lives.
t: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
qt: If these men stood *alone* to defend the precious doctrines of Christ and His Word, then what kind of cowards would we be if we, in this day, did not stand *together* to defend the lives of precious children as they are led to the slaughter by the millions?

q: What does the author say civil government is positively obligated to do about abortion?
a: Citing Romans 13 and the Mosaic homicide statutes, he argues government must protect life by criminalizing the intentional killing of a human being without exception, applying the same homicide laws inside the womb as outside.
t: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
qt: Men, it is time for you to demand the government to do what God has ordained it to do — namely, to protect life by opposing evil and rewarding good (Romans 13:1-4). It is time for abortion — the intentional killing of a human being — to be abolished (Psalm 82:2-4)! It must be illegal to murder a human being, period (Leviticus 20:1-5). The same homicide laws that protect you outside of the womb must protect those inside (Exodus 22:21-24).

q: How does the author frame the binary choice he wants every man to make?
a: He repeatedly poses the question as a stark either/or: cowardly boy approving of murder, or godly man rescuing the fatherless like Job. The framing is intentionally polarizing because he believes neutrality functions as approval.
t: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
qt: Will you act like a cowardly boy in the midst of this abortion holocaust or will you act like a courageous man to love your preborn neighbors?

q: Beyond legislation, what concrete actions does the author urge men to take?
a: He calls men to stand outside abortion mills, preach the gospel, offer spiritual and material help to mothers, and accept opposition from the world rather than seeking man's approval.
t: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
qt: Will you stand for your preborn neighbors not only politically but also outside of abortion mills, preaching the gospel and offering spiritual and material help? Will you stand against the world if you have to because you are more concerned with pleasing God than man (Galatians 1:10)?

q: Which abolitionist candidates won state legislative races in the 2022 midterms?
a: Silberman names four explicit abolitionist victories: Zach Dieken in Iowa HD5, Scott Herndon in Idaho SD1, Ernie Yarbrough in Alabama's seventh district, and Lorissa Sweet in Indiana's 50th district. He also notes Dan Foreman appears to have unseated a Democrat in Idaho LD6, and that with Heather Scott's reelection Idaho will have abolition bills pending in both chambers.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: For the Abolitionist Movement, there will be a few new abolitionist faces in state legislatures around the country in 2023.

q: How decisively did Lorissa Sweet beat the Republican establishment in Indiana?
a: Sweet defeated incumbent Dan Leonard, a close ally of Speaker Todd Huston, in the primary with more than 56 percent of the vote despite being outraised roughly 20-to-one, then won the general by more than 54 points and has committed to carry the bill to abolish abortion in Indiana.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: Leonard had close ties to Speaker Todd Huston and according to the Associated Press, outraised Sweet by an almost 20-to-one ratio. Still, Sweet defeated Leonard with more than 56 percent of the vote.

q: What happened to abortion-related referendums on the 2022 ballot?
a: Three blue states (Michigan 57-43, California 65-35, Vermont 77-23) enshrined abortion as a constitutional right, while pro-life referendums failed in Kentucky 52-48 and Montana 53-47. Silberman frames this as continuing the trend of the Kansas Value Them Both defeat, arguing Dobbs energized pro-abortion turnout far more than pro-life turnout.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: Abortion was protected in three blue states on Tuesday, as Michigan (57-to-43), California (65-to-35), and Vermont (77-to-23) voters chose to make abortion a Constitutional right.

q: Why does Silberman call the Montana born-alive referendum a damning case study?
a: He uses Montana to argue that even the softest possible pro-life ask, simply requiring care for infants who survive abortion, cannot win on the ballot, exposing the bankruptcy of moderate compromise messaging. The fact that it lost in a deep-red state is, for him, decisive evidence against the weak-bills-and-soft-messaging strategy.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: This is the weakest, softest, most milquetoast, pro-life compromise known to the abortion debate and it failed in a ruby red state.

q: What does Silberman say Republicans who avoided the abortion issue accomplished?
a: He claims silence on abortion is both sin and political malpractice because it cedes the messaging ground to Democrats, and he observes that politicians who actually leaned into abortion messaging, even weak pro-life messaging, outperformed those who ran from the issue.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: Running from the abortion issue is, of course, a sin before God, but it is also political malpractice.

q: How did pro-abortion campaigners reframe modest pro-life referendums to defeat them?
a: Silberman observes that Democrats deliberately turn every abortion referendum into a referendum on total abolition, citing Kentucky pro-abortion campaigners who falsely told voters that Amendment 2 meant no abortions with no exceptions, even though the amendment would not have changed any existing law.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: Democrats are going to make every abortion referendum a referendum on total abolition. In Kentucky, pro-abortion campaigners lied to voters, saying “Amendment 2 means no abortions, no exceptions.”

q: What offensive posture does Silberman recommend abolitionists adopt against pro-abortion voters?
a: Rather than defending modest proposals from attacks calling them cruel total bans, he urges abolitionists to put forward bold legislation, brand pro-abortion voters and legislators as accomplices to murder, and show images of abortion to make voters work to suppress the truth, accepting mockery as the cost of obedience.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: Instead of framing a pro-abortion vote as a failure to love them both, call pro-abortion voters and legislators accomplices to murder.

q: What did Silberman flag as a tragic outcome in the one state where the red wave actually arrived?
a: Florida Republicans gained four congressional seats and supermajorities in both legislative chambers, but party leaders reportedly intend to use that mandate to pass a heartbeat bill in 2023 rather than abolish abortion, meaning abortion will continue under the new GOP supermajority.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: Tragically, rumors out of Florida indicate that party leaders intend to use their new supermajorities to move ahead with a heartbeat bill in 2023 rather than abolish abortion.

q: What scriptural authority does Silberman invoke to justify aggressive exposure of the abortion industry?
a: He closes with Ephesians 5:11 to ground his call for offensive, image-bearing tactics, framing exposure of pro-abortion wickedness as a biblical command rather than an optional rhetorical strategy.
t: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
qt: “Take no part in the unfruitful works of darkness, but instead expose them.” -Ephesians 5:11

q: What political race did Mike Moon win, and what does the result mean for his legislative future?
a: Moon, a four-term state representative who hit his term limit in the House, won the Republican primary for Senate District 29 by defeating former Missouri GOP chairman David Cole. With no Democrat on the ballot, the primary win effectively secured him a seat in the Missouri State Senate beginning in 2021.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: Moon defeated former Missouri Republican Party Chairman David Cole 52.5% to 47.5% to become the Senate District 29 Republican nominee. There is no Democrat running for the seat so Moon will join the State Senate in 2021.

q: What was MO HB2285 and who joined Moon in authoring it?
a: HB2285 was Moon's bill of total and immediate abolition introduced in January 2020. Five other Republican representatives co-authored it: Dirk Deaton, Ben Baker, Jeff Pogue, Tom Hurst, and Brad Hudson.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: In January, Moon introduced MO HB2285, a bill of total and immediate abolition. It was co-authored by Reps. Dirk Deaton, Ben Baker, Jeff Pogue, Tom Hurst, and Brad Hudson.

q: Why did the bill fail to advance in the 2020 session, and who is responsible?
a: Republican Rep. Sheila Solon, the chair of the House Children and Families committee and an endorsee of Missouri Right to Life, blocked the bill from receiving a hearing. Free the States holds her personally responsible for keeping abortion legal in Missouri for another year.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: Missouri House Children and Families committee chair Rep. Sheila Solon, a pro-life Republican endorsed by Missouri Right to Life in 2018, denied the bill a committee hearing, keeping abortion legal in the state for another year.

q: What hypocrisy in current Missouri statute does Moon's bill target?
a: Existing Missouri law begins by declaring the state a 'sanctuary of life' that defends the right to life of the unborn, then immediately undercuts that declaration by saying the state will merely regulate abortion to whatever extent the U.S. Supreme Court allows. Moon's bill strips out that surrender clause.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: It clarifies that Missouri is actually the opposite of a sanctuary for unborn children; that the state will not defend the right to life but will “regulate” the killing of preborn children if given permission to do so by “decisions of the United States Supreme Court.”

q: How does Moon's bill rewrite the relevant statute?
a: It replaces 'Regulate abortion to the full extent permitted by the Constitution of the United States, decisions of the United States Supreme Court, and federal statutes' with a simple directive to 'Abolish abortion in this state.'
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: (3) ~~Regulate~~ **Abolish** abortion ~~to the full extent permitted by the Constitution of the United States, decisions of the United States Supreme Court, and federal statutes~~ in this state.

q: How does the bill address the authority of federal court rulings like Roe?
a: The bill explicitly nullifies any federal act, ruling, or regulation that would override Missouri's abolition of abortion, treating Roe-style precedent as void under both the Missouri and U.S. Constitutions.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: Any federal act, law, treaty, decision, order, rule, or regulation that purports to supersede, stay, or overrule section A of this act is in violation of the Constitution of the state of Missouri and the Constitution of the United States and is therefore void.

q: How does the article respond to the claim that abolition bills mandate prosecuting all women who have abortions for murder?
a: Silberman calls Cole's claim false. He argues that, just as with crimes against born humans, circumstances determine whether and how a mother is prosecuted. A woman coerced into an abortion under threat would not be prosecuted; abolition simply equalizes legal protection for preborn and born children.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: If someone is coerced into having an abortion under threat of bodily harm, they would not be prosecuted, just like any other crime committed against one’s will. There is nothing in Moon’s or any other abolition bill that specifies that a mother is to be prosecuted for murder no matter the circumstances.

q: What did Dr. Wes Scroggins argue against pro-life incrementalism in his letter to the editor?
a: Scroggins, a Missouri State University professor, argued that 'pro-life' regulatory legislation has cost more lives than it has saved by tolerating abortion under government regulation, and that the only Biblical, Constitutional, and moral position is total abolition.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: If preborn babies are innocent persons created in the image of God, the only Biblical, Constitutional, and moral position that can be taken is the total abolition of abortion. Anything else is an uncompassionate injustice called murder…

q: How did Scroggins frame Moon's bill in terms of an elected official's duty?
a: Scroggins framed HB2285 as nothing more than Moon honoring his oath of office to uphold the Missouri Constitution, which by the will of the people grants personhood at conception. He challenged readers to ask whether elected officials should not be expected to do exactly what they swore to do.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: State Rep. Moon has done nothing more with the filing of HB 2285 than uphold his oath of office to uphold the Missouri Constitution. Again, the Missouri Constitution, by the will of the people of Missouri, grants personhood at conception.

q: How did Moon characterize Cole's strategy of running to Moon's right on abortion?
a: Moon mocked Cole, a former leader in John Kasich's presidential campaign, for trying to pose as the genuine anti-abortion candidate, calling Cole's posture an unconvincing imitation of Moon himself.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: My opponent attempted to purport himself as me during the campaign. I guess the old saying, ‘Imitation is the sincerest form of flattery,’ is true in the case of this campaign.

q: What political conditions does the article say will exist in Missouri in 2021, and what conclusion does it draw?
a: Missouri is expected to have a Republican governor, attorney general, lieutenant governor, and supermajorities in both chambers. With Moon in the Senate carrying an abolition bill, the article argues the only thing that could keep abortion legal is pro-life officials again refusing to legislate what they claim to believe.
t: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
qt: There’s no reason that murdering preborn children would still be legal in Missouri this time next year unless, as Dr. Scroggins put it, Missouri’s pro-life elected official continue refusing to legislate according to what they say they believe and what they swear to uphold.

q: Why does the author dispute the claim that Missouri is 'abortion-free' as of December 2020?
a: Because the claim rests only on the fact that the lone surgical facility in St. Louis paused procedures for one month and referred women across state lines, while abortion itself remains fully legal in Missouri through hospitals, OBGYN offices, and abortifacient drugs.
t: Missouri is not Abortion-Free. But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, it Can Be.
qt: Murdering a child is still perfectly legal in the state. Babies can be torn to shreds at the St. Louis facility. They can still be murdered at hospitals and OBGYN offices. They can still be murdered by abortifacient drugs.

q: What did the St. Louis facility actually do in December that prompted the 'abortion-free' headlines?
a: It did not perform surgical abortions for the month and instead routed mothers to a facility roughly 22 minutes away across the Illinois border, where the killings continued.
t: Missouri is not Abortion-Free. But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, it Can Be.
qt: Instead the St. Louis facility referred mothers to a facility 22 minutes away across the Illinois border.

q: What does SB391 actually do, in legal terms?
a: It redefines 'person' to include human beings from conception and instructs Missouri to enforce that protection regardless of any contrary federal statute, ruling, or court order.
t: Missouri is not Abortion-Free. But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, it Can Be.
qt: The bill establishes that “‘Person’, includes a human being from the moment of conception,” and that “This chapter shall be enforced in relation to abortion regardless of any contrary or conflicting federal acts, laws, treaties, decisions, orders, rules, or regulations. Any court decision purporting to enjoin this state from protecting innocent human life from the moment of conception shall be treated as nonauthoritative, void, and of no force.”

q: Why does the author single out point three of the previously passed pro-life bill as a contradiction?
a: Because after declaring Missouri a 'sanctuary of life' that defends all humans born and unborn, the same bill turned around and limited the state's intent to merely regulating abortion within whatever the federal government and Supreme Court allowed.
t: Missouri is not Abortion-Free. But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, it Can Be.
qt: Defending the right to life of all humans and making the state a “sanctuary of life” would seem to indicate that the bill intended to abolish abortion. But then in point three, the bill contradicts itself by inexplicably stating that:

q: How exactly does Moon's bill rewrite that contradictory third point?
a: It strikes 'regulate' and the federal-deference language and replaces it with a direct command to abolish abortion in the state.
t: Missouri is not Abortion-Free. But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, it Can Be.
qt: Moon’s bill amends point three to read, “[it is the intention of the general assembly of the state of Missouri to] ~~[r]egulate~~ abolish abortion ~~to the full extent permitted by the Constitution of the United States, decisions of the United States Supreme Court, and federal statutes~~ in this state.”

q: Why does the author think prematurely declaring victory in Missouri is harmful, not just inaccurate?
a: Because real legislative efforts are underway to actually abolish abortion, and announcing the battle is already won undercuts the urgency and momentum needed to pass them.
t: Missouri is not Abortion-Free. But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, it Can Be.
qt: Given the existence of efforts to actually abolish abortion in the state, claiming the battle is already won is extremely unhelpful.

q: What is Mike Moon's legislative history on this issue?
a: He served in the Missouri State House from 2013 until terming out, authored the Abolition of Abortion in Missouri Act there in 2020, and is now carrying it in his first State Senate session.
t: Missouri is not Abortion-Free. But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, it Can Be.
qt: The 2021 session will be Moon’s first year in the State Senate after terming out of the State House in which he had served since 2013. Moon also authored The Abolition of Abortion in Missouri Act in the State House in 2020.

q: What concrete next step does the article ask Missouri abolitionists to take for SB391?
a: Once the bill receives a committee assignment, supporters need to pressure that committee's chair directly to grant it a hearing and a vote.
t: Missouri is not Abortion-Free. But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, it Can Be.
qt: SB391 now awaits a committee assignment. When it does, Missourians will need to be in contact with the chair of the committee demanding a hearing and vote for the bill.

q: How does Klusendorf rhetorically frame abolitionists in his Gospel Coalition opening, and why does Silberman call it 'garbage argumentation'?
a: Klusendorf opens by depicting abolitionists as predators with knives hunting pro-lifers to gut their legislative work. Silberman treats this as cheap rhetorical demonizing rather than substantive engagement, noting that ideological arguments can at least be answered, but smear language like this cannot.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: Now that the Supreme Court has let stand (for now) a Texas pro-life law that prohibits abortions after fetal heartbeat, the knives are out for pro-lifers, and not just from pro-abortionists. Abolitionists are also in the hunt to gut pro-life legislative efforts.

q: What does Silberman say Klusendorf misrepresents about Bill Ascol's call for church discipline?
a: Klusendorf claims Ascol called for disfellowshipping politicians who don't vote along abolitionist lines, but Ascol actually limited his call to politicians who vote against a bill that would abolish abortion outright. Silberman frames this as a deliberate, repeated sleight-of-hand because the honest version would not win Klusendorf's argument.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: He didn’t. He suggested church discipline for those who *vote against a bill to abolish abortion*. But Klusendorf can’t say that. Obviously, he can’t win the argument if he portrays Bill accurately. So he doesn’t. He lies about Bill repeatedly, even after being publicly corrected.

q: Why does Silberman say current pro-life leaders fit Klusendorf's 'Bob' character even more than 'Abraham' in the wife-beating governor analogy?
a: Klusendorf's Abraham signs incremental bills while pushing toward total protection, but Silberman observes that real pro-life leaders sign incremental bills and then refuse to enforce them, or even market them as moderate compromises that preserve abortion access. Governor Stitt's 'we'll let the courts work it out' and Governor Abbott's framing of the heartbeat bill as giving women 'at least six weeks' are presented as evidence.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: They sign incremental bills and promise not to enforce them. (As he signed a slew of pro-life bills Oklahoma Governor Kevin Stitt told the media, “we’ll let the courts work out if any of those get overturned.”) They sign incremental bills and promote them as moderate compromises that still allow for abortion.

q: Do abolitionists support repealing existing pro-life regulations?
a: Yes, but only inside an abolition bill that establishes a just standard by treating preborn children as persons under the homicide code. Abolitionists oppose stand-alone repeals like the New York Reproductive Health Act because such bills create an unjust standard rather than abolishing abortion.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: Abolitionists support the repeal of pro-life regulations, but only in the context of an abolition bill which is abolishing abortion. Abolitionists only support bills which establish a just standard, according to God’s definition of justice.

q: How do abolitionists answer the claim that pro-life regulations have meaningfully reduced abortion numbers?
a: Silberman lists four standing answers: any reduction does not erase the inherent injustice of regulating murder, regulations delay abolition by substituting for it, they delay abolition by dehumanizing the unprotected unborn, and the claimed reductions are usually exaggerated. He insists abolitionists have made these points repeatedly across many media.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt:
We have repeatedly made clear in articles, podcasts, pamphlets, conference presentations, and more that any alleged reduction in the abortion rate as a result of a pro-life regulation:

1. [Does not change the inherent injustice](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t1zMoSxevkg) of regulating abortion
2. [Delays the abolition of abortion](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4T-7szob5pA&t=3365s) by serving as a substitute for abolition, causing more deaths in the long run

q: Would abolitionists support a bill that bans abortion but does not address IVF deaths?
a: Yes, because abolition bills function by establishing personhood at fertilization and removing the abortion exception in the homicide code. Just as homicide statutes do not enumerate stabbing, shooting, or strangulation separately, abolition bills do not need to enumerate IVF, surgical, or chemical killing. The only IVF-related bill abolitionists would oppose is one that writes an explicit fertility-clinic exception into law.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: Abolition bills do not directly address each form of murder. All they do is establish that life begins at fertilization and remove the exception in the homicide code that allows for abortion. Upon the passage of such a bill, preborn children would be treated by law the same as everyone else.

q: Why does Silberman say the prisoner-of-war 'death march' analogy fails as a defense of incremental legislation?
a: The captured officer is not a lawmaker; he is a combatant whose duty is to win a war while preserving as many of his men as possible. Immediatism, by contrast, is a socio-political principle rooted in repentance, and incremental abortion bills fail to call the state to repentance because they refuse to present the righteous standard.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: The socio-political principle of immediatism does not apply to war because immediatism is about repentance. In the context of a culture that murders its children, repentance is the key. For repentance to occur, violators of God’s law need to be called to repent. For there to be a true call to repentance, a righteous standard has to be presented.

q: What is the abolitionist response to Klusendorf's claim that pro-lifers lack the legal authority to abolish abortion?
a: Silberman argues Klusendorf is treating the Supreme Court as the ultimate source of legal authority, when in fact God's Word, the U.S. Constitution's Equal Protection Clause, and state constitutions like Oklahoma's already authorize and require the abolition of abortion. State officials therefore have both biblical and constitutional duty to act, regardless of SCOTUS.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: In order to say this, Klusendorf must hold a view of legal authority which views the supreme court as the ultimate authority unto whom unconditional submission is due. He does not have a proper view of the hierarchy of authority, believing that there is no legal authority but that which is derived from SCOTUS.

q: How does Silberman use the Nuremberg trials to answer the 'just following orders' defense of pro-life incrementalism?
a: He cites Justice Robert H. Jackson's prosecution of Nazi defendants, where neither 'superior orders' nor 'acts of state' could excuse participation in mass murder. Applied to abortion, a state-level official cannot hide behind Supreme Court rulings to evade his obligation to outlaw the killing of preborn humans, because both SCOTUS and state officials are 'under God and the law.'
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: Applying this to the present situation, a State-level official cannot shirk his duty to uphold justice and outlaw murder by appealing to orders from the supreme court. Both the supreme court and State-level officials are “under God and the law.”

q: How does Silberman distinguish nullification from secession against Doug Wilson and Klusendorf?
a: Wilson claims that being a true abolitionist requires secession; Klusendorf softens this to nullification being 'akin to' secession. Silberman defines nullification as upholding higher law in defiance of a superior while remaining in the union, whereas secession declares full independence. He cites Jefferson Davis, an opponent of nullification, conceding that the two are 'antagonistic principles.'
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: To nullify is to uphold higher law in defiance of an order from a superior while not acting to separate from or remove the authority. To secede is to declare complete independence from the authority.

q: How does Silberman answer the 'And then what?' rhetorical question used by Van Maren and Klusendorf?
a: Abolitionists do not need a guaranteed forecast of every consequence because their duty is fixed and the results belong to God. Federal funding threats or troop deployments are possible but historically rare, and even if they occurred, the obligation to expose tyrants rather than appease them does not change.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: Abolitionists do not purport to know exactly how abolition and nullification would play out, and we don’t need to. Duty is ours, results belong to God.

q: Why does Silberman believe nullification actually increases the chance Roe gets overturned?
a: He frames justices like Roberts and Kavanaugh as politically reactive 'leaves in the wind' who responded to left-wing pressure on Obamacare and would respond to abolitionist pressure on Roe. State-level defiance, abolitionist amicus briefs, and unseating incrementalists in legislatures collectively raise the political cost of preserving Roe, making reversal more likely than continued appeasement has ever made it.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: It is conceivable that *Roe* would be overturned in *Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health* because of the rise of the Abolitionist Movement. Men like John Roberts and Brett Kavanuagh are leaves in the wind, always seeking to be in the center of wherever the political winds are blowing.

q: What does Silberman point to as the crowning example of pro-life movement self-deception?
a: He cites Focus on the Family VP Gary Bauer claiming victory after Planned Parenthood v. Casey, a decision in which an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority upheld and even strengthened Roe. Bauer celebrated because some pro-life regulations survived, which Silberman calls a pathetic posture toward a crushing defeat.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: An 8-1 Republican-appointed majority (six of whom had been appointed by pro-life Republicans in the previous 19 years to replace six pro-*Roe* justices) upheld and even strengthened *Roe*. But because a few of the pro-life regulations in question were allowed, Bauer and other pro-lifers claimed victory.

q: Why does Silberman insist scriptural argument is the decisive battleground rather than pragmatics?
a: Even if every pragmatic calculation favored Klusendorf, scripture alone would still settle the case for abolitionism. Silberman observes that attacks on the Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion, including from seminary professors, have largely avoided scriptural argument because incrementalists cannot 'out-exegete' abolitionists.
t: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
qt: If Klusendorf had every pragmatic calculation on his side, and all abolitionists had was scripture, we would be right and he would be wrong.

q: What procedural action was scheduled for the Oklahoma State Senate the day after this article was published?
a: A motion was set to be made on Wednesday to bring SB13 directly to the Senate floor for a hearing, with a competing motion to table it expected to receive an immediate vote.
t: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
qt: A motion is going to be made in the State Senate on Wednesday to bring SB13 directly to the floor for a hearing. A motion to table this motion will likely be made by an establishment Republican and that motion to table will get a vote immediately.

q: What was the practical stakes of Wednesday's vote?
a: The Senate would either vote to hear SB13 or to table it permanently, effectively deciding whether the bill survived the session.
t: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
qt: So, on Wednesday, the Senate will vote on whether to hear SB13 or table it for good.

q: What grassroots and institutional support did SB13 already have before this vote?
a: Three thousand people had rallied at the Capitol on February 11 for Abolition Day, abolitionists across Oklahoma had been continuously contacting senators, and the Oklahoma Baptists, Oklahoma Free Will Baptists, and Oklahoma Republican Party had all passed resolutions demanding the bill.
t: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
qt: On February 11, three thousand people showed up to the State Capitol for the Abolition Day rally to demand SB13. Thousands of abolitionists from across the state have been constantly emailing and calling their state Senators asking them to support SB13. The Oklahoma Baptists, Oklahoma Free Will Baptists, and Oklahoma Republican Party all passed resolutions demanding SB13.

q: Whom did the article name as responsible for blocking SB13 from being heard?
a: James Silberman names Senators Greg McCortney and Greg Treat as the men whose hardened hearts kept the bill from receiving a hearing, after it was tabled in committee and the committee deadline passed.
t: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
qt: Yet because of Greg McCortney and Greg Treat hardening their hearts, the bill hasn’t even been heard. It was tabled in committee and the deadline for bills passing out of committee has passed.

q: How can a bill reach the Senate floor in Oklahoma after missing the committee deadline?
a: Committee passage is not strictly required; a bill can be brought directly to the floor if a motion to do so is filed and a majority of the Senate votes in favor of hearing it.
t: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
qt: A bill does not have to pass out of committee, however. It can be brought straight to the Senate floor if a motion to do so is filed and a majority of the Senate votes in favor of hearing the bill.

q: What specific action did Free the States ask readers to take?
a: Readers were asked to email their own State Senator, signal that they were watching Wednesday's vote, frame a vote to table SB13 as a vote against abolition, and CC the rest of the Senate.
t: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
qt: Please email your State Senator to let them know you’re watching to see what they do on Wednesday and that a vote to table SB13 is a vote against the abolition of abortion. If you don’t know who your State Senator is, find out [here](http://www.oksenate.gov/findmylegislature.aspx). CC the rest of the Senate in the email as well.

q: How did the article frame a vote to table SB13 morally?
a: It framed any senator's vote to table the bill as equivalent to a vote against abolishing abortion, removing the procedural cover senators often use to avoid taking a recorded position.
t: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
qt: a vote to table SB13 is a vote against the abolition of abortion

q: What specific parliamentary action did Dr. David Norman take against the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion at the 2021 SBC, and what was its intended effect?
