qt: The best brief resource in the Abolitionist Movement for turning pro-lifers into abolitionists is the [Are You An Abolitionist pamphlet](https://store.abolitionistsrising.com/products/are-you-an-abolitionist). The best quadfold for outreach to pro-aborts and evangelism is the [Abortion is Murder And Everybody Knows it quadfold](https://store.abolitionistsrising.com/collections/dropcards-quadfolds).

q: Why does Free the States refuse to name the senator who made the 'challenge the throne' remark?
a: The author says the senator would face retaliation from Senate leadership if his identity were revealed, so they protect him. This is offered as evidence of an oppressive internal culture in the Oklahoma Senate, not as journalistic reticence.
t: OK State Senator: "What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?"
qt: For the sake of the Senator who would likely face punishment if Senate leaders knew what he said, we will not reveal his name.

q: What was the actual exchange that gave the article its title?
a: An abolitionist met cordially with the senator about SB13, broke into tears begging him to act to abolish abortion, and the senator responded by asking what he could possibly do, comparing dissent against leadership to challenging a throne.
t: OK State Senator: "What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?"
qt: Effected by her emotion, the Senator asked, “What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?”

q: According to Russell Hunter, why do large Republican majorities so rarely deliver on conservative priorities?
a: He argues that moderate and even left-leaning Republicans climb into leadership positions and then use that consolidated power to suppress conservative bills and the legislators who file them, including bills to abolish abortion.
t: OK State Senator: "What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?"
qt: Moderate and even left-leaning Republicans rise to positions of leadership and consolidate their own power which they use to squash conservative bills and legislators.

q: What concrete punishment did Senator Joseph Silk face for backing immediate abolition?
a: Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat censured Silk over his support for immediately abolishing abortion, and the year before the article Silk was stripped of his Vice Chair roles.
t: OK State Senator: "What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?"
qt: Senator Joseph Silk has been severely censured by Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat for his support of immediately abolishing abortion. Last year, Silk’s Vice Chair roles were stripped from him.

q: How did Silk himself describe the current Oklahoma Senate leadership compared to others he has served under?
a: In a letter to colleagues after the punishment, Silk drew on his experience in the Coast Guard, multiple jobs, business ownership, and three different Senate leadership teams to say this was the most authoritarian leadership he had ever seen.
t: OK State Senator: "What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?"
qt: I have served in the U.S. Coast Guard under many different commands, held many different occupations, run a business and served in the State Senate under three different leadership teams and have never seen a more authoritarian leadership than we currently have.

q: Who specifically does the article identify as wielding 'royal' power over the Republican caucus?
a: Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat and Senate Majority Leader Kim David are named as the king-and-queen pair whose threat of punishment keeps other Republican senators in line.
t: OK State Senator: "What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?"
qt: This is the type of oppressive power Treat and Senate Majority Leader Kim David exert over the rest of the Republican Senators who feel as though they are under the authority of a king and queen and that they will be punished if they step out of line.

q: What does the author say is the human cost of senators not standing up to leadership?
a: Hunter frames the consequence in blood: as long as Republican senators lack the courage to confront Senate royalty and the pro-life establishment, abortion in Oklahoma will continue, potentially without end.
t: OK State Senator: "What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?"
qt: And if Republican Senators don’t muster the courage to stand up to Senate royalty and the pro-life establishment, the blood could continue to flow indefinitely.

q: What action steps does the article ask readers to take after reading?
a: It asks readers to subscribe to email updates because social media reach has been throttled, to do in-person activism using the 'Are You An Abolitionist' pamphlet and the 'Abortion is Murder And Everybody Knows it' quadfold, and to become monthly financial supporters.
t: OK State Senator: "What am I supposed to do? Challenge the throne?"
qt: Big tech is cracking down on our ability to reach you! Our social media reach has been greatly decreased in recent months.

q: Who endorsed Warren Hamilton in the SD7 runoff, and what kind of organization made the endorsement?
a: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion, described as a pastor-led coalition of Southern Baptists in the state, publicly endorsed Warren Hamilton over incumbent Larry Boggs in the August 25, 2020 runoff for Senate District 7.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: On Tuesday, Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion endorsed Oklahoma Senate District 7 candidate Warren Hamilton. Oklahoma Baptist for Abolishing Abortion is a pastor-led coalition of Southern Baptists in the state.

q: What concrete legislative commitment distinguishes Hamilton from Boggs on abortion?
a: Hamilton has promised to author the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act in the State Senate, while Boggs literally walked out when the bill was up for a committee vote.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: Hamilton is an abolitionist who has promised to author the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act in the State Senate. Larry Boggs left the room when the motion was made to bring the bill out of committee.

q: How does Pastor Bill Ascol frame the choice between Hamilton and Boggs to voters?
a: Ascol frames it as a choice between a 'principled statesman' and a 'political swamp creature,' urging voters to replace the eight-year incumbent with the challenger.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: voters living in Oklahoma State Senate District 7 have a great opportunity to choose a principled statesman over a political swamp creature by voting FOR challenger Warren Hamilton over eight-year incumbent Senator Larry Boggs.

q: How does the coalition respond to Boggs' suggestion that Hamilton belongs to a cult?
a: They flatly reject it, attesting that Hamilton is a member in good standing of an Oklahoma Southern Baptist congregation and calling the cult charge a libelous lie.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: As Oklahoma Baptists we want everyone to know that Warren Hamilton is a member in good standing of an Oklahoma Southern Baptist congregation. Any suggestion that he is part of a cult would be laughable if it were not such a libelous lie.

q: What does the article say about the broader pattern of calling abolitionists a cult?
a: It identifies the cult slander as a popular tactic used by pro-life Republican establishment senators in concert with local tabloids, citing Boggs' comparison of Hamilton and his supporters to Westboro Baptist as a prominent example.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: The idea that abolitionism is a cult has become a popular slander among pro-life Republican establishment Senators and local tabloids working in concert with these politicians. Boggs has publicly slandered Hamilton by comparing him and his supporters to Westboro Baptist.

q: What incident at the Haskell County GOP meeting does the article point to as the backdrop for Boggs' slander?
a: On July 14th, Boggs allegedly shoved Hamilton and threatened a supporter, shouting 'Screw you!... If you don't like, step outside and you square right up!' before resorting to slander to cover his behavior.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: an incident at the Haskell County GOP meeting on July 14th where Boggs shoved Hamilton and threatened one of his supporters, shouting “Screw you!… If you don’t like, step outside and you square right up!”

q: Why does the article say the stakes of this race extend beyond a single seat?
a: Because Boggs intends to keep regulating abortion while Hamilton intends to abolish it, the article frames continuing to elect Boggs-style senators as actively perpetuating the legal cover for abortion in Oklahoma.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: If we continue sending men like Larry Boggs to Oklahoma City, nothing is going to change and babies will continue to be murdered under the cover of Oklahoma State law. Warren Hamilton is the type of man we need at the capitol to change that.

q: What is Hamilton's stated ecclesial and theological pedigree according to the endorsement?
a: Hamilton is described as a 'convictional conservative' who has personally endorsed the Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion, anchoring his political stance to a denominational confessional act.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: Warren Hamilton is a convictional conservative who has endorsed the Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion

q: What concrete actions does the article ask SD7-area readers to take?
a: It asks readers to share the article with anyone they know in the listed SD7 towns so those voters know to support Hamilton on August 25th.
t: Oklahoma Baptists for Abolishing Abortion Endorses Warren Hamilton for SD7
qt: Share this article with anyone you know in Senate District 7 (McAlester, Stigler, Wilburton, Hartshorne, East Eufala, Coalgate, Savanna, Stuart, Kiowa, Quinton, Whitefield, Spiro, and the surrounding areas) so they know to vote for Warren Hamilton on August 25th!

q: Which state Baptist conventions had passed resolutions calling for the immediate abolition of abortion before Oklahoma Baptists did so in November 2021?
a: Oklahoma Baptists were the fourth Southern Baptist state convention in 2021 to pass such a resolution, following Arkansas, South Carolina, and Utah-Idaho, all of which built on the Southern Baptist Convention's June 2021 Resolution on Abolishing Abortion.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: On Tuesday, Oklahoma Baptists became the fourth Southern Baptists state convention this year to pass a resolution calling for the immediate abolition of abortion following [Arkansas](https://freethestates.org/2021/10/arkansas-baptists-pass-resolution-on-abolishing-abortion-at-state-convention/index.html), [South Carolina](https://freethestates.org/2021/11/south-carolina-baptist-convention-passes-resolution-abolishing-abortion/index.html), [Utah-Idaho](https://freethestates.org/2021/10/utah-idaho-baptist-convention-calls-ut-id-governors-legislature-to-abolish-abortion/index.html).

q: What was the most consequential amendment added to the Oklahoma Baptists' 2021 resolution, and who introduced it?
a: Immediate past convention president Blake Gideon offered an amendment making explicit that what was being called for was the immediate end and abolition of abortion, without exception or compromise; he argued the language was needed to reflect the intent of the 2019 Oklahoma and 2021 SBC resolutions.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: The most important amendment is the final one — making clear that what is being called for is the immediate abolition of abortion. Blake Gideon, the convention's immediate past president, offered the amendment

q: Why did Blake Gideon argue that scriptural clarity required strengthening the resolution's language?
a: Gideon told the convention that the Bible models clarity and strength, and that resolutions should reflect the tone of scripture; he said the cultural moment did not allow tiptoeing around abortion, citing that 20 children would be murdered in Oklahoma that day.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: We live in a day where there's a culture of death all around us, and we can't tiptoe around issues. We must be clear and we must be strong. 20 children today will be murdered in the state of Oklahoma.

q: What two arguments did the Resolutions Committee, through Troy Taylor, give against Gideon's clarifying amendment?
a: Speaking for the committee, Comanche Cotton Baptist Association Missions Director Troy Taylor argued the added language was redundant and unnecessary, and that it did not reflect the spectrum of views on abortion within the convention.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt:
Speaking for the Resolutions Committee against Gideon's amendment, Comanche Cotton Baptist Association Missions Director Troy Taylor argued that:

1. the added language was redundant and unnecessary, and
2. the added language does not reflect the spectrum of views on abortion within the convention.

q: How did James Silberman rebut the Resolutions Committee's claim that Gideon's amendment was redundant?
a: Silberman argued that the existence of non-abolitionists within Oklahoma Baptists is precisely why clearer language was needed, and that the committee's own opposition proved the amendment's necessity, since incrementalism is what keeps abortion legal in Oklahoma.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: The fact that there are non-abolitionists within Oklahoma Baptists is precisely why the clearer language is necessary. The amendment would only be redundant and unnecessary if there was already uniform agreement on the subject. Beyond that, the Committee's own opposition to the amendment is further proof of the resolution's necessity. Incrementalism and incrementalist politicians are the only things keeping abortion legal in Oklahoma. That ideology is what needs to be specified and rooted out.

q: How did Dusty Deevers describe the unifying line of the 2019 Oklahoma and 2021 SBC resolutions?
a: As one of the primary authors of both resolutions, Deevers told messengers that the unifying, prophetic, and biblical call of both was the urgent line that called for the abolition of abortion immediately without exception or compromise.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: As one of the primary authors of the 2019 amendment that was presented to Oklahoma Baptists, and then the 2021 SBC Resolution on Abolishing Abortion that was passed this summer, I can tell you that the unifying, prophetic, and Biblical call that united both the 2019 Oklahoma Baptists and the 2021 SBC was that urgent line that called for the abolition of abortion immediately without exception or compromise…

q: How did Dusty Deevers frame the biblical command against murder in relation to abortion laws and court rulings?
a: Deevers preached that God's word commands 'you shall not murder' rather than 'you shall regulate murder,' and that any court opinion or law permitting the killing of preborn children defies God and must be abolished.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: God's Word commands 'you shall not murder.' If a court writes opinions or decrees, as those in Isaiah 10:1-2, that says women have the right to murder their children, that court is defying God and has blood on their hands. If any law is passed in any state saying when, where and how you can murder a baby, those laws must be abolished as God commands. God's Word does not say 'you shall regulate murder.' It says 'you shall not murder.'

q: What historical comparison did Deevers draw to convey the scale of abortion since Roe v. Wade?
a: Deevers told messengers that abortion in the United States over the past 50 years was ten times greater than Hitler's final solution against the Jews, and that Oklahomans send over 5,000 preborn citizens to slaughter every year.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: Brothers and sisters, the vicious holocaust against our preborn neighbors in the United States over the past 50 years since Roe v. Wade is ten times greater than Hitler's final solution against the Jews. Oklahomans lead over 5,000 preborn citizens to slaughter every year.

q: How wide was the margin by which Gideon's clarifying amendment passed?
a: After Pastor Brandon Allen of Ekklesia Muskogee called the convention to a vote, the overwhelming majority supported Gideon's amendment, with Deevers reporting that roughly three-fourths of the messengers voted in favor.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: Pastor Brandon Allen of Ekklesia Muskogee then called the convention to a vote, and the overwhelming majority supported Gideon's amendment. According to Deevers, roughly three-fourths of the messengers voted in favor.

q: What additional amendment did David Dunsworth offer, and how did it fare?
a: David Dunsworth of Northside Baptist Church motioned to amend the resolution by adding language affirming that life begins at conception and inserting the word 'preborn,' and the messengers affirmed his motion.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: David Dunsworth of Northside Baptist Church then motioned to amend the resolution by adding language about life beginning at conception, and adding the word "preborn;" a motion which was affirmed by the messengers.

q: What procedural attempt did Mike Keahbone make against the amendments, and what was the ruling?
a: Lawton First Baptist Church Pastor Mike Keahbone moved to cancel all of the amendments, calling the debate over semantics and redundancy ridiculous; his motion was ruled out of order because amendments already adopted cannot be canceled.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: Lawton First Baptist Church Pastor and former Oklahoma Baptists First Vice President Mike Keahbone attempted a motion to undo all of the amendments, saying, "To sit here and argue over semantics and redundancy is ridiculous. I motion that we cancel all the amendments." His motion was ruled out of order, as amendments already adopted cannot be canceled.

q: How did Pastor Tom Fortner respond to the charge that the convention was arguing over mere semantics?
a: Mill Creek First Baptist Church Pastor Tom Fortner argued that the work was not semantic but a matter of clarifying the resolution for a lost and dying world that habitually plays word games and distorts reports of Baptist life.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: We are not arguing over semantics. We are looking to clarify a statement for a lost and dying world. The world around us plays word games with what we say. If you look at how they report events that occur in Baptist life, you will see it distorted beyond recognition. We need to be absolutely clear in how we say what we say.

q: What earlier real-world example did the article cite to demonstrate that abolitionist resolutions get distorted without clear language?
a: After the 2019 Oklahoma Baptists resolution passed, Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat — described as the primary opponent of abolishing abortion in Oklahoma — told the Muskogee Politico that the abolitionist resolution was no big deal because the convention was simply standing for life as it always had.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: Yet, Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat — a pro-life professing Christian, and the primary opponent of abolishing abortion in Oklahoma — [commented to the *Muskogee Politico*](https://www.muskogeepolitico.com/2019/11/senate-president-treat-no-change-in.html) that the new abolitionist resolution was no big deal because the convention was simply standing for life as it always had.

q: What concrete obligations does the article say new Oklahoma Baptists President Chris Wall and Executive Director Todd Fisher now have?
a: The article argues Wall and Fisher must loudly speak on behalf of the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, stop championing incremental bills and incrementalist politicians at Rose Day, and partner with abolitionist organizations rather than incrementalist Roman Catholic figures.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: This means loudly speaking on behalf of the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. It means no more championing incremental bills and incrementalist politicians at Rose Day. It means partnering with Oklahoma-based abolitionist organizations like Rescue Those, Liberty Rising, Southern Baptists for Abolishing Abortion, and Free the States instead of incrementalist Roman Catholics like Abby Johnson, Tony Lauinger, and Brett Farley.

q: What sermon and scripture did Deevers invoke to call messengers to fearless unity?
a: Deevers referenced Pastor Michael Butler's sermon on Philippians 1:27-30, urging messengers to walk in a manner worthy of the gospel and stand firm with fearless unity, and quoted the 2021 SBC Resolution's call for pastors and entity leaders to use their gifts to abolish abortion immediately without exception or compromise.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion!
qt: As we were exhorted from Pastor Michael Butler's sermon on Philippians 1:27-30, because we understand the urgency, we must unite to walk in a manner worthy of the gospel; standing firm with fearless unity. I echo and quote a line from the 2021 Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion: 'We call upon pastors and entity leaders to use their God-given gifts of preaching, teaching, and leading with one unified, principled, prophetic voice to abolish abortion immediately without exception or compromise.'

q: What concrete action did the messengers at the 2019 Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma take regarding abortion?
a: Messengers voted overwhelmingly to amend Resolution 7 with explicit language directing the state legislature to end abortion immediately, transforming a typical proclamation into a directive to lawmakers.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: Representatives of the assembled Southern Baptist churches voted overwhelmingly in favor of a resolution calling for an immediate end of abortion in Oklahoma.

q: What language did Pastor Bill Ascol's amendment add to Resolution 7?
a: Ascol's amendment added a final sentence directing the Oklahoma State Legislature to pass legislation ending abortion immediately, with no exceptions and no compromise.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: Finally, we call upon the Oklahoma State Legislature to enact legislation that calls for the immediate end of abortion without exception or compromise.

q: Why did Brett Baggett and other pastors feel the original Resolution 7 was insufficient?
a: Baggett described the original as a typical pro-life statement with no specific directive, more declaration than action item, and the pastors wanted a resolution that explicitly told legislators to pass a bill ending abortion immediately.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: Resolution 7 was a typical pro-life resolution. It read more like a proclamation than a resolution. So a few pastors and myself who are from Oklahoma and are part of the Southern Baptist Convention – we came together to propose an amendment to try to make it more explicitly say something like calling the legislators in Oklahoma to pass legislation that would immediately end abortion.

q: What sparked grassroots Southern Baptist opposition to BGCO leadership in the first place?
a: In February 2019, BGCO leaders publicly opposed SB13, the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, which prompted pastors across the state to organize, hold a roundtable, and rally at the capitol declaring that the BGCO did not speak for them.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: The inspiration for the resolution was instigated in February by BGCO leaders when they announced their opposition to SB13, the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. Southern Baptist pastors around the state were stunned and responded by proclaiming that BGCO leadership did not speak for them.

q: Why does Free the States consider 'end' a weaker word choice than 'abolish' in the resolution?
a: 'End' does not specify a mechanism, allowing pro-life people who only want abortion to become 'unthinkable' (rather than illegal) to sign on, whereas 'abolish,' 'outlaw,' or 'criminalize' name the legal mechanism and tie the resolution to actual abolition legislation like SB13.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: The word "end" lacks specificity regarding the mechanism by which abortion is to end, which in a formal document establishing a policy position, is important. There are a lot of pro-life people who say things like, "I don't want abortion to illegal, I want it to be unthinkable." Such people could agree that abortion should end even though they don't agree that abortion should be abolished.

q: What is wrong, in the article's view, with the Alabama-style bill that BGCO leaders may try to substitute for SB13?
a: The Alabama bill purports to end abortion from conception but does not criminalize abortion as murder, gives immunity to mothers who have their child killed, and only takes effect pending Supreme Court approval, so it remains unenforced while Planned Parenthood expands.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: Bills like this one which give immunity to the parents who have their child murdered (thus depriving preborn human beings of equal protection and legalizing self-induced abortion) and/or submit to the Supreme Court's evil and unconstitutional opinion on abortion are anathema to abolitionism and to God's word upon which abolitionism is based.

q: What two features does Free the States identify as the distinguishing test of a true abolition bill versus a pro-life bill?
a: A true abolition bill must (1) fully criminalize abortion as homicide for everyone involved and (2) defy the Supreme Court rather than submitting to its rulings; bills lacking either feature are designed to deceive sincere supporters of abolition.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: Full criminalization of abortion as homicide for all involved in the murder and defiance of the Supreme Court are the two primary hangups for people regarding the abolition of abortion, so this new bill would likely lack one or both of these aspects.

q: Which BGCO leaders are named as wielding the institutional power that could either honor or sidestep the resolution?
a: Hance Dilbeck (Executive Director), Blake Gideon (President), and Brian Hobbs (Editor of The Baptist Messenger) are identified as the three leaders whose response will determine whether the convention's stated position is institutionally upheld.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: Hopefully, Oklahoma Baptist leaders Hance Dilbeck (Executive Director), Blake Gideon (President), and Brian Hobbs (Editor of *The Baptist Messenger*), who wield immense political power, adhere to their convention's agreed upon principles.

q: How does the article characterize the difference between denominational leaders and the grassroots on abolition?
a: Like many institutions, the leaders are politically cautious and have submitted to worldly wisdom and the Supreme Court, while the rank-and-file pastors and members are calling them back to a righteous standard.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: As is the case with many institutions, the leaders of Oklahoma Baptists are less willing to stand up for what's right when it's politically risky than the grassroots. Whereas the convention's leaders have operated thus far on worldly wisdom and have submitted to the Supreme Court rather than God, many Southern Baptist pastors and churches are calling the leaders of the Oklahoma Baptists back to a righteous standard.

q: What other Oklahoma denomination had already passed an abolitionist resolution earlier in 2019?
a: The Oklahoma Free Will Baptists, in May 2019, passed a resolution demanding the total and immediate abolition of abortion, introduced by Pastor Tim Gillespie of Stroud Free Will Baptist Church.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: In passing this resolution, Southern Baptists join Oklahoma Free Will Baptists who, in May, passed a resolution demanding the total and immediate abolition of abortion. It was introduced by pastor Tim Gillespie of Stroud Free Will Baptist Church.

q: Does Free the States believe abolition depends on getting BGCO leadership on board?
a: No. The author welcomes their cooperation but says the movement will succeed regardless because Christians, pastors, and entire congregations across denominations are mobilizing for immediate abolition.
t: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
qt: But if they do not change course and remain anti-abolitionist, they will not ultimately be needed to abolish abortion. Christians, pastors, and entire congregations of various Christian denominations are getting off the sidelines and making their call for the immediate abolition of abortion as murder heard. Abortion is going to be abolished in Oklahoma with or without Dilbeck, Gideon, and Hobbs.

q: What does SB1503 actually do, and how is it modeled on Texas's law?
a: SB1503 creates a Texas-style private civil cause of action allowing citizens to sue abortionists for at least $10,000, but it only protects preborn children once a heartbeat is detectable.
t: Oklahoma Legislature Passes One Texas-Style Abortion Law, Advances Another
qt: The bill is similar to the much-hyped private enforcement heartbeat bill passed in Texas last year which makes it possible for citizens to sue abortionists in civil court for at least $10,000.

q: Why does Silberman call the heartbeat threshold itself a moral failure?
a: Because tying legal protection to a detectable heartbeat denies personhood to younger preborn children, treating them as a class God's law refuses to discriminate against.
t: Oklahoma Legislature Passes One Texas-Style Abortion Law, Advances Another
qt: This is discrimination against children without detectable heartbeats and partiality that God says in His Word that He hates.

q: How does the bill treat the mother who procures an abortion?
a: SB1503 explicitly exempts the mother from any cause of action or prosecution, which Silberman argues only accelerates self-induced chemical abortions.
t: Oklahoma Legislature Passes One Texas-Style Abortion Law, Advances Another
qt: Nothing in this act shall be construed to… authorize the initiation of a cause of action against or the prosecution of a woman on whom an abortion is performed.

q: Why does the author object to enforcement being limited to private civil suits?
a: Because it strips the civil magistrate of its God-ordained duty to wield the sword against evildoers, abdicating government's responsibility under Romans 13.
t: Oklahoma Legislature Passes One Texas-Style Abortion Law, Advances Another
qt: the requirements of this act shall be enforced exclusively through a private civil action described in Section 9 of this act. No direct or indirect enforcement of this act may be taken or threatened by the state[.]

q: What unique provision in Oklahoma's bill goes beyond Texas's version, and why is it dangerous?
a: SB1503 carves out federal agents acting under federal law, which Silberman warns invites a hostile federal administration to keep ordering abortions in Oklahoma.
t: Oklahoma Legislature Passes One Texas-Style Abortion Law, Advances Another
qt: Sections 3 and 4 of this act do not apply to an abortion performed at the behest of federal agencies, contractors, or employees that are carrying out duties under federal law, if a prohibition on that abortion would violate the doctrines of preemption or intergovernmental immunity.

q: How does Silberman reframe the doctrine of preemption against the bill's drafters?
a: He argues preemption requires obeying the highest law—God's law and the Equal Protection Clause—over Supreme Court opinions, demanding immediate abolition rather than deference to federal agencies.
t: Oklahoma Legislature Passes One Texas-Style Abortion Law, Advances Another
qt: If we abide by the doctrine of preemption, Oklahoma must obey Christ and the Constitution by abolishing abortion in defiance of the lower authority of the supreme court's opinion.

q: How does HB4327 differ from SB1503, and is it an improvement?
a: HB4327 drops the heartbeat threshold and applies to any preborn child, but it still permits killing children conceived in rape or incest, so its expanded scope is undermined by exceptions.
t: Oklahoma Legislature Passes One Texas-Style Abortion Law, Advances Another
qt: HB4327 is different from SB1503 in two main ways; one good, one bad. First, HB4327 is not a heartbeat bill. It establishes that an abortionist can be sued for killing any preborn child. Second, HB4327 contains exceptions that allow for the murder of children conceived in rape and incest.

q: What is the bottom-line verdict Silberman wants Oklahomans to take away?
a: He insists none of the 2022 pro-life bills should impress voters because legislators chose them instead of an actual abolition bill that would establish equal justice for the preborn.
t: Oklahoma Legislature Passes One Texas-Style Abortion Law, Advances Another
qt: All four pro-life laws passed in Oklahoma thus far in 2022 were passed instead of legislation that would have actually abolished abortion in Oklahoma.

q: How long has Oklahomans for Life been the primary organizational opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act?
a: Since the bill was first introduced in 2016. OFL has supplied senators with anti-abolition talking points and devised strategies to deny the bill a hearing, with abolitionists obtaining copies of OFL strategy letters in both 2016 and 2019.
t: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
qt: Oklahomans for Life (OFL) has been the primary opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act since the bill was first introduced in 2016. They’ve given Senators anti-abolition talking points and provided strategies for denying the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act a hearing. Abolitionists were able to obtain copies of OFL’s anti-abolition strategy letters in 2016 and 2019.

q: What does Question 3 of OFL's 2020 candidate questionnaire actually ask candidates to commit to?
a: It asks candidates to pledge they will oppose attempts to repeal existing pro-life laws, even by groups that purport to oppose abortion, unless those efforts are part of a comprehensive restructuring approved by Oklahomans for Life. In context, this is a direct request to oppose the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.
t: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
qt: Question 3 of the Oklahomans for Life 2020 candidate questionnaire requests candidates’ opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, by asking them whether they will **“oppose attempts to repeal existing pro-life laws, even by entities purporting to oppose abortion, except as part of a comprehensive restructuring of pro-life laws designed both to withstand court scrutiny and to save the lives of unborn children as supported by Oklahomans for Life.”**

q: Why must an abolition bill repeal existing pro-life regulations to function?
a: Because regulatory pro-life laws presuppose that abortion remains legal so long as certain procedural conditions are satisfied. A statute declaring abortion legal-if-X cannot logically coexist with a statute declaring abortion illegal, so the regulations must be repealed for abolition to take effect.
t: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
qt: A law establishing that abortion is legal as long as the mother is offered an ultrasound cannot coexist with a law establishing that abortion is illegal. A law establishing that abortion is legal as long as the baby is younger than 20 weeks cannot coexist with a law establishing that abortion is illegal. A law establishing that abortion is legal as long as the mother is made aware of the abortion reversal pill cannot coexist with a law establishing that abortion is illegal.

q: How does Silberman characterize the structure of every existing pro-life regulation?
a: He argues every pro-life regulation establishes that abortion remains legal provided the abortionist or mother first complies with a procedural requirement. As long as such laws stand, abortion cannot be abolished, which is why repealing them is one of the five components of an abolition bill.
t: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
qt: Every pro-life law establishes that abortion is legal as long as the abortionist and/or the mother jump through a pro-life hoop first. Abortion cannot be abolished while these laws remain on the books. A bill to abolish abortion must repeal them. This is why the repeal of these abortion-regulations is one of the [five components of an abolition bill]

q: Who does the questionnaire's phrase about "entities purporting to oppose abortion" target, and how does Silberman respond?
a: It refers to Free the States and other state-level abolitionist organizations. Silberman counters that the label more accurately fits National Right to Life itself, which raises millions annually and uses some of that money to oppose efforts to immediately abolish abortion.
t: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
qt: However, the question of whether an organization purports to oppose abortion or actually opposes abortion could rightly be asked of an organization like National Right to Life (of which OFL is a state-level affiliate) which brings in millions of dollars annually and uses part of that money to oppose efforts to immediately abolish abortion.

q: What does Question 2 of the OFL questionnaire reveal about the organization's philosophy on exceptions?
a: Question 2 asks candidates whether, if a no-exceptions ban fails, they will vote for a fallback law allowing abortion in cases of life of the mother, forcible rape reported within seven days, or incest against a minor reported to authorities. Silberman argues this shows compromise is central to OFL's and National Right to Life's approach.
t: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
qt: “If efforts to enact such a law [to prohibit abortion upon reversal of *Roe v. Wade* except to prevent the death of the mother] failed, will you vote for a law that would prohibit abortion except when necessary to prevent the death of the mother, or when the pregnancy is the result of forcible rape (reported to law enforcement authorities within seven days), or when the pregnancy resulted from an act of incest committed against a minor (with the perpetrator reported to law enforcement authorities)?”

q: Why does Silberman say a rape exception is unnecessary in Oklahoma specifically?
a: Because pro-lifers control every lever of state government in Oklahoma, there is no political need to compromise. A firm line could pass a no-exceptions ban, but OFL prepares candidates to compromise their principles from the outset.
t: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
qt: In a state like Oklahoma, there’s no need to compromise and include the rape exception. Pro-lifers control everything. If you really wanted to enact a law to prohibit abortion upon reversal of *Roe v. Wade,* what you have to do is hold a firm line. Instead, OFL works to prepare candidates to compromise their principles from the get-go.

q: What practical voter guidance does the article offer regarding OFL's endorsement?
a: Silberman treats an OFL endorsement as a reliable indicator that a candidate will oppose abortion's immediate abolition, and urges both candidates and voters to treat the endorsement as a liability rather than an asset.
t: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
qt: Oklahomans for Life’s endorsement is a good way to know a candidate is going to oppose abortion’s immediate abolition. Candidates should not want OFL’s endorsement and Oklahomans should not want the candidates they support to have OFL’s endorsement.

q: Why does Silberman say SB612 is unenforceable despite passing the Oklahoma House?
a: Because the bill contains no clause directing the Attorney General or state agencies to enforce it in defiance of federal court rulings. Without that language, courts will simply enjoin the law and abortion will continue, exactly as happened with Alabama's Human Life Protection Act in 2019.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: There are a number of problems with this bill, the first being that it contains no language clarifying that the prohibition contained in SB612 is to be enforced in the face of judicial violations of the right to life.

q: What pending Oklahoma bill did contain the enforcement language SB612 lacks, and what did it require?
a: SB495, left pending in the Senate Health and Human Services Committee, would have required the Attorney General to monitor and direct enforcement of homicide statutes against abortion regardless of contrary federal authority.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: The Attorney General shall monitor this state's enforcement of [Oklahoma homicide statutes] in relation to abortion. The Attorney General shall direct state agencies to enforce those laws regardless of any contrary or conflicting federal statute, regulation, treaty, executive order, or court ruling.

q: What precedent does Silberman cite to predict SB612 will be enjoined?
a: Alabama's 2019 Human Life Protection Act, where Governor Kay Ivey openly admitted at signing that the bill might be unenforceable and Planned Parenthood was confident enough to announce a new Birmingham mega-center within a month.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: Planned Parenthood was so confident that the courts would not allow the law to stand they told the press they would be building a new mega-center in Birmingham a month after Ivey signed the Human Life Protection Act.

q: How does Governor Stitt's posture mirror Governor Ivey's, according to the article?
a: Stitt has openly deferred to the courts on previous bills like HB1102, signaling in advance that he will let federal judges decide whether the law stands rather than enforcing it as the supreme law of Oklahoma.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: We'll let the courts work out if any of those get overturned.

q: What does Silberman call the recurring pattern of pro-life legislation in Oklahoma?
a: He calls it a 'game' in which Republican politicians appear to abolish abortion, the courts predictably enjoin the law, and politicians repeat the cycle the next session without ever protecting preborn children.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: Stitt will sign it. Republican politicians will posture as if they just abolished abortion. The courts will enjoin enforcement of the law. Republican politicians will say "shucks," pretend they did all they could, and start the same process over again next year.

q: Why does the 10-year maximum sentence in SB612 violate equal protection?
a: Capping punishment for killing a preborn child at ten years treats those children as less valuable than born people, which Silberman frames as both biblical partiality and a 14th Amendment violation requiring equal protection of the laws.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: Providing only 10-year maximum sentence treats preborn children as less valuable than born people. This is partiality which God condemns in His Word, and it also violates the 14th amendment of the United States Constitution which establishes that "no state shall… deny to any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the law."

q: Why does Silberman argue SB612 actually protects a major form of abortion?
a: Because the bill explicitly exempts birth control drugs administered before pregnancy, even though hormonal birth control can thin the uterine lining and prevent an already-conceived embryo from implanting, making it abortifacient in those cases.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: These abortions are not recorded but are likely the most prolific form of abortion that take place and are explicitly protected by SB612.

q: How does SB612 treat mothers who procure their own abortions?
a: The bill grants mothers full criminal immunity, allowing only the abortionist to be prosecuted, which Silberman says effectively preserves a mother's ability to murder her own child through self-managed abortion.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: This section does not… authorize the charging or conviction of a woman with any criminal offense in the death of her own unborn child.

q: What is Silberman's overall verdict on whether SB612 is worth celebrating?
a: He flatly rejects the celebratory framing, arguing the bill will never take effect, contains structural injustices even if it did, and was passed in lieu of legislation that would actually have abolished abortion immediately.
t: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
qt: SB612 is not a bill to celebrate. It will not go into effect because of its refusal to stand up to the federal judiciary. Even if it did go into effect, it has major problems that allow for abortion and frame injustice into law.

q: Why does Taylor say repentance cannot be treated as a one-time event tied to conversion?
a: Taylor insists that Christ willed the entire life of believers to be one of repentance, so claiming 'I already repented when I was saved' misunderstands the doctrine. Conversion is only the first instance; the believer's mind must be continually renewed against the world's thought patterns until the very end of life.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: So a Christian who resists the Abolitionist's call to repentance saying "I already repented when I was saved" does not understand repentance well. Repenting from unbelief to belief in Christ is only the first instance of repentance.

q: How does Taylor warn abolitionists against treating their cause as a mark of spiritual superiority?
a: He warns that it is dangerously easy to silently regard abolitionism as a 'spiritual merit badge' that elevates abolitionists above other Christians. The honest difference, he argues, is not personal virtue but the order in which God opened eyes — a gift, not an achievement.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: Let us not think of Abolitionism as a spiritual merit badge that makes us better than other Christians. (I know, we won't *say* that, but how easy it is to *think* that.) The fundamental difference between me and a pro-lifer is that God opened my eyes first.

q: What does Taylor identify as the risk when abolitionists call others to repentance without modeling it themselves?
a: He compares it to wannabe evangelists whose self-righteous presentation guarantees rejection — and who then blame the listener's 'hardness of heart' rather than examining their own approach. Abolitionists who fail to lead by example will come across as obnoxious and shut down the very people they hope to win.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: If we are not leading by example in continued repentance, won't we come across as obnoxious? I suspect most of us have winced to see wannabe evangelists guarantee their hearers will reject their self-righteous Gospel presentation.

q: Why does Taylor argue that intellectual laziness — not lack of zeal — is a primary impediment for many pro-lifers?
a: Taylor confesses this was his own primary obstacle: pro-lifers already believe Roe is immoral, but most have never done the work to see that Roe is constitutionally hollow and can simply be ignored by states. Without that intellectual labor, they default to the incrementalist party line of waiting on the Supreme Court.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: I can't speak for all, but intellectual laziness was a key impediment for me as a pro-lifer. I don't ever expect to find a pro-lifer who does not believe that *Roe v. Wade* is immoral. They need to be *shown* that *Roe* is no "law of the land" but an emperor with no constitutional clothes.

q: How does Taylor frame spiritual blindness as a shared condition rather than a pro-lifer problem?
a: He recalls being a Christian for at least fifteen years before God opened his eyes to a basic truth he had read in Scripture, and notes that Jesus healed more blind people in the Gospels than any other affliction. Abolitionists, he says, are still blind in places they cannot yet see, and impatience with pro-lifers' blindness only feeds pride.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: My fellow abolitionists, not only were we once spiritually blind in many areas, we have no idea where we are *still* blind! Is there a reason the Gospels record Jesus healing more blind people than any other malady?

q: What does Taylor name as the most stubborn impediment to repentance, and how does he say it operates on both sides?
a: Pride is the most stubborn impediment. Pro-life leaders refuse to acknowledge incompetence after 49 years and 63 million dead, but Taylor immediately turns the warning back on abolitionists by citing Christ's teaching on judgment and Paul's question about boasting in what one has merely received.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: The most stubborn impediment to repentance, however, is pride.

q: How does Ezra's prayer model the kind of repentance Taylor wants abolitionists to imitate?
a: Ezra prayed about the offenders' sins as if he himself were guilty — saying 'we' rather than 'they' — even though he had not committed those sins. His humility moved others to confess and act, and Taylor proposes that abolitionists who mourned with the same identification with pro-lifers might see God do similar work in their movement.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: Notice that Ezra prays as if he was just as guilty as the offenders. He had not committed the sins they had, but he had his own, and confessed their sins as if he had joined in committing them, always saying "we", not "they".

q: How does Taylor link the example of Jesus to the abolitionist's posture toward pro-lifers?
a: Even Jesus, the only one who never needed repentance, submitted to John's baptism of repentance and identified Himself with sinners through the cross. Taylor argues that abolitionists who refuse to identify with the sins of pro-life brethren do not yet fully understand the Gospel they claim to preach.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: If we do not identify with the sins of our brethren whose eyes God has not yet opened the way He graciously opened ours, how well do we understand the Gospel?

q: What does Taylor's thief illustration teach about the shape of true repentance?
a: Repentance is multi-layered and progressive: the converted thief must first stop stealing, then learn to support himself by labor (even if it means a serious income drop and learning a trade from scratch), and finally earn enough to give to others in need. Repentance is ongoing rather than a vintage label dated to a single moment.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: Repentance is not like an old wine bottle: "Bottled in 2012." It is ongoing.

q: What sharp warning does Taylor give pro-lifers about complicity in murder?
a: Citing the Apostle's blunt statement that no murderer has eternal life, Taylor says pro-lifers must stop being accessories to murder by funding pro-life organizations that oppose abolition, by pushing state laws that permit murder, and by telling post-abortive women they are not murderers — which prevents full confession and forgiveness.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: They need to *stop* being accessories to murder by supporting pro-life organizations that oppose abolition, and by pushing State laws that allow murder. They must also *stop* telling post-abortive women that they are not murderers, for how can they be fully forgiven of their sin if they don't confess what it was?

q: What danger does Taylor warn even committed abolitionists about regarding their own activism?
a: He cautions that dedication to an activist cause — even a righteous one — can quietly eclipse zeal for Christ Himself. Taylor admits he has had to repent of this and will likely have to again, because Christ tolerates no rivals regardless of how worthy the competing devotion may seem.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: It is easy for dedication to an activist cause to eclipse zeal for Christ. I've had to repent of that, too, and I will do it again as an Abolitionist if I'm not careful. Christ tolerates no rivals, even if it is a good cause.

q: Why does Taylor distinguish between the sharpness owed to pro-lifers and the sharpness owed to those clearly outside the kingdom?
a: Taylor notes that while New Testament epistles addressed church problems, the harshest letters were rare, focused on issues that would keep readers out of the kingdom, and were written by those who knew the readers personally. He has read many critiques of pro-lifers but no argument that being pro-life itself condemns one to hell, so the rebuke must be measured.
t: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
qt: Nevertheless, the harsh epistles were few, they generally addressed issues that would keep readers out of the Kingdom, and the writer knew the readers personally. I have read many complaints about pro-lifers, but so far, no argumentation that being pro-life will drag one to hell.

q: Why is Dan Fisher publicly opposing the leadership of the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma?
a: Fisher believes the BGCO is siding with a forty-five-year incrementalist strategy that merely regulates abortion while waiting for Supreme Court permission, and he feels duty-bound to speak up for those calling for immediate abolition before more babies are killed.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: The leaders of the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma and other groups are standing against preachers and leaders like me who are calling and working for the total and immediate abolition of abortion – as opposed to the incremental approach that has been employed by the Pro-Life movement in general, and the BGCO in particular, for the past forty-five years.

q: What credibility does Fisher bring to this critique of pro-life politics?
a: He is a forty-three-year Southern Baptist pastor, served two terms in the Oklahoma Legislature, and ran for governor on a state-sovereignty and abolition platform, finishing fourth in a ten-candidate Republican primary.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: In addition, I served two terms in the Oklahoma Legislature and ran a strong campaign for governor on a platform of state sovereignty and abolition of abortion (coming in fourth in a field of ten Republican candidates).

q: What did Fisher learn about Republican politicians during his time in the legislature?
a: From inside the Capitol he saw that most self-described pro-life Republicans treat abortion as a political football and refuse to take the steps actually required to end it, hiding behind deference to the federal courts.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: There, “from the inside,” I learned firsthand the way abortion is used as a football in a political pro-life game played by “conservative, pro-life” politicians. I learned that while most Republican politicians *say* they want to end abortion, they are completely unwilling to *do what it takes* to end it.

q: How does Fisher reconcile civil disobedience with biblical commands to submit to authority?
a: He affirms Romans 13 and a healthy respect for office, but insists that submission has limits and that God never requires Christians to give unlimited obedience to evil rulers or evil laws.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: I have consistently preached from biblical passages like Romans 13 that Christians should submit to and honor those in authority.  But, living in a representative republic as I do, I believe submission to authority has its limits and that God does not require *unlimited submission* to evil leaders or laws.

q: What historical Christian precedent does Fisher invoke for resisting tyrannical government?
a: He points to the patriot pastors of the American War of Independence and quotes Rev. Samuel West's 1776 Massachusetts election sermon, which taught that obedience to magistrates cannot be unlimited because rulers exist for the good of the people.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: Having done a good deal of research about the “patriot pastors” of our War of Independence (godly preachers who led the men of their churches and communities to fight against tyranny in the eighteenth century), I have come to believe there are times when faithful, law-abiding people must reluctantly stand against their own government.

q: According to Samuel West, when does a magistrate forfeit his authority?
a: When the ruler punishes the good and rewards the evil, he becomes a tyrant and a minister of Satan, losing his right to be obeyed and instead deserving to be opposed.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: Whenever, then, the ruler encourages them that do evil, and is a terror to those that do well, i.e., as soon as he becomes a tyrant, he forfeits his authority to govern, and becomes the minister of Satan, and, as such, ought to be opposed.

q: Which biblical figures does Fisher cite as models of righteous defiance?
a: He lists the Hebrew midwives, Moses, Esther, Shadrach Meshach and Abed-Nego, Daniel, Jesus, and the apostles as scriptural heroes who refused to comply with ungodly rulers.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: - The Hebrew midwives defying the command of Pharaoh by saving the Jewish baby boys – including Moses’s parents saving him (Ex 1:15-21, 2:1-10)

q: What modern figures does Fisher use to argue that defying unjust law is honored, not condemned?
a: He points to nineteenth-century slavery abolitionists, Corrie Ten Boom, Oskar Schindler, and Dietrich Bonhoeffer hiding Jews from the Nazis, and Martin Luther King Jr. in the civil rights movement.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: We praise the abolitionists of the 1800s who saw slavery as unconscionable and defied the law to free their neighbors trapped in slavery.  We rightly honor people like Corrie Ten Boom and her family, Oskar Shindler, and Dietrich Bonheoffer for defying the law to save Jews and other political dissidents from the evil clutches of the Nazis in the 1930s and 40s.

q: How does Fisher answer the argument that we must obey the Supreme Court because its rulings are constitutional?
a: He lists Dred Scott, forced sterilization, Japanese internment, and same-sex marriage rulings as proof that the Court routinely declares immoral things constitutional, so its declaration on abortion deserves no submission either.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: Should Americans have bowed to the Court’s *ruling* in 1857 whey they declared that blacks were *constitutionally* mere property?  Are we compelled to bow to the Court’s 1927 decree that it is *constitutional* to sterilize American citizens against their will?

q: How does Fisher evaluate the track record of the pro-life movement?
a: Despite good intentions, he sees the movement as a failure, with sixty-two million dead babies nationally and fifteen to twenty killed every business day in Oklahoma.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: Without diving deeply into a debate about the merits and motivations of those in the pro-life movement, let me simply say that the evidence is overwhelming, at least to me, that pro-life efforts, though maybe well intentioned, have, for the most part, failed.  I would hardly reckon sixty-two million dead babies (and counting) a success.

q: Does Fisher dismiss the idea that incremental laws save any babies at all?
a: No, he concedes pro-life efforts have saved some babies, but insists that saving some can never be the goal because Christian faith demands an immediate end to all abortion.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: Have pro-life efforts saved babies?  Of course they have.  But is saving *some* babies by slow, *incremental regulations* the goal?  No!  The goal must be the *immediate* and *complete* end of abortion!  Christian faith demands it!

q: What analogy does Fisher use to expose the moral failure of partial measures?
a: He says no one would call for some slaves to be freed or some Nazi camps to be closed, and if we were the ones being killed we would demand immediate justice rather than a phased solution.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: Knowing what we now know, we would never call for *some* slaves to be freed or for *some* Nazi concentration camps to be closed.  We would call for the immediate end to both atrocities.  I can assure you, if it were you or I who were bound in slavery, imprisoned in a concentration camp, or the target of some abortionist’s suction device or scissors, we would not be calling for an incremental solution.

q: What direct question does Fisher leave for BGCO leaders?
a: He asks whether their measured response is really expected to fix abortion or whether it is just a way to ease a guilty conscience while the killing continues.
t: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
qt: So, why are the leaders of the BGCO calling for slow, measured responses to the wickedness of abortion while thousands/millions continue to be murdered?  Do we really think more regulations will fix the problem – or are we simply attempting to soothe a guilty conscience?

q: What heart-level change does Brewer say American Christians need before any policy victory will matter?
a: Brewer argues the change is not material or political comfort but corporate repentance among pastors, churches, and evangelicals. He frames the underlying problem as slothfulness in the Great Commission rather than mere bad laws, and calls for weeping anguish before God as the necessary precondition for ending abortion.
t: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
qt: The change that we pastors, churches, evangelicals, and anyone who associates themselves with Christ need is a heart change. We need to repent.

q: How does Brewer distinguish his earlier pro-life conviction from the abolitionism he now embraces?
a: He says he had always despised abortion and rejected the excuses offered for it, but other Oklahoma preachers showed him a different doctrine and methodology that reframed abortion as murder requiring immediate abolition rather than incremental regulation, leading him to identify as an abolitionist pastor.
t: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
qt: They showed me the difference in abolitionism and pro-life doctrine and methodology and quickly helped me realized I am an abolitionist pastor.

q: On what biblical basis does Brewer indict supporters of abortion, even indirect ones?
a: He cites Romans 1:32 to argue that those who support the murder of the unborn directly or indirectly are themselves in sin under God's judgment. The verse, in his framing, extends moral culpability beyond the act itself to anyone who takes pleasure in or endorses it.
t: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
qt: I preach that it is a sin to murder the unborn and that anyone who supports it directly or indirectly is in sin also according to Romans 1:32, "Who knowing the judgment of God, that they which commit such things are worthy of death, not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them."

q: Why does Brewer insist abortion is a spiritual matter and not merely a legislative one?
a: He treats abortion law, the rescue of preborn lives, and gospel outreach to mothers as inseparable spiritual concerns. For Brewer, fighting abolition is part of being a soldier of Christ rather than a partisan civic project, which is why he calls pastors to put feet on their faith.
t: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
qt: Abortion laws are a spiritual matter, saving the innocent lives that are murdered every day of the year is a spiritual matter, and reaching these mothers with the gospel is a spiritual matter.

q: What pulpit posture does Brewer demand of fellow pastors regarding incremental pro-life regulations?
a: He calls pastors to wake up, preach hard against sin, offer forgiveness in Christ to women who have had abortions, and explicitly preach against pro-life regulations that he sees as conceding the legitimacy of abortion so long as procedural requirements are met.
t: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
qt: Preach hard against these pro-life regulations that that accept the idea that a woman can murder her child as long as they cover every base and jump every hurdle to get to that point.

q: What concrete event is Brewer recruiting churches to join, and when?
a: He is promoting Abolition of Abortion Month, taking place in January 2021, and asking other pastors to bring their congregations into the fight. The framing combines repentance, anti-apathy preaching, and active participation in the movement.
t: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
qt: I hope to see other pastors get involved with their churches in the fight to abolish abortion, to stop the shedding of innocent blood and seek the forgiveness of God for our collective apathy and compromise.

q: How can a pastor or church practically plug into Abolition Month according to the article?
a: Brewer points readers to the Abolition Month Pastors Group on Facebook, a private group accessible by reaching out to him, Tim Gillespie, Dusty Deevers, other Oklahoma abolitionist pastors, or Free the States. Participating churches receive resources such as educational videos, pamphlets, and weekly themes for engaging their congregations.
t: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
qt: You can learn more by joining the [Abolition Month Pastors Group on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/groups/3475031459233397). It's a private group, so reach out to me, Tim Gillespie, Dusty Deevers, other Oklahoma abolitionist pastors, or Free the States and we can add you.

q: What broader vision of saving the preborn does Brewer articulate beyond simply outlawing abortion?
a: He frames abolition as evangelistic as well as legal: not just stopping the killing but seeing preborn children grow up to live lives pleasing to God and seeing their parents come to faith in Christ. This positions abolition as part of building the kingdom of God under the Great Commission.
t: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
qt: It is about seeing millions of babies saved and given the opportunity to live a life pleasing to God, to see them accept salvation and to see their mothers and fathers come to know Christ in an intimate, personal way.

q: What event is Pastor Ketcher inviting churches to attend, and when is it?
a: He is calling churches to Abolition Day at the Oklahoma State Capitol in Oklahoma City on February 9th, where activists will demand legislators immediately end abortion in Oklahoma rather than continue to regulate it.
t: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
qt: Would you also be willing to join us on February 9th at the State Capital in Oklahoma City to let your voices be heard in defense of the fatherless, in defense of the unborn?

q: How does Ketcher characterize churches that ignore abortion?
a: He likens silent or accommodating churches to wartime traitors and oblivious nobles, accusing them of being absorbed by a culture of death rather than confronting it as the salt of the earth they are commanded to be.
t: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
qt: Some churches have become like Hanoi Jane fawning over the instruments of death in the name of love and compassion.  Some churches are a modern day Tokyo Rose who seeks to demoralize any who dare cross the gate to say “babies are murdered here.” But some churches are just contemporary nobleman who abstain from the fight because their only concerns are land, deed, and titles.

q: What biblical analogy does Ketcher use to force a moral choice on his readers?
a: He invokes the judgment of Solomon, asking readers whether they would be the woman who pleads for the child's life or the one willing to let the child be cut in two—framing passive pro-lifers as collaborators with the executioners.
t: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
qt: If King Solomon were to stand before you and say “bring me a sword and dividing the living child in two,” would you be the woman who says “Let the child live or the woman who says let the child die?

q: What specific posture does Ketcher want churches to take during January?
a: He asks whether congregations will spend the full month of January in prayer for unborn children scheduled for execution in Oklahoma, treating the entire month as a sustained season of intercession leading up to Abolition Day.
t: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
qt: would your church spend the whole month of January in prayer for the unborn awaiting their execution in Oklahoma?

q: How does Ketcher describe the cultural disorientation surrounding abortion?
a: He paints contemporary America as a moral inversion where evil is treated as good, victims of abortion are dehumanized into 'a clump of cells,' and the public has been psychologically captured into defending its captors.
t: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
qt: Like a bad case of Stockholm syndrome we convince ourselves that our captors are benevolent and that a tiny human is just a clump of cells.

q: What is Ketcher's theological framing for why Christians must show up?
a: He grounds the call directly in Christ's command to love one's neighbor and care for 'the least of these,' arguing that whatever is done—or not done—to the unborn is done to Christ Himself.
t: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
qt: Join us on Abolition Day like lives depend on it. Because in fact they do. Join us like Christ has commanded us. Because in fact He has. Whatsoever you have done to the least of these He says “you have done unto me”.

q: What does Ketcher say is the demand abolitionists are bringing to elected officials?
a: They are demanding, without compromise, that elected officials immediately end the murder of the unborn in Oklahoma and pass abolition rather than incremental regulation.
t: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
qt: Would you gather with us to demand action from those we elected to cease immediately the murder of the unborn in Oklahoma? To call, without compromise, for the abolition of Abortion.

q: How does Ketcher critique churches that wave the Great Commission while doing nothing about abortion?
a: He accuses them of substituting slogans for action, calling their version of Christianity 'the great omission' and saying they have been culturally hellenized into accepting child sacrifice.
t: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
qt: Waving their banners siting the great commission, they live like the great omission. Professing to be good samaritans in reality they’ve become hellenized by a culture of death and child sacrifice.

q: What does HB16 actually change about the legal protection of babies who survive abortion attempts?
a: Despite Leach and Abbott's claim that HB16 grants 'full protection of the law,' the only substantive change is upgrading the penalty for letting such a baby die from a misdemeanor to a third-degree felony. That is far short of treating the killing as murder, which is what equal protection would require.
t: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
qt: On September 1st, the penalty for allowing a baby born alive to die on the table will become a third-degree felony rather than a misdemeanor. The change is miniscule, and babies who survive abortions are not given anywhere near full protection of the laws against murder like they should be.

q: Why does Silberman call the pro-life legislative cycle a 'game'?
a: He argues the pro-life lobby manufactures a perpetual stream of incremental bills, each spun as a victory, so that voters remain emotionally satisfied and politically dependent on the same politicians who refuse to abolish abortion. Because the supply of marginal bills is essentially unlimited, the cycle can run forever if voters never demand more.
t: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
qt: This is the game pro-life politicians play to keep the pro-life voting bloc dependent on them. That's all HB16 is – an unjust law that has absolutely no effect on anything except to give pro-life politicians and establishment lobbyists something to show pro-life voters. They will continue to play this game in perpetuity if we let them because there's an infinite number of worthless and/or unjust bills that could be spun as pro-life victories.

q: How does the Oklahoma episode with Treat and Smalley illustrate the same dynamic?
a: After Senators Greg Treat and Jason Smalley killed the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act and faced flyers, billboards, and a flood of calls, they passed three pro-life bills that accomplish nothing. Because pro-life voters still treated the bills as wins, the legislators were able to weather the backlash and keep their seats.
t: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
qt: In response, they passed three pro-life bills which accomplish nothing and saved their careers because people still see these pro-life bills as victories, even when these bills are transparently used as the excuse not to abolish abortion.

q: What role did Jeff Leach play in the fate of HB896?
a: Leach is named as the gatekeeper who blocked the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act from receiving a committee vote, and HB16 functions as his political alibi. Authoring and passing HB16 gives him a 'pro-life' credit to wave at constituents while obscuring his role in killing the bill that would have ended abortion in Texas.
t: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
qt: Jeff Leach is the man responsible for preventing the Abolition of abortion in Texas Act (TX HB896) from getting a committee vote. For the sake of his career, he needs to distract people from the fact that he killed HB896 with the fact that he authored and helped pass HB16.

q: What promise did Abbott make to Jeremiah Thomas, and how did he handle it?
a: In June 2018, Abbott personally promised dying 16-year-old Jeremiah Thomas that he would work for abortion's abolition in the 2019 session. After receiving more than 70,000 petitions reminding him of that pledge before his state of the state address, Abbott declined to mention abolition and, according to a source close to him, did nothing to support HB896 because he did not actually support it.
t: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
qt: In June of 2018, Abbott promised dying 16-year-old Jeremiah Thomas that he would work for abortion's abolition in the 2019 session. In February, Abbott's office was delivered more than 70,000 petitions by Jeremiah's family and friends reminding him to keep his promise to Jeremiah.

q: What hypothetical legislative escalation does Silberman predict, and what is its rhetorical purpose?
a: He sketches a session-by-session pattern in which the same politicians upgrade the born-alive penalty from third-degree to second-degree to first-degree felony before finally writing equal protection in 2025. The point is to expose how the inadequacy of HB16 is itself the product line: each future 'fix' becomes another marketable victory rather than the actual abolition that could have been enacted in the first place.
t: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
qt: It wouldn't be surprising if next session (which for Texas is in 2021), a pro-life politician points out the injustice of this born-alive act and introduces a bill to make murdering a baby born alive after an abortion attempt a second-degree felony. In 2023, the pro-life lobby will call that law unjust and pass a law making it a first degree felony, and then in 2025, they'll actually write a law providing equal protection of the law against murder for babies who survive botched abortions.

q: According to Silberman, what specifically must voters do for abortion to actually be abolished?
a: He says abolition will not happen until voters refuse to accept the staged 'victories' that politicians and lobbyists use as cover and instead demand total and immediate abolition. The mechanism is moral and political: as long as voters celebrate marginal bills, the politicians have no incentive to do more.
t: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
qt: When we call out the game for what it is, reject the worthless "pro-life victories" that the politicians and lobbyists use as their excuse not to abolish abortion, and demand abortion's total and immediate abolition, abortion will be abolished. But not until then.

q: What procedural failure nearly killed the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion at the 2021 Missouri Baptist annual meeting?
a: The resolutions committee was supposed to bring Cochrun's motion (and one other) to the floor at the close of their report but instead walked off stage after presenting only their own five resolutions.
