Wash yourselves; make yourselves clean; remove the evil of your deeds from before my eyes; cease to do evil, learn to do good; seek justice, correct oppression; bring justice to the fatherless, plead the widow’s cause.” – Isaiah 1:12-17

q: Why can no Christian plead ignorance about the duty to oppose abortion, according to the article?
a: Because Leviticus 20:4-5 holds people accountable for failing to act against known child sacrifice, and Proverbs 24:12 anticipates and rejects the excuse 'we did not know.' God weighs the heart and repays men for what they did and did not do.
t: Refuting Toby Sumpter's "Eight Tenets of Smashmouth Incrementalism"
qt:
> “If you say, “Behold, we did not know this,” does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? Does not he who keeps watch over your soul know it, and will he not repay man according to his work?

We all know that abortion is happening among us. We cannot hide behind the excuse of ignorance. God holds men accountable for what they do and what they don’t do regarding the killing of our neighbors.

q: Why does it matter that the Oklahoma Legislature operates on a two-year cycle when I'm trying to get an abolition bill heard?
a: Bills filed in the first year of a legislature don't die at the end of that session. They remain alive for the second year, which means an abolition bill that was bottled up in 2021 is still in play in 2022. Understanding the cycle keeps activists from giving up too early or accepting the claim that a bill is 'dead' before it actually is.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: The legislature works on a two-year cycle.  The 58th Legislature was seated following the 2020 elections.  They have two regular sessions: the 2021 Session and the 2022 Session. Bills submitted for the 2021 session that were not either voted down or voted through are still alive for the 2022 Session.

q: When can a bill actually be considered 'dead' for the session?
a: Procedural deadlines are not absolute. Leaders can route around them, so a bill is only truly dead once it has been voted down on the merits or once the legislature gavels out at the end of the second session of the cycle. Organizers should keep pressing through every supposed deadline.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: That said, there are ways around these deadlines. No bill is ever really dead until it is either voted down or until the final gavel falls in late May of even-numbered years.

q: What is a 'shell bill' and why should abolitionists know the term?
a: A shell bill is a near-empty bill containing only the title of law and the title of the act, with the substantive language added later by amendment or substitute. Knowing this lets activists recognize when a legislator is keeping a vehicle alive that could later carry abolition language, rather than being fooled by the bare bones text.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: A term you will hear often is "shell bill." A shell bill is a bill that has minimal language in it, usually just the title of law and the title of the act.

q: If a legislator says 'this bill has to go to the health committee,' how should I respond?
a: Push back. There is no rule binding an abolition bill to a particular committee. Gillespie points to multiple committees that could legitimately receive an abolition bill, including Judiciary, Public Safety, and the House State Powers Committee, and notes the committee's name is largely irrelevant.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: In short, there are any number of committees to which a bill of abolition could be assigned. In fact, the committee name really doesn't matter at all. Do not let them tell you otherwise.

q: Why does Tim Gillespie insist abolition bills belong in Title 21 rather than Title 63?
a: Title 63 is the public health and safety code, which is appropriate for regulating abortion as health care. Title 21 is the criminal code where homicide laws live. Because abolitionists treat abortion as murder, the law criminalizing it must be placed alongside other homicide statutes, not among medical regulations.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: If your goal is to regulate abortion, then yes, Title 63 is where the law belongs. Our goal is not the regulation of abortion as health care.  The criminalization of abortion belongs with the other homicide laws in Title 21. Do not let them tell you otherwise.

q: When my representative says 'I'll have to get with staff,' is that a legitimate answer?
a: It can be a stalling or blame-shifting tactic. Staff serve at the pleasure of the legislator, not the reverse. Gillespie has personally watched legislators correct staff errors, so the constituent should make clear that the legislator, not unelected staff, is responsible for the bill.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: The respective staffs work for the legislators, not the other way around. However, weak-kneed legislators that want to avoid controversy will often try to deflect blame onto the House or Senate staff.

q: What is 'striking title' and why is it dangerous for an abolition bill?
a: Striking title removes the section-of-law designation that a bill must have to be enacted. The Senate uses the maneuver routinely, sometimes legitimately to keep a bill alive while wording is fixed, but often as a quiet way to kill the bill, because a hostile colleague can simply refuse to consent to reinstating the title.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: A bill cannot be passed into law without its Title. Remember, the Title is the section of law where the new law will reside. It is its permanent residential address, so to speak. You cannot put a law into statutes if you don't specify where it goes.

q: How do I tell whether a strike-title is a real working pause or a stealth kill?
a: Watch the posture of the legislator who moved to strike title. If that person is hostile to the bill, they will simply withhold consent to reinstate the title, ending the bill quietly. Friendly intent versus hostile intent is the tell.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: It is easy enough to determine which is the case. If the one asking to strike the Title is hostile toward the bill, he simply will not give consent to reinstate the title, thus killing the bill. Do not let them tell you otherwise.

q: Why might a legislator file an abolition bill they have no intention of pushing?
a: Filing the bill placates a constituent who asked for it. Behind the scenes the legislator can quietly tell the committee chair not to hear it, sometimes with caucus-wide coordination. The bill exists on paper while everyone involved deliberately ensures it goes nowhere, deceiving the activists who requested it.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: If a constituent approaches their representative or senator with an issue, sometimes they will submit a bill just because they don't want to anger a constituent but will tell the chairman they don't really want them to hear the bill. Sometimes this is done between the representative/senator and the chair and sometimes this is a caucus-wide decision. In other words, there is often collusion with the intent to dupe a constituent.

q: When my legislator blames the committee chair, the pro tem, or the governor, how should I evaluate that?
a: Treat blame-shifting as evidence of complicity. A legislator who refuses to use the influence they actually possess is no better than the leaders they are blaming. Their inaction is a moral choice, not a procedural inevitability.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: But the truth is, if your legislator won't support the abolition bill with the influence they have, they are no better than the committee chair, pro tem, and governor. Do not let them tell you otherwise.

q: How do I answer the claim that there simply isn't enough time to hear an abolition bill?
a: Point to the frivolous bills that absorb legislative time. Gillespie cites a proposed cloth-napkin mandate for barbeque restaurants and a Bigfoot hunting season as examples of trivia the legislature has time to entertain. If they have time for that, they have time to consider laws protecting unborn children.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: A few years ago, there was a bill filed to require that barbeque restaurants in Oklahoma use cloth napkins. Another bill would have implemented a Bigfoot hunting license and a Bigfoot hunting season. These are frivolous bills that are a waste of taxpayers' money and time.

q: What does the inner culture of the Oklahoma Capitol look like, and why does it matter?
a: Gillespie compares the Capitol to a middle-school playground where chambers retaliate against one another and individual legislators refuse to vote for each other's bills out of personal grievance. Activists need to expect this immaturity rather than assume good-faith deliberation.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: First, when you walk into the Capitol building you are walking onto the archetypal middle school playground. Almost every year, at some point the Senate gets mad at the House and refuses to hear any House bills until the House agrees to the Senate's demands…and vice versa.

q: Is there proof that the Oklahoma Legislature can move quickly when leadership wants it to?
a: Yes. When Governor Stitt prioritized Constitutional Carry in 2019, the legislature jettisoned its normal deadlines and put the bill on his desk in roughly a month. The same machinery is available for abolition if leaders treat it as a priority.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: In 2019, when Governor Stitt took office, he let it be known that he wanted the bill allowing permitless carry of a handgun (Constitutional Carry) to be the first bill he signed into law. They threw out the process and deadlines and got that bill on the Governor's desk in about a month.

q: What is the bottom-line test for whether the abolition of abortion is actually a legislator's priority?
a: Effort, not rhetoric. If the bill isn't moving, leadership has chosen not to move it. Procedural excuses are a tell that abolition is not on their priority list, and constituents should hold them accountable on that basis.
t: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
qt: When it is a priority, they will make it happen. If it isn't happening, it isn't a priority. Do NOT let them tell you otherwise.

q: Why does Free the States refuse to call Waskom, TX a sanctuary city for the preborn?
a: Because the ordinance that actually passed contains exceptions allowing for abortion and explicitly conditions enforcement on Supreme Court permission, both of which surrender the moral and legal premise that abortion is murder. By those concessions, the city affirms rather than defies the legal regime that permits child sacrifice.
t: Regarding Waskom: Still Searching for a Sanctuary City
qt: Waskom, TX is NOT a sanctuary city for preborn boys and girls. It is a city that almost passed a righteous ordinance but chose to pass an iniquitous and servile one instead.

q: What was the original Waskom ordinance like before it was changed?
a: The original draft, brought by Jon Speed, Mark Dickson, and Rusty Thomas, contained no exceptions, no loopholes, and openly defied the Supreme Court — a genuine attempt at a sanctuary city ordinance.
t: Regarding Waskom: Still Searching for a Sanctuary City
qt: The original ordinance they presented to the Waskom City council had no exceptions, no loopholes and legitimately defied the Supreme Court. Jon, Mark, Rusty, and others worked hard to pass a good ordinance.

q: How does Silberman say the lawyer who reshaped the ordinance repeated the mistakes that produced Roe v. Wade?
a: Silberman argues that Justice Blackmun rejected Texas's 14th Amendment claim in Roe precisely because Texas itself did not treat preborn human beings as equal persons under its own laws — abortionists got fines while accomplices got nothing. By writing exceptions into the Waskom ordinance, the lawyer recreated that same self-defeating inconsistency.
t: Regarding Waskom: Still Searching for a Sanctuary City
qt: Justice Blackmun, the author of the Roe decision, wrote that the State of Texas’ claim that preborn human beings had a Constitutional right to equal protection according to the 14th amendment was nonsense because even Texas didn’t provide preborn human beings equal protection.

q: What is the theological case against writing exceptions into an anti-abortion ordinance?
a: Including exceptions abandons God's command 'you shall not murder,' fails the biblical duty to defend the fatherless (including children conceived in rape), and violates the command to love one's preborn neighbor as oneself. Compromise on these points surrenders the moral authority that comes from God's word.
t: Regarding Waskom: Still Searching for a Sanctuary City
qt: by including exceptions and bowing to the Supreme Court, they’ve given up their moral authority. They’ve given up the Word of God which says “you shall not murder.” They’ve failed in God’s command to stand up for the fatherless, for who is more fatherless than a child conceived in rape?

q: How does the Waskom ordinance compare to Greg Treat's Oklahoma SB195, and why does that matter to abolitionists?
a: Free the States booed Treat's SB195 out of the chamber for being a watered-down substitute for an actual abolition bill, and they consider Waskom's ordinance to be even worse. The comparison is meant to bind activists to a consistent standard: if SB195 deserved rebuke, so does Waskom.
t: Regarding Waskom: Still Searching for a Sanctuary City
qt: When Greg Treat rejected an abolition bill and put forward a bill similar to Waskom’s ordinance, we booed him out of the chamber. The Waskom ordinance is actually worse than Treat’s SB195. It needs to be thoroughly rebuked by all abolitionists.

q: What lesson does the 2016 Oklahoma abolition bill offer to legislators tempted to add exceptions to win passage?
a: Holding a hard line against exceptions in 2016 was uncomfortable, but it produced legislator and lobbyist converts (Senator Silk and John Michener), educated thousands of Oklahomans, and reframed the entire fight. Compromise would have produced a bill but none of the long-term movement gains.
t: Regarding Waskom: Still Searching for a Sanctuary City
qt: Holding a hard line through that process made for some uncomfortable conversations, but is what educated the legislators, lobbyists, and public about abolitionism. Senator Silk, who authored the bill, is now a bold leader of the abolitionist cause. John Michener who lobbied for the bill is now a bold leader of the abolitionist cause.

q: What does Silberman say is the proper response when a court rules that murder is legal?
a: He frames it through a thought experiment: if the Supreme Court declared it legal to murder residents of Norman, OK or Waskom, TX, citizens would obviously ignore the ruling. The same defiance is morally required for the preborn, which is why conditioning enforcement on Supreme Court permission is unacceptable.
t: Regarding Waskom: Still Searching for a Sanctuary City
qt: if the Supreme Court ruled that it were legal to murder people who live in Norman, OK or Waskom, TX, we would, of course, ignore that court decision. We need to do the same for our preborn neighbors.

q: How many years has the Oklahoma Senate refused to even hear the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, and how does Silberman characterize the legislature's annual pattern?
a: Since 2016 the Senate has refused even to give the bill a hearing, and each year a newly passed regulation is presented as a victory while abortion continues unabated. Silberman points to this cycle as the backdrop against which SB1728 is being celebrated.
t: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
qt: Every year since 2016, the Senate has refused to even hear the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. And every year, we’re told that, even though the mass murder continues, a newly passed abortion regulation is cause for celebration.

q: What does SB1728 actually authorize, despite being called the Unborn Person Wrongful Death Act?
a: It does not ban abortion; it merely lets the mother or grandparents sue the abortionist when specific regulatory violations occur, such as failing to document parental consent, informed consent, or screening for impaired judgement. The bill leaves abortion itself fully legal.
t: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
qt: SB1728 allows the mother who had the abortion or the grandparents of the murdered child to sue the abortionist if the abortionist violates an abortion regulation; for instance if the abortionist fails to document parental consent or judicial bypass before murdering the child of a minor, if the abortionist fails to obtain and document compliance with informed consent regulations, or if the abortionist fails to document that they screened the mother for impaired judgement, among a few other regulatory violations.

q: Why does Silberman call SB1728's use of "unborn person" language schizophrenic?
a: Because the bill's title implies that unborn children are persons, yet its substance still treats their killing as legal so long as paperwork is in order. Personhood language without equal protection, he argues, contradicts itself.
t: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
qt: Senate Bill 1728 fully affirms the legality of the practice of murdering unborn children. It simply provides the abortionist with a few hoops to jump through to make the unborn person’s death rightful.

q: What dramatic prediction did Senator David Bullard make about SB1728's impact on Oklahoma abortions?
a: Bullard claimed the bill would save thousands of unborn babies annually and turn Oklahoma into an "abortion desert" once mothers and their parents started suing abortionists. Silberman flags this as a remarkable claim worth holding the senator accountable for.
t: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
qt: Senator David Bullard claimed that because of SB1728, “thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year,” and that Oklahoma will be made into an “abortion desert” when mothers who have abortions or their parents choose to sue abortionists.

q: What statistic does Bullard rely on to justify the prediction, and where does he claim it comes from?
a: He cites a Justice Foundation figure that up to 69 percent of U.S. abortions are coerced, alleging that mothers are lied to about gestational age or safety and even drugged before consenting. The press release attributes the figure to the Justice Foundation directly.
t: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
qt: According to the Justice Foundation, up to 69 percent of abortions in the U.S. are performed without legal consent and are coerced.  They’ve found that mothers are persuaded to go through with abortions through illegal coercion including being lied to about the age or health of their babies or about the safety of the actual procedure.  Many women are drugged prior to actually agreeing to the procedure thinking they are only getting examined, but wake up to realize the procedure is complete.

q: What problem does Silberman identify with the 69 percent coercion figure?
a: The Justice Foundation study cannot be located on its website and the organization didn't respond to inquiries; meanwhile, a similar pro-life-cited study only found 64 percent of post-abortive women felt "pressure," which is not the same as illegal coercion. Silberman concludes Bullard and the foundation likely conflated the two.
t: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
qt: The study doesn’t appear to be anywhere on the Justice Foundation website and the organization did not respond to a request for the study, but there is a similar [study](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/8265918_Induced_abortion_and_traumatic_stress_A_preliminary_comparison_of_American_and_Russian_women) frequently cited by pro-lifers in which 64% of American post-abortive mothers reported feeling pressure from others while deciding to have the abortion. “Feeling pressure” is obviously not the same thing as being coerced. Bullard and the Justice Foundation study have likely conflated the two concepts.

q: Mathematically, what would have to happen in 2021 for Bullard's promise to be vindicated?
a: With roughly 5,000 abortions performed in Oklahoma each year, the 2021 totals would need to drop by thousands for the prediction to come true. Silberman calls this so absurd it is hard to believe Bullard takes his own claim seriously.
t: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
qt: There are roughly 5,000 abortions in Oklahoma annually. For Bullard’s claim to be true, the 2021 abortion totals will have to be “thousands” less than that. This is an absurd claim, so absurd that it’s hard to believe Bullard honestly believes it himself.

q: Why does Silberman insist abolitionists not forget Bullard's claim once next year arrives?
a: Because remembering broken promises arms abolitionists to expose the next round of compromise bills as the same tactic dressed up differently, helping the public see incremental pro-life legislation as a way to avoid abolishing abortion.
t: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
qt: Remember it next year when the abortion facilities are just as busy and the pro-life politicians are trying to sell a new compromise bill as something that will save thousands of lives.

q: What did Rep. John Talley say when asked about abolishing abortion at the June 16 debate?
a: Talley flatly stated that Oklahoma already has sufficient abortion legislation and reaffirmed that position when challenger Brice Chaffin asked him to confirm it.
t: Rep. John Talley (R): Keep abortion legal because "we have enough abortion laws"
qt: Talley's shocking position on the issue: "We have enough abortion laws right now."

q: Does SB13 actually criminalize doctors who treat life-threatening tubal or ectopic pregnancies the way Talley implied?
a: No. The bill contains an explicit immunity clause for physicians who act in a triage situation to save the mother after exhausting treatments to preserve the fetus, including in tubal or ectopic pregnancies.
t: Rep. John Talley (R): Keep abortion legal because "we have enough abortion laws"
qt: "A physician or other health care professional shall be immune from criminal or civil liability for the death of a fetus resulting from an emergency, including but not limited to an ectopic or tubal pregnancy, if the physician or professional exhausts all possible treatments to preserve the life of the fetus."

q: Why does Free the States distinguish between "laws against abortion" and "laws regulating abortion"?
a: The article argues that incumbents like Talley defend the status quo as adequate, but the existing legal regime only regulates abortion rather than prohibiting it, leaving thousands of babies legally killed in Oklahoma each year.
t: Rep. John Talley (R): Keep abortion legal because "we have enough abortion laws"
qt: The reality is, we don't have laws against abortion. We have laws regulating abortion.

q: What did Talley say about abortifacient drugs like Plan B?
a: Talley argued that abolishing abortion is problematic because it would affect the legality of Plan B and similar drugs, indicating he wants those drugs to remain legal even though they can cause an abortion after conception.
t: Rep. John Talley (R): Keep abortion legal because "we have enough abortion laws"
qt: Talley also explained that "the problem with saying you're going to abolish [abortion]" is that it's going to effect the legality of abortifacient drugs like Plan B which can either prevent conception or cause an abortion after conception has already occurred. Talley apparently wants abortifacient drugs to remain legal as well.

q: How does the article characterize the current legal status of abortion procedures in Oklahoma under Talley's preferred regime?
a: Silberman describes the status quo as one in which dismemberment abortions are still legal, and he argues Talley considers that legal landscape acceptable.
t: Rep. John Talley (R): Keep abortion legal because "we have enough abortion laws"
qt: It is literally legal to tear human beings limb-from-bloody-limb in Oklahoma and Republican incumbent John Talley believes the law is just fine as it is.

q: What does HB1539 actually do, and how soon would it take effect?
a: HB1539, the Indiana Protection at Conception Act, would totally and immediately end abortion in Indiana by adding a new Article 51 to the state code that asserts a compelling state interest in protecting human life from the moment of fertilization.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: HB1539 would totally and immediately abolish abortion in the state. It has been assigned to the Indiana House Judiciary Committee, which is made up of nine Republicans and four Democrats, and is chaired by Rep. Jerry Torr (R-Carmel).

q: How does the bill define when human life begins?
a: The bill anchors the protection of life at fertilization, treating the union of human ovum and human sperm as the legal beginning of human physical life subject to Indiana's protection.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: Chapter 1 of the new article establishes that life begins “when a human ovum is fertilized by a human sperm.”

q: On what constitutional grounds does the bill claim Indiana can act despite Roe v. Wade?
a: The bill grounds Indiana's authority in the Tenth and Ninth Amendments, the Declaration of Independence, and the consent-of-the-governed origin of both the federal and Indiana governments, asserting that protecting life is a reserved and inalienable-rights matter.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt:
Sec. 2. Indiana’s authority to assert its interest in protecting human physical life from the moment that human physical life begins is drawn from the following:
>
> 1. The Tenth Amendment to the Constitution of the United States, which provides that all powers not delegated to the United States elsewhere in the Constitution are reserved to the states or to the people.
> 2. The Ninth Amendment to the Constitution of the United States, which provides that the enumeration of certain rights within the Constitution must not be construed to deny or disparage other rights retained by the people.
> 3. The Declaration of Independence, which acknowledges that life is endowed to all persons as an inalienable right.
> 4. The fact that the governments of the United States and Indiana were instituted by the consent of the people in 1787 and 1816, respectively, to secure the inalienable rights acknowledged by the Declaration of Independence.

q: What does the bill say Indiana officials should do if a federal court tries to strike it down?
a: The bill instructs Indiana to treat any contrary federal court decision as nonauthoritative and void, and directs all state officials and agencies to enforce the law regardless of any court ruling against it.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: Sec. 4. Indiana’s interest in protecting innocent human physical life from the moment of conception shall be enforced by Indiana government officials and agencies, regardless of any court decision to the contrary.

q: Does the bill criminalize federal agents who try to interfere?
a: Yes. Section 2 of the enforcement chapter makes it unlawful for any federal official, agent, or contractor to enforce any federal action that interferes with Indiana's protection of life from conception.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: Sec. 2. It is unlawful for any official, agent, or employee of the United States government or an employee of a private entity providing services to the United States government to enforce any act, law, treaty, order, rule, or regulation of the United States government that interferes with Indiana’s interest in protecting human physical life from the moment that human physical life begins…

q: How is John Jacob persuading hesitant Christian Republican colleagues?
a: Jacob says he confronts fellow Christian legislators directly with a moral ultimatum: regulating abortion is sin, and a Christian has no option but to abolish it.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: “I’m having conversations with these guys and I’m saying, ‘If you’re a Christian, you don’t really have an option here. It’s not an option to sit here and say that we’re going to regulate abortion. That is sin. We have to abolish abortion.’”

q: Why is the 2021 session being framed as more hopeful than past attempts?
a: There is a new Speaker, Todd Huston, who reportedly told Nisly and Jacob he wants to start fresh, and the bill has been moved out of the Public Policy Committee (where it usually died) into the Judiciary Committee.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: “The other thing that is different is the that the bill has been assigned to a different committee. In previous years the bill was assigned to The Public Policy Committee, which is where bills go to die. This year it has been assigned to the Judiciary Committee chaired by Jerry Torr.”

q: Who was the previous main obstacle to abolition in Indiana, and how did he treat Nisly?
a: Former Speaker Brian Bosma, a self-described pro-life Republican, was the chief opponent who publicly called abolition unconstitutional and used his power to sideline Rep. Nisly for supporting it.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: Former Speaker Brian Bosma, a pro-life Republican, had been the primary opposition to abolition, once telling reporters that abolishing abortion was unconstitutional. Bosma also used his power to sideline Nisly because of Nisly’s support of abolishing abortion.

q: What does Jacob say his primary win means for the rest of the Indiana GOP?
a: Jacob argues his upset of incumbent Republican Dollyne Sherman shows voters are done tolerating do-nothing pro-life Republicans and want clear support for abolition over regulation.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: “People are sick and tired of the do-nothing Republicans who have so departed from even their affirmed platform. And that’s why I won,” Jacob said. “I didn’t run any kind of smear campaign. I just made it clear that I stood for abolition and that my [Republican] opponent stood for the regulation of abortion.”

q: What concrete actions does the article ask Indiana residents to take?
a: Sign the Abolish Abortion Indiana petition, call Judiciary Chair Rep. Jerry Torr to demand a hearing and vote, call seven specific representatives who promised to coauthor the bill but haven't, and attend the February 16 March for Abolition at the State Capitol.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt:
- Call Rep. Jerry Torr at (317) 234-9499 asking him to give a hearing and vote to the Protection at Conception Act.
- The following IN State Representatives publicly promised to coauthor the Protection at Conception Act while campaigning but have yet to do so. Call them to remind them of their promise to their voters.

q: Why does Jacob argue the bill has no excuse to die in committee this year?
a: Because Republicans hold a supermajority and dominate the Judiciary Committee, Jacob says there is no procedural or political reason for the Protection at Conception Act not to receive a hearing and vote.
t: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
qt: “The Protection at Conception Act is a good and righteous bill,” Jacob said. “It should be voted on, and with a Republican majority in that committee, there’s no reason it should die in committee.”

q: Why does Silberman insist that Christians cannot blindly trust Republican politicians on abortion?
a: Because Republican politicians, like all sinful people, primarily act in their own self-interest rather than on principle or even on their stated platform. Christian voters therefore have a duty to hold them accountable just as forcefully as they criticize Democrats.
t: Republican Establishment Plays Pro-Life Organizations Like a Fiddle
qt: What this means for Christians is that we can’t take for granted the idea that Republican politicians will legislate and govern righteously. Though we may consider them to be “our team,” we have to hold their feet to the fire. We cannot blindly support them. We have to speak prophetically to them when they act immorally just as we do to Democrats.

q: What dynamic does Silberman identify that causes social movements to lose their independent voice?
a: The desire for power and access incentivizes movements to stop criticizing their nominal political allies, and over time the aligned political party absorbs the movement and bends it to the party's purposes rather than the movement's stated goals.
t: Republican Establishment Plays Pro-Life Organizations Like a Fiddle
qt: As a result, social movements rarely speak critically of the political party more closely aligned with their objectives, and there is a tendency for movements to be subsumed by political parties that seek to harness those movements for the party’s own purposes.

q: What concrete evidence does the article offer that GOP leadership does not actually want pro-life policy victories?
a: Silberman cites Rand Paul's reported account that a Senate Republican leader refused to allow a vote on defunding Planned Parenthood specifically because they feared the vote might succeed, demonstrating that party leaders see the abortion issue as more useful unresolved than resolved.
t: Republican Establishment Plays Pro-Life Organizations Like a Fiddle
qt: Rand Paul was famously told by a Senate Republican leader that party leadership wasn’t going to allow a vote on defunding Planned Parenthood because “we might win.”

q: How does Silberman characterize the gap between Republican capability and Republican action at the state level?
a: He argues many Republican-controlled state legislatures already possess the political power necessary to abolish abortion outright, but instead deliberately choose to pass weaker regulatory bills year after year, which is precisely where pro-life lobbyists should be applying pressure but instead provide cover.
t: Republican Establishment Plays Pro-Life Organizations Like a Fiddle
qt: It’s not that Republican politicians in many states don’t have the power to abolish abortion; it’s that they do, but choose to pass unjust abortion regulations year-after-year instead. This is a clear instance where pro-lifers need to hold Republican politicians to a higher standard, but instead, the pro-life lobbyists are in lockstep with the Republican establishment.

q: According to Alaska activist Matt Johnson, why does the Republican establishment love incrementalism?
a: Because nibbling at the edges of Roe gives politicians political cover to pay lip service at election time without ever having to take a courageous or risky vote on legislation that would actually end abortion.
t: Republican Establishment Plays Pro-Life Organizations Like a Fiddle
qt: Alaska Family Action wants to pursue an incremental strategy of nibbling away at the edges of Roe v. Wade and the abortion industry. The Republican Establishment, of course, loves this idea. It gives them cover. They can continue to pay lip-service at election time, and they don’t really have to vote on legislation that requires much courage or risk on their part.

q: What is the transactional relationship Johnson describes between pro-life organizations and Republican power brokers?
a: Pro-life groups receive insider access to politicians, and in exchange they deliver exactly what those politicians want, namely a strategy that protects political power without forcing any decisive action against abortion. Johnson concludes the politicians want power, period.
t: Republican Establishment Plays Pro-Life Organizations Like a Fiddle
qt: Organizations like Alaska Family Action are happily given access, because they’re giving the Republican Establishment exactly what they want. They want political power. Period. That’s all.

q: What does Silberman say Christians must do if pro-life organizations refuse to break their alignment with the GOP establishment?
a: If pro-life organizations will not change course, then Christians themselves bear the responsibility of refusing to be played by those organizations, just as those organizations are being played by the Republican establishment.
t: Republican Establishment Plays Pro-Life Organizations Like a Fiddle
qt: It’s time for pro-life organizations to stop being played by the Republican establishment. If they refuse to change course, then it will be incumbent upon Christians to stop being played by the pro-life organizations.

q: Why does Free the States insist that SB1859 is not what Oklahoma Christians and Republicans were asking for?
a: Multiple Oklahoma religious and political bodies — the Oklahoma Baptists, the Oklahoma Association of Free Will Baptists, and the Oklahoma State Republican Party — had recently amended their platforms to demand abortion's total and immediate abolition, and the thousands who rallied at the Capitol were demanding SB13. SB1859, which only tweaks health codes and definitions of professional conduct, falls far short of that demand.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: Senate Bill 1859 is not what the Oklahoma Baptists, the Oklahoma Association of Free Will Baptists, and the Oklahoma State Republican Party had in mind when they all passed amendments to their respective platforms in the past nine months calling for abortion’s total and immediate abolition. They were demanding Senate Bill 13.

q: What does Hunter accuse the committee of doing by advancing SB1859?
a: He charges that the committee has effectively begun killing SB13 — the abolition bill — by offering SB1859 in its place as a lesser substitute, and warns that Oklahoma Christians will not be appeased by the swap.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: What you’ve done instead is you’ve begun the process of killing SB13 and you’ve offered a lesser substitute bill in its place. The Christians of Oklahoma will not be appeased or fooled by it.

q: Why does the letter argue that compromising with abortion is no longer politically safe for legislators?
a: Hunter says the political paradigm has shifted: platform changes by Christian and Republican organizations and the massive Capitol crowd show that legislators will now be condemned, not praised, for compromising with abortion.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: Compromise with abortion is iniquitous, but it’s also political folly. As demonstrated by the various organizations’ platform changes and the enormous crowd last Tuesday, the paradigm has shifted and you no longer will receive praise for compromising with abortion.

q: On what biblical and constitutional grounds does Hunter say SB1859 is discriminatory?
a: Because life begins at fertilization — not at the detection of brain waves or a heartbeat — a bill that draws its line elsewhere fails to extend equal protection to every human being, violating both Scripture and the U.S. and Oklahoma Constitutions.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: Life does not begin when an abortionist detects brain waves or a heartbeat, it begins at fertilization. By not defining life as beginning at fertilization, SB1859 is a discriminatory bill that violates God’s word, and by not providing the equal protection of the law to all human beings, SB1859 is in violation of the US and Oklahoma Constitutions.

q: Why does the letter predict that SB1859 will never actually go into effect?
a: SB1859 contains no language defying federal court abortion jurisprudence, so it will be struck down under the “undue burden” standard. Hunter argues the only viable path is for the state to treat Roe and related rulings as legal nullities, since the Supreme Court has no authority to legalize murder.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: SB1859 contains no language defying evil and unconstitutional court opinions. It thus has no chance of ever going into effect. The courts will rule SB1859 a violation of the “undue burden” standard and we all know it.

q: What lesson does Hunter draw from Alabama's Human Life Protection Act?
a: Even though Alabama's bill was widely cheered, it bowed to the Supreme Court and never took effect — evidenced by Planned Parenthood breaking ground on a new mega center in Birmingham only months later. The takeaway: pro-life bills that defer to the federal courts cannot stop the bloodshed.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: The Alabama Human Life Protection Act may have sounded good to many when it passed last year, but because it bowed to the supreme court, Planned Parenthood broke ground on a new mega center in Birmingham just a few months after its passage.

q: Why is it absurd, according to the letter, to trust abortionists to enforce SB1859's heartbeat or brainwave threshold?
a: Even setting aside its other defects, SB1859 hands enforcement to the very people committing the murders — expecting abortionists to make a good-faith effort to detect a heartbeat or brain waves before proceeding.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: Even if the bill weren’t unjust and certain to never go into effect, the bill trusts the murderers themselves to detect the heartbeat or brain waves, as if these are people who will make a good faith effort to do so.

q: What is wrong with framing abortion as "unprofessional conduct"?
a: Hunter calls the descriptor a "shocking understatement" — reducing mass murder to a matter of professional ethics is a failure of legislative duty, and merely revoking medical licenses is woefully inadequate to the scale of the killing.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: “Unprofessional conduct” as a descriptor for abortion is a shocking understatement. To treat murder as something that is merely unprofessional is to fail utterly in your duties as a legislator.

q: How does Hunter distinguish between legislators with bad intentions and those with good intentions who still back compromise bills?
a: He says some legislators are intentionally using incremental bills to appease Christians while blocking abolition, while others mean well but mistakenly think compromise is acceptable. He calls the well-meaning ones to recognize that incremental bills practically delay abolition and empower its opponents.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: There are some in this legislative body who are intentionally supporting incremental compromise bills as part of a concerted effort to appease Oklahoma Christians while these very same people are the ones preventing abolition. There are also some who have good intentions but errantly think we should try to make compromises where we can make them.

q: What posture does Free the States promise toward legislators who change course and support abolition?
a: Hunter pledges that abolitionists will not hold past disagreements against any legislator who repents and acts justly going forward — prior conduct will be "water under the bridge" for those who do the right thing.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: We will not turn anyone who does the right thing away because of past differences. We will honor you if you do what is right and actions taken previously will be water under the bridge.

q: What concrete action does the letter ultimately ask the committee to take?
a: Vote SB1859 down and give SB13 a hearing — the letter frames voting down the substitute as the necessary first step toward stopping the bloodshed.
t: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
qt: Senate Bill 1859 will be opposed by the Christians of Oklahoma. You should oppose it as well. Please vote against SB1859 and give SB13 a hearing.

q: Why does Silberman commend Randy Alcorn even while disagreeing with him?
a: Silberman recognizes Alcorn as someone genuinely seeking to understand abolitionism with humility, openness, and a willingness to consider that he might be wrong. That posture, Silberman argues, puts a Spirit-indwelt believer on a path toward abolitionism.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: There is much to take issue with ideologically which we will discuss in this article, but Alcorn's disposition and curiosity toward the issue are admirable. Alcorn appears to be seeking to ask questions and understand.

q: How does Silberman answer the claim that abolition bills cannot pass in places like Oregon?
a: He argues abolitionists are not ignorant of political realities; they simply ask different questions. Pro-lifers ask how to cater to the political reality, while abolitionists ask what has the power to change it - which is uncompromising proclamation of God's word, not watered-down messaging.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: The question an Oregon Christian must ask is "What has the power to change this political reality?" But instead, the pro-lifer asks "How can I cater to this political reality?"

q: What does Silberman identify as the fundamental difference between pro-life and abolitionist strategy?
a: Pro-lifers tailor their strategy and message to their audience, while abolitionists tailor theirs to Christ. Abolitionist messaging is therefore consistent in every setting - proclaiming repentance, faith in Christ, and submission to His Word that outlaws murder.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: One of the fundamental differences between pro-lifers and abolitionists is that pro-lifers cater their strategy and messaging to their audience while abolitionists cater our strategy and messaging to Christ.

q: Does Silberman believe Alcorn lacks personal radicalism or sacrifice?
a: No. He explicitly affirms Alcorn's history of suffering for the pro-life cause and commends him for it. The disagreement, Silberman says, is not about personal courage but about whether Alcorn will ask biblical questions and arrive at biblical political solutions.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: It is not that Alcorn is opposed to radical thinking or action. It's that when it comes to the political applications of abolishing abortion, he and the Pro-Life Establishment do not ask Biblical questions or arrive at Biblical solutions.

q: How does Silberman respond to the charge that abolitionism amounts to "save all babies, or save none"?
a: He reframes the question. Abolitionists do not ask whether a measure saves babies but whether it is just and conformed to God's word. They save babies one-by-one through activism but refuse to support laws that regulate when and how children may be murdered, because good ends do not justify immoral means.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: The abolitionist does not ask, "Does this save babies?" The abolitionist asks, "Is this just? Is this in accordance with God's word?"

q: Are abolitionists opposed to gradual progress over time?
a: No. Silberman concedes social change happens over time and that abolition will likely take years. The immediatism-incrementalism debate is not about how long the fight takes but about what abolitionists demand - and that demand must be as uncompromising as justice itself.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: The immediatism-incrementalism debate does not revolve around how long the battle takes (though the immediatist strategy will certainly achieve the desired end more quickly). The debate revolves around *what our demand should be*.

q: Why does Silberman say incremental laws cannot ground their arguments in anything substantial?
a: Because laws like 20-week bans or heartbeat bills imply abortion is legal before the arbitrary cutoff, they violate both the Sixth Commandment and the Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection guarantee. That lack of foundation makes them powerless to change culture and can even dehumanize the unprotected.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: An incrementalist's demand for a 20-week abortion ban or a heartbeat bill cannot be defended from God's word or the Constitution, as these laws – by implying the legality of abortion prior to the arbitrary standard they set for who counts as fully human – violate both.

q: What does Silberman say is wrong with the pro-life narrative about Wilberforce supporting incremental slave-trade bills?
a: He calls the narrative scandalously false. Wilberforce in fact opposed the slave-carrying regulation bill and explicitly argued that gradualists - not slave traders themselves - became the most effective supporters of the slave trade by cooling parliamentary zeal with half-measures.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: This is exactly opposite of the truth. If the truth is the North Pole, Alcorn and Challies are on the South Pole. If the truth is black, Alcorn and Challies are white. If the truth is hot, Alcorn and Challies are ice cold.

q: How did Wilberforce himself describe the gradualists in Parliament?
a: Wilberforce wrote that the gradual abolitionists, by proposing gradual rather than immediate abolition, dashed the cup of happiness from the African's lips and effectively became the system's most efficient supporters - the real maintainers of the slave trade.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: [L]et me most seriously urge on the conscientious deliberations of those who…by their fatal proposal of gradual, instead of immediate abolition, dashed the cup of happiness from the lips of the wretched African, at the very moment when at last he appeared likely to taste it, and who thus proved in fact the most efficient supporters of the Slave Trade.

q: Who does Silberman say modern pro-life politicians actually resemble in the British abolition story?
a: Henry Dundas, not William Wilberforce. Dundas professed agreement with abolition's goal but used a procedural amendment to gut Wilberforce's bill into a gradualist measure - the same maneuver pro-life leaders use today to kill abolition bills while claiming pro-life credentials.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: This is precisely the scheme pro-life politicians and lobbyists have concocted wherever an abolition bill has been introduced. The pro-lifers kill the abolition bill and pursue the incremental strategy with more gusto than they ever have before.

q: Were the leading 19th-century American abolitionists also immediatists?
a: Yes - unanimously. The 1832 American Anti-Slavery Society declaration rejected any scheme as a substitute for immediate and total abolition, and figures like Garrison, Douglass, Stowe, Tubman, and the Tappan brothers all rejected incrementalism. Historians and reference works treat "immediatist" and "abolitionist" as synonyms.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: The American Anti-Slavery Society's 1832 declaration of sentiments stated, "We regard as delusive, cruel and dangerous, any scheme of expatriation which pretends to aid, either directly or indirectly, in the emancipation of the slaves, or to be a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of slavery."

q: Why does Silberman call Alcorn's drowning-children analogy a category error?
a: Personal rescue - jumping into the water or going to the abortion mill - is inherently good. But legally regulating which preborn children may be killed is not analogous to saving the reachable child; it is doing evil that good may come. The analogy collapses two morally distinct categories.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: Taking personal action to save children (jumping in the water, going to the abortion mill) is inherently good. Doing evil that good may come by legally regulating which preborn children can be drowned or murdered in the womb is not.

q: What does Silberman believe will actually bring revival to a culture that loves child sacrifice?
a: Not telling people what they want to hear and not asking for compromised regulations. Revival comes through the Spirit moving through uncompromising proclamation of God's Word; making compromised demands actually stunts revival.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt: The Spirit moving through the uncompromising proclamation of the Word of God brings revival. Stop asking political questions and playing political games. Ask Biblical questions and come to Biblical solutions.

q: What two questions does Silberman think will determine Alcorn's trajectory on abolitionism?
a: First, whether Alcorn will ask biblical questions and arrive at biblical solutions in the political realm. Second, whether he will be willing to risk his relationships with other pro-life leaders if he comes to see abolitionism as biblically faithful.
t: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
qt:
1. Will he ask Biblical questions and come to Biblical solutions in the political context?
2. Is he willing to risk losing relationships with other pro-life leaders upon discovering the Biblical fidelity of abolitionism?

q: Why do Klusendorf and Newman take such strong issue with the SBC Resolution on Abolishing Abortion?
a: Riley argues that the resolution, by calling for abortion's immediate abolition without exception or compromise, condemns the regulatory bills that constitute the entire pro-life legislative playbook. Because incremental restrictions are the only strategy professional pro-lifers will tolerate, a resolution that brands those tactics as compromise threatens the legislative model their organizations exist to defend.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: The reason Newman and Klusendorf take serious issue with the resolution is because it condemns bills that regulate murder in the hopes of saving lives, which is the only legislative strategy deemed acceptable by professional pro-lifers; if not by their words, then certainly by their actions

q: What is 'Scott's Magical Declaration Fallacy' and why does Riley name it after the article's authors?
a: Riley coins the term to mock the recurring strawman that abolitionists believe abortion will vanish the instant a legislature declares it abolished, like Michael Scott yelling 'I declare bankruptcy!' He names it after Michael Scott and Scott Klusendorf because Klusendorf and Newman explicitly accuse abolitionists of magical thinking, and the label sticks the strawman back to its source.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: Indeed, the authors write, "The abolitionist response is a kind of magical thinking: just decree it." This notion is inextricably linked to the frequently touted incrementalist idea that "current political realities" make immediate abolition impossible. Due to the popularity of this strawman, we shall refer to it as Scott's Magical Declaration Fallacy, in honor of Michael Scott and Scott Klusendorf.

q: Do abolitionists actually believe states can defy the federal government on abortion, given current 'political realities'?
a: Riley concedes that defying the federal government on abortion will be harder than defying it on guns, marijuana, or immigration, but he flatly denies it is impossible. He points to the historical precedent of nullification over slavery, a far more controversial issue, to show abolitionists are demanding something difficult, not something fantastical.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: Incrementalists often respond that defying the federal government over abortion will be more difficult than defying for those issues, which is true. But that doesn't mean it's impossible. It was done over much more [controversial issues like slavery]

q: How does Riley use a hypothetical Supreme Court ruling on child sexual slavery to expose the incrementalist view of judicial authority?
a: Riley argues Klusendorf and Newman would never extend their 'comply with the court' logic to a ruling that legalized seizing children for sex trafficking or legalized all murder, which proves the disagreement is not about strategy but about whether preborn humans count as full image-bearers. Once that ageist exception is removed, defiance of an unjust court becomes obligatory rather than utopian.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: We do not believe for one moment that Klusendorf and Newman would go along with a supreme court decision that allowed for government officials to seize children and sell them into sexual slavery until they reach the age of eighteen, nor do we believe they would go along with a decision to legalize all murder. The issue here is one of ageism, not of strategy.

q: What theological grounds does Riley give for civil governments defying Roe v. Wade?
a: He grounds defiance in Christ's universal authority and Romans 13's framing of civil rulers as deacons of God's wrath against evildoers. Because every level of government answers to Christ, refusing to punish the murder of preborn neighbors is dereliction of a divinely assigned office, not principled restraint.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: Beyond any kind of argument from the way our government has been set up or what our culture is willing to accept is the notion that Christ is on the throne and all the earth belongs to Him (Matthew 26:64, Matthew 28:18). Christ owns America and every level of Government are deacons of His wrath against evildoers (Romans 13:4).

q: Did pastor Bill Ascol really call for disfellowshipping any politician who disagrees with abolitionists?
a: Riley says Klusendorf and Newman strip the context out of Ascol's remark to make it sound like a purity purge. Ascol was specifically targeting Oklahoma pro-life politicians who have personally killed abolition bills, not legislators whose only offense is preferring an incremental approach.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: A reader might be led to believe Ascol is calling for the disfellowship of legislators who support incremental bills when the actual context Newman and Klusendorf conveniently leave out is that Ascol was calling for pro-life politicians in Oklahoma – who *have been solely responsible for killing the bills of abolition –* to be disciplined.

q: Do abolitionists really believe everyone who disagrees with them is evil?
a: Riley denies the charge and reframes it: the appeal is not to abolitionist tribal loyalty but to Scripture as the standard before which all consciences are bound. The accusation of evil is leveled at voting against immediate protection of preborn humans, which they argue is condemned by passages like Romans 1, Psalm 94:20, and Isaiah 10:1-2.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: We do not claim, "if you're not with us, you're evil." We claim that if you're against Scripture, you need to repent and submit to Christ. Abolitionists appeal to the standard upon which all men will give an account: Scripture.

q: Do abolitionists oppose every incremental step, or do they distinguish between kinds of increments?
a: Riley draws a sharp line between 'ungodly increments' that frame abortion as healthcare to be regulated and 'godly increments' like the SBC resolution itself, which advance the cause without legitimizing the killing. Abolitionists welcome incremental progress toward abolition; they reject incremental laws that codify the legality of murder.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: the real reason is because we oppose *ungodly increments* that treat abortion as healthcare instead of like the holocaust that it is, all while acknowledging that the effort to abolish abortion will gain steam in increments – the resolution itself being *a godly increment* to that end.

q: Who is actually trading babies' lives for ideology, abolitionists or pro-lifers?
a: Riley reverses the charge by pointing out that abolitionists hold no legislative majorities capable of killing incremental bills, while pro-life politicians demonstrably kill abolition bills in states like Oklahoma every year. The 'undisclosed future date' rhetoric, he says, applies to pro-lifers stalling for the next Supreme Court vacancy, not to abolitionists.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: Every year a bill of total and immediate abolition is proposed in Oklahoma, it is pro-lifers who kill it. The fact is, abolitionists do not have the votes in any state government to kill incremental bills, and if they did they would immediately pass an abolition bill and establish justice for our preborn neighbors making incremental bills forever irrelevant.

q: If pro-life states have the most regulations, why is Riley so dismissive of incrementalism's track record?
a: He points to Oklahoma, one of the most pro-life states with record numbers of regulatory laws, where the abortion rate is rising rather than falling. After roughly fifty years and sixty million dead children, Riley argues the strategy of waiting for the court to change its mind has been empirically falsified.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: These regulatory bills are used as the excuse to delay the abolition of abortion, and – had it not been for that delay – every baby in a state like Oklahoma would not have been murdered. In such states, the abortion rate is going up – not down – despite being one of the most pro-life states in the Union and having record amounts of regulatory laws on the books.

q: How does Riley summarize what abolitionism actually is, against the strawman?
a: He frames abolition as the consistent Christian response to legalized genocide: it treats every preborn person as an image-bearer, treats abortion as a holocaust rather than a healthcare issue, demands righteousness from civil government in defiance of a rogue court, and rests on Christ's lordship rather than judicial supremacy. Far from a 'pie in the sky' fantasy, he calls it the only position internally coherent with Christianity.
t: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
qt: Abolition is a biblical, consistent, righteous response to legalized genocide. It acknowledges the truth that all humans are made in the image of their Creator; it treats abortion like the holocaust that it is; it demands righteousness from the government in defiance of lawless, unconstitutional opinions of a rogue court; and, above all, it asserts that Christ is on the throne, not man, and He gives life and takes it away

q: What incident is being responded to, and who was involved?
a: At a Haskell County GOP meeting, Senator Larry Boggs physically shoved his primary opponent Warren Hamilton and verbally threatened Daniel Navejas of The Ekklesia of Oklahoma after being challenged on his abortion record and his refusal to support SB13.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: At Tuesday's Haskell County GOP meeting, Senator Larry Boggs shoved his opponent Warren Hamilton and made threats against Daniel Navejas of The Ekklesia of Oklahoma.

q: How did Boggs himself describe the confrontation to the press?
a: Boggs told the McAlester News Capital that he was simply inviting Hamilton to step outside for a quieter conversation and framed the criticism as out-of-state 'sleazy attacks' from someone who recently moved from Texas.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: I said let's go outside and talk without disturbing this meeting… Where I come from, offering to 'take it outside' is an invitation to stand down. My opponent just recently moved here from Texas, so maybe these kinds of sleazy attacks work in Austin, but Oklahomans stand up for themselves.

q: Why does the author find Boggs' 'let's talk outside' explanation implausible?
a: Because Boggs' actual words on video were a confrontational challenge, not an invitation to dialogue, and the voters can decide for themselves which interpretation matches the footage.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: His exact words were, "step outside and you square right up." The voters of Senate District 7 can decide whether this language invites civil dialogue or whether it sounds like an invitation to brawl.

q: How does Sam Riley use Scripture to challenge Boggs' professed Christianity?
a: By citing Proverbs to argue that a wise believer welcomes rebuke rather than retaliating, contrasting Boggs' defensive tirade and 'screw you' with the biblical posture toward correction.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: That word says, "Better is open rebuke than hidden love (Proverbs 27:5)," "A fool despises his father's instruction, but whoever heeds reproof is prudent (Proverbs 15:5)," and "A rebuke goes deeper into one who has understanding than a hundred blows into a fool (Proverbs 17:10)."

q: How did the Boggs campaign characterize the abolitionists at the meeting?
a: The campaign called them a 'violent mob' of 'thugs' who allegedly surrounded Boggs and shouted 'baby killer' and other insults, comparing the experience to his post-Vietnam reception.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: he claims a "violent mob" of "thugs" surrounded him to call him "baby killer" and other "vile insults" like the people who said the same thing to him after returning from Vietnam.

q: What does the article say the video Boggs himself shared actually proves?
a: The video Boggs released contradicts his own claims by showing no insults from the crowd, his own campaign manager physically restraining him, and Boggs being the one who approached and threatened Navejas.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: No one in the video called him a baby killer, used any vile insults, or was violent. In fact, Boggs' campaign manager is seen physically restraining Senator Boggs and Boggs is the only person using vile language.

q: How did Warren Hamilton respond during the heated moment, according to the author?
a: Hamilton attempted to de-escalate by appealing to Boggs' campaign manager about the political optics and urging him to take responsibility for getting Boggs out of the situation.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: Hey man, we probably disagree wholeheartedly on just about everything. But a sitting senator getting western is not gonna look good for anybody, all right? You take care of him and you follow him around. You got a responsibility. You take care of him, okay?

q: How does the author answer claims that abolitionists 'provoked' Boggs?
a: He argues calling for repentance is a normal part of abolitionist witness and that any wise person, especially a Christian, should welcome rebuke rather than treat it as provocation.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: A Christian telling another Christian to repent should not be provocative. To the wise, rebuke is welcome. It gives a person an opportunity to evaluate themselves and realign their thinking and actions to the mind of Christ.

q: What does the article identify as the real reason to vote Boggs out, beyond the confrontation?
a: His refusal to support SB13, the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, which the author argues will result in another 5,000 Oklahoma children being killed in 2021.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: The main reason he should be opposed is because his refusal to support The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act helped seal the fate of 5,000 more Oklahoma babies who he is supposed to represent.

q: What metaphor does the author use to describe pro-life Republican promises on abortion?
a: He compares the establishment's promise to end abortion to a carrot dangled in front of a horse that never actually gets eaten, accusing pro-life politicians of perpetually deferring justice.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: For the Republican pro-life establishment, the end of abortion is a carrot on a string and Oklahoma voters are the horse. *The horse never gets the* *carrot*.

q: What concrete electoral action does the article ask SD7 voters to take?
a: Vote Boggs out in the August 25th run-off and replace him with Warren Hamilton to dramatically improve the chances of passing SB13 in the next legislative session.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: In the August 25th run-off, SD7 voters have an opportunity to replace someone who does not intend to abolish abortion soon with a righteous man who will strive to see abortion ended immediately.

q: How does the author frame Oklahoma's collective responsibility for abortion?
a: He treats the state as corporately guilty for the killing of 5,000 image-bearers each year and calls both legislators and citizens to repent of either sanctioning or tolerating the practice.
t: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
qt: The State of Oklahoma is guilty of allowing the murder of 5,000 image bearers of God annually. We need to repent.

q: What is the central thesis Myers wants pro-life Christians to confront when watching this documentary?
a: Myers argues the film exposes an inconsistency between what pro-lifers say they believe about the personhood of the preborn and what pro-life legislation actually does. The documentary lays out how typical pro-life bills fail to treat the preborn as equally valuable to the born, even though pro-lifers verbally affirm that equality.
t: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
qt: the film expertly shows that pro-life legislation is not consistent with what most pro-lifers say they believe.

q: How does the film define Abolitionism, and what mnemonic structures its tenets?
a: The documentary frames Abolitionism as the doctrine answering how the Bride of Christ should combat great societal evil. Pastor Dusty Deevers organizes the five tenets into the acrostic G.A.T.E.S., and the film walks through each tenet one at a time.
t: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
qt: In the film, Pastor Dusty Deevers puts the five tenets of Abolitionism into an acrostic, saying “Abolitionists have their G.A.T.E.S.”

q: What specific critique does Bradley Pierce voice in the film against the Pro-Life Movement's legislative record?
a: Bradley Pierce says that while the Pro-Life Movement has spent nearly five decades affirming preborn personhood verbally, the bills the pro-life lobby promotes do not legally treat preborn human beings as equally valuable to those already born. The rhetoric and the legislation, in his telling, do not match.
t: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
qt: the bills that the pro-life lobby has been introducing and pushing and calling on all of the grassroots to support don’t actually do that. They don’t actually treat a person before they are born as equally as valuable as a person after they are born.

q: What range of activism does the film visually depict?
a: Myers notes the documentary captures an unusually wide spectrum of testimony and on-the-ground engagement. It moves from sidewalk witness at abortion mills to settings inside churches and statehouses, and from intimate family conversations to large-scale public demonstrations.
t: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
qt: it shows a spectrum of engagement from footage outside abortion mills to houses of worship and legislatures, from living rooms to street engagement and massive rallies.

q: How does Myers describe his emotional response to viewing the film?
a: Even as a longtime abolitionist who anticipated the film favorably, Myers reports being deeply moved on watching it. He describes recurring tears as he heard pastors and laypeople speak hard truths in love and humility, including testimonies of repentance over abortion, apathy, or resistance to abolition.
t: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
qt: I was smitten afresh and blown away by the message and delivery of *A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain*. Tears kept springing to my eyes again and again as I heard piercing truths, spoken in love and humility, by brothers and sisters in Christ.

q: What kinds of repentance does the documentary highlight among Christians it features?
a: The film foregrounds testimonies of Christians, including pastors, who have been brought to repentance over distinct failings: a personal abortion in the past, apathetic inaction in the present, or defending pro-life methodology while resisting biblical abolitionist responses to abortion.
t: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
qt: fellow Christians, including pastors, share how God brought them to repentance, whether it be over an abortion in their past, or apathetic inaction, or clinging to pro-lifeism and fighting against the Biblical response to the abortion Holocaust.

q: What does Myers ask viewers to do after watching the film?
a: Myers issues a four-step charge: watch the film, weigh its message against Scripture, listen to testimonies and consider the tenets, and if the case holds up biblically, embrace it, act on it, and share the film widely.
t: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
qt: Listen to the testimonies. Weigh out the tenets. If this is according to the Scriptures, then embrace it and take action, and pass on this film far and wide!

q: Why does Myers describe the present moment as a turning point for serious Christians?
a: Myers opens the review by framing abolitionism as a swelling, audible movement that demands the attention of Bible-believing Christians. He treats the documentary as evidence that the movement has reached a stage of visibility and momentum that should not be ignored.
t: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
qt: It is happening! The thunder of Abolitionism is rumbling across the land. And serious, Bible believing Christians need to take notice.

q: When is intransigence a virtue rather than a vice, according to this article?
a: Burkey argues that uncompromising stubbornness becomes a duty when it concerns holding to scripture as the sole rule of faith and refusing to compromise with what she calls modern-day child sacrifice. In those domains compromise is not merely strategically foolish but morally offensive to God.
t: Righteous Intransigence, Regardless of Results
qt: In certain regards, however, this stubborn, uncompromising attitude is not only good, but it is our duty. For example, we must be intransigent in holding to the scriptures as our sole infallible rule of faith. When it comes to the fight against modern-day child sacrifice, compromising with our culture of death is not merely a foolish move strategically, but it is a stench in God’s nostrils.

q: What was the abolitionist resolution at the Missouri Baptist convention actually asking the legislature to do?
a: The Missouri Baptists for the Abolition of Abortion Resolution called the state legislature to enact only just, righteous, and God-fearing legislation on abortion and to repent of its previous pro-life compromise that approved legalized, regulated abortion.
t: Righteous Intransigence, Regardless of Results
qt: The goal was to pass an abolitionist resolution on abortion that was consistent with God’s Word, calling the state legislature to only enact just, righteous, and God-fearing legislation on abortion, and to repent of their previous pro-life compromise and approval of legalized, regulated child sacrifice.

q: Why does Burkey refuse to count a 'strongest pro-life resolution ever' as a real win?
a: Even though the abolitionist pressure produced the strongest pro-life language Missouri Baptists had ever adopted, the final resolution still praised and celebrated compromise with abortion regulation. She compares treating that as victory to perfuming a manure pile, because dressing up compromise with iniquity does not stop it from stinking before God.
t: Righteous Intransigence, Regardless of Results
qt: But these men were not interested in decorating the manure pile of compromise with iniquity, the regulation of abortion. They were not attracted by throwing perfume bombs on the stench of child sacrifice that rises to God’s nostrils. Instead, they were calling the convention to repentance.

q: How should an abolitionist behave after investing months in an effort that ends in public defeat?
a: Rather than spinning the loss into a personal win or taking credit for moving the needle, the two men Burkey watched cast minority votes against the compromised resolution and continued to call the convention to repentance. They cared more about God’s assessment than their reputation among brothers and friends who took their stance as an insult.
t: Righteous Intransigence, Regardless of Results
qt: But these men cared more about what God thought of them than their own reputations among men. They concerned themselves first and foremost with standing on the Word of God, even when their brothers, sisters, and friends took their position as an insult, and forced them to stand alone in a room full of professing Christians.

q: Whom does Burkey say Christians should choose as role models, and on what basis?
a: She warns against worldly criteria like accolades, fame, accomplishments, age, or perceived success and instead urges Christians to honor those who refuse to abandon God’s Word and walk with the multitude. The defining mark of a Christian life worth imitating is righteous intransigence regardless of the personal cost.
t: Righteous Intransigence, Regardless of Results
qt: We should point each other to examples like these, and look up to those who refuse to compromise with iniquity, regardless of the results. We likewise must hold fast to a biblical position even when it means standing against the hundreds of professing Christians around us. Our lives as children of God must be marked with righteous intransigence, whatever sacrifices we may face as a result.

q: What does Burkey think the convention’s rejection of the abolitionist amendment revealed about the body?
