a: She reads the vote as an expression of pride: the messengers were repulsed by the suggestion that they themselves had something to repent of for celebrating pro-life legislation that compromises with the culture of death, so they killed the amendment.
t: Righteous Intransigence, Regardless of Results
qt: Long story short: the convention reacted in pride, repulsed at the thought that they might have something to repent of, and voted down the abolitionist amendment to their resolution.

q: How can abolitionists cite a Confederate apologist like Dabney without endorsing his racism?
a: Riley is unsparing about Dabney's sin: he calls Dabney's views toward black people 'rebellion against God and sin against Dabney's black contemporaries' and devotes the first half of the article to documenting his white supremacy in detail. The point of citing Dabney is precisely that even a man this morally compromised on slavery saw the logical force of immediatism—if anyone should listen to Dabney's argument for abolitionism, it is the modern incrementalist who already shares his sympathy.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: There is much to appreciate about Dabney, who was a skilled writer and insightful theologian on many subjects. But his views toward black people were rebellion against God and sin against Dabney's black contemporaries. This is no small matter.

q: What exactly did Dabney concede that makes him 'right on abolitionism'?
a: Dabney conceded that the only thing keeping him from being a thorough abolitionist was his belief that the Bible permitted slavery. Strip that premise away and his own reasoning forced him into immediatism: a moral evil cannot be lawfully continued for the sake of convenience or feasibility, and consequences are God's business while duty is ours.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: If I did not believe that the bible taught [that slavery was morally innocent], **I must, in consistency,** **be a thorough abolitionist**. I cannot see how men can say in one breath, that slavery is a *malum per se* and in the next, **that a conscientious man may lawfully continue it for the present, because of the difficulties of emancipation**.

q: Why is immediatism a biblical demand and not just a political strategy?
a: Riley argues that Scripture knows no scheme of partial repentance—God demands total and immediate obedience. Abolition bills are simply the legal expression of the prophetic call to repent, while incremental bills assume a posture of submission to the Supreme Court rather than to Christ enthroned. Even Dabney conceded the principle: sin must be ceased at once.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: My conscience and my bible teach me that, if an act is *wrong*, in its own essential nature, sin, **I am to cease it at once**. I have no right to look at the supposed evil consequences or difficulties of the reformation.

q: Doesn't the difficulty of abolishing abortion all at once justify a more cautious legislative approach?
a: Riley says no—and uses Dabney's adultery analogy to make the point. Telling a man he should not leave an adulterous relationship because doing so would be inconvenient or dangerous is absurd; the same logic applies to abortion. Faithfulness commits the consequences to God: 'Fiat justitia, ruat cælum'—let justice be done though the heavens fall.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: What would be the thought of the man, who should plead that he ought not to cease living in an adulterous connexion, because a change would be dangerous and inconvenient?—Would not you answer, "unless you cease that connexion at every risk, you are an immoral man?"

q: How does Riley critique Doug Wilson specifically given Wilson's reliance on Dabney?
a: Riley notes that Wilson cites *A Defence of Virginia* in *Black and Tan* and even credits Dabney with persuading him to revise his view of the Civil War. Yet Wilson refuses to apply Dabney's own immediatist logic to abortion, instead warning that Idaho would face federal troops if it abolished. Riley charges that Wilson's persistent emphasis on consequences scares Christians away from abolition—a faithless posture Dabney himself would have rejected.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: Men like Doug Wilson would do well to listen to Dabney in this narrow regard, but instead Wilson says things like, "if anybody thinks they would let Idaho [abolish abortion]… without sending in the troops, is just out of their mind."

q: How is the inconsistency of pro-life legislation parallel to the inconsistency of gradualist anti-slavery?
a: Dabney pressed gradualists by arguing that if they really believed slavery was wrong, they were obligated to immediate abolition; treating an evil as evil while legislating as though it were not is incoherent. Riley applies the same razor to pro-life Christians: professing that abortion is murder while passing bills that regulate, license, or partially permit it preaches with one's actions that abortion is healthcare.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: The Christian, to be consistent, must not profess with his mouth that abortion is murder and preach with his actions and his legislative efforts that abortion is healthcare. That is what pro-life legislation does.

q: What does it look like to act 'by faith' on abolition rather than by political calculation?
a: Riley grounds the answer in Hebrews 11—the saints who conquered kingdoms, escaped the sword, were sawn in two, and went about destitute did so by faith without weighing whether the consequences were tolerable. Modern abolitionists are called to the same posture: demand officials pass and enforce bills that defy *Roe v. Wade* regardless of judicial reprisal.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: By faith, abolitionists demand our governing officials pass and enforce bills that defy *Roe v. Wade*, consequences be damned and God be praised. Wilson's endorsement of incremental measures must be rejected on the grounds that they are not only unjust, but they are faithless.

q: Should John MacArthur's recommendation of Dabney trouble his Reformed admirers?
a: Riley flags that MacArthur speaks highly of Dabney without ever mentioning Dabney's 'unabashed white supremacy-supporting publications.' That silence matters because Dabney's racism was not a side issue—it infected even his systematic theology, where he denied the African in America a natural right to self-control over labor and locomotion. Recommending Dabney without confronting that record sanitizes a heresy that denies the gospel's power to sanctify.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: Notably, reformed pastors Doug Wilson and John MacArthur have both spoken highly of Dabney, with the latter not mentioning Dabney's unabashed white supremacy-supporting publications and the former sometimes relying on them for his own writings.

q: What is the article's bottom-line claim about how Dabney would treat abortion today?
a: Riley contends that Dabney's own pen commits him to abortion abolitionism. If the Bible teaches abortion is wrong—and no orthodox Christian disputes this—then by Dabney's stated reasoning the Christian is bound to hold with the decided abolitionists, refusing to excuse continued injustice on grounds of expediency or inconvenience.
t: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
qt: If the bible teaches that abortion is wrong, "we should be obliged to hold with the decided Abolitionists. We could never be of the number of those, who attempt to transmute the essential traits of moral right and wrong, at the demand of expediency, and to excuse the continuance of a radical injustice, by the inconvenience of repairing it. Duty belongs to man; consequences to God." – R.L. Dabney

q: Does Romans 13 actually require Christians to comply with Roe v. Wade?
a: No. Gillespie argues pro-lifers truncate the passage at verses 1-2 and ignore that Romans 13 describes rulers who praise good and avenge evil. A regime that protects the slaughter of image-bearers fits none of those descriptions, so invoking Romans 13 to demand compliance with Roe inverts the text.
t: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
qt: The problem is pro-lifers usually either stop right there or at the end of verse 2.

q: Why is calling Roe 'the law of the land' a category error?
a: Because legislatures make laws while courts only issue opinions. Roe is a Supreme Court opinion, not a statute, and nothing in the Constitution authorizes the Court to compel states to permit abortion. Treating an unconstitutional opinion as binding law confuses the actual structure of American government.
t: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
qt: Legislatures make laws.  Courts hand down opinions.  Roe is NOT the law of the land; it is a court’s opinion – and it is wrong.

q: Who is the 'governing authority' Romans 13 has in view in the American context?
a: Gillespie contends that in a constitutional republic the people themselves are the governing authority. Presidents, governors, legislators, and judges are hired employees bound by the Constitution, not crowned rulers like Caesar or Herod, so applying Romans 13 to them as if they were sovereign monarchs misreads both the text and our form of government.
t: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
qt: The president, governor, legislators and judges of America and the states are hired, not crowned.  We are the governing authority.

q: Is there historical Christian precedent for defying unjust court decrees?
a: Yes. Gillespie points to Christians who resisted the Dred Scott decision and the Fugitive Slave Act, recognizing that pro-slavery rulings from black-robed judges were evil and that conscience required defiance. The same principle applies to Roe.
t: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
qt: This is the same reason Christians stood up to black-robed oligarchs over the Dred Scott decision and the Fugitive Slave Act.  They knew slavery and pro-slavery decrees were evil and their [Christian conscience dictated they defy evil]

q: Why does Gillespie say incrementalism cannot accomplish its own stated goal?
a: Because SCOTUS will never voluntarily revisit Roe; only a state defying the opinion can force the issue back before the Court. Incremental regulations leave Roe untouched, so the strategy is logically incapable of producing the overturn its advocates claim to want.
t: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
qt: Incrementalism will never cause Roe to be overturned.  It will only be overturned when a state stands up to the oligarchs and defies Roe, forcing SCOTUS to revisit the issue.

q: Even if Roe were upheld after defiance, would defiance still be justified?
a: Yes. Abolitionism is not contingent on a favorable court ruling. Because abortion is the murder of image-bearers, refusing to enforce or obey laws that authorize it is the only righteous response regardless of how the Court rules.
t: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
qt: That said, that isn’t really even the point of abolitionism because if the court were to uphold Roe, defiance would still be the only right and Godly reaction.

q: What does Gillespie say is at stake when Christians stay complacent under Romans 13 rhetoric?
a: He warns that motives may vary, but the outcome of accepting the pro-life movement's reading of Romans 13 is uniform: the ongoing killing of unborn children. Conscience cannot be soothed by passivity in the face of mass slaughter sanctioned by an oligarchic court.
t: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
qt: But the result is the same: the continued slaughter of the unborn image-bearer of God on the altar of convenience and hedonism.

q: Why does Silberman think appointing an anti-Roe justice would not necessarily lead to Roe's reversal?
a: He points to the 1992 Planned Parenthood v. Casey decision, where a court of eight Republican appointees and a pro-life Democrat still upheld Roe. History shows Republican-stacked courts repeatedly decline to overturn abortion precedent, so a sixth Republican appointee does not guarantee a different outcome.
t: Ruth Bader Ginsburg Passes Away at 87
qt: In 1992, the US supreme court was made up of eight Republican nominees, including three from Reagan and two from Bush. The lone Democrat nominee on the court was Justice Byron White who dissented in *Roe v. Wade*. So there were eight Republicans and a pro-life Democrat. The *Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992)* decision should have been a slam-dunk overturning of *Roe*. Yet even the '92 court upheld legal abortion.

q: Which sitting justice has Silberman identified as the only one to publicly call for Roe to be overturned?
a: Clarence Thomas. Silberman notes that Thomas has consistently written separate opinions urging the court to scrap its abortion jurisprudence, including in June Medical Services v. Russo, but no other sitting justice has joined him.
t: Ruth Bader Ginsburg Passes Away at 87
qt: The only current justice who has ever publicly stated that *Roe* should be overturned is Clarence Thomas. Thomas has written separate opinions calling for the court's abortion jurisprudence to be overturned in nearly every abortion-related case that has come before him, including the recent *June Medical Services v. Russo* decision. These opinions have not been joined by any other current justice.

q: Why does Silberman insist that Ginsburg's death changes nothing for abolitionists' duties?
a: Because the moral obligation to refuse cooperation with mass murder is grounded in God's word and equal protection, not in the composition of the court. The court's order remains the same and the moral standard remains the same, so the states' obligation to disregard Roe stands regardless of who occupies the bench.
t: Ruth Bader Ginsburg Passes Away at 87
qt: Even if an anti-*Roe* judge gets appointed, nothing changes for abolitionists. As Christians who strive for obedience to God's word and to a lesser extent, as Americans who care about equal protection under the Constitution, when we're ordered to allow mass murder, we're obligated to say 'no.' Ginsburg has passed away, but the court's order to the states that they must allow mass murder in their jurisdictions hasn't changed, and the objective standard of morality hasn't changed.

q: What does Silberman, citing Russell Hunter, identify as the real ideology that needs to die in the fight against abortion?
a: Not any particular justice, but the ideology of unconditional submission to the Supreme Court regardless of how unjust or irrational its rulings are. The battle is fundamentally against judicial supremacy itself rather than against any individual on the bench.
t: Ruth Bader Ginsburg Passes Away at 87
qt: As my colleague Russell Hunter [said](https://www.facebook.com/FreeTheStates/posts/1035683880197396) on Facebook, the key to the battle against abortion isn't about anyone dying or leaving the court. It's about an ideology of unconditional subservience to the court against all reason and humanity that needs to die.

q: How does Silberman characterize the upcoming nomination fight following Ginsburg's death?
a: He expects it to be even more bitter than the Brett Kavanaugh confirmation, citing Mitch McConnell's immediate announcement that Trump's nominee would receive a Senate floor vote.
t: Ruth Bader Ginsburg Passes Away at 87
qt: US Senate majority leader Mitch McConnell has already released a statement revealing that "President Trump's nominee will receive a vote on the floor of the United States Senate." The upcoming nomination battle is likely to make the Kavanaugh confirmation battle look tame by comparison.

q: What course of action does Silberman urge for state governments regardless of the court's makeup?
a: States must move toward total and immediate abolition of abortion in defiance of the Supreme Court rather than waiting for the court to overturn Roe. Waiting on judicial permission to protect preborn humans is itself a dereliction of duty.
t: Ruth Bader Ginsburg Passes Away at 87
qt: The court is still not likely to overturn *Roe*, and even if it were, we would be derelict in our duty as Christians if we waited for them to do so before giving equal protection to preborn humans. Regardless of who fills Ginsburg's former seat on the court, our work and objectives remain the same. We must continue working toward the total and immediate abolition of abortion in defiance of the genocidal United States supreme court.

q: What did the coalition of Southern Baptist pastors actually announce, and when will the resolution be considered?
a: On Tuesday, June 9, 2020, a national coalition of SBC pastors unveiled a Resolution on Abolishing Abortion, along with a dedicated website and Facebook page, with the goal of having it introduced at the 2021 SBC annual meeting in Nashville.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: On Tuesday, a coalition of Southern Baptist pastors from across the country announced a [resolution](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLScgVHlX3klYqQthBEJ-6YGgANtFuNaLjYXiQ6loUzaHOLu8OA/viewform) to abolish abortion in America as well the launch of an accompanying [website](https://southernbaptistsabolishingabortion.com/?fbclid=IwAR0hEkrjkSxhaUdJ-7TSkF6kWr0AZNB2RZX0jkAJXA845mGsib5KiGxVteU) and [Facebook page](https://www.facebook.com/SBAbolitionists/). The resolution will be introduced at the 2021 Southern Baptist Convention’s annual meeting.

q: Why does the article say this is a significant moment for the broader church, not just the SBC?
a: Because the SBC carries enormous influence among Christians worldwide, an SBC-level commitment to abolition would echo far beyond Southern Baptist pews. The author also frames it as part of a wave that started with Oklahoma Baptists' 2019 resolution calling for abolition without exception or compromise.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: Due to the considerable sway the Southern Baptist Convention (SBC) has with Christians nation and worldwide, this is a big development. It comes on the heels of Oklahoma Baptists (the SBC-affiliated convention in Oklahoma) passing a [resolution](https://freethestates.org/2019/11/oklahoma-baptists-resolution/index.html) calling for abortion’s abolition “without exception or compromise” in Oklahoma last November.

q: What previous abolitionist effort inside the SBC does the article point to, and what happened to it?
a: Indiana Pastor Derin Stidd, who is featured in the new resolution's announcement video, made an abolitionist motion at the 2019 National SBC annual meeting that was killed on procedural grounds rather than on its merits.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: Notably, Indiana Pastor Derin Stidd – who is featured in the video announcement of the resolution – made an abolitionist motion at the 2019 National Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting. The motion was [struck down](https://freethestates.org/2019/08/squashed-like-a-bug/index.html) on a technicality.

q: How does the article distinguish abolitionism from the mainstream Pro-Life Movement?
a: Abolitionism treats abortion as sin demanding a Gospel response, while pro-life leaders are described as relying on secular arguments and political compromise. The article argues this difference is not stylistic but theological: sin requires repentance, not regulation.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: Abortion is sin, and the answer to sin is the Gospel. This stands in stark contrast to the leading politicians and organizations in the Pro-Life Movement who have fought abortion with worldly arguments and by worldly political strategies which consider compromise as the key to success.

q: What is the abolitionist critique of the "just wait on the Supreme Court" strategy?
a: The article casts the dominant pro-life political playbook as a perpetual delay tactic that surrenders to Roe and refuses to demand immediate justice. The Resolution on Abolishing Abortion is presented as an explicit call for the SBC to stop waiting and instead exercise prophetic, immediate action.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: The political strategy practiced by the leading pro-life politicians and lobbyists can be summed up in two words: “Just wait.” We are to wait until the Supreme Court changes their opinion, according to the pro-life leaders. The Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion is a call for the SBC to stop waiting.

q: What does the resolution say governing authorities must do when higher courts legalize abortion?
a: It asserts that lower governing authorities have a duty before God to interpose, ignore, or nullify iniquitous decisions like Roe v. Wade rather than defer to them. This explicitly invites state and local officials to defy federal court rulings that sanction the killing of preborn children.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: *(10) WHEREAS, governing authorities at every level have a duty before God to uphold justice asserting their God-ordained and constitutional authority to establish equal protection under the law for all, born and preborn, by intervening, ignoring, or nullifying iniquitous decisions when other authorities condone such injustices as the legal taking of innocent life (Daniel 3; 1 Kings 12; 2 Kings 11; Jeremiah 26:10-16; 36:9-31; 37:11- 21; 39:7-10), and*

q: Why does the resolution explicitly reject incremental pro-life laws?
a: Because, in the resolution's view, decades of incremental laws have failed to establish equal protection and have instead codified the conditions under which abortion is legally permitted. Incrementalism is treated as a theological problem, not just a tactical one, because it asks God to settle for partial repentance.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: *(11) WHEREAS, over the past 48 years with 60+ million abortions, traditional Pro-life laws, though well intended, have not established equal protection and justice for the preborn, but on the contrary, appallingly have established incremental, regulatory guidelines for when, where, why, and how to obtain legal abortion of innocent preborn children, thereby legally sanctioning abortion, and*

q: How does the resolution describe the SBC's own track record on abortion?
a: It admits the SBC has passed many resolutions affirming the value of human life since 1980 but has never taken the final step of demanding immediate abolition without exception or compromise. The new resolution frames this as incomplete repentance that needs to be corrected.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: *(12) WHEREAS, since 1980, the SBC has passed many resolutions reaffirming the importance of human life at all stages of development, our repentance is still lacking as we consistently have stopped short of calling for the immediate abolition of abortion without exception or compromise, and*

q: What does the resolution declare about exceptions to legal protection for the preborn?
a: It states unequivocally that abortion is murder and rejects every category of exception, including those grounded in circumstance, mental or physical development, or method of conception. There is no carve-out for rape, incest, or fetal anomaly under this framework.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: *(14) RESOLVED, that the messengers of the SBC meeting in Nashville, Tennessee, June 15-16, 2021, do state unequivocally that abortion is murder, and we reject any position that allows for any exceptions to the legal protection of our preborn neighbors, compromises God’s holy standard of justice, or promotes any God-hating partiality (Psa 94:6; Isa 10:1-2; Prov 24:11; Psa 82:1-4), and be it further*

q: What does the resolution say should happen to those who commit or perform abortion?
a: It calls for the murder of preborn children to be punished equally under the law, treating it as a crime against humanity rather than a regulated medical procedure. This is one of the most concrete policy demands embedded in the resolution.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: *(16) RESOLVED, that we affirm that the murder of preborn children is a crime against humanity that must be punished equally under the law, and be it further*

q: How does the resolution want pastors and SBC leaders to use their pulpits?
a: It calls on pastors and entity leaders to deploy their gifts of preaching, teaching, and leading with one unified, prophetic voice to abolish abortion. The expectation is convention-wide engagement, not silence or polite policy statements.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: *(18) RESOLVED, that as Southern Baptists we will engage, with God’s help, in establishing equal justice and protection for the preborn according to the authority of God’s Word as well as local and federal law, and call upon pastors and entity leaders to use their God-given gifts of preaching, teaching, and leading with one unified, principled, prophetic voice to abolish abortion, and be it finally*

q: What theological framing does the announcement video give to abortion?
a: The video calls abortion a modern-day holocaust and an assault on God's glory, citing 60 million abortions since Roe v. Wade and casting it as an attempt to usurp God's sovereignty. It explicitly invokes the language of God hating the shedding of innocent blood.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: Since the Roe vs Wade Supreme Court decision in 1973, over 60 million babies have been led to slaughter in our nation alone. Abortion destroys the life of the most innocent, vulnerable among us, whom God has knitted together in their mother’s womb from fertilization. Abortion is an assault on God’s glory and an aim to usurp God’s sovereignty. God hates abortion.

q: What scale does the video claim for the SBC's potential impact?
a: It cites a membership of 15 million people across over 47,000 churches and urges them to act with one collective voice for abolition without exception or compromise. The framing is that an SBC-aligned movement represents one of the largest mobilizable Christian blocs in the country.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: Southern Baptists have a membership of 15 million men and women and represent over 47,000 churches. It’s time we rise up with one collective voice, pray for God’s mercy and forgiveness, pray that He would heal our land, and act now to abolish abortion in our land, without exception or compromise.

q: What does the article identify as the historical call of the Abolitionist Movement since 2010?
a: The movement's central call has been for Christians to repent of apathy toward preborn image bearers and of complicity with regulatory abortion laws. Repentance and refusing to compromise with abortion are framed as the spiritual heart of the resolution.
t: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
qt: The call of the Abolitionist Movement since its reignition in 2010 has been for Christians individually and the Body of Christ collectively to repent of our apathy toward the thousands of preborn image bearers who are murdered daily in the U.S. alone, and of our comfort with supporting laws regulating abortion.

q: What specific event triggered Bart Barber's public 'repentance,' and what only did he actually retract?
a: On September 15, 2022, Barber launched a Twitter attack on SBC pastor Dusty Deevers (followed the next day by a thread against the Abolitionist Movement broadly). Weeks later he posted a Twitter 'repentance' that retracted only one word, 'obsession,' while leaving every other slanderous claim intact.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: On September 15, Southern Baptist Convention (SBC) President Bart Barber [unleashed](https://twitter.com/bartbarber/status/1570377775030349827) a vicious, dishonest Twitter attack against SBC pastor Dusty Deevers.

q: Why does Silberman call partial repentance no repentance at all?
a: Drawing on Joel Lindsey at The Gospel Coalition, Silberman argues that genuine repentance requires full confession of every sin committed; throwing a bone to critics while refusing to name the actual offenses is not repentance but a public-relations gesture.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: Deep repentance demands full confession. Though it seems counterintuitive, the only way to be truly covered by Christ is to fully expose your sin. In the process of repentance, we must fight to be utterly transparent before God about the depth and breadth of our sin. Only ruthless honesty will suffice—and lead to freedom and joy.

q: What is the most serious unrepented slander the article identifies in Barber's thread?
a: Silberman points to Barber's accusation that Deevers wants to imprison coerced 16-year-old girls who have abortions. He calls this the most viciously slanderous sentence of both threads and notes Deevers has never said anything that could even remotely suggest such a position.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: [U]nless you 100% agree with every jot and tittle of Deevers's obsession with sending 16-year-old girls to prison for succumbing to the coercion of their parents to have an abortion, he will label you 'against the innocent preborn.'

q: How does the article say Barber misrepresented Deevers's original criticism of Brent Leatherwood?
a: Deevers's tweet specifically charged Leatherwood with siding against the preborn in Louisiana and at the SBC annual meeting. Barber stripped that quote from its Louisiana context and framed it as if Deevers were accusing the entire SBC of opposing the preborn.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: Barber extracted Deevers' quotation from its context and applied it as if Deevers was leveling the accusation against the entire convention.

q: What role did the Ethics and Religious Liberty Commission play in the Louisiana abolition bill?
a: Brent Leatherwood, before being hired as ERLC president, signed an open letter during Louisiana's deliberation pledging that the ERLC would never support any law that allowed prosecution of women who have abortions. The bill had sailed through committee with universal Republican support, and Silberman argues it likely would have passed had the ERLC and other pro-life groups not publicly opposed it.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: Given the bill's easy passage through committee, it's safe to say there's a good chance it would have passed had the ERLC and other pro-life groups not sabotaged it.

q: How does the article rebut Barber's claim that abolition bills ignore ectopic pregnancies?
a: Silberman points to screenshots from Bradley Pierce of Abolish Abortion Texas showing actual bill language, and notes Pierce (the drafter of most abolition bills) also offered Barber a phone call to discuss them, which Barber opened but never answered.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: Pierce, an attorney and the drafter of most abolition bills, also messaged Barber to ask him for a phone call to discuss the screenshots, messages which Barber opened but did not respond to.

q: Why does Silberman call Barber's attack on abolitionists' motives an aggravation rather than an isolated mistake?
a: Barber didn't merely misstate the content of abolition bills; he leveraged that misrepresentation to accuse abolitionists of deliberate deception, telling readers that abolitionists' tweets and laws say different things on purpose.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: If an abortion abolitionist succeeds at the legislature, your state won't be governed by his tweets; you'll be governed by his laws. He knows that full well. You deserve to know it, too.

q: How does the article distinguish godly grief from worldly grief in repentance?
a: Quoting 2 Corinthians 7, Silberman defines true repentance as spirit-wrought, mournful over harm done to the victim, eager to confess all guilt, indignant at sin, and zealous to restore. Performative repentance, by contrast, is staged for an audience and aimed at managing reputation rather than restoring the wronged party.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: True repentance is spirit-wrought and heart-felt. It mourns the harm committed against the victim. It is eager to confess all guilt. It is indignant at the sin that made it necessary. It is zealous to restore what was broken. It fears the holiness of a watching God. Performative repentance is a public relations show put on for the watching world.

q: What practical evidence does Silberman cite that Barber's repentance was directed at his audience rather than the victim?
a: Deevers privately texted Barber requesting a conversation when the original attack hit; Barber ignored him. After the partial 'repentance,' Deevers again asked publicly for a private conversation, and Barber again ignored him, indicating the apology was a performance rather than restoration directed at the wronged brother.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: When Barber first launched his attack, Deevers texted him politely asking for a conversation about Barber's accusations. Barber ignored him. After Barber's partial, performative repentance, Deevers [again asked Barber for a private conversation](https://twitter.com/DustyDeevers/status/1583526540041936896). Once again, the victim of slander was ignored by the slanderer.

q: Why does the article treat Barber's continued accusations against Deevers as proof of unrepentance?
a: Inside the apology itself Barber twice charged Deevers with 'hyperbolic incivility' and once with 'unwarranted and unprovoked attacks' on Leatherwood, the same finger-pointing parents teach children to drop when apologizing. Silberman argues this displays disdain for the victim and zero sorrow for the damage caused.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: The 'repentance' serves not Deevers, but Barber, by continuing to malign Deevers while Barber feigns humility before his Twitter followers. His thread is the opposite of repentance. It is nothing but a continuation of his brutal character assault on Deevers thinly veiled in a display of worldly grief and performative repentance.

q: How central is repentance to the abolitionist movement, according to the article?
a: Silberman calls repentance a defining theme and effectively a rite of passage into abolitionism, noting that the 2022 Abolition Now Conference's theme was 'Repent With Us' and featured public repentance testimonies from leaders like T. Russell Hunter, Blake Gideon, and Jon Speed.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: Repentance is so central to abolitionism that the theme of the 2022 Abolition Now Conference was 'Repent With Us', and featured public [repentance testimonies](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Lot-1dC1fyE) from abolitionist leaders like T. Russell Hunter, Blake Gideon, and Jon Speed.

q: What does the addendum say about how abolition bills actually treat coerced abortive mothers?
a: Most abolition bills restate or strengthen existing duress law so that anyone coerced into committing a crime is afforded the same protection as in any other criminal case. Whatever immunity or affirmative-defense framework a state already provides for coerced defendants applies equally to coerced abortive mothers, contrary to Barber's framing of unequal treatment.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: Most abolition bills include restatements of existing law which protect those who commit acts under duress, and some abolition bills amend existing duress language to offer clearer, surer protection to the coerced. The exact protection that anyone coerced into committing a crime would receive would be afforded to women coerced into abortion.

q: Why does Silberman tie the discussion of repentance to revival in the SBC?
a: He argues every revival in scripture begins with repentance, and the SBC, riddled with worldliness, compromise, malice, and hatred, will not be reformed without it. Barber, as president, has the chance to lead the convention by example.
t: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
qt: It's no secret to anyone that the SBC is not a healthy denomination. Worldliness, compromise, malice, and hatred are running rampant through the convention. If a sovereign act of God is coming to bring reformation and revival, it will begin with repentance. Barber has an opportunity to lead by example.

q: What specific tweet from Dusty Deevers triggered the public dispute with Bart Barber?
a: On September 14, 2022, Deevers tweeted that Brent Leatherwood, newly leading the ERLC, had previously sided against the preborn in Louisiana and at the SBC annual meeting, and lamented that the SBC glories in its shame for elevating him.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: In Louisiana this spring and at SBC annual meeting this summer, Brent Leatherwood sided against the innocent preborn now he leads the ethics and policy arm of the SBC. We glory in our shame.

q: How did SBC President Bart Barber characterize Deevers' tweet in his response?
a: Barber quote-tweeted Deevers the next morning, calling the tweet a low point of dishonesty driven by secular political tone rather than the way of Christ.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: This tweet represents the lowest point of dishonesty to which we can descend when we sell out to the crass tone of secular politics instead of following the way of Christ.

q: What concrete action did Brent Leatherwood take that Deevers was referring to?
a: In May 2022, Leatherwood signed an open letter from pro-life organizations and leaders asking pro-life legislators to oppose HB813, the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act, which would have criminalized abortion as murder.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: In May of this year, Leatherwood joined the effort to kill [HB813](https://freethestates.org/2022/05/abolition-of-abortion-in-louisiana-act-passes-committee/index.html), (the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana act) via an [open letter](https://freethestates.org/www.nrlc.org/uploads/communications/051222coalitionlettertostates1e28.pdf?fbclid=IwAR3gJmBpnoSFll0eQCzRC9h2PzDtBbdTEH-p4HKLHNP8WSeuhDSiVCz-mtg) from Pro-life organizations and leaders that pleaded with pro-life legislators to oppose all legislation that criminalized abortion as murder

q: Does Burkey concede any point Barber made about Deevers?
a: Yes. Burkey grants Barber's observation that Deevers is attacking the historical pro-life position of the SBC, because Deevers is openly an abolitionist who sees the pro-life and abolitionist movements as theologically opposed.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: Barber states that Deevers is attacking the historical pro-life position of the Southern Baptists. This is true. Deevers is not pro-life: he is an abolitionist and an exemplary leader within the movement at that.

q: How does the article answer Barber's worry that abolition laws would imprison coerced 16-year-old girls?
a: Burkey says abolition bills complement existing duress and coercion laws that protect those who commit crimes under coercion and prosecute the aggressors, so the fear is misplaced and Barber's framing is dishonest.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: He brings up coerced abortions, ignorant to or ignoring how an abolition bill complements the coercion laws we already have on the books: laws protecting those who commit a crime under duress, and prosecuting the aggressors.

q: How does Burkey respond to Barber's invocation of ectopic pregnancy?
a: She argues that current homicide laws already require medical professionals to treat all humans with dignity and save as many lives as possible, and the same protections that keep adults from being murdered by doctors should equally apply to image-bearers in the womb and in fallopian tubes.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: The current laws that protect Barber from being murdered by a medical professional should equally protect image-bearers of God in utero and in fallopian tubes from murder, and where needed, spur on research towards sparing those lives.

q: What logical fallacies did Grace Family Baptist say Barber's thread contained?
a: Grace Family Baptist tweeted that Barber's thread crammed in appeal to authority, hyperbole, no true Scotsman, straw man, hasty generalization, anecdotal evidence, Texas sharpshooter, and personal incredulity, and added that the worst part was Ninth Commandment violations.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: How many logical fallacies can you cram into one tweet thread: appeal to authority, hyperbole, no true scotsman, straw man, hasty generalization, anecdotal evidence, Texas sharpshooter, and personal incredulity. But, the worst part is the Ninth Commandment violations.

q: What attempts have abolitionists made to engage Barber after the exchange?
a: Deevers and attorney Bradley Pierce both individually asked Barber to talk, dozens of SBC Christians and pastors have urged him to repent, Pierce offered written corrections on legislation, and Deevers and Pierce hosted a roundtable on September 20 that Barber was invited to but could not attend due to the SBC Executive Committee meeting.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: Since this offense, Deevers and Bradley Pierce – an attorney and abolitionist – have both individually asked Barber to have a conversation with them. Barber has been confronted by dozens of Christians and pastors throughout the SBC, explaining to him where he’s wrong, and exhorting him to repent and apologize to Deevers for his error.

q: How does Burkey distinguish wanting abortion to end from wanting it abolished?
a: She says pro-lifers and abolitionists alike pray for an end to abortion, but pro-lifers oppose abolishing it, where abolish is a legal term meaning to do away with the practice by criminalizing it as murder under the same laws that protect any other person.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: Abolish is a word describing legal action, and it means to do away with a system or practice: in this case, the practice of modern-day child sacrifice. To do away with this practice, it must be illegal – that is, criminalized as murder.

q: Why does Burkey argue criminalization, not regulation, is necessary to actually stop abortions?
a: She appeals to the wickedness of the human heart and Romans 13, arguing that as long as a mother can murder her child without consequence she will, and that the threat of prosecution would deter selfish, murder-minded parents in a way that pro-life regulation cannot.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: As long as a mother can murder her own baby without consequences, she will. Murder-minded parents are selfish. And because their main concern is self, the possibility of facing prosecution for the murder of their child would lead them away from murdering their child.

q: What closing exhortation does Burkey leave for Christians who still oppose criminalizing abortion?
a: She calls it a grave error, signaling bigotry toward preborn neighbors, lack of love for abortion-minded parents, disregard for God's commands, and endorsement of what God calls an abomination, while urging readers to test her claims against Scripture themselves.
t: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
qt: It is a grave error to oppose criminalizing abortion as murder. This indicates [bigotry towards our preborn neighbors](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QYFDuNWmqTE&t=2s), lack of [love for abortion-minded parents](https://fb.watch/fEY6IFtKWY/), lack of care for what [God commands](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLScgVHlX3klYqQthBEJ-6YGgANtFuNaLjYXiQ6loUzaHOLu8OA/viewform), and an endorsement of what God calls an [abomination](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Proverbs%2017%3A15&version=ESV).

q: What are the four ways Silberman says the Supreme Court could rule in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health?
a: Silberman lists four possible outcomes: (1) strike the 15-week ban by reaffirming an absolute pre-viability right to abortion, (2) uphold the ban by declaring the Constitution neutral and returning the issue to Congress and the states, (3) uphold the ban by rolling the Casey line back from roughly 20 weeks to 15 weeks or some other arbitrary point, or (4) uphold the rights to life and equal protection by abolishing abortion outright. Only the fourth, he argues, is what the court ought to do.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt:
1. Strike down the 15-week ban by upholding the supposed absolute right to abortion pre-viability.
2. Uphold the 15-week ban by ruling that the Constitution is neutral on abortion and thereby leave the issue to Congress and the States.
3. Uphold the 15-week ban by compromising and rolling back the line established in *Planned Parenthood v. Casey* from roughly 20 weeks to 15 weeks or some other arbitrary line before 15 weeks.
4. Uphold the rights to life and equal protection by abolishing abortion.

q: Why does Silberman fault Mississippi's litigation strategy even while praising Solicitor General Stewart for refusing to hedge?
a: Silberman credits Stewart for refusing the compromise route in his exchange with Thomas, but criticizes the state for advancing a 'Constitution is neutral on abortion' theory rather than the equal-protection argument that preborn children are persons. He says jettisoning the equal-protection claim is harmful long-term because, if life begins at conception, the only constitutionally faithful outcome is abolition.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: He argued that the Constitution is neutral on the subject of abortion, jettisoning the less popular but more Biblically and Constitutionally faithful argument that preborn children are fully equal in value to everyone else and should be afforded the equal protection of the law. If life begins at conception — and everyone knows it does, despite the pro-abortion justices pretending it’s a mystery — there is no option but abolition given the Constitutional guarantees of life and equal protection. This decision by Stewart is harmful to the cause long-term.

q: How does Silberman characterize the historical posture of pro-life leaders, and what does the Casey decision illustrate about it?
a: He describes the movement as 'defined by leaders who exhibit extreme timidity and tolerance for compromise,' citing Casey as the most crushing defeat in its history because an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority voted to maintain legal abortion. He points to Focus on the Family VP Gary Bauer claiming victory simply because some regulations survived as proof of that compromising posture.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: The Pro-Life Movement has long been defined by leaders who exhibit extreme timidity and tolerance for compromise. The *Casey*decision, in which an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority voted to maintain legal abortion, was the most crushing defeat in the history of the Pro-Life Movement.

q: What did Justice Alito's hypothetical about Plessy v. Ferguson expose in Solicitor General Prelogar's stare decisis defense of Roe?
a: Alito asked whether Plessy could have been overruled in 1897 simply because it was egregiously wrong on the day it was handed down, with no new facts. Prelogar refused to answer yes or no and kept retreating to other stare decisis factors, which Silberman says exposed her position as untenable: she could not say yes without conceding Roe could likewise be overruled for being wrong, nor say no without endorsing 60 years of state-mandated segregation.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: In the more than three-minute exchange, Prelogar was twisted into a knot and refused to plainly answer Alito’s question. She couldn’t give a plain “yes” because she had already argued for keeping *Roe* and *Casey* on the grounds that new information is always necessary in order to overturn precedent. But she couldn’t say “no” because she doesn’t want to admit that the supreme court needed to wait 60 years to overturn state-mandated segregation.

q: How does Silberman use Julie Rikelman's drug-use answer to attack the bodily-autonomy argument?
a: Thomas pressed Rikelman on whether a woman could be liable for child neglect for harming her preborn child via drug use; Rikelman conceded states can regulate drug use throughout pregnancy. Silberman points out the contradiction: at, say, 8 weeks the same child can be lawfully killed but cannot be non-lethally harmed, which he compares to antebellum white-supremacist legal schizophrenia about counting black persons.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: She admits that in the exact same moment in the child’s life — say, 8 weeks — she believes that states cannot protect that child from being murdered, while affirming that states can protect the child from harm caused by drug use. In Rikelman’s world, you can’t non-lethally harm preborn children, but you can rip their heads off. This is no different than the legal schizophrenia of antebellum white supremacists who wanted black people to count as people for population counting and other purposes, but did not want black people to have the rights and protections afforded to citizens.

q: Why does Silberman believe Chief Justice Roberts is positioning the Court for a 15-week compromise rather than full reversal?
a: He points to Roberts repeatedly steering Rikelman back to the 15-week line and his comments that the undue burden standard need not be tethered to viability. Silberman reads this as a clear effort to retain Casey's undue-burden framework while merely shifting the line backward from viability to about 15 weeks.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: Roberts didn’t want to address going any further than 15-weeks, and was making the case for 15 weeks as a moderate compromise. Combine this line of questioning with Roberts’ statements throughout the hearing about the undue burden standard not necessarily being tethered to the viability line and you get a very clear picture of a man intending to maintain the same undue burden standard laid out in *Casey* while rolling back the line from viability to around 15 weeks.

q: Why does Silberman doubt that Kavanaugh, Gorsuch, or Barrett will join Thomas in fully overturning Roe?
a: He notes that no other justice has ever signed onto Thomas's prior opinions calling Roe and Casey 'grievously wrong,' and that the three Trump appointees have 'been very cautiously avoiding issuing broad rulings in favor of social conservatives' so far in their SCOTUS careers. Combined with Roberts's near-certain opposition to a full reversal, that leaves only Alito as a likely Thomas ally.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: But I saw nothing from any of the other three justices that would lead me to believe they back a full reversal in this case. As a point to the contrary, all three have been very cautiously avoiding issuing broad rulings in favor of social conservatives thus far in their SCOTUS careers.

q: What probabilities does Silberman assign to each possible Dobbs outcome?
a: He estimates a 60 percent chance the Court adopts a 15-week compromise that preserves the undue-burden framework, a 20 percent chance of total reversal of Roe and Casey, and a 20 percent chance the Court doubles down and upholds Roe in full.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: To put it in percentages, I would say there’s a 60 percent chance of a 15-week compromise, 20 percent chance of total reversal, and 20 percent chance of the court doubling down (for the umpteenth time) on *Roe*.

q: Why does Silberman argue a 15-week compromise would actually be the worst possible outcome?
a: He warns that a compromise ruling would let pro-lifers and pro-choicers alike treat the case as the demise of Roe while in fact preserving the undue-burden framework, merely shifting the protected line by five weeks. He links to Free the States' rapid-reaction video arguing this would be 'the worst case scenario for preborn children and the cause of justice.'
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: The problem with this thinking is that even if it is correct, there is no guarantee the court will overturn its abortion precedents completely rather than simply roll back the line five weeks; which, for reasons explained in our [rapid reaction video earlier this afternoon](https://www.facebook.com/FreeTheStates/videos/669179817800368), would actually be the worst case scenario for preborn children and the cause of justice.

q: What duty does Silberman say Christians and Americans retain regardless of how Dobbs is decided?
a: He argues the Court's opinion does not alter every American's immutable obligation to demand officials uphold the Constitution and Christ's law by abolishing abortion, framing it as a duty to 'rescue those being led to slaughter.' Calling for state defiance of Roe is, he says, the only Biblically justifiable response.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: Of course, none of these possibilities effect the immutable obligations of every American. Whatever the court has said in past opinions or will opine in this case, you, brother or sister, have an obligation to demand your governing officials obey Christ and uphold the Constitution by abolishing abortion. You have a duty to rescue those being led to slaughter.

q: Why does Silberman argue that calling for nullification is actually the most pragmatic path to overturning Roe?
a: He claims that calling for abolition through ignoring Roe is the best way to get the Court itself to overturn Roe, because if the justices sense the states and the people are about to abolish abortion and expose the Court as a paper tiger, they will be more likely to correct their wrongs.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: Further, calling for abolition through [ignoring *Roe*](https://freethestates.org/nullification-and-interposition/index.html) is the best way to get the court to overturn *Roe*. If they sense the states and the people are on the verge of abolishing abortion and exposing the court as a paper tiger, the justices will be more likely to correct their wrongs.

q: What 'wildcard' scenario does Silberman warn abolitionists not to overlook?
a: He raises the possibility of Republican-appointed justices defecting to uphold Casey and Roe in full, citing the historical precedent of Souter, Kennedy, and O'Connor banding together in Casey despite being Reagan and Bush appointees. If an 8-1 Republican majority could uphold legal abortion then, the current 6-3 (or really 5-4 once Roberts's 'consistent betrayals' are counted) certainly could now.
t: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
qt: Another outcome that can’t be overlooked is the possibility of wildcard betrayals that lead to *Casey* and *Roe* being upheld in full. In *Casey*, no one saw coming the coalition of Reagan and Bush-appointees (David Souter, Anthony Kennedy, and Sandra Day O’Connor) who joined to determine the outcome of the case in favor of legal child sacrifice.

q: What does SB1728 actually do, and why does Silberman say it does not abolish abortion?
a: SB1728 only allows the mother or grandparents of a murdered preborn child to sue an abortionist who violates existing abortion regulations (such as parental consent documentation, informed consent, or screening for impaired judgement). It treats abortion itself as legal and merely adds a regulatory checklist for abortionists to satisfy.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: SB1728 allows the mother who had the abortion or the grandparents of the murdered child to sue the abortionist if the abortionist violates an abortion regulation; for instance if the abortionist fails to document parental consent or judicial bypass before murdering the child of a minor, if the abortionist fails to obtain and document compliance with informed consent regulations, or if the abortionist fails to document that they screened the mother for impaired judgement, among a few other regulatory violations.

q: What did Senator Bullard admit on the Senate floor about his own bill?
a: Under cross-examination by Senator Joseph Silk on March 9th, Bullard conceded that the bill does nothing more than impose a checklist on abortionists, and went further to claim that this kind of checklist is the most effective way to protect preborn children.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: Bullard said not only is providing a checklist for abortionists all his bill does, but further, he admitted his belief that providing abortionists a pre-murder checklist is the best possible way to protect preborn children.

q: What does Silberman say is the actually effective alternative to SB1728?
a: Writing a law that treats unborn human beings as fully equal to born human beings. Legislators are obligated as God's deacons to write just laws and uphold justice even when superiors pressure them otherwise; the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act has done exactly this since 2016 but has been killed every year.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: The best thing to do to protect unborn children is to write a law treating unborn human beings as fully equal to born human beings. Senator Bullard and his colleagues are legislators. Their job is to be God’s deacon of wrath against the evil-doer. Their job is to write just laws.

q: What evidence does the article give that incremental regulations are not even reducing abortion?
a: Despite a steady stream of regulatory bills passed every session, abortions have actually increased in Oklahoma in recent years, undermining the claim that incrementalism is achieving even its modest stated goals.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: These bills don’t even accomplish what we’re told they accomplish. Abortions have [increased in Oklahoma](https://lozierinstitute.org/abortion-reporting-oklahoma-2017/) in recent years despite the abortion regulations passed every session!

q: How does Silberman use a hypothetical about brown-haired 25-year-olds to frame the love-of-neighbor obligation?
a: He argues that if SCOTUS declared it legal to murder a class of adults, no one would respond by lobbying for regulatory tweaks; they would demand outright criminalization. Loving the preborn as oneself therefore demands the same response: total and immediate abolition, not regulation.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: If SCOTUS opined that it was legal to murder brown-haired 25-year-olds, I assure you I would not ask the Oklahoma State Government to fiddle with regulatory codes. I would demand with every bit of passion within me that the State of Oklahoma make it illegal to murder me in defiance of that supreme court opinion. To do anything less for preborn human beings would thus be a failure to love my neighbor as myself.

q: Why did Bullard, Allen, and Murdock turn against SB13 after previously supporting abolition work?
a: Silberman attributes the reversal to pressure from Senate Pro-Tem Greg Treat, who had already punished Senator Silk for publicly campaigning for abolition. He suggests Treat likely threatened the three senators' committee leadership assignments, though he acknowledges this cannot be confirmed.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: But pressure from Senate Pro-Tem Greg Treat caused Bullard, Senator Mark Allen, and Senator Casey Murdock to stop supporting abortion’s immediate abolition. Senator Silk was punished by Treat for publicly campaigning for abortion’s abolition, so it’s conceivable that Treat threatened to take committee leadership assignments from Bullard, Allen, and Murdock too unless they stopped supporting the bill.

q: How is Senator Larry Boggs cited as an example of incrementalism being used as electoral cover?
a: Boggs, facing an abolitionist primary challenger in Warren Hamilton, has become a chief champion of SB1728 to rebuild anti-abortion credibility he lost by refusing to back SB13. He has even smeared abolitionist organizations as 'wolves in sheep's clothing' for pushing past pre-murder checklists.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: Boggs [regularly posts](https://www.facebook.com/boggsforsenate/posts/3446068382110951?__xts__[0]=68.ARBQ2wkQxD5lAgjl_korOpwNvhrTsG685SKle2HbufIQ1cZelYQMymE8ooAUd6qnqJGfsJF53b3W-8GcmT6miiJvkEflCOaUFXQQ28-ShPZR1fxPJQVZTcgrEPkuxpSXJB5Q1hhTxe6Zg-R_SK6IDq5cbCE4xZghNKGEQq0BI8d6aYw-JAKDDOOWEkXxMIGSOfPgMwK3pF9hasYZcm264U919hKKXazzzY1bEXWBJmlG6oeeQh6VbIeHZfaf00c7b5kEiBy332jPiA7dfWnY8FDxr2AeBWttGZMNInGjQinR5duSsNMlWXP-&__tn__=H-R) about the progress of SB1728 and has even called abolitionist organizations “wolves in sheep’s clothing” for calling state Senators to move beyond pro-life compromises and pre-murder checklists to actually abolish abortion.

q: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison to frame the moral status of compromise bills?
a: He invokes Garrison's 1832 condemnation of any scheme presented as a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of slavery, applying that same judgement to SB1728: regardless of intent, a substitute for abolition is itself delusive, cruel, and dangerous because it delays the abolition of abortion.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: We regard as delusive, cruel and dangerous, any scheme of expatriation which pretends to aid, either directly or indirectly in the emancipation of the slaves, **or to be a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of slavery**

q: What does Silberman call activists and Christians to do instead of supporting bills like SB1728?
a: Stop tolerating compromise from pro-life politicians and lobbyists, treat Roe as the legal nullity it is, and demand total and immediate abolition grounded in the equal-protection guarantees of the U.S. and Oklahoma Constitutions and the word of God.
t: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
qt: It’s time to stand firmly on the word of God, as well as the U.S. and Oklahoma Constitutions which demand equal protection of the law for all. It’s time to treat Roe as the crime against humanity and legal nullity that it is by abolishing abortion.

q: What incident triggered the Facebook debate that exposed Sen. Mary Boren's support for Larry Boggs?
a: A debate erupted on JJ Francais' Facebook post in the wake of the July 14 Haskell County GOP meeting at which Boggs physically shoved his primary challenger Warren Hamilton and threatened one of Hamilton's supporters.
t: Senator Larry Boggs Supported by Pro-Abortion Democrat Sen. Mary Boren
qt: Shortly after the July 14th Haskell County GOP fiasco where Larry Boggs shoved his opponent Warren Hamilton and threatened one of Hamilton's supporters, a debate broke out on a Facebook post by local writer JJ Francais.

q: How does Sen. Mary Boren describe Roe v. Wade on her own website?
a: Boren frames Roe as well-settled precedent that supposedly balances the interests of the woman, her unborn child, and the state, treating it as a protection of women's privacy, dignity, and moral agency.
t: Senator Larry Boggs Supported by Pro-Abortion Democrat Sen. Mary Boren
qt: Roe v. Wade effectively balances the interests of the woman*, her un-born [sic] child*, and the state.  Roe v. Wade is accepted as well settled [sic] precedent and protects the privacy, dignity, and moral agency of women.

q: Why does the author call Boren's reasoning sociopathic rather than merely mistaken?
a: Because Boren explicitly acknowledges the unborn as a child and yet endorses procedures that decapitate, suffocate, and dismember that child, the author argues her position is not ignorance but a deliberate endorsement of murder.
t: Senator Larry Boggs Supported by Pro-Abortion Democrat Sen. Mary Boren
qt: The fact that she recognizes them as a child and endorses wholesale murder is astonishing. Though it is typical of abortion supporters, this position and reasoning is nothing short of sociopathic.

q: What does the article claim Boren's endorsement reveals about Larry Boggs' legislative record?
a: It claims Boren naturally prefers Boggs because he sponsors pro-life bills that accomplish nothing, leaving abortion legal, instead of pushing for the immediate and total abolition of abortion that Warren Hamilton would.
t: Senator Larry Boggs Supported by Pro-Abortion Democrat Sen. Mary Boren
qt: Unsurprisingly, she prefers Boggs who supports worthless pro-life bills that do nothing, as opposed to a candidate who is going to consistently push for immediate and total abolition.

q: How did the Boggs campaign respond to receiving a pro-abortion senator's public support?
a: Rather than disavowing it, Boggs' campaign manager Connor Alford liked Boren's comments, signaling the campaign welcomed the endorsement of an avowed pro-abortion radical.
t: Senator Larry Boggs Supported by Pro-Abortion Democrat Sen. Mary Boren
qt: Her comments in support of Boggs were even liked by Boggs' campaign manager Connor Alford. They happily took the support of the pro-abortion radical.

q: How does the author respond to Boren and the local journalist labeling abolitionists a "cult"?
a: He calls the label hypocritical, noting Free the States merely told the truth about Smalley keeping abortion legal while it is the pro-abortion side that resorts to slanderous name-calling and victim posturing.
t: Senator Larry Boggs Supported by Pro-Abortion Democrat Sen. Mary Boren
qt: Name-calling is their game, not ours; hence "cult." This is the game they play, disparaging abolitionists with all sorts of slanderous names and accusations while playing the victim because we call them out on substantive grounds for keeping abortion legal.

q: What concrete voting recommendation does the article give SD7 voters?
a: It urges voters who care about preborn children and consistent conservatism to reject the candidate endorsed by a pro-Roe senator and instead cast their ballot for Warren Hamilton.
t: Senator Larry Boggs Supported by Pro-Abortion Democrat Sen. Mary Boren
qt: Don't choose the guy supported by a Senator who believes that decapitating, poisoning, and ripping preborn children to pieces effectively "balances their interests." Vote for Warren Hamilton.

q: What concrete legislative event was the article reacting to?
a: The agenda for the final scheduled Senate Health and Human Services committee meeting before the February 27 deadline was published without SB13 listed, meaning the bill would die in committee unless McCortney altered the agenda or called a special hearing.
t: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
qt: The agenda for Monday’s Senate Health and Human Services committee meeting has been [published](http://www.oksenate.gov/committees/meetingnotices.aspx) and Senate Bill 13 is not on it. Monday is the final scheduled committee meeting prior to the February 27 deadline for bills to be passed out of committee.

q: What forms of public pressure had already been brought to bear on McCortney?
a: A capitol rally of thousands, a statewide petition of nearly 2,000 Oklahomans, hundreds of petitions specifically from his own district, the Oklahoma Republican Party platform, and resolutions from the Oklahoma Baptists and Free Will Baptists had all called on him to grant SB13 a hearing.
t: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
qt: On February 11, thousands showed up at the capitol to demand SB13 receive a hearing. He was delivered a petition of nearly 2,000 Oklahomans demanding SB13 receive a hearing. This morning, he was delivered hundreds of petitions from the people of his district (Ada area) demanding SB13 receive a hearing. The platform of the Oklahoma Republican Party demands SB13 be given a hearing. The Oklahoma Baptists and Free Will Baptists have both passed official resolutions demanding SB13’s passage and enforcement.

q: On what theological and constitutional grounds does the article claim SB13 is uniquely necessary?
a: The author argues that the U.S. and Oklahoma constitutions require equal protection of the law for all people, which only SB13 would extend to the preborn, and that God Himself demands the abolition of child sacrifice.
t: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
qt: The Constitution of the United States and the State of Oklahoma demand equal protection of the law be provided to all people, something which no bill other than SB13 accomplishes. The Creator of the Universe demands iniquity like child sacrifice be abolished.

q: How does the author frame McCortney's likely place in the historical record?
a: Silberman predicts abortion will eventually be abolished and that history will single out the pro-life Republican leaders and committee chairs who delayed that abolition, casting McCortney as one of the figures responsible for prolonging the killing.
t: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
qt: The history books will tell that story, and part of that story is the pro-life Republican leaders and committee chairs who delayed abolition and perpetuated the killing for an extra few years. Tragically, this is the role McCortney is currently writing for himself in the story – a story his grandchildren and great grandchildren will read.

q: Was there still a procedural off-ramp for McCortney at the time of writing?
a: Yes. The article notes McCortney could amend the agenda up to 24 hours before the meeting or call a special hearing on Tuesday, Wednesday, or Thursday of the following week to consider SB13 before the deadline.
t: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
qt: He can make changes to the agenda up until 24 hours prior to the meeting. He can call a special hearing on Tuesday, Wednesday, or Thursday of next week to hear SB13 prior to the deadline.

q: What specific action does the article ask readers to take?
a: Readers are urged to call and email McCortney immediately at the published addresses, pleading with him to take the warnings seriously and grant SB13 a hearing before time runs out.
t: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
qt: But time is running out. He needs to act quickly. So please call and email him now, pleading with him to take seriously the admonitions he’s receiving and give SB13 a hearing.

q: How does the author characterize McCortney's apparent motives or self-awareness?
a: Silberman suggests McCortney does not grasp the gravity of his choice and warns that the decision will dog him personally and politically for the rest of his life, not just in the next election cycle.
t: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
qt: McCortney likely doesn’t grasp the seriousness of what he’s choosing. If he did, he would choose differently. This is not something that he will be able to weather. There will be political repercussions, but more significantly this will follow him the rest of his life.

q: Why does the author insist that abortion exceptions for rape and incest are unjust rather than compassionate?
a: Silberman frames children conceived in rape as the most vulnerable class of human beings precisely because they are fatherless, and argues that allowing their execution punishes them for their father's crime — turning a 'compassion' clause into legalized predation on the defenseless.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: Abolitionism is about upholding justice for the fatherless. No one is more fatherless than the child conceived in rape. This is probably the most vulnerable group of human beings on the planet. A law which allows for abortion in this situation is an atrociously evil law which makes the fatherless prey (See Isaiah 1:16-17; 10:1-2). The law must prohibit the execution of the child for the crime of his or her father.

q: How does SB13 handle situations where the mother's life is threatened by pregnancy complications?
a: Rather than carving out a blanket life-of-the-mother exception (which Silberman warns would be exploited as a loophole), SB13 protects doctors from murder liability when they have exhausted every option to save both lives — preserving the physician's role as healer while still treating both mother and child as persons.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: As for the life of the mother, SB13 specifies that the doctor cannot be held liable for murder if they did everything they could to save both lives. It does not and should not have a blanket life of the mother exception. That would be a loop hole that would be abused severely. Doctors are supposed to be healers, not killers, and SB13 specifies that they must exhaust all options at their disposal to save both human beings in cases of pregnancy complications demanding triage.

q: Why must existing pro-life statutes — like dismemberment bans and parental notification laws — be repealed for abortion to actually be abolished?
a: Silberman argues these regulatory statutes implicitly codify the legality of abortion by specifying which methods, locations, providers, and notification procedures make the killing acceptable; leaving them on the books would directly contradict any law that treats abortion as murder.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: All of these laws passed by pro-life politicians and lobbyists regulate where babies can be murdered, how they can be murdered, by whom they can be murdered, and the licensing needed  to murder them. Such laws explicitly contradict SB13 which abolishes abortion as murder. **Abortion cannot be abolished while there are still laws on the books saying abortion is legal as long as you jump through pro-life hoops.** They have to be repealed. SB13 repeals them.

q: Why does the article reject classifying abortion as a 'health service'?
a: Because abortion intentionally ends a human life, the author insists it cannot legitimately occupy any place in the health code and must be relocated to the criminal homicide statutes — where any other intentional killing is treated.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: Abortion is not health care. It’s murder. Abortion must be taken out of the health codes and put into the criminal homicide codes. The pro-life author of the resolution appears to want abortion to continue to be legislated as if it were health care.

q: How does the article respond to the objection that SB13 would expose mothers, nurses, and physicians to murder charges?
a: Silberman treats this not as a flaw but as the whole point of equal protection: granting preborn humans the same legal status as the born necessarily means anyone who kills or aids in killing them faces the same penalties — and exempting mothers, he argues, was the very hypocrisy that helped Roe succeed.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: Put simply, all an abolition bill does is make preborn human beings completely equal under the law. This means that murdering or being an accomplice to the murder of a preborn human being comes with the same legal penalty as murdering or being an accomplice to the murder of a born human being. Pro-life bills which give automatic legal immunity to the mother deprive preborn human beings of equal justice and protection. This hypocrisy of treating the murder of a preborn human being differently than the murder of a born human being actually led to the decriminalization of abortion in the first place.

q: How does footnote 54 of Roe v. Wade figure into the argument against automatic immunity for mothers?
a: Silberman cites Justice Blackmun's own reasoning that the Texas statute's blanket immunity for mothers proved Texas didn't actually regard the preborn as persons; abolitionists therefore see equal penalties as essential not just morally but to legally rebut the Roe rationale.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: The Texas abortion statutes in question in *Roe* gave automatic immunity to the mother in all cases. Justice Blackmun, author of the *Roe* ruling, wrote in footnote 54 of the decision that if Texas really believed preborn human beings were persons deserving of legal protection, the state would legally penalize those who murder them to the same degree as those who murder born persons.

q: What does the rise of abortion-pill technology have to do with whether mothers should receive automatic immunity?
a: The author warns that exempting mothers effectively legalizes self-induced abortion at home, a practice growing more common as chemical-abortion technology spreads — making 'mother immunity' a hollow ban that can be circumvented by mail-order pills.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: Another enormous problem with providing automatic immunity to the parents who murder their preborn child is the fact that it creates a right to self-induced abortion, a practice which is increasingly common as abortion pill technology advances. **To write a law outlawing abortion but giving automatic immunity to the parents means that the mother is free to induce her own abortion.** SB13 does not fail to provide equal protection to preborn humans and therefore does not legalize self-induced abortion.

q: On what grounds does the article justify a state defying Roe v. Wade and federal court orders?
a: Silberman invokes both constitutional fidelity and the doctrine of the lesser magistrate, contending that orders to permit mass killing have no rightful authority and that state officials are morally obligated to interpose themselves between the federal judiciary and the unborn.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: *Roe* is an evil, unconstitutional supreme court opinion ordering the states to allow mass murder within their jurisdictions. An abolition bill recognizes that the courts have no rightful authority to legalize murder. Court opinions legalizing murder are null and void by their nature and will be treated as such upon the passage and enforcement of an abolition bill.
