In addition to a magistrate’s duty to uphold the Constitution in the face of judicial tyranny, all men have a transcendent, immutable moral obligation to disregard an order from a superior to allow mass murder, which is exactly what *Roe* is. Lower magistrates must interpose themselves between the victims of abortion and any higher magistrate who would deprive preborn human beings of the right to life.

q: Why does the article point to Alabama's 2019 abortion law and a new Planned Parenthood megacenter as a cautionary tale?
a: Because Alabama's bill explicitly invited court review rather than nullifying Roe, Planned Parenthood was confident enough to break ground on a Birmingham megacenter — proof to Silberman that bills lacking nullification language will simply be enjoined and never enforced.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: As an example of a bill that lacks language nullifying *Roe*, in 2019, Alabama passed a court-submissive bill establishing that it’s illegal for an abortionist to perform abortion. Yet Planned Parenthood is so confident the court will not allow the law that they broke ground on a new megacenter in Birmingham a few months after the bill passed. SB13 does not submit to *Roe* or any other government edict purporting to legalize abortion.

q: What does Silberman ultimately diagnose as the root problem with the resolution's 'We do support pro-life' closer?
a: He argues that the regulatory pro-life strategy has cultivated a class of activists who are now more comfortable managing abortion than ending it — the same posture he attributes to Oklahoma Republican leaders Greg Treat and Kim David — and calls for jettisoning that mindset entirely.
t: Shocking Pro-Life Resolution Filed at Pittsburg County (OK) GOP Meeting
qt: Here’s the kicker. This person is a pro-lifer, through and through, who pays lip service to the idea that abortion is bad. This resolution is being introduced at a Republican Committee Meeting. Like Greg Treat and Kim David and the Republican establishment, the resolution’s author supports pro-life regulations on abortion but opposes actually establishing justice for preborn human beings. And that’s the fruit of a strategy of regulating abortion – many pro-lifers are now comfortable with regulating murder and uncomfortable with abolishing it. We’ve got to leave behind entirely this mindset, this political strategy of regulating abortion. Abortion must be utterly abolished.

q: Which prior denominational resolutions did the South Carolina Baptist Convention follow when passing its abolition resolution?
a: The SCBC followed the national Southern Baptist Convention, the Arkansas Baptists, and the Utah-Idaho Baptists in passing a resolution calling for the total and immediate abolition of abortion.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: Following in the footsteps of the [national Southern Baptist Convention](https://freethestates.org/2021/06/abolitionists-pass-resolution-make-huge-strides-at-sbc-convention/index.html), [Arkansas Baptists](https://freethestates.org/2021/10/arkansas-baptists-pass-resolution-on-abolishing-abortion-at-state-convention/index.html), and [Utah-Idaho Baptists](https://freethestates.org/2021/10/utah-idaho-baptist-convention-calls-ut-id-governors-legislature-to-abolish-abortion/index.html), the South Carolina Baptist Convention passed a resolution calling for the total and immediate abolition of abortion on Tuesday.

q: How did the South Carolina Resolutions Committee modify the original abolitionist resolution presented by the pastors?
a: While affirming the call for abortion's immediate abolition and equal justice for preborn children, the committee weakened the language by adding appreciation for pro-life defenders who have used their influence to restrict abortions through incremental measures.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: While the committee affirmed certain important aspects of the resolution such as the need for the Church to demand abortion’s immediate abolition and equal justice for preborn children, they added that they appreciate “the passionate dedication of pro-life defenders who have used their influence to restrict abortions.”

q: What did the committee say about the effectiveness of past pro-life incremental efforts?
a: The committee acknowledged that incremental efforts have not led to the end of abortion and concluded that the focus must shift to supporting laws that will actually end abortion.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: they “acknowledge that these efforts have not led to the end of abortion, so we must now primarily focus our attention, concentrate our efforts, and bring our influence to bear in supporting laws that will end abortion.”

q: What did the committee's added article say about repenting of incrementalism?
a: The article expresses repentance for being too quick to substitute pragmatic methods of protecting life when the focus should remain on the ultimate goal of ending abortion, while still supporting good faith efforts to restrict abortion.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: While we continue to support all good faith efforts to restrict and reduce abortion, acknowledging the saving of any preborn life is a step toward saving all preborn life, we also acknowledge that our most fervent efforts must be focused on abolishing abortion once and for all, and we repent of sometimes being too quick to substitute pragmatic methods of protecting life when we should always be working toward the ultimate goal of ending abortion.

q: How does Silberman characterize the committee's underlying view of pro-life incrementalism?
a: Silberman argues that the committee believes in moving past incremental substitutes for abolition and even repenting of the distraction, but does not view pro-life incrementalism as intrinsically wrong.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: Essentially, the committee believes in moving on from pro-life incrementalism and even in repenting of being distracted with incremental substitutes for abolition, but doesn’t believe there is anything intrinsically wrong with pro-life incrementalism.

q: Who met with the Resolutions Committee in advance, and what materials did they share?
a: Pastors Kiley Waldrop and Brad Starnes, along with SC abolitionist organizer Matt Brock, met with the committee a few months earlier and supplied copies of the Are You An Abolitionist pamphlet.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: Pastors Kiley Waldrop, Brad Starnes, and SC abolitionist organizer Matt Brock met with the committee a few months ago to discuss the resolution and give them copies of our [Are You An Abolitionist pamphlet](https://store.abolitionistsrising.com/products/are-you-an-abolitionist).

q: What two amendments were offered from the floor, and how were they received?
a: Waldorf moved to tighten the language regarding the life of the mother, and Starnes moved to add an exhortation calling abortion providers and participants to repent and believe the gospel; both amendments were accepted by the committee and the messengers before the resolution was passed.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: Starnes then offered an amendment to include language exhorting “abortion providers and participants to repent and believe the gospel of Jesus Christ.” Like Waldorf’s, the amendment was received by both the committee and the messengers, before the full resolution was then passed.

q: How does the resolution describe the duty of governing authorities at every level?
a: The resolution states that governing authorities at every level have a duty before God to uphold justice and to establish and enforce equal protection under the law for all born and preborn human beings.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: (8) WHEREAS, Governing authorities at every level (federal, state, county, and municipal) have a duty before God to uphold justice asserting their God-ordained and constitutional authority to establish and enforce equal protection under the law for all born and preborn human beings (Romans 13:1-7; Micah 6:8; James 2:1-13)

q: What position does the resolution take on exceptions in laws intended to abolish abortion?
a: The resolution unequivocally rejects any position that allows exceptions to laws intended to abolish abortion and to provide legal protection for all human life from the moment of conception.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: we reject any position that allows exceptions to laws that are intended to abolish abortion and provide the legal protection for all human life from the moment of conception (Psalm 94:6; Proverbs 24:11; Psalm 82:1-4)

q: How does the resolution treat ectopic pregnancy?
a: It distinguishes ectopic pregnancy from abortion, calling it a tragedy requiring medical triage in which doctors must continue to treat both mother and baby as human beings while exhausting all options to save lives.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: (13) RESOLVED, That we recognize that an ectopic pregnancy is not an abortion, but a tragedy during which medical triage is necessary, with doctors never ceasing to treat both mother and baby as human beings, and upholding the Hippocratic Oath by exhausting all possible options to save as many human lives as they can

q: Why does Silberman argue that the resolution, despite its weaknesses, still represents real progress?
a: Even though it leaves the door open to unjust incremental bills, it represents tremendous progress thanks to the work of South Carolina abolitionists and gives them leverage to demand officials support Jonathan Hill's bill to abolish abortion in South Carolina.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: Even so, while not being an abolitionist victory, the resolution does represent tremendous progress that has been made thanks to the work of many South Carolina abolitionists who have had their hands to the plow for years. It also provides South Carolina abolitionists the ability to demand their governing officials support SC State Rep. Jonathan Hill’s bill to abolish abortion in South Carolina, lest they reject the position of both the Southern Baptist Convention and South Carolina Baptist Convention.

q: How does Jonathan Hill characterize the convention's action and the role of the church in ending abortion?
a: Hill called it a first step with hopefully many more to come, and insisted that ending abortion is a political battle the church must lead, calling on the church to repent of its idolatry of political power and politicians.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: “But the issue of ending abortion is a political battle that the church must lead. I am convinced that this is absolutely vital. It is my hope and prayer that this issue will be a catalyst for the church to repent of its idolatry of political power, politicians, and government institutions. The Gospel itself begins with a political demand to bow to the absolute authority of God over all. A Gospel that does not inform and direct our politics is not the true Gospel!”

q: On what theological basis does the resolution declare every human being equally valuable?
a: The resolution grounds equal worth in the doctrine that all humans are God's image-bearers possessing equal, objective worth that does not vary based on ethnicity, age, means of conception, mental or physical development, gender, potential, or contribution to society.
t: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
qt: (2) WHEREAS, As God’s image-bearers, all humans display divine worth, and possess equal, objective worth before God, that does not vary based on characteristics such as ethnicity, age, means of conception, mental development, physical development, gender, potential, or contribution to society (Romans 1:19-20; Genesis 1:27; 9:6; Matthew 18:6)

q: What procedural action did SBC messengers take on Tuesday, and how unusual was it?
a: Messengers voted to override the Resolutions Committee and force the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion onto the convention floor. According to Bill Ascol, this kind of override had not happened since 1979, signaling extraordinary grassroots support for abolition within the SBC.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt: First, according to Ascol, this is the first time that the Resolutions Committee has been overridden in this way since he began attending SBC Annual Meetings in 1979. This demonstrates the immense popular support within the SBC for abolition.

q: How does the SBC normally decide which resolutions reach the floor, and what threshold is required to override that process?
a: Ordinarily the Resolutions Committee selects which resolutions are voted on, but messengers can move to override that choice and a two-thirds vote is required for the override motion to succeed.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt: Southern Baptists can file resolutions, but it is ordinarily the prerogative of the Committee to decide which resolutions are voted on by messengers. However, messengers can motion to override the Committee's decision. A two-thirds vote is needed for this sort of motion to pass.

q: Why did the Resolutions Committee initially refuse to bring the abolition resolution forward?
a: The Committee preferred Resolution #3, which framed the issue around defending the Hyde Amendment and blocking Medicaid funding of abortion providers, rather than the abolitionist call for justice to be immediately established.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt: While the Committee believes that the Southern Baptist Convention messengers would agree with some aspects of this resolution, the Committee believes Resolution #3 addresses these issues in the context of the current threats to the Hyde Amendment and related pro-life appropriations riders in the United States Congress.

q: Who introduced the override motion, and what was the response from the floor?
a: Bill Ascol, pastor of Bethel Baptist Church in Owasso, Oklahoma, introduced the motion late in Tuesday's meeting, and an estimated 80-90% of messengers backed the override against the Committee's recommendation.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt: Near the end of Tuesday's meeting, abolitionist Bill Ascol – pastor of Bethel Baptist Church in Owasso, OK – motioned to override their decision. An astonishing eighty to ninety percent of messengers responded in support of the motion, over the objections of the Resolutions Committee.

q: Why does the article call this vote historic beyond just procedural significance?
a: Because it marks the first time the largest Christian denomination in the United States has taken a step toward becoming a prophetic witness demanding the immediate abolition of preborn child-killing, rather than merely settling for incremental pro-life policy.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt: But second – and more importantly – this is the first time that the largest Christian denomination in the United States has ever taken a step toward being a prophetic witness in calling for the immediate abolition of the murder of preborn children.

q: How does the resolution distinguish abolitionism from being merely 'pro-life'?
a: The promotional pamphlet acknowledges that Southern Baptists already produce pro-life statements, sermons, articles, and conferences but argues they have failed to act as a unified, prophetic voice demanding magistrates abolish abortion totally and immediately in Jesus' name.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt: Southern Baptists have not failed to be pro-life. We have pro-life statements. We have pro-life Sanctity of Life sermons. We have pro-life articles, videos, and conferences. But we have failed to be a unified, prophetic voice demanding of our magistrates the total and immediate abolition of abortion in Jesus' name.

q: What three objections from SBC leaders does the article promise to answer?
a: The article lists objections that women should be immune from prosecution as victims of abortion, that there must be a life-of-the-mother exception, and that abolitionists should accept incremental gains rather than push for total abolition.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt:
Objection #1: Women are victims of abortion and should be immune in all cases

##### Objection #2: What about the life of the mother exception?

##### Objection #3: We should take what we can get

q: What is the abolitionist response to the 'take what we can get' incrementalist objection?
a: The article points readers to a presentation by abolitionist attorney Bradley Pierce arguing that every facet of the pro-life incrementalist strategy has failed.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt: Abolitionist attorney Bradley Pierce explains in this presentation how all seven facets of the pro-life incrementalist strategy have been utter failures.

q: What framework does the article use to summarize the foundational beliefs of abolitionism?
a: The Five Tenets of Abolitionism, captured by the acronym GATES: Gospel-centered, Aligned providentially, Through the Church, Engaged Biblically, and Sought immediately without exception or compromise.
t: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
qt:
The five tenets are the foundations of our public theology and can be referred to by the acronym GATES. Abolitionism is:

1. Gospel-centered
2. Aligned providentially
3. Through the Church
4. Engaged Biblically
5. Sought immediately without exception or compromise

q: What actually happened at the November Southern Baptists of Texas Convention regarding the abolition resolution?
a: The convention took up a Resolutions Committee proposal celebrating the Texas Heartbeat Act, and abolitionist Pastor Aaron Wright moved to strike its language and replace it with the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion. After testimony on both sides, the convention rejected the abolitionist amendment and adopted the heartbeat-bill resolution instead.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: Pastor and abolitionist Aaron Wright motioned to amend the resolution by striking all of the language of the heartbeat bill resolution and replacing it with the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion.

q: Why does Silberman think Nathan Lino's argument was especially weak?
a: Lino objected to calling abortion a crime against humanity that should be treated as murder by suggesting such language is harsh toward young women in church youth groups who might have sex outside of marriage. Silberman reads this as edging toward the position that warning Christian women away from killing their own children is itself unloving.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: It seemed that he was on the verge of suggesting that it is unloving and mean-spirited to warn young, Christian women away from murdering their own children by calling abortion a crime against humanity that should be treated as murder.

q: Does softening the language about abortion actually serve post-abortive mothers, as Lino implies?
a: Silberman argues the opposite: refusing to call abortion the killing of an equal human being denies post-abortive mothers access to the gospel of forgiveness, and it leaves possibly pre-abortive mothers thinking abortion is not that serious a sin.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: To do so is unloving to post-abortive mothers because it denies them access to the gospel, and it’s unloving to possibly pre-abortive mothers because it makes them think abortion isn’t that bad.

q: How much practical power does the church in states like Texas actually have to end abortion, in Silberman's view?
a: He claims the church in states like Texas and Oklahoma could end abortion almost immediately if Bible-believing churches repented of apathy, engaged the fight, and disciplined any legislator refusing to support the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: If every Bible-believing Church repented of apathy, got engaged in the battle, and announced that any legislator who refuses to do justice by supporting the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act would face Church discipline, abortion would be abolished.

q: Why should I be wary of someone who leads with their pro-life credentials?
a: Silberman observes that politicians and pastors blocking abolition routinely open by reciting their pro-life resume. He argues the label has become so elastic that it functions as cover for opposing actual abolition, which is why he says listeners should listen for a concrete commitment to immediate abolition rather than to the brand.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: Being super pro-life is how the pro-lifers at the top get away with keeping abortion legal. This is why it’s important to be suspicious of anyone calling themselves pro-life. Being pro-life can mean anything or nothing at all. What you need to listen for is a promise to fight for the immediate abolition of abortion.

q: Did the abolitionist resolution really call SBTC leaders compromisers of the Word of God, as Lino claimed?
a: Silberman, who wrote the disputed sentence, says the resolution made a general claim that compromise is not the language of the bride of Christ rather than naming individuals, and that the underlying point that incremental laws compromise from the standard of "you shall not murder" is simply true.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: Page five, sentence one, paragraph two (which I was privileged to write) reads: “Compromise is the language of worldly politicians, not of the bride of Christ.”

q: Is it fair for Lino to call abolitionists harsh while Bart Barber says nearly identical things about abortion being a holocaust?
a: Silberman points out that Barber called abortion a holocaust and called for criminalizing all abortion in his own testimony, yet Lino would not accuse him of being mean. He sees the "abolitionists are mean" line as a deliberate, repeatedly used talking point of the pro-life establishment.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: “aBoLiTiOnIsTs aRe mEaN” has been a favorite talking point of the Pro-Life Establishment for years. They believe it works, so they keep using it specifically against abolitionists.

q: Does Bart Barber back up his claim to support total abolition with action on actual abolition bills?
a: Silberman searched for any record of Barber engaging the Texas abolition bills (HB3326, HB896, the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act) and found nothing relevant; the only "Bart Barber abolition" results were videos of him opposing abolitionism.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: Search engine and social media searches for “Bart Barber HB3326,” “Bart Barber HB896,” and “Bart Barber Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act” all turn up no relevant results. Searches for “Bart Barber abolition” turns up only various videos of Barber opposing abolitionism.

q: Is abolitionism the same as expecting abortion to end overnight?
a: No. Silberman argues abolitionists, like their 19th-century predecessors, expect a long struggle. The abolitionism vs. incrementalism dispute is not about how long the fight takes but about what the movement's demand should be.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: The abolitionism-incrementalism debate does not revolve around how long the battle takes (though the abolitionist strategy will certainly achieve the desired end more quickly). The debate revolves around *what our demand should be*.

q: On what authority does an abolitionist demand for immediate abolition rest, and why can't an incremental demand rest there too?
a: Silberman grounds the abolitionist demand in Scripture ("You shall not murder") and the Fourteenth Amendment's equal-protection clause. A 20-week ban or heartbeat bill, he argues, cannot stand on either, because by drawing an arbitrary line it implies that abortion before that line is permissible.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: An incrementalist’s demand for a 20-week abortion ban or a heartbeat bill cannot be defended from God’s Word or the Constitution, as these laws – by implying the legality of abortion prior to the arbitrary standard they set for who counts as fully human – violate both.

q: Why does Silberman claim incremental laws can actually move culture in the wrong direction?
a: Because compromise bills rest on compromised foundations, they cannot deliver a powerful prophetic witness, and in some cases they actively dehumanize the preborn children they leave unprotected by classifying them as non-persons under the law.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: Compromise bills and arguments mean compromised foundations; compromised foundations mean no powerful prophetic witness from our demands; and no powerful prophetic witness from our demands means no positive cultural change from our demands.

q: What's wrong with Barber's analogy comparing pro-life incrementalists to Schindler, Ten Boom, and Allied soldiers?
a: Silberman, citing J.R. Haas, says Barber conflates two different things: taking territory over time (which abolitionists endorse) versus passing unjust laws as a tactic. A good general fully liberates each town as he takes it; an incrementalist, in the analogy, frees only some hostages per town because doing it slowly is supposedly pragmatic.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: Abolitionism is the strategy of a good general: completely freeing the hostages of the Nazis from one town to the next (just as abolitionists work to abolish abortion from State and country to the next). Incrementalism is like taking a town and only freeing some of the hostages because it’s “pragmatic” to do it slowly.

q: How prevalent is abortion among churchgoing women, according to the article?
a: Silberman cites surveys finding that more than 40 percent of American mothers obtaining abortions are regular churchgoers, and notes abolitionists routinely see cars with church stickers and Jesus fish pulling into abortion mills.
t: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
qt: Surveys have found that upwards of 40 percent of American mothers having abortions are regular churchgoers.

q: What exactly did Pastor Derin Stidd's motion at SBC 2019 ask the ERLC to do?
a: Stidd moved that the ERLC study how SBC churches, local associations, and state conventions could work with legislators and governors to promote the immediate abolition of human abortion, and that the SBC reaffirm its commitment to article 15 protecting the preborn. The motion explicitly rebuked SBC-affiliated state conventions in Texas and Oklahoma that had opposed bills which would have abolished abortion in those states.
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: I would like to make a motion that the ERLC of the Southern Baptist Convention do a study on how churches, local associations, and state conventions can work with all entities within their state including legislators, governors, and local associations to promote and admonish and encourage the immediate abolition of human abortion, and reaffirm our commitment to a Biblical view that all people are created in the image of God, and our commitment as Southern Baptists to article 15 which speaks to the protection of the preborn.

q: On what grounds did the SBC Executive Committee deny Stidd's motion, and was that reasoning unavoidable?
a: President J.D. Grear announced the next morning that the motion was denied because the convention can only request, not direct, an entity. Silberman argues this was a discretionary technicality the committee could easily have fixed by amending the wording to 'requesting,' allowing a real debate on abolition bills instead of killing the motion outright.
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: The handling of technicalities like this one are largely up to the discretion of the committee. They could have amended the motion to address Stidd's concerns and facilitate a discussion on bills which abolish abortion.

q: How did Matt Hawkins characterize the abolitionist position, and what did he celebrate?
a: Hawkins, the former ERLC policy director, took to Twitter to deride immediatism as 'pointless perfectionism' and to praise Southern Baptist messengers for crushing it. He framed the rejection of Stidd's motion as the SBC astutely defeating an extreme, fantastical position rather than as the convention sidestepping a serious challenge to pro-life lobbying.
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: At #SBC19 an assertion of extreme immediacism (I call it pointless perfectionism) on #prolife lawmaking crept into a floor debate. Southern Baptist messengers astutely squashed the fantasy like the bug it is.

q: Why does Silberman say Hawkins's 'pointless perfectionism' label is theologically bankrupt?
a: Because Hawkins's premise treats the Supreme Court as an authority that cannot be defied, which is neither biblical nor constitutional. Scripture's response to cultures practicing child sacrifice is never to negotiate a compromise; prophetic speech demands immediate repentance, so calling immediatism 'perfectionist' reveals an atheological starting point.
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: Biblical examples of speaking Godly truth to a culture practicing child sacrifice never include starting from an assumption that a compromise on child sacrifice should be struck (or some other evil). Prophetic exhortations are always calls to immediate repentance.

q: How does the article rebut the claim that immediatists 'have nothing to show for their efforts'?
a: Silberman responds that anti-abortion immediatism is less than a decade old and has already accomplished what the pro-life movement could not in 46 years: getting actual abolition bills introduced. The reason those bills haven't passed in red states isn't immediatist failure but active opposition from SBC-affiliated conventions and pro-life incrementalists who kill the bills in committee.
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: Further, Hawkins and Carter also are speaking from complete ignorance regarding the history of anti-abortion immediatism, which has existed for less than a decade. In that time, we've done what the pro-life movement hasn't been able to do in 46 years: introduce bills which abolish abortion. Again, these bills would be passing were it not for the pro-life incrementalists who are killing them in committee.

q: What evidence does the article give that SBC and ERLC leaders don't speak for ordinary Southern Baptists on abortion?
a: Stidd's motion drew a 'significant cheer' from the floor, attendees personally thanked him afterward, and in Oklahoma Southern Baptists organized an entire rally and an hour-long public forum and authored a petition letter rebuking the BGCO for opposing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. Silberman frames this as proof of a widening gap between the cooperative-program-funded leadership and the rank-and-file.
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: This was made abundantly clear in Oklahoma as an entire rally was led by Southern Baptists to rebuke the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma (BGCO) which opposed the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. On top of that, a gathering a Southern Baptist pastors and elders led an hour-long public forum on the matter and authored a petition letter addressed to the BGCO.

q: What does Stidd say the ERLC's prophetic standard should actually be?
a: Stidd argues that the ERLC, as the prophetic voice of the SBC to the culture, must measure right and wrong on abortion by the unchanging word of God rather than by what the Supreme Court will tolerate. The article extends this point to call out Graham, Grear, Hawkins, and Carter for behaving as humanistic political operatives rather than prophets.
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: It is important for the ERLC, which is supposed to be the prophetic voice of the SBC to the culture, that our standard of right and wrong as it relates to abortion is not built upon what the supreme court might permit, but rather is built upon the unchanging word of God.

q: What constitutional argument does the article offer for ending the deference to SCOTUS on abortion?
a: Silberman insists the courts must be put in their rightful place beneath the Constitution and beneath God by proclaiming that human life begins at conception and that the 14th Amendment was written precisely to forbid the separation of 'person' from 'human being.' He couples this with the prophetic warning, 'Woe to those make iniquitous decrees.'
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: It's time to stop begging the tyrant for scraps and put them in their rightful place beneath the Constitution and ultimately beneath God by proclaiming the truth that human life begins at conception, that the separation of "person" from "human being" is what the 14th amendment was written to prohibit, and to warn them, "Woe to those make iniquitous decrees."

q: What does the author claim happened when he previously published criticism of Texas and Oklahoma Baptist leaders?
a: Silberman says he wrote a piece for a prominent conservative publication exposing how Texas and Oklahoma Baptist leaders helped prevent abortion's abolition; high-level SBC figures responded by bombarding his editors with emails and calls for nearly a month until the article was pulled and an apology posted, even though nothing in it was inaccurate. He cites this as evidence of the SBC's willingness to wield political and cultural power against critics.
t: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
qt: High-level SBC people responded by bombarding my editors with emails and phone calls for almost a month and got the article taken down and an apology posted despite nothing in the article being inaccurate. This is what we're up against.

q: What does the author identify as the central disagreement between pro-lifers and abolitionists?
a: Silberman frames the divide as a question of authority: whether the Supreme Court (or any governing body) has the legitimate power to declare murder lawful. Pro-life leaders, in his telling, treat SCOTUS rulings as final and binding, while abolitionists insist no human authority can sanction the killing of innocents.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: One of the primary differences between pro-lifers and abolitionists is the disagreement regarding whether the supreme court – or any governing entity – has the authority to make murder legal.

q: How does Joe Pojman characterize the states' obligation to obey Roe v. Wade?
a: Pojman, executive director of Texas Alliance for Life, treats SCOTUS rulings as effectively a law of nature that states cannot resist. Silberman quotes him as saying defiance is as impossible as defying gravity, which the article uses as the paradigm case of pro-life unconditional submission.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: We could no sooner ignore SCOTUS than the force of gravity.

q: What did Scott Klusendorf say at a 2016 Christian conference about the federal courts and unborn children?
a: Klusendorf described federal court rulings in Roe and Casey as having dictated 'from on high' that unborn children have no right to life. Silberman argues that treating the judiciary as 'on high' contradicts both Scripture and the Constitution.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: the federal courts have already decided in Roe vs. Wade, the Casey decision, and others, that no unborn children have the right to life. They’ve already dictated that from on high.

q: What is the abolitionist's claim about the legitimacy of any order that legalizes mass murder?
a: Abolitionists hold that such orders are void from the moment they are issued, regardless of who issues them, and that the only acceptable response is open defiance. Silberman appeals to a 'natural right to nullify' grounded in America's founding documents.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: Roe, and any other order to allow mass murder like it, is null by its nature. The *only* proper response to an order to allow mass murder is active defiance.

q: How does Bryan Zollinger's position on nullification shift between general state sovereignty and abortion?
a: Zollinger championed Idaho's State Sovereignty Responsibility Act, which declared undelegated federal acts void, but reversed himself on abolishing abortion, claiming nullification is unrealistic in practice. Silberman uses Zollinger as the pattern of pro-life politicians who will defy Washington on other issues but never to save preborn children.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: It’s pretty well accepted that the supreme court is the final say of what’s constitutional… I don’t see any way to get around that unless we went nullification and we all know that doesn’t happen in reality.

q: What evidence does Silberman offer that nullification already happens in practice?
a: He points to the more than half of states that have decriminalized marijuana in defiance of the federal Controlled Substances Act and Gonzales v. Raich. The example is meant to refute the claim that nullification is a fringe or impossible tactic.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: Nullification happens *constantly* in reality. More than half the states have decriminalized marijuana in defiance of the federal Controlled Substances Act and the *Gonzales v. Raich* supreme court decision.

q: How does Greg McCortney's record on marijuana compare to his record on abortion abolition?
a: McCortney championed Oklahoma's bill to defy federal marijuana law but, as committee chairman in 2020, refused to grant The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act a hearing. Silberman uses the contrast to charge pro-life politicians with selective courage.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: Oklahoma State Senator Greg McCortney was the champion of the bill to defy the feds and legalize marijuana in the state. In 2020, he was the committee chairman who denied The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act a hearing.

q: What did Greg Treat say about nullifiers, and how does that contrast with his prior legislative work?
a: Treat had authored a 2016 bill nullifying EPA regulations, yet in a 2019 hearing he denounced nullifiers as the 'villains of history' when the topic was abolishing abortion. Silberman highlights the inconsistency as evidence that pro-life politicians weaponize anti-nullification rhetoric only against abolition.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: nullifiers are the villains of history.

q: Why, according to the author, do pro-life politicians embrace submission specifically on abortion?
a: Silberman blames the pro-life lobby itself, which (unlike the marijuana or Second Amendment lobbies) actively pressures legislators to submit to federal authority even when submission means continued mass killing. The implication is that the movement's own institutions have produced the cowardice.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: Pro-life politicians do this, in large part, because while the marijuana lobby, and the second amendment lobby, and many other groups urge state legislators to defy the federal government, the pro-life lobby encourages unconditional submission even when it means the mass murder of image bearers of God.

q: What is the author's final verdict on the moral status of complying with Roe?
a: Silberman labels failure to defy Roe and Casey as either cowardice or idolatry, leaving no neutral middle ground. He concludes that abortion will be abolished state-by-state regardless of what SCOTUS does.
t: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
qt: Failing to do so is cowardice or idolatry. Abortion can, must, and shall be abolished state-by-state regardless of the supreme court’s position on the matter.

q: What exactly does HB3326 do, and how does it differ from a typical pro-life regulation?
a: HB3326 does not regulate abortion at all; it abolishes it by extending equal protection of Texas homicide law to every unborn child from fertilization. Mechanically, it repeals the carve-out in Penal Code 19.06 that currently exempts abortion from the murder statute, so that an unborn person receives the same legal protection any born person already has under Texas law.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: HB 3326 simply repeals the exception for abortion in PC § 19.06, giving unborn persons in Texas the same legal protection as any born person.

q: Why is the bill explicitly written to defy Roe v. Wade and Planned Parenthood v. Casey rather than try to work around them?
a: The bill treats unconstitutional federal court rulings as void rather than binding, and asks Texas legislators to keep their oath to the U.S. Constitution by refusing to pretend the Constitution forbids the state from prohibiting homicide. The drafters frame Roe and its progeny as a 'legal fiction' that Texas has wrongly surrendered to, and they call on the state to reassert its reserved police powers.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: as legislators, to fulfill our oaths of office to the United States Constitution, so help us God, by disavowing the legal fiction that the constitution prohibits this state from exercising its reserved police powers to prohibit and criminalize homicide and from exercising its constitutional and God-given duties to provide equal protection to all persons within its jurisdiction

q: How does Rep. Slaton ground the bill theologically and philosophically?
a: Slaton appeals to the Declaration of Independence's claim that governments exist primarily to secure God-given rights, with the right to life as the first among them. Any law or court decision contradicting that self-evident truth is, in his framing, not merely unconstitutional but contrary to natural law and must be rejected outright.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: As the Declaration of Independence makes clear, governments are instituted primarily to secure God-given rights, the principal among them being the right to life. The right to life is the first among all natural rights, and must be protected to the utmost. Any law, rule, or court decision that is opposed to this self-evident truth is not only unconstitutional but contrary to natural law and must be rejected.

q: Why does the article say HB3326 is the only bill that fulfills the Texas GOP's own legislative priority?
a: Texas abolitionists succeeded in 2020 in making 'Abolish Abortion' one of eight official priorities of the Republican Party of Texas, with explicit language requiring equal protection from the moment of fertilization. HB3326 is the only bill filed in the 87th legislature that actually does this, exposing every other 'pro-life' bill as falling short of the party's own platform.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: This is the only bill currently filed in the 87th legislature which meets the "Abolition of Abortion" mandate of the [2020 Republican Party of Texas Legislative Priority]

q: What rhetorical trap does the press release set for politicians who claim to believe an unborn child is a person?
a: The release argues equal protection is the logical corollary of personhood: if a fetus really is a person, the laws shielding born humans must equally shield those not yet born. Anyone refusing to support equal protection therefore reveals either that they don't actually believe the unborn are persons or that they don't actually want to follow the Constitution, leaving no honest middle ground.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: If we truly believe that a fetus is a person, then the laws which protect born human beings must equally protect those not yet born. Those who oppose equal protection either do not believe that a fetus is a person or do not want to follow the Constitution.

q: How does the bill use Greg Abbott's own past words to pressure him into supporting it?
a: In 2018 Gov. Abbott personally promised dying teenager Jeremiah Thomas that he would pursue outlawing abortion altogether in the next legislative session. The article frames HB3326 as the precise vehicle by which Abbott can finally honor that bedside promise, and publicly calls on him to do so.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: ..its what we are going to be pursuing this next legislative session [2019] and that is to outlaw abortion altogether in the state of Texas.

q: How significant is the body count the article cites, and how does it reframe abortion in Texas?
a: The press release points to over 55,000 babies killed by abortion every year in Texas, calling it a leading cause of death in the state. That framing rejects abortion as a political niche issue and treats it as the largest ongoing homicide crisis in Texas, demanding immediate and equal legal response rather than incremental regulation.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: More than [55,000 babies] are murdered by abortion in our state every year, makingabortion a leading cause of death in our state. Thousands of innocent lives are ended unjustly every year without being provided the legal protection enjoyed by every born person in Texas.

q: What does the bill's stated reliance on Justice Thomas signal about its legal strategy?
a: By citing Justice Thomas's concurrence in June Medical Services v. Russo, the drafters anchor the bill in a sitting Supreme Court justice's own published view that the Constitution does not constrain states' authority over abortion. This positions HB3326 not as fringe nullification but as a state acting consistently with at least one current member of the Court's reading of the Constitution.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: to follow Justice Thomas’s statement in *June Medical Services, L.L.C. v. Russo*, 140 S. Ct. 2103 (2020) that “[t]he constitution does not constrain the States’ ability to regulate or even prohibit abortion”

q: How is the Texas Senate, and Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick specifically, being publicly challenged?
a: Lt. Gov. Patrick had just released his top 31 priorities for the 87th session, including two abortion-related items, but none of them abolish abortion. The release calls on Patrick to publicly support HB3326 and urges 'life-loving senators' to file a Senate companion bill so that the priority can move quickly through both chambers.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
qt: The Lt. Governor can further demonstrate his commitment to ending abortion in Texas by publicly supporting this bill. We also urge life-loving senators to file a companion bill in the Senate to speed the passage of this important priority.

q: What bill did Bryan Slaton file on the opening day of the July 2021 Texas special session, and what does it do?
a: On the opening day of the special session called by Governor Abbott, Slaton filed HB103, formally titled the Abolition of Abortion Through Equal Protection for All Unborn Children Act, which would extend equal protection of the law to preborn children rather than merely regulating abortion.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: On the opening day of the session, State Rep. Bryan Slaton filed [HB103](https://capitol.texas.gov/BillLookup/History.aspx?LegSess=871&Bill=HB103), the Abolition of Abortion Through Equal Protection for All Unborn Children Act.

q: What did Slaton already do during the regular session to fight for preborn children, and how does that establish his track record?
a: During his freshman regular session Slaton filed HB3326 to immediately give preborn children equal protection, attempted to amend House rules to force a vote on abolition before any vote naming bridges or roads, and filed an amendment to a counter-productive pro-life bill to convert it into immediate abolition.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: Slaton fought valiantly for preborn children in Texas during his freshman session in the House — introducing [HB3326](https://legiscan.com/TX/text/HB3326/2021) which would have immediately provided preborn children the equal protection of the law, [motioning to amend](https://www.facebook.com/watch/AbolishAbortionTexas/) House rules to force a vote on abolishing abortion before any vote to name bridges or roads, and [filing an amendment](https://www.facebook.com/1091574574227150/videos/798420244378197) to a counter-productive pro-life bill to make it immediately abolish abortion.

q: Who killed HB3326 in the regular session, and how was it killed?
a: Public Health Committee chair Stephanie Klick, a self-identified pro-life Republican, denied HB3326 a committee hearing, and her office reportedly told constituents falsehoods about the bill to justify the move even though three other Republicans had coauthored it.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: HB3326 was coauthored by Reps. James White, Kyle Biedermann, and Tom Oliverson, but it was denied a committee hearing by Public Health Committee chair Stephanie Klick, a pro-life Republican. Klick’s office went as far as telling her constituents [blatant lies](https://abolishabortiontx.org/2021/04/21/responding-to-misinformaton-about-hb-3326/) about the bill in an attempt to defend her actions.

q: What strategy did the pro-life establishment use to protect Klick after she killed the abolition bill, and where else has this tactic shown up?
a: Texas Right to Life and other pro-life groups rallied around Klick by publishing celebratory pieces touting unrelated incremental bills she let through committee; Silberman says the same playbook of using small pro-life bills as political cover for committee chairs who kill abolition has been deployed in Oklahoma, Missouri, and other states.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: This same tactic has been applied in Oklahoma, Missouri, and other states where abolition bills are introduced. Abolition bills were assigned to the same committee as a bunch of useless pro-life bills, and the pro-life bills were used as political cover for the committee chair who killed the abolition bill. Be on the lookout for this strategy again in the special session.

q: What did Governor Abbott actually call for instead of abolition, and how does Silberman characterize it?
a: Abbott's special-session call asked for legislation strengthening reporting of abortions and abortion complications and ensuring informed consent for abortion-inducing drugs—measures Silberman dismisses as politically useful theater that cannot save lives because they remain within the bounds of Roe and Casey.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: Instead of the abolition of abortion, Governor Abbott called for legislation that “strengthens the laws applicable to the reporting of abortions and abortion complications,” whatever that is supposed to accomplish, and “ensures that no abortion-inducing drugs are provided unless there is voluntary and informed consent.” Like all pro-life legislation that can be upheld within *Roe* and *Casey*, this bill is completely useless, but it allows pro-life politicians and lobbyists to look like they’re doing something, which is politics 101.

q: Why is it significant that Stephanie Klick is the primary House author of Abbott's preferred special-session bill?
a: Silberman argues it is the same recycled cover-fire pattern: the legislator who killed equal-protection abolition is now positioned to pass an incremental bill that will be marketed as a pro-life win, allowing her to be hailed as a hero by the same organizations even as roughly 110,000 Texas children are killed annually.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: Unsurprisingly, the primary House author of the bill is none other than Stephanie Klick. Her useless bill has a good chance of passing and if it does, the Representative who made sure that 110,000 babies are murdered in Texas will be touted as a pro-life hero by the pro-life organizations once again. This is one of the reasons it is so important to oppose incremental pro-life legislation.

q: Why hasn't HB103 been assigned to a committee yet, and what's the irony Silberman points out?
a: Texas House Democrats had flown to Washington, D.C. to lobby against the U.S. Senate filibuster, which paralyzed Texas House activity and effectively functioned as a filibuster of its own—an irony Silberman calls 'too rich.'
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: As for HB103, it has yet to be assigned to a committee. Part of the holdup is due to Texas democrats [flying to D.C.](https://www.salisburypost.com/2021/06/04/byron-york-in-texas-democrats-thwart-will-of-majority/), essentially filibustering all Texas House activity, to lobby against the U.S. Senate filibuster. The irony really is too rich.

q: How does the Republican Party of Texas platform relate to HB103, and why does Silberman emphasize the platform's specificity?
a: The Texas GOP again named the abolition of abortion as a legislative priority for the special session and explicitly called for passage of a bill modeled after HB3326—language deliberately precise enough to exclude pro-life groups who have been redefining 'abolition' to cover their incremental bills.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: Texas abolitionists have worked hard in recent years to [make abolition a legislative priority of the party](https://freethestates.org/2020/07/texas-abolitionists-make-abortions-abolition-an-official-tx-gop-priority/index.html), and accordingly, the Republican Party of Texas has once again [publicized](https://freethestates.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/07/Screen-Shot-2021-07-15-at-2.39.37-PM-1.png) the abolition of abortion as a legislative priority of the party going into the special session. Even better, they specifically called for the passage of a bill like HB3326 from the previous session.

q: How are Texas pro-life organizations co-opting the language of abolition, and what specific examples does the article cite?
a: Texas Right to Life has rebranded its set of incremental bills as the 'Texas Abolition Strategy,' and Texas Alliance for Life has publicly described its trigger bill—which Silberman says actually makes Texas abortion statutes more unjust—as a bill 'to abolish abortion in Texas,' both attempts to capture the abolitionist label without supporting equal protection.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: Texas Right to Life has called their collection of incremental bills the [Texas Abolition Strategy](https://freethestates.org/2019/09/faux-abolitionism/index.html). Texas Alliance for Life [posted](https://www.facebook.com/TexasAllianceForLife/photos/10159694796246807) on Facebook that their trigger bill, which actually makes Texas abortion statutes [even more unjust than they already are](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8X2537Gui9g), is a bill to “abolish abortion in Texas.”

q: What concrete actions does the article ask readers to take?
a: Readers are told to follow the Abolish Abortion Texas and Free the States Facebook pages for updates on HB103, sign up for email lists to bypass declining social media reach, do conversion-style activism using resources like the 'Are You An Abolitionist' pamphlet, and become monthly donors to Free the States.
t: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
qt: Stay tuned to the Facebook pages of [Abolish Abortion Texas](https://www.facebook.com/AbolishAbortionTexas) and [Free the States](https://www.facebook.com/FreeTheStates) for further updates about HB103!

q: What does the Preborn Child Equality Act of 2021 actually do, and how is it different from a typical pro-life regulation?
a: It does not regulate abortion; it abolishes it by redefining personhood from conception and extending equal protection of the laws (including homicide statutes) to preborn children in Alaska.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: The Preborn Child Equality Act of 2021 is set to be filed by Alaska State Rep. Christopher Kurka in the coming days. The bill would totally and immediately abolish abortion in Alaska.

q: How does the bill define a 'natural person,' and why is that definition foundational to the equal-protection strategy?
a: It defines a natural person as a human being from conception, deliberately stripping out every common pretext (age, location, dependency, stage of development) used to deny personhood to the preborn so that existing laws against murder must apply equally.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: Kurka's bill defines a "natural person" as "a human being, regardless of age, race, religion, size, sex, citizenship, parentage, ancestry, disability, deformity, location, stage of development, life expectancy, or condition of dependency from the moment of conception."

q: How does the bill establish state residency for preborn children, and why does that matter?
a: It ties the preborn child's residency to the mother's, ensuring Alaska's jurisdiction and laws (including the protection against being murdered) attach to that child as a resident of the state.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: It also establishes that "A preborn child is a resident of the state if the mother of the preborn child is a resident of the state under this section."

q: Why is the bill targeting the Alaska Supreme Court, not just abortion providers?
a: Because the Alaska Supreme Court, mirroring Roe, judicially manufactured a state constitutional 'right' to abortion under privacy; abolishing abortion in statute requires repudiating that judicial fiction.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: Just as was done federally in *Roe v. Wade*, the Alaska Supreme Court wrote a right to abortion into the State Constitution by arguing that murdering a preborn child is a protected right within the State Constitution's right to privacy.

q: How does the bill attempt to remove abortion from judicial review?
a: It explicitly declares that intentionally killing a natural person before, during, or after birth is not protected by Alaska's constitutional right to privacy and that this section is not subject to judicial review.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, intentionally causing the death of a natural person before, during, or after birth is not protected by a right to privacy under art. I, sec. 22, Constitution of the State of Alaska. This section is not subject to judicial review.

q: Isn't it improper for the legislature to put a topic off-limits to the courts?
a: Free the States argues no, because Alaska's own constitution gives the legislature the power to prescribe court jurisdiction, providing a deliberate check on judicial overreach.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: Some object to the idea that the legislature may declare certain topics off limits for the judiciary, but article IV, section 1 of the Alaska Constitution states that "The jurisdiction of courts shall be prescribed by law."

q: How does the bill propose to handle conflicting federal court orders?
a: It expands an existing Alaska nullification statute (44.99.040) to add federal court orders to the list of federal actions Alaska may treat as null and void, and it adds the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendment right to life to the protected rights triggering that defiance.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: Kurka's bill adds federal court orders to the list of federal actions which may be defied, and adds the "right to life under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments to the Constitution of the United States" to the list of rights which Alaska will defy the federal government to protect.

q: What does Free the States ask supporters to do in response to this bill?
a: Pray for Kurka, Eastman, and the bill, and follow Free the States' blog and Facebook page for ongoing updates on Alaska and other state abolition bills.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: Free the States thanks Representatives Kurka and Eastman for their bold stand. Be in prayer for them and for the Preborn Child Equality of Act of 2021.

q: Why should an Alaska voter care that David Eastman is cosponsoring rather than authoring this round?
a: Eastman is the veteran abolition-bill author in Alaska; his cosponsorship signals continuity with prior abolition efforts while Kurka steps forward as the new lead author, broadening the legislative coalition for total abolition.
t: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
qt: State Rep. David Eastman, who has authored abolition bills in the past, will be a cosponsor.

q: What position is Students for Life formally advocating in their new presentation and flyer?
a: Students for Life is teaching that abortion laws should hold only the abortionist criminally liable for the child's death, leaving the mother immune from prosecution. Kristan Hawkins, the group's president, is touring the country to promote this view as the proper pro-life legal framework.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: Students for Life has a new presentation and flyer which explains their position that laws against abortion should make only the abortionist liable for prosecution for the child’s death. Kirstan Hawkins, the president of Students for Life is currently touring the United States presenting on this topic.

q: Why does Silberman call mother-immunity laws fundamentally unjust?
a: Because granting blanket immunity to one class of participants in the killing of a preborn child—the mother—creates two tiers of justice for human victims, treating the preborn as less worthy of legal protection than the born. A consistent law would simply apply existing murder statutes to all human beings without carve-outs.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: A just abortion law would simply be the current law against murder applied to all human beings. The justice applied to someone who plays a part in the murder of a born person would be the same justice applied to someone involved in the murder of a preborn person. That would be justice.

q: How did pro-life immunity provisions contribute to the reasoning of Roe v. Wade?
a: Justice Harry Blackmun seized on the inconsistency in the Texas statute—which simultaneously claimed the fetus was a person but exempted the mother from being a principal or accomplice and set lower penalties than for murder—as evidence the law did not actually treat the preborn as full persons. Pro-life hypocrisy, in Silberman's reading, helped supply the reasoning Blackmun used to strike down protective laws.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: It’s pro-life hypocrisy on this matter that led to Roe v Wade in the first place. Roe author Justice Harry Blackmun wrote that the Texas law in question which gave blanket immunity to the mother was not consistent with the other claims of the pro-life defenders of that law.

q: What inconsistency did Blackmun specifically identify in Texas's abortion statute?
a: Blackmun pointed out that Texas refused to treat the woman as a principal or accomplice to abortion, and that the criminal penalty for abortion was significantly lower than for murder. He argued these features were impossible to square with the claim that the fetus was a person entitled to Fourteenth Amendment protection.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?

q: What does Silberman say is at stake if pro-life laws themselves refuse to treat the preborn as human beings?
a: He argues the entire moral case for ending abortion collapses into incoherence. If the people writing the laws will not afford full legal protection to preborn children, the public has no reason to take seriously the claim that the preborn deserve such protection.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: If pro-life laws and the pro-life leaders who write them don’t treat the preborn child like a human being, why would anyone else? We must demand equal justice for preborn human beings. To not do so is to be incoherent so as to betray our the foundation of our case: that the victims of abortion are due full protection of the law.

q: Why does Silberman claim the pro-life movement is not actually about criminalizing abortion?
a: Because the movement is committed to treating the mother as a victim and granting her automatic immunity, its proposed laws never criminalize abortion as an act—they only criminalize the act of an abortionist performing one. The mother's choice to procure or perform an abortion remains lawful.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: Most people probably think that the pro-life movement is about criminalizing abortion. This is incorrect. The pro-life movement is dogmatically wedded to the idea that the parents and especially the mother who has her child murdered is a victim. Thus, they grant immunity from prosecution to the mother in all pro-life laws.

q: What concrete loophole does mother-immunity create in any 'pro-life' statute?
a: It leaves do-it-yourself abortion entirely legal. The statute reaches only licensed abortionists assisting in a killing; a mother who self-induces—by pill, instrument, or any other means—commits no crime under such a regime.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: Such a law does not criminalize abortion, it merely criminalizes abortion performed by a licensed abortionist. A pro-life law that gives automatic immunity to the mother in all cases does nothing about the legality of abortion itself. All it does is *make it illegal for an abortionist to help a mother murder her baby*. Do-it-yourself abortion is still perfectly legal under pro-life laws.

q: How does Silberman respond to the historical claim that American law has never prosecuted parents for having their children aborted?
a: He concedes the historical fact but rejects it as a guide for the future. As Roe footnote 54 itself shows, that legacy was used to undermine the personhood of the preborn—it is a precedent of injustice, not a model worth preserving.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: On their flier, Students for Life points out that the American legal system has never prosecuted mothers and fathers for having their children murdered. They’re right, but as demonstrated by Roe footnote 54, that legacy of foolishness and injustice is not an example to return to but a terrible mistake to learn from.

q: What additional resource does the article point readers to for a fuller treatment of these arguments?
a: Silberman directs readers to a presentation by Texas family attorney Bradley Pierce, delivered at the 2018 Church Arise Conference, which examines the equal-justice and DIY-abortion problems with mother-immunity laws in greater depth.
t: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
qt: The below presentation from Texas family attorney Bradley Pierce from the 2018 Church Arise Conference delves deeply and articulately into all of these matters.

q: Why does Silberman accuse Joe Pojman and Texas Alliance for Life of idolatry rather than mere strategic disagreement?
a: Because Pojman framed obedience to the Supreme Court as a fixed law of nature on par with gravity, which elevates the Court above God. Silberman reads this as making the Court the functional deity to which Pojman owes ultimate allegiance, with God demoted to a temporary ally only when His word agrees with SCOTUS.
t: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
qt: In equating obedience to the Supreme Court with a law of nature, Pojman is telling us who his god is. His allegiance to the true and living God is reduced to a temporary alliance whenever God's word happens to match the Supreme Court's opinion.

q: What pattern does Silberman see across pro-life leadership in multiple states?
a: He says no major pro-life group has backed abolition bills in Oklahoma, Indiana, Idaho, or Texas. Instead they keep returning to the Supreme Court to ask permission to do what is right, treating the Court as the legitimate arbiter of whether murder may be outlawed.
t: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
qt: It's why not one prominent pro-life organization has supported the efforts to abolish abortion in Oklahoma, Indiana, Idaho, and now Texas. They think returning every few years to grovel for the Supreme Court's permission to do what's right is the appropriate way to fight the arbitrary federal legalization of murder.

q: What biblical commands does Silberman say make compliance with Roe impossible for Christians?
a: He cites the duty to rescue those being led to slaughter, to bring justice to the fatherless, and to love one's neighbor as oneself. He insists these commands and submission to Roe are mutually exclusive: a Christian can do one or the other, not both.
t: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
qt: The Bible tells us to "rescue those being led to slaughter" (Prv 24:10), "bring justice to the fatherless" (Is 1:15), and love our neighbors as ourselves (Matt 22:39). You can do these things or you can submit to Supreme Court opinions stating that murdering certain humans should be legal, but you cannot do both.

q: On constitutional grounds, why does Silberman say Roe v. Wade has no claim on the states?
a: He argues the 14th Amendment's equal protection clause already covers preborn humans, and Article VI says only the Constitution and laws made pursuant to it are legitimate. Roe is neither a law nor pursuant to the Constitution, a point he reinforces by quoting Stanford Law's John Hart Ely dismissing Roe as not even attempting to be constitutional law.
t: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
qt: As former dean of Stanford Law John Hart Ely put it, "[Roe v Wade] is not constitutional law and gives almost no sense of an obligation to try to be."

q: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison to address the hypothetical that the Constitution itself protected abortion?
a: He notes Garrison publicly burned the Constitution in 1854 over its slavery compromises, illustrating that a higher moral law against killing innocents would override even the founding document. Silberman then clarifies that no such conflict exists for abortion, so the Constitution can be enlisted alongside, though not as the foundation of, the abolitionist case.
t: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
qt: During a July 4th, 1854 speech to the Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society, William Lloyd Garrison burned a copy of the US Constitution, among other documents, because of the pre- 13th and 14th amendment compromises which allowed for slavery.

q: What does Silberman propose as the practical course of action for legislators and citizens?
a: Repent of treating SCOTUS as ultimate, ignore Roe, and pass laws abolishing abortion. The decisive consideration should not be fear of judicial reprisal but fear of divine judgment for continuing to allow the killing.
t: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
qt: We need to repent of this idolatry. We need to ignore Roe and abolish abortion. This requires less concern over what men will do if we outlaw murder and more concern about what God will do if we don't.

q: What did the Supreme Court strike down in this June 2020 decision, and how narrow was the ruling?
a: The Court issued a 5-4 ruling against a Louisiana law that would have required abortion clinics to maintain hospital admitting privileges within 30 miles. The law was a modest health regulation, not a ban, yet still failed at the Court.
t: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
qt: They have struck down another milquetoast pro-life regulation, a Louisiana law which would have required abortion clinics to have hospital admitting privileges within 30 miles.

q: Why did Chief Justice Roberts vote with the majority despite previously opposing the same legal position?
a: Roberts had dissented from the 2016 Texas case that struck down a similar law, but he sided with the four Democrat-appointed justices in 2020 on the basis of stare decisis, treating the prior ruling as binding precedent regardless of his view of its merits.
t: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
qt: Even though Roberts previously dissented the 2016 decision, he concurred with Monday's decision on the basis of the “precedent” that the 2016 decision set.

q: Why does Lockwood say strategies built on overturning Roe through precedent are doomed?
a: Because the Court treats precedent, not the Constitution, as its governing authority, and Roe itself is precedent. Any reasoning chain that respects precedent will leave Roe in place.
t: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
qt: So long as its decisions are made on the basis of precedent, *Roe* will never be overturned. *Roe* *is* precedent.

q: What does the failure of a mere regulation imply about the prospects for an outright abortion ban at the Supreme Court?
a: If the Court will not even let states regulate which clinics may operate based on safety credentials, there is no realistic basis to expect it would uphold legislation criminalizing abortion as murder. The regulatory path offers no on-ramp to abolition.
t: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
qt: If the Supreme Court won't even uphold a law which merely regulates murder, how can we expect it to uphold a law criminalizing abortion altogether?

q: Why does Lockwood argue the Louisiana law was destined to lose on its own terms?
a: The law conceded the pro-abortion frame by treating abortion as a legitimate medical procedure that simply needed safety regulation. Building a defense from that false premise hollowed out any moral or legal force behind it.
t: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
qt: It treated abortion like a medical procedure, then argued from that false premise that it should be regulated in order to keep those getting abortions safe.

q: What course of action does Lockwood prescribe for state officials in response?
a: He calls for magistrates to defy the Supreme Court and criminalize abortion as murder, grounding the duty in the Fourteenth Amendment and Amos 5:15 rather than waiting for federal permission.
t: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
qt: It is the constitutional and God-given duty of the states to criminalize abortion as murder, as outlined in the 14th Amendment and Amos 5:15.

q: How does the article frame the abolitionist plan in contrast to continued pro-life advocacy?
a: Lockwood describes abolition as a straightforward biblical mandate: obey God by defying tyrants, establish justice for the vulnerable, and trust God with the outcome rather than tailoring legislation to win Court approval.
t: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
qt: Abolitionists, on the other hand, have a simple, feasible, and biblical plan for abolishing abortion: Obey God by defying tyrants! Establish justice for the least.

q: Why does the author refuse to celebrate Tennessee's SB2196 even though pro-life friends call it a step in the right direction?
a: Because evaluating direction requires markers—our current location, a destination, and a map—and by those measures the bill moves away from biblical justice rather than toward it. It will not actually save a single baby and submits to the very court opinions it claims to oppose.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: All in all, this bill is not going to save one single baby. It will be blocked by the courts before it ever goes into force.

q: What three things does the author say we need in order to know whether our steps are going in the right direction?
a: We need an honest assessment of where we are, a clearly defined goal, and a reliable guide for getting there. Without all three, claims of progress are arbitrary.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt:
If we want to know if our steps are going in the right direction, we need:

1. an understanding of our current location
2. a specific destination to aim for
3. a map so we don’t get lost

q: How does the author describe the current state of abortion in America after 47 years of pro-life activism?
a: She describes a normalized, legalized holocaust in which roughly one in four women have an abortion by age 45 and hundreds of thousands of babies are killed every year, with chemical abortions increasingly going uncounted.
