t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: In the fight against abortion our current location is smack dab in the middle of a legalized holocaust. The shedding of innocent blood is so common that according to [Guttmacher Institute](https://www.guttmacher.org/fact-sheet/induced-abortion-united-states), approximately one in four women will have an abortion by age 45.

q: What does the author identify as the proper destination of legislation against abortion?
a: Justice—not merely cultural change or saving some babies along the way, but establishing justice for victims and restraining evil, because that is what God commands of civil magistrates.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt:
Because law isn’t about erasing all evil from society, it is about establishing justice for victims and restraining evil as much as is possible.

*Justice*, that’s our destination.

q: What biblical case law does the author cite to argue God demands justice for the child in the womb?
a: Exodus 21:22-23, which she reads as case law requiring eye-for-eye, life-for-life justice when a preborn child is harmed, demonstrating that the image-bearing child in the womb is owed equal protection.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: On top of these crystal clear instructions, He gave us case law in Exodus 21:22-23 explaining that if someone strikes a woman and the child she is carrying is delivered early and harmed in any way, the one who struck her must pay eye for eye, tooth for tooth, life for life. We can deduce from this that God demands justice for the child in the womb, the child He fearfully and wonderfully made in His own image.

q: Why does leaving heartbeat detection to abortionists undermine SB2196's pragmatic effectiveness?
a: Because ultrasound at six weeks is operator dependent and the staff with financial incentive to abort are the same people deciding whether a heartbeat is found, with no enforcement mechanism beyond a self-reported note in the medical record.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: **The abortionists are left on the honor system**. All this bill requires is that the abortionist note in the woman’s medical record that an ultrasound was done and no heartbeat was detected. No one is checking up on this. There is no enforcement mechanism.

q: How does SB2196's narrow ban on sex, race, and Down syndrome abortions actually fail in practice?
a: It permits abortion for any other reason or no reason at all, and since abortionists are not required to ask why a woman is aborting, women who want to abort for the prohibited reasons will simply lie—rendering the carve-out unenforceable.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: SB2196 allows women to abort for literally any reason besides race, sex, or Downs syndrome diagnosis, or for no reason at all. Women are not stupid. If they want to abort their children for any of these *off-limits* reasons, they will lie about it.

q: What is the central justice problem the author identifies with treating abortion as healthcare rather than murder?
a: Categorizing abortion as healthcare turns the bill into a regulation of how killing may be done rather than a criminalization of the killing itself, fundamentally misnaming the act.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: Throughout this bill categorizes abortion as healthcare, regulates it like healthcare, discusses the health effects on women and the state’s interest in whether or not the fetus feels pain during the procedure. This is a regulatory bill on how something may be done, not the criminalization of a murderous practice.

q: Why does the author say Tennessee lawmakers are complicit in evil rather than resisting it?
a: Because they admit preborn children are human beings from conception yet voluntarily submit to SCOTUS opinions that require them to permit those children's killing, instead of nullifying wicked court rulings as their constitutional duty demands.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: Instead of defying and nullifying wicked and unconstitutional court opinions, they choose to submit to and enforce them, making them active participants in the evil.

q: On what grounds does the author argue that mothers seeking abortion should not be exempted from prosecution?
a: She argues that a woman who deliberately schedules, pays for, consents to, and physically cooperates in the killing of her child meets the legal definition of premeditated murder, and exempting her from prosecution as perpetrator, accomplice, or conspirator is unjust nonsense.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: **Penalties for women are prohibited**. SB2196 prohibits the prosecution of women for their involvement in killing their children even when they are doing so in defiance of this law. They cannot be prosecuted as perpetrators, accomplices, or conspirators. This is complete nonsense and utterly unjust.

q: What track record does the author cite to show that 'heartbeat' and similar bills have not actually saved babies?
a: She points out that every heartbeat bill, six-week ban, and near-total ban touted in pro-life headlines has been blocked in court before saving a single baby, and the Tennessee lawsuit was already drafted before passage.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: None of the bills you have seen in pro-life headlines touted as *Heartbeat Bills*, *6 Week Bans*, *Near Total Abortion Bans*, etc. have ever gone into force—not a single one. They have all been blocked by the courts right out of the gate before one single baby was ever saved by them. In Tennessee, the lawsuit is already drawn up.

q: What closing challenge does the author leave with readers who keep calling such bills steps in the right direction?
a: She challenges them to look at where they actually are—because if regulating, not abolishing, abortion is the result of decades of effort, the destination has already been reached.
t: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
qt: So friends, when you say a heartbeat bill is *a step in the right direction*, I simply ask, what direction are you going? Please take a look around you because it is quite possible you have already arrived at your destination.

q: What does the author say SB1236 actually accomplishes, as opposed to what its supporters claim?
a: Silberman argues the bill is a substitute for real action. Rather than abolishing abortion, it begs the Supreme Court for permission to act and downgrades the murder of a preborn child to a Class C felony, which he says further dehumanizes those children and entrenches judicial supremacy.
t: Tennessee SB 1236 Fails to Abide By its Own Preamble
qt: This bill accomplishes nothing but entrench the judicial idolatry that is perpetuating the murder and further dehumanize preborn children by treating their murder as only a Class C felony.

q: Why does the author label Section 1(a) of the bill 'judicial idolatry'?
a: Because that section conditions the entire bill on the Supreme Court's approval, the author says the drafters effectively concede that Tennessee lacks authority to protect life if the Court disagrees. He calls this constitutionally and morally illiterate cowardice and insists states have both the authority and the obligation to defy the Court when it sanctions mass murder.
t: Tennessee SB 1236 Fails to Abide By its Own Preamble
qt: Section 1 (a) of the bill explicitly subjects the bill to judicial tyranny, establishing that the authors of the bill do not believe the state of Tennessee has the authority to uphold the unalienable right to life if the Supreme Court opines that they shouldn't.

q: How does treating abortion as a Class C felony violate equal protection in the author's view?
a: Silberman argues that assigning a lesser penalty to the killing of preborn humans than to the killing of born humans creates an unequal class of personhood, violating the 14th Amendment's equal protection clause and committing the sin of partiality. He says it dehumanizes preborn boys and girls by codifying that their lives merit less legal protection.
t: Tennessee SB 1236 Fails to Abide By its Own Preamble
qt: By providing preborn human beings a lesser level of protection than born human beings, the authors of the bill have dehumanized preborn boys and girls, written an unjust law, committed the sin of partiality, violated the 14th amendment which prohibits unequal protection, and have entirely undermined the case against abortion.

q: How does the author use Roe v. Wade itself to argue against unequal penalties?
a: He points to Justice Blackmun's footnote 54, which exploited the fact that Texas treated abortion with lighter penalties than murder and exempted the woman from prosecution. Silberman says this gave the Court a hook to deny preborn personhood, and he warns that SB1236 repeats the same self-defeating mistake that helped produce Roe.
t: Tennessee SB 1236 Fails to Abide By its Own Preamble
qt: That the state of Texas did not provide equal protection for preborn human beings by treating the murder of a preborn human being as murder betrayed the fact that even the pro-lifers didn't think the preborn child was a human being.

q: What does the author mean when he says SB1236's authors must 'abide by their own preamble'?
a: The bill's preamble cites the 14th Amendment's prohibition on depriving any person of life or denying equal protection. Silberman argues that if the legislators actually believe their own preamble, they are bound to refuse enforcement of any law, court opinion, or regulation, including pro-life regulations and Roe itself, that lets preborn people be killed.
t: Tennessee SB 1236 Fails to Abide By its Own Preamble
qt: The authors of Tennessee SB1236 must abide by their own preamble and refuse to enforce any law, court opinion, or regulation which deprives preborn human beings of life.

q: What action does the author say Tennessee should take instead of passing SB1236?
a: He calls for the state to ignore Roe outright and pass total, immediate abolition rather than seeking judicial permission or settling for partial penalties. Anything short of that, he argues, is itself unconstitutional under the 14th Amendment standard the bill's own preamble invokes.
t: Tennessee SB 1236 Fails to Abide By its Own Preamble
qt: They must disregard Roe as the moral atrocity and legal nullity that it is and immediately and totally abolish abortion, thereby upholding the unalienable right to life.

q: What exactly did the Republican Party of Texas adopt regarding abortion at its biennial convention?
a: The RPT passed a legislative priority calling for the complete abolition of abortion by guaranteeing the right to life and equal protection of the laws to every preborn child from fertilization onward.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: During their recent biennial convention, the Republican Party of Texas (RPT) passed a legislative priority to abolish abortion completely. It reads: “Abolish abortion by ensuring the right to life and equal protection of the laws to all preborn children from the moment of fertilization.”

q: How decisively did the abolitionist position prevail over incrementalism at the convention hearings?
a: Abolitionists outnumbered incrementalists testifying at the hearings by five-to-one, and the resulting abolition priority became one of the party's eight official priorities, while the competing incremental proposal failed to crack the top fifteen.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: Despite Texas Right to Life and Texas Alliance for Life’s insistence on an incremental approach to ending abortion, the RPT chose to listen to the abolitionists, who outnumbered those testifying in favor of incrementalism five-to-one at the hearings. The complete abolition of abortion is now one of Texas Republicans’ eight official legislative priorities, while the incremental proposal did not even make the top fifteen.

q: What argument did delegate David Sims make against the prevailing pro-life strategy?
a: Sims told the Legislative Priorities Committee that begging the Supreme Court for permission to compromise with evil is unprincipled and ineffective, and that Texas must instead stand on Scripture and the Constitution to extend equal protection against murder to preborn children.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: “We’ve got to stop with the strategy of begging the supreme court to allow us to compromise with evil. It’s unprincipled and it clearly isn’t working. It’s time to stand firmly on God’s word and on the Constitution and provide equal protection of the laws against murder to preborn children. It’s time to abolish abortion.”

q: How did Wesley Thomas respond to fears that abolishing abortion in Texas could trigger civil war?
a: Thomas reframed the question by pointing out that more than 55,000 children are aborted in Texas each year, arguing that war is already underway and that refusing to risk our own lives to stop it amounts to demanding that those children die in our place.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: “55,000 plus children per year are currently being aborted… That *is* a war, okay? And so… if we’re not willing to lay down our lives to save 55,000 children, are we not asking them to lay down their lives for us instead?”

q: What does delegate David Bellow's mid-convention conversion suggest about the appeal of abolitionism?
a: Bellow heard the abolitionist testimonies, was persuaded on the spot, and offered his own testimony declaring that an unborn person has a right to life from conception regardless of federal opinion, illustrating that Christians not pre-poisoned by pro-life slander tend to embrace the abolitionist call.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: “We need to say… that an unborn person is a person and has a right to life from conception, and if the federal government wants to say otherwise, who cares?” Bellow’s on-the-spot conversion is a testament to the growth of abolitionism. When Christians who haven’t already been fed slander about abolitionists by pro-life leaders hear the call of abolition, they are often persuaded to the cause.

q: Why was the fight over keeping the words 'equal protection' in the priority so significant?
a: A committee member moved to strike 'equal protection' so the priority would call for some lesser protection of preborn children rather than treating them as legal equals; abolitionist J.R. Haas argued that denying equal protection to persons is intolerable before God and the Constitution, and the motion was withdrawn, exposing how readily incrementalists were willing to deny preborn children equal standing under law.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: “He is saying that we should deny equal protection to preborn children. Friends, I don’t know about you, but I have a real problem with that,” Haas said. “Do we believe that these babies are persons? If so, we must not, before God and under our Constitution, deny them the equal protection of our laws. Please do not strike these two words. Thank you.”

q: What concession did incrementalists actually win in the final wording?
a: They successfully removed the explicit language calling for defiance of Roe and Casey, though Lockwood notes that the priority still implicitly defies the Supreme Court because abortion cannot be abolished from conception without doing so.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: Incrementalists were able to strike other language from the legislative priority however, most notably the wording which explicitly called for *Roe* and *Casey* to be defied. The language of the bill still implies defiance of the supreme court, however, as abortion cannot be abolished from conception without defying the supreme court.

q: What practical pressure does this legislative priority put on Texas state representatives?
a: According to Rep. Briscoe Cain, the priorities function as commands from the grassroots, so legislators who refuse to support the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act will face constituents who can point directly to the official Republican legislative priorities and demand to know why.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: “With abolition being a clearly-written legislative priority, I expect more legislators to sign on [to The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act]. They may do so willingly or because there’s a mandate from the grassroots… I think legislators who don’t support it will find many upset constituents who are able to point to the Texas Republican legislative priorities and say ‘why didn’t you support this?’ After the legislative session, Republican legislators who rebuffed the grassroots will have a lot of questions to answer.”

q: What happened the last time the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act came up for consideration?
a: Two years earlier, the bill drew 11 cosponsors and over 300 supportive witnesses in a hearing that ran until almost 3 a.m., yet committee chairman Jeff Leach refused to grant it a vote, leaving abortion legal in Texas and resulting in the legal killing of an estimated 110,000 preborn children.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: Two years ago, the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act had 11 cosponsors and over 300 people who testified in favor of it, causing the hearing to last until 2:58AM. Nevertheless, committee chairman Jeff Leach denied the bill a vote, keeping abortion legal in Texas for two more years and guaranteeing 110,000 preborn children would be legally murdered.

q: What position has new RPT chairman Allen West taken on a bill of abolition?
a: West has publicly committed that he would speak in full support of any bill that abolishes abortion and saves the lives of unborn babies beginning at conception.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: “If a bill were introduced that would abolish abortion – saving the lives of unborn babies, beginning at conception – I would speak in full support.”

q: What does the author argue Texas must finally do, and on what authority?
a: Lockwood calls Texas to abandon incremental strategies and oppose unjust court rulings by extending equal protection to every preborn human, framing this duty as commanded by God, the U.S. Constitution, and the Texas Constitution alike.
t: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
qt: It’s time for Texas to stop with the fruitless incremental strategies and do what it should have done from the very beginning: oppose unjust court rulings and provide equal protection for all preborn humans as mandated by God, the US Constution, and the Texas Constitution.

q: What does Silberman say is the most reliable indicator that someone knows they are losing an argument?
a: Silberman flips Van Maren's own framing back on him: while avoiding key points is one tell, an even stronger tell is a pattern of dishonesty in debate. He claims Van Maren is establishing exactly that pattern.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Avoidance of important points is always a good indicator that someone knows they're wrong, as Van Maren astutely, yet obliviously pointed out. But an even greater indicator of someone who knows they're wrong is when they are consistently unable to engage in a debate without dishonesty. Van Maren is beginning to demonstrate an undeniable pattern of deception.

q: How does Silberman defend Wilberforce's prioritizing the slave trade before emancipation against the charge that this made him an incrementalist?
a: He argues Wilberforce and Clarkson believed that criminalizing the trade would functionally kill the institution of slavery itself, citing Clarkson's 'axe at the very root' language and Wilberforce's 1807 'what shall we abolish next?' to show they treated the trade ban as the decisive blow, not as a regulatory compromise.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Manstealing was the foundation of slavery, and by criminalizing that act, the abolitionists believed they were delivering the death blow to slavery. In his article on the subject from 2014, Van Maren quotes from Bury the Chains by Adam Hochschild which talks about the decision of the Society for Effecting the Abolition of the Slave Trade to abolish the trade rather than go for emancipation. Van Maren's excerpt stops a few sentences short of the crucial line: "Abolishing the trade, Clarkson was certain, was 'laying the axe at the very root' of Caribbean slavery itself."

q: What distinction does Silberman draw between the British abolitionists' approach and modern pro-life incrementalism?
a: He argues British abolitionists rejected dehumanizing increments — they would never have accepted a bill protecting only people who were 25 percent white, then 50 percent — whereas pro-life incrementalism does the equivalent by pegging protection to gestational age (24-week, 20-week, 15-week, heartbeat).
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Had someone suggested protecting those who were 25 percent white, then 50 percent, then 75 percent, then abolition, it would have been roundly rejected by both American and British abolitionists. This is the sort of incrementalism pro-lifers practice with 24-week bans, 20-week bans, 15-week bans, heartbeat bills, etc.

q: Why does Silberman defend the abolitionist coinage 'overnightism'?
a: He concedes abolitionists made the word, but says they did so to label a straw man invented by incrementalists — the caricature that immediatists believe abortion can be ended overnight by 'snapping fingers' — so that the difference between immediatism and that caricature could be clearly named.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Overnightism is a word we made up, but it is a concept pro-life incrementalists made up. They straw man immediatism as being an ideology where you can simply "snap your fingers" or "wave a magic wand" and abortion is gone overnight without any hard work or faithfulness.

q: What parallel does Silberman draw between Henry Dundas and modern pro-life leaders?
a: He cites Dundas's 1792 speech professing agreement with Wilberforce's end goal while opposing immediate abolition on grounds of prudence and practicability, and argues this is the identical posture taken today by Van Maren, Klusendorf, Abby Johnson, and Right to Life.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Wait a second. Dundas agreed with Wilberforce's end goal but opposed immediate abolition because he disagreed with the abolitionists as to what was the most effective and practicable strategy? Where have we heard that exact argument before? Ah, right. Van Maren. And Klusendorf. And Abby. And Right to Life. The Pro-Life Movement clearly – clearly! – is Dolben and Dundas, not Wilberforce and Clarkson.

q: How does Silberman use Van Maren's own podcast statement about the Dolben Act against him?
a: He notes that Van Maren explicitly called the Dolben Act 'the brainchild' of pro-life clinic-regulation strategy, then shows from Wilberforce's own writing that Wilberforce condemned the Dolben Act as ultimately beneficial to slaveholders — meaning Van Maren has, by his own analogy, identified pro-life strategy with what Wilberforce opposed.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Van Maren is on record calling the Dolben Act "the brainchild" of the pro-life political strategy, and Wilberforce is on record opposing the Dolben Act.

q: How does Silberman respond to Van Maren's 'And then what?' challenge about federal pushback against state abolition bills?
a: He says abolitionists fully expect pushback — likely economic sanctions under a Democratic administration, conceivably even federal force — but argues the question presupposes cowardice abolitionists do not have, and insists they will not retreat regardless of cost.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: BABIES ARE BEING MURDERED. You can put a gun to ours heads and we won't budge from demanding abortion's total and immediate abolition.

q: What statistic does Silberman use to argue that pro-life messaging has produced a watered-down movement?
a: He cites Gallup showing 46 percent self-identify as pro-life while Pew shows only 12 percent want abortion illegal in all cases, meaning only roughly one-in-four pro-lifers actually wants total abolition — a result he attributes to bills like pain-capable acts that teach pain-capability rather than humanity as the criterion for protection.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: While it is true that pro-life vs. pro-choice polls remain roughly even, with 46 percent of the country identifying as pro-life, only 12 percent support abortion being illegal in all cases. That means that only one-in-four pro-lifers desires to see abortion completely abolished. This is the fruit of pro-life compromise.

q: What evidence does Silberman give that the Abolitionist Movement was not in decline during the years Van Maren claims Russell Hunter needed to 'resurrect' his influence?
a: He points to the first abolition bill in 2016, abolition bills in nine states over the next four years, the 2018 Dan Fisher gubernatorial campaign, the 2019 Oklahoma Southern Baptist and Free Will Baptist resolutions, and a 2020 capitol rally of nearly 3,000 people.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Russell joined the Dan Fisher for Governor campaign in 2018 which put the Abolitionist Movement on the map politically in Oklahoma. Free the States was founded at the conclusion of the Fisher campaign. 2019 is when the Oklahoma Southern Baptists and Free Will Baptists passed resolutions demanding abortion's immediate abolition without any compromise. 2020 saw the biggest abolitionist rally ever with almost 3,000 people at the Oklahoma State Capitol.

q: Why does Silberman call Van Maren's framing of bloodguiltiness as 'oddly catholic' theologically problematic?
a: He argues that treating repentance — including national or corporate repentance for systemic sin — as Roman Catholic penance betrays an antinomian instinct, citing Luther's first thesis that the entire life of believers is meant to be one of repentance.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: That Van Maren thinks Christians and nations repenting of sin is a Roman Catholic doctrine makes me worried about his understanding of sin and repentance. The very first of Martin Luther's 95 theses was, "Our Lord and Master Jesus Christ. willed the entire life of believers to be one of repentance."

q: How does Silberman rebut Van Maren's claim that the Pro-Life Movement is the first human rights movement to lack press support?
a: He counters that the slavery abolitionists had only their own publications — The Liberator, The North Star, The Genius of Universal Emancipation — while the modern Pro-Life Movement is regularly published in conservative outlets and even mainstream papers like the New York Times, USA Today, and Bloomberg. He quotes Garrison's 1858 'Depravity of the American Press' to show abolitionists faced near-total press hostility.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: The Pro-Life Movement has the support of every conservative outlet, and pro-lifers are regularly published in mainstream leftist publications such as the New York Times, USA Today, Bloomberg, and others. Of the fact that the vast majority of the mainstream press is pro-child sacrifice, there can be no reasonable doubt. But pro-lifers still enjoy far, far greater media support than what the abolitionists of slavery enjoyed.

q: What kind of abolition bill does Silberman offer as one Van Maren should be willing to support?
a: He points to Idaho H0056, an abolition bill that does not formally repeal pro-life regulations but supersedes them by directing the attorney general to enforce the new chapter, neutralizing the standard pro-life objection that an abolition bill would wipe out existing restrictions.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: ID H0056 is one such example. It repeals only one section of the Idaho homicide statutes which protects legal abortion. It does not officially repeal pro-life regulations. It simply supersedes them while leaving them on the books by establishing that "Notwithstanding any other provision of law, the attorney general shall monitor this state's enforcement of this chapter in relation to abortion."

q: Why does Silberman argue that pain-capable bans actively miseducate the public about abortion?
a: He says pain-capable bans frame the conversation around the horror of aborting pain-capable children rather than the humanity of all preborn children, teaching people that pain-capability is the basis for legal protection — and offers an anecdote of a woman entering an abortion clinic saying her baby would not feel pain.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Pain-capable bans do not create a conversation about the horror of abortion, they create a conversation about the horror of aborting pain-capable children. These laws establish pain-capability as the basis for protection under the law. Beyond being fundamentally unjust, this educates the public that pain-capability is what makes humans worthy of protection. This is what led a young woman to walk into the Norman child sacrifice center in 2016 saying, "It's okay. My baby won't even feel any pain."

q: Why does Silberman say abolitionists discuss criminal liability for women at all, given Van Maren's 'camping out' accusation?
a: He says abolitionists only address it because pro-lifers make automatic immunity one of their primary objections, and lists four reasons such immunity is wrong: it denies equal protection, allows self-induced abortion, removes deterrence, and undercuts the anti-abortion argument both morally and legally.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt:
We wouldn't dwell on the topic a single minute if it weren't for pro-lifers making this one of their biggest objections to abolition bills. So, often, we must explain why giving women automatic immunity in the murder of their children:

1. Is fundamentally unjust by denying equal protection to the preborn child,
2. allows self-induced abortion,
3. provides no deterrent against abortion, and
4. undercuts the anti-abortion argument morally and legally.

q: How does Silberman answer the coercion objection to fully criminalizing abortion?
a: He notes that abolition bills already incorporate the longstanding legal principle that those acting under involuntary subjection are not capable of committing crimes, citing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act's restatement of existing law.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, for instance, restates existing law which establishes that "Persons who committed the act, or make the omission charged, while under involuntary subjection to the power of superiors" are not capable of committing crimes.

q: How does Silberman correct Van Maren's use of Blackstone's Ratio?
a: He argues Blackstone's Ratio is properly a counsel to jurors and judges to require certainty before convicting — quoting John Adams on the catastrophe of condemning the innocent — and is not an argument against passing legislation, since by that logic no laws could exist.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: In its correct context, Blackstone's Ratio is an argument for cautiousness on the part of jurors and judges. They must not convict a man of a crime unless they are absolutely certain of his guilt because the consequences of a wrongful conviction are enormous... But Blackstone's Ratio cannot be employed against legislation, and certainly not against legislation which makes all humans equal under law. If it can, then all laws must be done away with because they make possible the prosecution and conviction of the innocent.

q: How does Silberman answer the claim that no single law can address the hundred different reasons women have abortions?
a: He argues a prospective murder charge would deter the vast majority of those women — and would also deter the upstream immorality that leads to abortion by removing it as a safety net — and frames this as the Romans 13 obligation of government to be a terror to evildoers.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: A prospective murder charge would have disincentivized every single one of those women from murdering their sons and daughters. A handful likely would have murdered their child anyway, but the vast majority wouldn't. Even better, many men and women wouldn't be so irresponsible and immoral if they didn't have abortion as a safety net. That murder charge doesn't only disincentivize abortion, it would disincentivize the immorality that leads to abortion.

q: How does Silberman address the claim that varying degrees of moral culpability among post-abortive women preclude criminalization?
a: He affirms the premise but says varying culpability is exactly what prosecutors, jurors, and judges exist to weigh case-by-case. The legislature's role is the equal treatment of all human beings under law, which only an abolition bill accomplishes.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: But varying degrees of culpability do not mean we refrain from criminalizing murder, theft, assault, fraud, etc. It means that prosecutors and jurors must treat every instance individually, taking into account all context relevant to the crime and making their prosecutorial and sentencing decisions accordingly. Prosecutors, jurors, and judges sort out the details of each case. The role of the legislator is to treat all human beings equally under the law.

q: Where does Silberman locate the actual origin of abolitionism, and what does he credit Russell Hunter and the Abolitionist Movement with specifically?
a: He says abolitionism originates in Scripture — Genesis through Revelation — and was practically applied by the slavery abolitionists. Russell and the post-2010 movement did not invent the doctrine but were the first to comprehensively apply it to abortion, with Senator Joseph Silk filing the first true abolition bill in 2016.
t: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
qt: Is that to say abolitionism originated with Russell? Of course not. It originates in Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Proverbs, Isaiah, Amos, Ezekiel, Matthew, Romans, Acts, Ephesians, Revelation, and many other books of scripture. These principles were then put into practice by the abolitionists of slavery. But they were not comprehensively applied to the abortion battle until Russell and the abolitionists came on the scene in 2010.

q: What is Warren Hamilton actually running on, and how does Silberman distinguish that from typical pro-life candidacies?
a: Hamilton is running on total, immediate abolition rather than negotiation, delay, or symbolic regulatory victories. Silberman frames every other posture, including the kind that the establishment rewards on surveys, as a way of avoiding abolition.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: Warren Hamilton is running for Oklahoma 7th Senate District on a platform to completely abolish abortion. He is not running to compromise with it. He is not running to delay abolition with more regulations that do nothing to establish justice.

q: Why did Hamilton refuse to fill out the Oklahomans for Life survey, and what does Silberman say a perfect score on it actually requires?
a: Silberman says OFL has been the chief obstacle to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act since 2016, and that earning a 100 percent score requires opposing abolition and supporting bills that allow babies conceived in rape to be killed. Hamilton declined the survey on those grounds.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: To get 100% on OFL's survey, as Boggs did, [a candidate has to agree to oppose the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act and support legislation that allows for human beings conceived in rape to be murdered]. Warren did not fill out OFL's immoral survey because he is running to abolish abortion.

q: How does Silberman explain the political function that incremental pro-life bills served for Senator Greg Treat in 2019?
a: After Treat killed SB13 and faced public outrage, he authored a trigger bill and helped pass three incremental bills that did nothing to abolish abortion but rehabilitated his pro-life reputation among voters who didn't grasp the maneuver.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: What these bills did accomplish, though, was help Treat save face. The outrage against him was somewhat quelled. These bills preserved Treat's pro-life reputation in the minds of those who did not understand what he was doing.

q: Beyond political cover, what cultural damage does the article say incremental bills inflict?
a: Silberman argues these bills teach the public dehumanizing falsehoods, citing a woman who justified her abortion because the baby couldn't feel pain (a line she absorbed from the Pain-Capable Unborn Child Protection Act) and an Ohio senator who believed life begins at a heartbeat because of the Heartbeat Bill.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: Furthermore, incremental compromise bills teach people dehumanizing misinformation about preborn human beings. Free the States lobbyist Russell Hunter discovered this when he was outside a killing center in Norman trying to talk to abortion-minded mothers and fathers in 2016. After engaging one young woman who was going in for an abortion, she told Russell that she was pro-life, but that the abortion was okay because the baby was too young to feel pain.

q: What does Silberman say is really blocking abolition in Oklahoma, given Republican supermajorities?
a: He insists the obstacle isn't a vote count but an ideology: Republicans choose to pass regulations instead of an abolition bill they actually have the votes to pass.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: In a state like Oklahoma where pro-life Republicans hold super-majorities in the Senate and the House, it's not that we cannot pass a bill abolishing abortion because we do not have the votes, **it's that we could pass a bill abolishing abortion but choose to pass pro-life bills regulating abortion instead.**

q: How does the article use 19th-century antislavery voices to indict gradualism?
a: Silberman quotes the American Anti-Slavery Society's 1833 Declaration of Sentiments, William Lloyd Garrison's 'NO COMPROMISE WITH SLAVERY' banner, and Elizabeth Heyrick's Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition, all to argue that calls for gradual emancipation were a slaveholder ploy that merely entrenches the evil.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: Abolitionist movement leader William Lloyd Garrison said similarly, "Come what may – cost what it may – inscribe on the banner which you unfurl to the breeze, as your religious and political motto – 'NO COMPROMISE WITH SLAVERY!'"  There are innumerable principled and pragmatic reasons why Garrison was correct. May we similarly proclaim "NO COMPROMISE WITH CHILD SACRIFICE!"

q: Why does Silberman call SB1721, the Unborn Person Equality Act, hollow despite its language about personhood?
a: He argues that Section 2 Part C makes every protection conditional on the Supreme Court agreeing, which negates the bill given that the federal courts have already rejected such positions 37 times.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: In other words, this bill establishes that life begins at conception **if the supreme court agrees** that life begins at conception. It establishes that there is a right to life and equal protection **if the supreme court agrees** that there is a right to life and equal protection.

q: What is the article's core complaint about SB1728, the Unborn Person Wrongful Death Act?
a: Silberman says the bill calls the unborn child a person yet still permits the killing, only requiring the abortionist to follow a procedural checklist to make the death 'rightful.' Senator Bullard reportedly admitted on the floor that the checklist is the bill's whole substance and even called it the best way to protect babies.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: **Senate Bill 1728 fully affirms the legality of the practice of murdering unborn children. It simply provides the abortionist with a few hoops to jump through to make the unborn person's death rightful.**

q: Why does Silberman dismiss HB1396, the sex-selection abortion ban, as pointless?
a: Sex of the baby usually isn't disclosed until 18-21 weeks, but abortion is already illegal in Oklahoma after 20 weeks, so the bill bans something that is largely already prohibited.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: This bill makes it illegal to have an abortion if the mother says she is aware of the sex of the child. Parents are typically first offered information about the sex of the child when the baby is 18-21 weeks. Abortion is already illegal in Oklahoma after 20 weeks, meaning the bill has no point.

q: How does Silberman read Heyrick's argument about why gradualism is satanic?
a: He quotes Heyrick's claim that 'gradual emancipation' originated with slaveholders themselves because contemplating gradual abolition breeds gradual indifference, allowing people to tolerate evil indefinitely. Silberman applies the same logic to incremental abortion bills.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: "But this GRADUAL ABOLITION, has been the grand marplot of human virtue and happiness; the very masterpiece of satanic policy. By converting the cry for immediate, into gradual emancipation, the prince of slave holders, 'transformed himself, with astonishing dexterity, into an angel of light,' and thereby 'deceived the very elect.'"

q: Why does Silberman think the establishment is panicking specifically about Hamilton's race?
a: With Joseph Silk leaving the Senate to run for Congress, the establishment doesn't want another abolitionist on the floor pressing for SB13-style bills, and Hamilton plus other endorsed candidates threaten to install several.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: The establishment is flailing with absurd attack mailers like this one because Warren has a real chance to unseat Boggs and introduce a bill to abolish abortion in the State Senate.

q: What is the article's bottom-line framing of the choice in Senate District 7?
a: Silberman reduces the contest to a single sentence: Boggs regulates abortion, Hamilton abolishes it.
t: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
qt: Don't be fooled by the establishment's use of a dark money group to attempt to deceive you. Larry Boggs is running to regulate abortion. Warren Hamilton is running to abolish it.

q: Why does Diffner say the immediatism vs. incrementalism debate is not really about clarity of argument?
a: Because, in Diffner's framing of Blake Gideon's point, God has already spoken on the matter. The dispute therefore is not whether the Bible is unclear but whether the disputant is willing to submit to God's revealed holiness rather than negotiate around it.
t: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
qt: There's no issue for debate between immediatism and incrementalism when God has already spoken. The issue is not clarity on the topic–the issue is the holiness of God, and whether or not you bow before the holiness of God.

q: How does the article define holiness, and why does that definition matter for the abolition argument?
a: Holiness means 'set apart'—utterly other, separate, and beyond human grasp. Diffner uses this otherness to insist that God's commands cannot be domesticated to fit human pragmatism; they stand on their own weight and demand conformity rather than negotiation.
t: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
qt: This is not surprising, given that holiness is literally the most other-worldly word that can be uttered. It does, after all, mean “set apart”–separate, completely different, other than.

q: What does Diffner say God's holiness actually does to creation?
a: He argues God's holiness is not Deistic distance but active, exclusive presence. It either annihilates what is unholy or, through Christ, produces complete conformity in the redeemed.
t: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
qt: God's separate otherness is exclusive, and *actively so*. God is separate, and He is transcendent, but He is not distant–He is present. His holiness proceeds forth from Him and affects everything in existence.

q: How does Isaiah's response in Isaiah 6 model the obedience Diffner is calling for?
a: Once cleansed, Isaiah responds without question or qualification, ready to obey before he even hears the assignment. Diffner uses this as the template for how Christians should respond to God's command to establish justice—immediately, not incrementally.
t: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
qt: He didn't ask any questions. In fact, God hadn't even told him what his mission was yet. Isaiah was immediately ready to do whatever God was going to tell him.

q: What role does pragmatism play in incrementalism, according to the article?
a: Diffner says pragmatism is the engine of incrementalism—a 'cunning arithmetic' that calculates apparent good while ignoring God's clear command. Under the weight of God's holiness, that arithmetic is incinerated as chaff.
t: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
qt: Pragmatism is now ruled out. Pragmatism is blown apart like straw before the incomprehensible weightiness of God's glory. It is incinerated like chaff before the consuming fire of God's holiness–namely, by His holy command to establish justice.

q: Which specific pro-life measures does the article classify as INjustice?
a: Diffner names parental consent initiatives, pain-capable unborn child protection acts, and heartbeat bills as examples of incremental laws that fall short of justice and therefore, in his view, establish injustice rather than abolish it.
t: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
qt: Of course, the pragmatism we speak of here is the one that very many use to argue for and justify the establishment of INjustice (which is anything short of justice, including parental consent initiatives, pain-capable unborn child protection acts, heartbeat bills, and the like), contrary to God's law, on the altar of “success” as this incrementalist sees it.

q: Why does Diffner think incrementalists rarely cite Scripture to defend their position?
a: Because, he argues, love of God's holy commands and worldly pragmatism are mutually exclusive. If incrementalism rests on pragmatic calculation, it cannot legitimately appeal to Scripture as its foundation.
t: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
qt: It is interesting to note that one is hard pressed to find the incrementalist appealing to Scripture as the basis for his incrementalism. This, however, is not surprising in light of our discussion showing that love of God's holy commands and worldly pragmatism do not mix.

q: What is the practical prescription the article ends with?
a: A renewed vision of God's holiness producing fresh love for His commands, which then yields a fixed, unwavering pursuit of justice that no circumstance or political expediency can warp.
t: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
qt: What we need here is a renewed vision of the holiness of God, flowing from it a new love for the commands of God, and therefore a fixation on establishing justice that won't be warped by any winds of circumstance or expediency.

q: Why does the author insist the abolitionist movement must be explicitly Christian rather than a religiously neutral coalition?
a: Because abolitionists believe Christianity is the foundation of the case against abortion, and they view the pro-life movement's avoidance of explicit Christian framing as a strategic and theological error.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: The Abolitionist Movement is explicitly Christian. Abolitionists believe that it should be an explicitly Christian movement and we think the Pro-Life Movement has erred in not being explicitly Christian.

q: What concrete example does Silberman give of a pro-life organization actively suppressing the Gospel in its outreach?
a: He cites a prominent pro-life public outreach organization that requires its volunteers to sign a form pledging not to share the Gospel unless the person they are engaging brings up religion first.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: As an especially egregious example, one prominent pro-life public outreach organization makes its volunteer activists sign a form establishing that they won't share the Gospel with people unless the person they're talking to brings up religion first.

q: If the secular pro-life argument is correct that killing innocent humans is wrong, why does Silberman call it insufficient?
a: Because the foundational claim of secular humanism is that humans have no intrinsic moral value, and a secular pro-life argument leaves that foundation untouched, refuting only a surface-level claim while conceding the underlying worldview.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: The foundation of the secular humanist position on abortion – that human beings do not have intrinsic moral value – is left untouched by secular pro-life arguments, as these arguments fail to get at the foundation.

q: What outdated assumption about abortion supporters does Silberman say has been falsified by years of public outreach?
a: The assumption that once people acknowledged the humanity of the preborn child they would oppose abortion. In reality, most abortion supporters now concede the child's humanity but still defend the mother's right to kill.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: It has long been common pro-life thought that once people acknowledged the humanity of the preborn child, they would oppose abortion. This is understandable but is ultimately an outdated and falsified pro-life speculation.

q: How does the author characterize the typical abortion supporter he encounters in public engagement?
a: Not as someone confused about embryology, but as someone morally comparable to a tyrant who knowingly endorses the killing of human beings.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: Most abortion supporters are not the embryology equivalent of a flat-earther, they are the moral equivalent of Genghis Kahn.

q: Which Salon writer does Silberman cite to show that some abortion supporters openly admit abortion ends a human life?
a: Mary Elizabeth Williams, who concedes that life begins at conception while still defending abortion on the grounds that not all life is equal.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: "So what if abortion ends life? I believe that life starts at conception. And it's never stopped me from being pro-choice."

q: Beyond rhetorical strategy, what philosophical defect does the author identify in any moral argument that does not appeal to God's word?
a: Without an objective standard rooted in God's word, even a correct moral claim is arbitrary and rests on a foundation no firmer than the secular humanist's.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: An argument against abortion (or any moral claim whatsoever) without reference to the objective standard by which something can adequately be called wrong is an arbitrary argument.

q: According to Schaeffer and Koop, what is the only thing that can stem the tide running from abortion to infanticide and euthanasia?
a: The certainty of the absolute uniqueness and value of people, which can only be grounded in the knowledge that human beings are made in the image of God.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: The only thing that can stem this tide is the certainty of the absolutely uniqueness and value of people. And the only thing which gives us this is the knowledge that people are made in the image of God.

q: Why does Silberman insist that the battle against abortion cannot be fought in piecemeal fashion?
a: Because abortion is a manifestation of an entire worldview, secular humanism, that is opposed point-for-point by Christianity, and only a comprehensive worldview-level confrontation can address the root.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: We have to understand that [the battle] is one total entity [secular humanism] opposed to the other total entity [Christianity].

q: What twofold theological foundation does the author identify as the heart of the abolitionist movement?
a: The image of God in man as the key to the anti-abortion argument, and the incarnation of Christ as the engine that empowers the movement through God's saving and redeeming Spirit.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: The image of God in man is the key to the anti-abortion argument, and the incarnation of God in Christ for the purpose of defeating death and redeeming all who believe is the engine of the abolitionist movement.

q: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison to answer the modern claim that 'all life is not equal'?
a: He invokes Garrison's appeal to Genesis, arguing that the image of God in every human being is a death-blow to all hierarchies of human worth and all claims of dominion over the lives of others.
t: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
qt: "'So God created man in his own image – male and female he created them.' This is a death-blow to all claims of superiority, to all charges of inferiority, to all usurpation, to all oppressive dominion."

q: How was SB13 actually killed in committee, and who was involved in the decision?
a: Senate Health and Human Services chairman Jason Smalley convened a private meeting with Republican committee members (excluding the bill's author Joseph Silk) to count votes. When it became clear there weren't enough votes to kill the bill openly, Smalley conferred with Treat and unilaterally denied SB13 a hearing.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: When the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (OK SB13) was introduced, Senate Health and Human Services chairman Jason Smalley (R-Stroud) held a private meeting with his Republican committee members except for SB13 author Joseph Silk (R-Broken Bow) to determine whether there were enough votes to kill it, according to a source close to the situation. When it became apparent there weren't, Smalley, after deliberating with Treat, denied SB13 a hearing.

q: What grassroots tactics did abolitionists use to pressure Smalley and Treat after SB13 was denied a hearing?
a: Activists rented billboards naming Smalley as the man keeping abortion legal, distributed thousands of doorhangers throughout his district, and drove email and phone-call campaigns to both Treat's and Smalley's offices.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: Billboards were rented sharing with Oklahomans the message that Smalley is the one keeping abortion legal in Oklahoma. Thousands of doorhangers were placed on front doors in his district. Large amounts of emails and phone calls were sent to the offices of both Treat and Smalley voicing displeasure.

q: What was SB195, and why does Silberman consider it a cowardly substitute for SB13?
a: SB195 was a trigger bill that would only restore pre-Roe Oklahoma abortion law if Roe were first overturned by the Supreme Court or amended out of the U.S. Constitution. Silberman calls this the move of someone who pretends to care about babies but is committed to obeying the federal courts.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: Treat quickly authored and introduced OK SB195, a bill which would trigger a return to pre-Roe state abortion law upon Roe being overturned or abortion being abolished in the US Constitution. Killing an abolition bill and putting forward a bill like this is what someone does when they pretend to care about babies being murdered but are dead set on licking the boots of the feds.

q: What is the constitutional basis the article gives for states refusing to obey Roe?
a: Silberman argues that states are not bound by court opinions that are themselves illegal or immoral. He claims states are both constitutionally and morally obligated to ignore rulings like Roe, and links to a separate piece on nullification and interposition.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: Contrary to popular belief, states are under no obligation to obey arbitrary, illegal court opinions, especially opinions as depraved as Roe's legalization of murder. In fact, states are constitutionally and morally obligated to ignore such rulings.

q: How did Treat amend SB195 after abolitionists rejected the first version, and why is that even worse?
a: Treat changed SB195 into a ballot referral asking voters to amend the state constitution to clarify it doesn't allow abortion, while explicitly preserving enforcement of the Supreme Court's pro-abortion opinion. Silberman calls this weaker than the original because it lets Treat appear to defer to voters while ensuring nothing actually changes.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: This time, he amended SB195 so it gives Oklahoma voters a chance to vote on amending the State Constitution to clarify that it doesn't allow for abortion. The bill specifies that the Supreme Court's opinion that states must allow abortion will still be enforced. This is even weaker than the previous version of the bill, but for Treat, it represents washing his hands of the matter and pretending that he's putting abortion's abolition in the hands of voters when he's doing no such thing.

q: Why does the article frame Treat's deference to the Supreme Court as a violation of Matthew 15?
a: Silberman casts judicial supremacy as a man-made tradition that voids the law of God, paralleling the Pharisees Jesus rebuked for elevating their traditions over divine commands. By killing SB13 to honor Roe, Treat allegedly enshrines a 'commandment of men' as doctrine.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: The concept of obsequious submission to the Supreme Court up to and including obeying its opinion that murder should be legal is an evil, unconstitutional "commandment of men" which Treat holds as doctrine. By killing SB13, Treat is making the tradition of judicial supremacy his doctrine while voiding God's word and the US Constitution.

q: How does the article connect Jesus's greatest-commandment teaching to legislative votes on abortion?
a: Silberman invokes Matthew 22 to argue that obeying Roe is incompatible with both loving God (treating His law as worthless) and loving neighbor (upholding rulings that classify some neighbors as subhuman and killable). The vote becomes a direct test of the two greatest commandments.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: One does not love God if they treat His law as worthless while treating the Supreme Court's whims as unimpeachable and supreme. They do not love their neighbor if they help uphold evil rulings that their neighbors are subhuman and can be legally murdered.

q: What is the rhetorical irony Silberman draws from Treat's earlier use of Matthew?
a: Treat himself once cited Matthew to call colleagues who opposed a tax increase 'cowards.' Silberman turns that label back on Treat by pointing to two other Matthew passages he says Treat needs to read.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: Treat once quoted from the Gospel of Matthew while calling his colleagues in the legislature who opposed a tax increase cowards. There are two other passages from Matthew which Treat needs to read.

q: What concrete action does the article say Treat must take to redeem his record?
a: Silberman tells Treat to bring SB13 directly to the Senate floor for a vote and to actively work to pass it. If he does, abolitionists will welcome him and forgive his prior actions; if not, he will be remembered as the man who kept abortion legal in Oklahoma.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: Senator Treat must stop hating his neighbor and stop nullifying the Constitution and God's word by obeying Roe. He must bring SB13, the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, directly to the Senate floor for a vote and do everything in his power to make sure it passes.

q: What political bind does Silberman say Treat is trying to navigate?
a: Treat is portrayed as trying to keep abortion legal while still appearing pro-life to voters who are catching on to GOP lip-service. The article frames every successive bill (SB195 and its amendment) as another attempt to thread that needle without ever genuinely ending abortion.
t: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
qt: Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat (R-Oklahoma City) is going into overdrive to keep abortion legal while maintaining pro-life voters.

q: What exactly did the SBC's 2021 resolution call for, and how did it characterize pro-life incrementalism?
a: The resolution called for the immediate abolition of abortion without exception or compromise and asked the convention to lament its complicity in incremental pro-life regulations that codify when, where, why, and how legal abortion may be obtained. It treated incrementalism as a compromise of God's standard of justice rather than a strategic stepping stone.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: the Southern Baptist Convention (SBC) adopted a mold-breaking resolution calling for the “immediate abolition of abortion without exception or compromise,” and further, for the convention to “lament any complicity” in pro-life incrementalism which “compromises God’s holy standard of justice” by establishing “incremental, regulatory guidelines for when, where, why, and how to obtain legal abortion of innocent preborn children.”

q: How did the modern abolitionist movement begin within the SBC, and who sparked it?
a: It began roughly a decade before the resolution, when an Oklahoma graduate history student (T. Russell Hunter) was tasked with leading a small SBC church discussion on supporting the Pro-Life Movement. Studying 19th-century abolitionists alongside contemporary pro-life messaging convinced him the movement needed reform, and the resulting Abolitionist Society of Norman launched as a ministry within an SBC church.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: A decade ago, a small group within a Southern Baptist church in Norman, OK met to discuss supporting the Pro-Life Movement. The task of leading the discussion was assigned to a graduate student in history at the University of Oklahoma.

q: What historical figures shaped the founder's reframing of pro-life work as abolitionism?
a: He drew from Christian abolitionists who demanded immediate abolition of the great evils of their day in the name of Christ — Wilberforce, Garrison, and Heyrick — and noticed how little the modern Pro-Life Movement's tactics and messaging resembled theirs.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: his historical studies simultaneously led him to the works and biographies of William Wilberforce, William Lloyd Garrison, Elizabeth Heyrick, and other abolitionists who demanded the immediate abolition of the evil of their age in the name of Jesus Christ.

q: Why did the early movement struggle to gain traction with American clergy?
a: After internal church frictions led to the disfellowshipping of core members of the Norman society, slander related to the excommunication followed the abolitionists wherever they went, scaring off pastors — even ones who initially praised the movement's gospel-centeredness, like Paul Washer.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: However, slander related to the excommunication seemed to follow them everywhere they went, causing pastors they encountered to reject them, with only a few brave exceptions. Some pastors — even prominent ones like Paul Washer — loved the gospel-centeredness of the movement and said so publicly before the slander frightened them away.

q: What was the political breakthrough that gave abolitionists their first foothold in legislation?
a: In 2016, after meeting with two abolitionists, Oklahoma State Senator Joseph Silk became an abolitionist and filed the first bill to abolish abortion immediately in explicit defiance of Roe v. Wade, marking the movement's first legislative beachhead.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: In 2016, abolitionists finally got the break they had been working and praying for. After meeting with two abolitionists — one of whom was the aforementioned history student — Oklahoma State Senator Joseph Silk became an abolitionist and filed the first bill to abolish abortion immediately in explicit defiance of Roe.

q: What event in 2019 finally galvanized Oklahoma Southern Baptists against their own denominational leadership?
a: On February 21, 2019, the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma published an open letter to every SBC church in the state announcing that the convention would oppose Sen. Silk's abolition bill. The reaction from rank-and-file Baptists was swift: a capitol rally, pastor roundtables, and a flood of complaints to BGCO leadership.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: On February 21, 2019, the Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma (now called Oklahoma Baptists) proved true what many had chalked up as an abolitionist conspiracy theory, by publishing an open letter to every Southern Baptist church in the state explaining that they, as a convention, were going to oppose Senator Silk’s abolition bill. The response from Southern Baptists in the State was swift and severe.

q: Which pastoral conversion does Silberman call the most stunning, and why does it matter?
a: BGCO president Blake Gideon — who had co-authored the 2019 letter opposing immediate abolition — publicly joined the abolitionists in 2020. Silberman frames it as the symbolic moment abolitionists ceased being ecclesiological outcasts and began preaching the cause from pulpits that had once opposed them.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: The most stunning pastoral conversion came when BGCO president Blake Gideon, who co-authored the letter opposing immediate abolition in 2019, joined the ranks of the abolitionists in 2020. No longer were abolitionists ecclesiological outcasts.

q: How did the SBC Resolutions Committee initially try to block the abolition resolution, and what made the response especially damning?
a: The Committee declined the resolution on the grounds that a Hyde Amendment funding resolution (Resolution #3) supposedly already addressed abortion. Yet the same Committee approved a resolution demanding the Chinese government cease its Uyghur genocide "immediately," exposing an inconsistency in calling for prophetic justice abroad while settling for funding language at home.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: Yet, the Committee approved Resolution #8 regarding the ongoing genocide of the Uyghur people in China. This resolution, as opposed to Resolution #3, called for the Chinese government to “cease its program of genocide against the Uyghur people immediately” (emphasis mine).

q: What procedural maneuver forced the resolution before the full convention, and how rare is it?
a: Pastor Bill Ascol motioned to override the Resolutions Committee — a move that requires a two-thirds majority and, by Ascol's account, had not succeeded since at least 1979. More than 80 percent of messengers voted in favor, scheduling the resolution for deliberation the next day.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: abolitionist pastor Bill Ascol motioned to force the Committee to bring the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion before the full convention. Overriding the Committee takes a two-thirds majority, which, according to Ascol, had not been successfully accomplished since at least 1979.

q: What floor amendment did Silberman argue rendered article 14 incoherent?
a: A messenger added the word "alone" to article 14, so it only rejected pursuing incrementalism by itself. Silberman argues this nullifies the article's logic: if incrementalism truly challenges God's lordship and rejects His call to repent immediately, it cannot coherently be pursued alongside immediatism.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: It doesn’t compute that we should pursue both incrementalism and immediatism if incrementalism “challenges God’s Lordship over the heart and the conscience, and rejects His call to repent of sin completely and immediately.”

q: Despite the article 14 amendment, why does Silberman insist the resolution still represents an immediatist victory?
a: He notes that more than a dozen other places in the resolution describe incrementalism as "appalling" and "God-hating partiality" to be "lamented," so the document's overall posture remains unmistakably immediatist — a point even mainstream outlets like The Tennessean and the New York Times noted in their coverage.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: While article 14 no longer makes sense, there are more than a dozen other places in the resolution where incrementalism is referred to as “appalling” and “God-hating partiality” to be “lamented.” Immediatism remains the perfectly clear position of the resolution.

q: What does Silberman predict about SBC and ERLC leaders' efforts to walk the resolution back?
a: He predicts their repudiation efforts will fail, comparing them to trying to stop the wind by grabbing it, and frames abolitionism as the only biblical response to abortion — a truth he says the broader Church is finally grasping.
t: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
qt: Since the passage of the resolution, pro-life SBC and Ethics & Religious Liberty Commission leaders have worked to repudiate the resolution; but it will be as if they were trying to stop the wind by grabbing hold of it.

q: What three groups does Dusty Deevers identify within the current Pro-Life Movement?
a: Deevers divides Pro-Lifers into the Establishment (Big Pro-life), the Conflicted-consistent who hold the biblical view but still self-label as pro-life, and the Blurry Middle who sense the compromise but remain stuck in midwayism.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt:
1. Establishment or Big Pro-life
2. Conflicted-consistent
3. Blurry middle

q: What are the four defining tenets of the Pro-Life Establishment according to their May 12, 2022 coalition letter?
a: The Establishment treats women seeking abortion as second victims, refuses to support equal protection from fertilization, treats post-abortive trauma as evidence of victimhood, and commits to incrementalism that never criminalizes the act of preborn homicide.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt:
1. “Second-victim.” Women seeking preborn homicide (abortion) are always victims.
2. Deny equal protection from fertilization/conception. Never support any measure or legislation to criminalize the act of preborn homicide from fertilization for all parties to the homicide.
3. Post-abortive trauma is evidence of victimhood.
4. [Incrementalism](https://freethestates.org/immediate-not-gradual/index.html). Continue to pursue a pragmatic, incremental strategy that at no point criminalizes the act of preborn homicide.

q: Why does Deevers say the Pro-Life Establishment results in implicit Christological heresy?
a: By denying personhood from fertilization and refusing to criminalize preborn homicide as murder, the Establishment’s logic also denies the true personhood of Christ and refuses Him equal protection from conception.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt: 3. Results in implicit Christological heresy—not affirming the true personhood of Christ or granting Him equal protection from fertilization/conception.

q: How does Deevers characterize the relationship between Big Pro-life and equal-protection legislation in the states?
a: He says Big Pro-life actively wars against equal protection bills and has a documented record of slandering the Christians pushing them, with examples in Idaho, Indiana, Oklahoma, and Texas.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt: Big Pro-life wars against equal protection/criminalization bills in every state and actively [slanders, misaligns, and lies](https://freethestates.org/2022/09/sbc-president-slanders-sbc-pastor/index.html) about Christians holding to a consistent, biblical position.

q: What does the Conflicted-consistent group affirm, and what does Deevers tell them to do about it?
a: They affirm life and personhood from fertilization and the duty to grant equal protection by criminalizing preborn homicide, but Deevers calls them to stop assuming the Establishment can be reformed and to #PLEXIT into Abolitionism.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt: This group needs to #PLEXIT. Leave the Pro-Life Movement for the biblically consistent [Abortion Abolition](https://freethestates.org/abolitionism-101-resources/index.html) position.

q: Why does Deevers argue the Pro-Life Establishment cannot be reformed from within?
a: Because Big Pro-life is structurally compromised—funded by donors who want abortion exceptions and committed to secular pragmatism—so individuals may convert but the institution itself will remain biblically compromised.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt: It is to say that Big Pro-life always will be biblically compromised, well-funded by those wanting abortion exceptions and allowances, and politically secular and pragmatic.

q: How does Deevers describe the Blurry Middle's posture and the recipe that keeps them stuck?
a: The Blurry Middle sees Big Pro-life’s compromises and feels pulled toward consistency, but a mix of pragmatism, tradition, and Pro-life pride yields a lukewarm “midwayism” rather than abolition.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt: The third Pro-life group sees the compromises of Big Pro-life and is tugged toward a more biblically consistent position, but a pint of pragmatism, a teaspoon of tradition, and an ounce of Big Pro-life pride is a sure recipe for a bland, lukewarm loaf of midwayism.

q: What does the GATES acrostic stand for in Abolitionism?
a: GATES summarizes the five biblical tenets of Abolitionism: Gospel-centered, Aligned Providentially, Through the Church, Engaged Biblically, and Sought Immediately without Compromise.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt:
G – Gospel-centered  
A – Aligned Providentially  
T – Through the Church  
E – Engaged Biblically  
S – Sought Immediately without Compromise

q: What five things must an actual abolition bill do, per Deevers?
a: An abolition bill must outlaw abortion from fertilization, include no homicide exceptions, criminalize preborn homicide with equal protection, refuse to submit to unconstitutional rulings like Roe, and repeal or supersede statutes permitting preborn homicide.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt:
1. Outlaw abortion from fertilization/conception.
2. Do not include exceptions for preborn homicide (ectopics and emergencies are *not* preborn homicide).
3. Criminalize preborn homicide and establish justice as equal protection for the preborn.
4. Do not submit to unconstitutional rulings (like Roe).
5. Repeal or supersede all statutes which allow for preborn homicide.

q: What is Deevers' closing call to action for Christians still inside the Pro-Life Movement?
a: He calls for a mass exodus of Christians from the Pro-life Movement into Abolitionism—the #PLEXIT—because abortion is murder and it is past time to act like it.
t: The Three Categories of Pro-Lifers
qt: It’s past time for a mass exodus of Christians from the Pro-life Movement. It’s past time for a Christian Pro-life exit in favor of abolitionism. It’s time to #PLEXIT.

q: What does Oklahoma SB13 actually propose to do?
a: SB13, titled 'The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act,' would extend equal protection of state law to unborn children from the moment of fertilization and treat any contrary federal statutes, regulations, executive orders, or court rulings as void.
t: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
qt: It is the intent of the Legislature to provide to unborn children the equal protection of the laws of this state; to establish that a living human child, from the moment of fertilization upon the fusion of a human spermatozoon with a human ovum, is entitled to the same rights, powers, privileges, justice and protections as are secured or granted by the laws of this state to any other human person; and to treat as void and of no effect any and all federal statutes, regulations, executive orders and court rulings, which would deprive an unborn child of the right to life.

q: Why does the article say abortion remains legal in Oklahoma despite a supermajority Republican legislature?
a: Silberman names three men, National Right to Life VP Tony Lauinger and State Senators Jason Smalley and Greg Treat, as the specific obstacles. Lauinger lobbies against SB13, Smalley chairs the committee where it has been buried, and Treat presides over the Senate.
t: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
qt: Abortion remains legal in Oklahoma because of National Right to Life Vice President Tony Lauinger, and because of pro-life State Senators Jason Smalley (R-Stroud) and Greg Treat (R-Oklahoma City). Lauinger, who also is President of Oklahomans for Life, has long lobbied against SB13 and previous versions of the bill. Smalley is chairman of the Oklahoma Senate Health and Human Services committee where SB13 has been assigned and Treat is the Pro Tempore of the Oklahoma State Senate.

q: What is the human cost the author attributes to Smalley and Treat refusing to hear the bill?
a: Silberman estimates that roughly 5,000 babies will be murdered in Oklahoma in 2019 because the men refuse to grant SB13 a committee hearing.
t: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
