a: Working with a single egg is inefficient, so doctors use fertility drugs to bring multiple eggs to fertilization, which inevitably leaves more embryos than parents intend to implant.
t: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
qt: When IVF started, we were just working with the one egg…The problem is going after one egg isn't as efficient because you might not get that egg, the egg may not fertilize, it might not grow. So using fertility drugs, what a doctor is doing is rescuing those extra eggs…so rather than just one egg getting to the finish line, multiple eggs are getting to the finish line…The problem is, as soon as you have more embryos than you're comfortable placing back in utero, now you have to do something with those extra embryos…

q: What estimate does Dr. Gordon give for the number of embryos currently frozen in the United States, and how fast is that number growing?
a: He estimates at least 1.5 million frozen embryos nationally, growing by roughly 18 percent each year, many of whom were never reclaimed by their parents.
t: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
qt: at least probably 1.5 million frozen embryos in the country and it goes up by about 18 percent a year.

q: How does Dusty Deevers define idolatry in the context of the IVF discussion?
a: He defines it as elevating something good into an ultimate thing whose absence makes you unable to be satisfied.
t: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
qt: Idolatry is taking a good thing and make it an ultimate thing, such that you cannot be satisfied unless you have it.

q: How does C.R. Cali extend Deevers' definition of idolatry as it applies to fertility treatment?
a: Cali adds that idolatry pushes a person to do anything to obtain the idolized thing because it has become the source of their happiness and identity.
t: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
qt: And you would be willing to do anything to get that thing because that's the source of your happiness. That's the source of your identity.

q: How does Mr. P. summarize his repentance and the moral logic he had used to justify IVF?
a: He calls his participation an evil means to a good end, an idolatrous self-deception in which his envisioned life justified the unethical method.
t: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
qt: I have repented of my involvement in IVF. It is something I deeply regret. As I wrap it up from a conclusionary standpoint, I was willing to participate in an unethical and evil way of trying to accomplish a good thing. But the ends don't justify the means. I think so many couples get to the place where they self-deceive; at least I did, where it was the ends will justify the means. At the end of the day, it's idolatry, but it's basically — what I've envisioned for my life, that coming to fruition justifies it.

q: What lesson did Mr. P. draw from Job that reframed how he and his wife approached infertility?
a: He concluded that, like Job, infertile couples are ultimately left with the question of whether they will trust God, even without an explanation, and that trust must be enough.
t: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
qt: I guess after that reflection and prayer, I came to the realization that that is basically what we are left with when it comes to infertility. We are left with the decision — and I do believe God gives us the decision — 'are you going to trust me?' And at the end of the day, that has to be enough.

q: How does Silberman characterize the value of Cali's work for the Church given his limited resources?
a: He calls the documentary an invaluable resource produced on a small budget that pushes the Church to think more deeply about IVF's theology and impact on image bearers.
t: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
qt: Cali, a small town pastor, did not have a large budget when he began this project, but he has provided an invaluable resource to the Church that we may come to think more deeply about this practice, its theological implications, and its impact upon God's image bearers.

q: What setting does Haggard choose for his parable, and why does the timing matter?
a: He stages the encounter on Christmas, during the candlelight service, just as the gathered church is about to receive the benediction. By placing the unborn child's cry inside the very moment Christians celebrate the Incarnation, Haggard pointedly contrasts the church's worship of the Word-made-flesh with their refusal to defend other unborn children.
t: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
qt: It was Christmas, and the church was gathered for the candlelight service. The carols were sung, the prayers were said, the sermon was preached. But as the people bowed their heads for the benediction, there came from a dim corner the sound of one weeping, though no one could be seen.

q: What reason does the church give for refusing to intervene on the unborn child's behalf?
a: The congregation, after whispered discussion, claims they cannot find a specific biblical instruction that addresses the unborn child's situation. This appeal to alleged scriptural silence becomes the parable's central indictment of churches that demand explicit commandments before acting against injustice.
t: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
qt: After more whispered discussion they answered, “No, child. We can find no specific instruction in the Scripture regarding such cases.”

q: How does the child interpret the church's refusal, and how does the church respond?
a: The child reads inaction as hatred and asks what he has done to deserve it. The church protests that they do not hate him, exposing the gap between their self-perception and the moral weight of their refusal to love a neighbor in mortal danger.
t: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
qt: The child wept bitterly. “Why do you hate me? What have I done?”

q: How does the church narrow the scope of the love command to excuse itself?
a: They claim that nowhere in Scripture are they expressly commanded to love those still in the womb, treating the absence of a verse that names the unborn as a license for inaction. Haggard exposes this as a theologically dishonest move that demands hyper-specific commandments rather than applying the plain moral logic of Scripture.
t: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
qt: “Dear child”, they said, “it is simply that we have no clear commandment from the Lord. Nowhere are we expressly bidden to love those yet in the womb.”

q: What is the unexpected request the still, small voice finally makes?
a: After falling silent, the child asks the church to regard him as their enemy. It is a devastating reversal: he would rather be hated under a clear commandment than ignored under a manufactured one, because hatred would at least obligate the church to act.
t: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
qt: “Regard me as your enemy.”

q: What scriptural commandment does the child invoke to corner the church's reasoning?
a: He cites Jesus' explicit command to love one's enemies. By framing himself as an enemy, the child traps the church's own logic: if they will not love him as a neighbor for lack of a verse, they are still bound by the commandment to love enemies, and so their refusal to act has no excuse left.
t: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
qt: “Because then you would have clear commandment, for the Lord said, ‘*love your enemies*.’”

q: Why does the parable describe the child's voice as a 'still, small voice,' and what does that allusion accomplish?
a: The phrase echoes the prophet Elijah's encounter with God on Horeb, where the Lord came not in wind or fire but in a still, small voice. By giving that biblical idiom to the unborn child, Haggard implies that God himself is speaking through the child's plea, and that the church's deafness to abortion is deafness to God.
t: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
qt: But then, just as the people were about to turn away, a still, small voice said through fresh tears, “then will you do this one thing for me?”

q: According to Silberman, who reignited the modern Abolitionist Movement and how?
a: Silberman credits Russell Hunter with reanimating the Garrisonian principles of immediatism and applying them to the fight against abortion roughly a decade before this article was written, which he describes as the spark that reignited abolitionism.
t: Why Abolitionists Are Going to Win
qt: A decade ago, he reanimated the Garrisonian principles of immediatism, applied them to the fight against abortion, and reignited the Abolitionist Movement.

q: If the movement has so many talented people, why does Silberman insist talent is not the reason it will win?
a: Silberman concedes that diverse gifts have grown the movement, but argues that talent alone cannot guarantee victory because many talented groups have failed; victory will instead come from the moral weight and justice of the abolitionist message itself.
t: Why Abolitionists Are Going to Win
qt: After all, lots of groups of talented people have failed to achieve their objectives. **Abolition** is going to be achieved because of the weightiness of the truth abolitionists proclaim.

q: How does Silberman characterize the pro-abortion 'choice' argument, and what does he say will end the practice?
a: He calls the 'choice' justification for killing preborn children 'unashamedly depraved' and predicts that sustained, faithful preaching by the Church about the horrors of abortion will eventually bring it to an end, even though nations can sink to such depravity for a time.
t: Why Abolitionists Are Going to Win
qt: Nations can and do succumb to this level of depravity, but a relentless and “faithful delineation of the horrors” of abortion by the Church of the living God will bring its practice to an end.

q: What is Silberman's case against the establishment pro-life position of submitting to Roe?
a: He labels unconditional submission to the Supreme Court 'unashamedly absurd' in light of 63+ million deaths, points out that even eight Republican-appointed justices upheld Roe in 1992, and argues that the recent June Medical Services v. Russo decision shows there is no overturn of Roe in sight.
t: Why Abolitionists Are Going to Win
qt: The establishment pro-life claim that though 63+ million human beings and counting have been murdered by abortion, we must submit unconditionally to the supreme court’s bloody tyranny, is unashamedly absurd.

q: What is the core abolitionist pronouncement that Silberman says cannot be denied forever?
a: He frames it as a two-part claim: that abortion is murder, and that it is sinful for any officeholder at any level to submit to Roe and thereby deliver children to abortionists. He admits this can be denied 'for a time' but not in perpetuity.
t: Why Abolitionists Are Going to Win
qt: The pronouncements of the abolitionists –  that abortion is murder and that it is a sin for any office holder at any level to submit to the evil of *Roe* and thereby submit the children to the abortionists’ knife – cannot be seriously denied in perpetuity.

q: What concrete obstacles does Silberman acknowledge abolitionists currently face in elections?
a: He admits incumbent pro-life politicians often have roughly seven times the funding of abolitionist challengers, that wealthy PACs spend tens of thousands more against abolitionists, and that opponents spread slander painting abolitionists as 'cultists' or 'violent thugs.'
t: Why Abolitionists Are Going to Win
qt: For now, incumbent pro-life politicians with seven times as much money as their abolitionist challengers and wealthy PACs spending tens of thousands more on their behalf can survive reelection.

q: What is Silberman's final reason for confidence that abolitionism will succeed?
a: He grounds confidence in the proposition that 'truth is weighty' and that if the abolitionist message reaches the masses (which he says it is), it is only a matter of time before equal protection for preborn human beings is established, provided abolitionists keep their hands to the plow.
t: Why Abolitionists Are Going to Win
qt: If abolitionists keep their hands to the plow, it is only a matter of time before equal protection for preborn human beings is established.

q: Whom does Silberman name as exemplary pastors and organizations within the movement?
a: He highlights pastors including Dusty Deevers, Thomas Lowery, Tim Gillespie, Brett Baggett, Sam Ketcher, Bill Ascol, Kelly Greene, and Chris Gore, alongside organizations like Abolish Abortion Texas, Apologia Studios, Operation Save America, Missionaries to the Preborn, and the state abolitionist societies.
t: Why Abolitionists Are Going to Win
qt: Pastors like Dusty Deevers, Thomas Lowery, Tim Gillespie, Brett Baggett, Sam Ketcher, Bill Ascol, Kelly Greene, and Chris Gore are stellar expositors and practicers of God’s word.

q: What was the author's initial theory about why pro-life leaders rejected abolitionism, and how was it disproven?
a: Silberman initially believed pro-life leaders rejected abolitionism because of abolitionists' attitudes, so he set out to be the 'nice abolitionist.' His theory collapsed almost immediately when even a maximally gentle, nuanced critique of Abby Johnson provoked belligerent slander and accusations from a pro-life friend, demonstrating that the issue was not tone but substance.
t: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
qt: I decided I was going to be the nice abolitionist. Surely people wouldn’t reject the ideas if they were explained to them kindly and articulately, I thought. My theory was quickly disproven.

q: According to Silberman, what is the real reason pro-life leaders reject abolitionism?
a: He argues the rejection stems from threats to political power and personal pride, not from any genuine grievance about abolitionist behavior. Pro-life leaders cannot admit that decades of incremental strategy have failed, so they manufacture justifications for refusing immediate abolition.
t: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
qt: The pro-life leaders who reject abolitionism do so because the Abolitionist Movement is a threat to the political power of the Pro-Life Establishment and we’re a threat to the pride of people who don’t want to admit that they haven’t been going about the fight against abortion rightly.

q: How does Silberman defend the harsh tone abolitionists sometimes take toward pro-life politicians?
a: He concedes abolitionists are harsh, but insists the harshness is a consequence of pro-life refusal to abolish abortion, not the cause of it. The criticisms follow specific failures, such as senators going to the media against SB13, rather than preceding them.
t: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
qt: But that harshness is a response to the refusal of these people and groups to immediately abolish abortion, not what causes them to refuse to abolish abortion.

q: Why does Silberman say abolitionist criticism targets the pro-life lobby rather than the pro-abortion lobby?
a: Because in states like Oklahoma the pro-abortion lobby lacks the political power to stop abolition. With pro-life supermajorities holding every relevant office, the actual decision-makers blocking abolition are pro-lifers, so that is where pressure must be applied.
t: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
qt: It’s pro-lifers who have the choice before them of whether to abolish abortion. That is where the pressure must be applied.

q: What role does repentance play in Silberman's argument, and why does he frame it as a Christian obligation?
a: Silberman cites pastor Brett Baggett to argue that repentance is normal Christian life, not something shameful. Since incrementalism has driven groups like National Right to Life to actively oppose immediate abolition, the foundational ideology is wrong and demands public repentance rather than continued defense.
t: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
qt: When we’re wrong, the Christian obligation is to own it. The fact that adherence to pro-life incrementalism has driven organizations like National Right to Life to become the primary opposition to the immediate abolition of abortion is evidence that something is terribly wrong at the foundation of the ideology, and therefore the movement.

q: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison as a model for handling past pro-life involvement?
a: He highlights the lesser-known passage in The Liberator where Garrison publicly recanted his earlier endorsement of gradual abolition. Silberman explicitly mirrors this act, recanting his own past support for incremental pro-life bills and calling others to do the same.
t: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
qt: I, too, recant and repent for my unthinking past endorsement of incremental/gradual pro-life bills. As difficult as it may be for some, I earnestly plead and pray that many more join me in that repentance and join me in demanding total and immediate abolition without compromise.

q: What kinds of slander does Silberman say pro-lifers spread about abolitionists, and how does he respond?
a: He recounts being publicly labeled 'evil,' equated with 'God hates fa*s' sign-holders, and accused of calling unwed mothers 'whores.' He flatly denies these characterizations and says anyone who actually behaved that way would not be welcome in the movement.
t: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
qt: This is vicious and willful slander. Anyone who refers to unwed mothers as “whores” or holds signs saying “God hates fa*s” is not welcome in the Abolitionist Movement.

q: What pattern does Silberman identify in how Free the States escalated its rhetoric against specific Oklahoma senators?
a: He documents that Free the States only criticized senators like Smalley, Treat, and McCortney after those officials publicly worked against SB13. The group even ran positive flyer campaigns urging McCortney toward heroism before he ultimately killed the bill.
t: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
qt: Free the States didn’t make one ill-statement toward Senators Jason Smalley or Greg Treat until they went to the media to spread anti-SB13 talking points.

q: What is the scriptural foundation Free the States invokes to justify defying Roe v. Wade?
a: Silberman opens with Acts 5:29, framing SB13 as Oklahoma's institutional act of obeying God rather than men in the face of a Supreme Court order to allow mass murder. He treats Roe as a direct command to neglect the biblical duty to love one's neighbor and establish justice for the fatherless.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: When the government demands we choose between obedience to man or obedience to God, scripture is perfectly clear about what we are to do. In Acts 5, Peter and the apostles get into trouble for teaching in the name of Jesus. Verse 29 records their response to the governing authorities: "But Peter and the Apostles answered, 'We must obey God rather than men.'"

q: What are the five components that make a bill a true bill of abolition?
a: SB13 (1) recognizes life begins at conception and grants equal legal protection from that moment, (2) makes no exceptions for murder including rape or life-of-the-mother, (3) criminalizes abortion equally for all participants including the mother, (4) is not contingent on Supreme Court approval and contains nullification language, and (5) repeals existing pro-life regulations because those regulations codify the legality of abortion under certain conditions.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: Here are the five components of SB13 that make it consistent with these verses and make it a bona fide bill of total and immediate abolition.

q: Why does SB13 refuse the standard pro-life exception for pregnancies conceived in rape?
a: Silberman argues that the child conceived in rape is the most fatherless person imaginable and that punishing the child for the father's crime is exactly the iniquitous decree Isaiah 10 condemns. He further contends that any exception undermines the entire moral case against abortion, because murder cannot stop being murder based on the circumstances of conception.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: There is no one more fatherless than the child conceived in rape. It is unacceptable to allow for the murder of a baby because of the crime of the father. Furthermore, allowances for abortion in certain scenarios undermine the whole case against abortion.

q: Why does SB13 criminalize the mother rather than granting her automatic immunity like most pro-life bills?
a: Silberman cites Justice Blackmun's footnote 54 in Roe, which held that Texas's automatic immunity for mothers proved Texas did not really view the preborn as persons. Granting immunity, he argues, both denies the preborn equal protection and effectively legalizes self-induced abortion via abortion pills.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: To write a law outlawing abortion but giving automatic immunity to the parents means that the mother is free to induce her own abortion. SB13 does not fail to provide equal protection to preborn humans and therefore does not legalize self-induced abortion.

q: How does SB13 handle the Supreme Court's authority over state abortion law?
a: SB13 nullifies Roe rather than submitting to it, leaning on the doctrine of the lesser magistrate: state executives, legislators, and judges are obligated to interpose between abortion victims and a tyrannical federal judiciary. The bill includes explicit language voiding any contrary federal statute, regulation, executive order, or court decision.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: Any federal statute, regulation, executive order or court decision which purports to supersede, stay or overrule this Act is in violation of the Constitution of the State of Oklahoma and the Constitution of the United States of America and is therefore void.

q: Why must SB13 repeal Oklahoma's existing pro-life laws like the partial-birth abortion ban and parental notification?
a: Silberman argues every pro-life regulation codifies the legality of murdering a preborn child under specified conditions: parental notification legalizes abortion when grandparents are informed, the dismemberment ban legalizes abortion when a different technique is used, and so on. Because these statutes contradict an outright abolition of abortion as murder, they must be repealed for SB13 to function.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: Abortion cannot be abolished while there are still laws on the books saying abortion is legal as long as you jump through pro-life hoops.

q: Could a court strike down only the abolition portions of SB13 while keeping the repeals, leaving Oklahoma with no abortion regulations at all?
a: No. Section 34 of SB13 declares the bill's provisions inseverable, so a judge cannot uphold the repeals while striking the abolition. Silberman accuses Tony Lauinger and the Oklahoma Baptists of repeating this claim despite being directly informed it is false.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: Section 34 of SB13 reads "The provisions, words, phrases, and clauses of this act are declared to be inseverable." A judge could not strike down parts of the bill while upholding others.

q: What is the problem with Senator Bullard's SB1721, the 'Unborn Person Equality Act'?
a: SB1721 begins with strong language about life beginning at conception but caveats Part C with a clause subjecting all of its protections to Supreme Court 'decisional interpretations,' which renders the entire bill contingent on a court that has reaffirmed Roe thirty-seven times. Silberman frames this as obeying men while merely asking the court for permission to obey God.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: Where SB13 says, "We will obey God rather than a supreme court opinion that Oklahoma must allow the mass murder of preborn humans," SB1721 says, "We will obey men rather than God when the supreme court opines that we should allow the mass murder of preborn humans, but we ask the supreme court to let us obey God."

q: Why does Silberman call HB1182 (Rep. Jim Olsen's medical-license bill) 'legal nonsense'?
a: HB1182 only strips a doctor's medical license and adds a small criminal penalty without nullifying Roe, so it cannot take effect without court approval and undermines its own moral logic by treating abortion as a licensing infraction rather than murder. A state cannot coherently revoke a license for an act that state law still permits.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: How can a state take away a medical license from a doctor for performing certain acts when that same state's laws say that the act is legal? And how can a state argue that abortion is murder if the penalty for doing it is a mere loss of license and a possible one-to-three year sentence and $500 fine? HB1182 is legal nonsense that undermines itself and would not abolish abortion.

q: Why are incremental pro-life bills not merely insufficient but actively counterproductive?
a: Incremental bills give politicians like Greg Treat cover to refuse abolition, codify the supreme court's claim of authority over the question, and teach the public dehumanizing falsehoods. Silberman cites a young woman at a Norman killing center who believed her abortion was acceptable because the Pain-Capable Unborn Child Protection Act had taught her babies who can't feel pain may be killed.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: Incremental pro-life bills are not simply less good than abolition bills. They are counterproductive. They are the excuse politicians use to not abolish abortion. They instill in the culture dehumanizing misinformation about the preborn and the idolatrous idea that we owe unconditional submission to the supreme court.

q: What historical abolitionist precedent does Silberman draw on to attack the strategy of gradual abolition?
a: He marshals the American Anti-Slavery Society's 1833 Declaration of Sentiments, William Lloyd Garrison's 'No compromise with slavery!' and Elizabeth Heyrick's pamphlet Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition. Heyrick's argument is that gradualism originated with the slaveholder himself as a satanic device to dull the moral outrage that would otherwise destroy the system.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: But this GRADUAL ABOLITION, has been the grand marplot of human virtue and happiness; the very masterpiece of satanic policy. By converting the cry for immediate, into gradual emancipation, the prince of slave holders, 'transformed himself, with astonishing dexterity, into an angel of light,' and thereby 'deceived the very elect.'

q: What evidence does Silberman offer that a 'paradigm shift' toward abolition is underway?
a: He points to three concrete actions in Oklahoma: the Free Will Baptists' May 2019 unanimous resolution authored by Pastor Tim Gillespie, the Oklahoma Baptists' (SBC affiliate) November 12, 2019 platform amendment introduced by Pastor Bill Ascol calling for the immediate end of abortion, and the Oklahoma Republican Party amending its platform to call for an SB13 floor vote.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: These resolutions are the result of a paradigm shift that is taking place toward rejecting compromises with abortion and demanding the killing be stopped immediately. This paradigm shift is happening around the country, but is especially far along in Oklahoma.

q: What does Silberman say is the actual obstacle to passing SB13 in Oklahoma?
a: Republicans hold supermajorities in both chambers, so the votes exist; the obstacle is the political choice to pass incremental regulations instead of abolition, and the silence of the church. Silberman quotes a December 2019 conversation with a legislator who said 10,000 Oklahomans showing up at a rally would force passage.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: In a state like Oklahoma where pro-life Republicans hold super-majorities in the Senate and the House, it's not that we cannot pass a bill abolishing abortion because we do not have the votes, it's that we could pass a bill abolishing abortion but choose to pass pro-life bills regulating abortion instead.

q: What is the church's specific obligation in a culture practicing industrial-scale child sacrifice?
a: Silberman invokes R.C. Sproul's warning that a silent church in the midst of holocaust ceases to be a real church, then turns to Isaiah 1:12-17 to show God's refusal to hear the prayers of those whose hands are full of blood. The remedy is to seek justice, correct oppression, and bring justice to the fatherless.
t: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
qt: A Christian who lives in a culture that practices child sacrifice on an industrial scale is obligated by the law of love to demand the abolition of that practice. It must be demanded with passion and it must be demanded by all.

q: Why does the author insist that vehement, severe rhetoric is necessary rather than gentle persuasion?
a: Riley argues that the entrenched culture of death is so massive and frozen that only sustained heat can dissolve it, quoting Garrison's metaphor of an iceberg larger than any in the arctic. Soft persuasion may suit the willing listener, but for those who persist in evil, heat is required.
t: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
qt: There is a place for speaking gently to those who are willing to listen, but to those who won't listen who persist in their evil, heat is necessary.

q: How does the author justify mocking and ridiculing evil rather than only reasoning with it?
a: He grounds the practice in God's own mockery of pagan idols in Scripture, citing Psalm 135 and Isaiah 46, and argues that idols stop looking beautiful once they are exposed as empty and pathetic. Verbal assault on idols is presented as a form of liberating those who cling to them.
t: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
qt: How do you dislodge idols from the hands of those who love them? You assail them verbally and make them look as empty, evil, and pathetic as they truly are. You expose idolaters as creatures of the dark and call them to the light.

q: What model of historical reform does the article hold up, and what does it reject?
a: Riley holds up reformers who refused to compromise truth for coalition-building and sought infamy and division rather than popularity. He rejects the modern instinct to soften principle in order to assemble broad alliances, arguing past reformers drew sharp lines and demanded purity.
t: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
qt: Reformers of the past didn't compromise truth to coalition build; they didn't seek popularity, they sought infamy and division. They set themselves apart from their culture and drew lines in the sand.

q: What biblical figures are offered as examples of the dangerous heat the author commends?
a: He names Stephen, whose witness brought stoning, and John the Baptist, whose rebuke of Herod cost him his head, alongside Garrison's printing press as a modern parallel. The pattern is that uncompromising speech invites violent reaction but ultimately topples evil regimes.
t: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
qt: It was the brilliant heat that Stephen gave off that got his skull crushed by hundreds of stones, it was the heat off of John the Baptist's tongue that burned Herod and got John's head chopped off, it was the heat off of Garrison's press that brought the slave nation to its knees, and it will be the heat of a fiery sword that slays the abortion dragon of the West.

q: What is the stated goal of abolitionist agitation, according to the article?
a: The aim is not merely to disturb or pressure the existing system but to destroy it entirely. Persistent attack, not the popularizing of life-affirming alternatives, is presented as the mechanism by which the culture of death is killed.
t: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
qt: The goal is not to merely rock the boat but to sink the ship. The culture of death dies by persistent attack, not by popularizing life.

q: How does Riley extend Garrison's critique from southern slaveholders to northern allies?
a: He notes Garrison attacked not only the slave state of the South but the racism of northerners who professed to oppose slavery while benefiting from the system and dehumanizing slaves through their own efforts. The parallel implied is that today's pro-life establishment can profess opposition to abortion while perpetuating it.
t: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
qt: The attacks he leveled not only against the slave state of the South but the racism of northerners who benefited from the system while simultaneously purporting to oppose it with show bills and efforts that dehumanized the slave.

q: Why does the article reject incremental pro-life bills as inconsistent with being "all on fire"?
a: Riley argues that proposing legislation which sacrifices some children to save others uses the same altar of judicial supremacy as the abortion industry itself. Only immediate abolition matches the consistency of God's love and avoids being a stench before Him.
t: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
qt: Immediate, not incremental, abolition is the only standard we can adopt if it be truly principled. You cannot be "all on fire" as Garrison while proposing bills that sacrifice some children to save others, burning them on the same altar of judicial supremacy the world burns them on.

q: What does the author identify as the core reason the corrupt status quo persists?
a: He claims that comfort and stability are the human default, while change feels chaotic and uncertain, so people instinctively defend whatever they already know. This frames the abolitionist's vehemence as the necessary counterweight to inertia, not gratuitous aggression.
t: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
qt: The abortion-loving, pornified, plastic status quo exists because people love the status quo. The norm is comfort and stability. Change is chaotic and uncertain. People are sure to cling to what they know.

q: What is the central moral claim Sam Riley is making in this piece?
a: Riley's thesis is that passive toleration of abortion is itself a form of participation in it. He treats inaction by citizens, officials, and Christians as morally indistinguishable from the act of killing, because the system of legal abortion only continues with their consent.
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: You uphold the system! You allow the murder! Your hands are as bloody as theirs!

q: Why does Riley think a sincere pro-life sentiment isn't enough?
a: Because feelings that don't translate into the actual abolition of abortion accomplish nothing for the children being killed or, in his framing, for God. Emotion without effective action is merely evidence of one's humanity, not a discharge of moral duty.
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: You can feel deep hatred in your soul when you hear about abortion and know that it is a horrible injustice, which shows your humanity, but if it doesn't result in abolition what good is it to anyone?

q: Which specific groups does Riley name as complicit in abortion?
a: He lists a chain of enablers: police, delivery drivers, pastors, ordinary Christians, abortionists' customers and neighbors, mothers seeking abortions, judges bound by precedent, and legislators who refuse to act. The breadth of the list is itself the argument: complicity is widespread, not confined to the abortion provider.
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: Police officers who allow it to continue, delivery men supply weapons on command, pastors who fail to denounce it, Christians who won't take a stand, abortionists eat at your businesses, mothers fill the demand, judges rule unjustly by precedent, legislators held back by their hand!

q: How does Riley respond to calls for patience and incremental progress?
a: He treats appeals to patience as a pathetic excuse offered while children continue to die. For Riley there is no morally legitimate timeline of delay; justice for victims of homicide cannot be deferred to a politically convenient future.
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: "Patience! Peace!" You pathetically cry as children are fed to the feds. The time for justice is always now.

q: What does the article say about pinning hopes on Trump or Amy Coney Barrett to end abortion?
a: Riley dismisses it as 'passing the buck'—a way to volunteer politicians as a substitute for personal obedience. He frames the posture as cowardly outsourcing of a duty that scripture lays on the reader directly.
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: "Amy Coney Barrett will do it, Trump will do it, there they are Lord, send them, not me." How long can you pass the buck?

q: What kind of action does Riley call readers to instead of trusting courts and political compromise?
a: He calls for ending compromise, abandoning hope in human courts and creeds, appealing instead to divine authority, and treating the neighbor-love command with the seriousness of someone facing personal judgment ('act like you will get the axe').
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: End the slaughter, stop your compromise, destroy your hope in courts and creeds and appeal to the divine Court and the divine Creed. Love your neighbor as yourself and act like you will get the axe!

q: What religious accusation does Riley level at silent Christians and pastors?
a: He accuses them of idolatry—comparing forty-seven years of legal abortion to burning incense to foreign gods, and likening silent religious people to a group bowing to Baal. Silence in the face of child-killing is, in his framing, a form of false worship.
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: Forty-seven years are in the hole where we dumped the children, forty-seven years of burning fetal incense to foreign gods.

q: Why does Riley use such graphic, confrontational language?
a: He argues that people refuse to recognize the reality of abortion until they are forced to confront the physical horror of it. The graphic register is a deliberate rhetorical attempt to strip away the abstraction that makes complicity comfortable.
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: Must we sift through the remains of their bloody carcasses before we can say we created the hell we live in?

q: How does Riley characterize people who try to soften or moderate abolitionist demands?
a: He says they ask for regulated killing rather than its abolition, give legal cover to murder, and brand consistent abolitionists as extremists in order to stay socially comfortable. In his framing, moderation is itself the mechanism by which the killing is preserved.
t: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
qt: Your demands are for sharper knives for butchers and not the abolition of the slaughter! You frame iniquity by statute and give approval to the killer! You call those who want it ended extremists and you hastily disavow!
